frontend/src/app/workspace/agents/[agent.description: This folder implements the agent-scoped chat UI for a specific conversational thread inside the workspace. It contains a Next.js client page that wires thread lifecycle hooks, streaming, and UI primitives, and a layout component that composes the chat runtime providers for that thread. The folder is responsible for per-thread behavior (settings, streaming lifecycle, token usage display, notifications) and must be edited with awareness of provider contracts and router handling for thread creation/navigation.
key_behaviors: Thread lifecycle: initialize or resume a Thread when the chat page is opened, and reflect thread creation in browser history without remounting the route.
Message submission and streaming: send user messages to the thread stream, consume and render streaming agent responses, and expose a stop control to halt streaming.
Per-thread settings and model overrides: load and apply thread-scoped settings (model selection, overrides) distinct from global/local settings.
Token usage and telemetry display: surface token usage (TokenUsageIndicator) in the agent chat header and keep metrics updated during/after streaming.

Focus/notification behavior and artifact access: show browser notifications when a conversation finishes while the page is not focused; provide access to artifacts/exports and per-thread controls (title, todo list).

insights.title: Thread-scoped settings must be used instead of global local settings
insights.problem: Agent-specific chat pages were previously wired to the global local settings hook, which prevented per-thread model overrides from being applied.
insights.root_cause: The page consumed a global/local settings hook rather than a thread-scoped settings hook, so per-thread configuration (model, temperature, etc.) was ignored.
insights.solution: When working in this folder, ensure the chat page consumes and respects thread-scoped settings hooks/providers; otherwise edits risk removing the ability to override model/config per thread and will change runtime behavior for agent-specific chats.

insights.title: Token usage indicator must be present in agent chat header
insights.problem: Agent-specific chat pages did not display the TokenUsageIndicator, so users lacked visibility into token consumption for agent interactions.
insights.root_cause: The TokenUsageIndicator component was not integrated into the agent chat page header composition.
insights.solution: Maintain the token usage indicator integration in the agent chat header composition and ensure any changes preserve the data flow that feeds usage metrics; omission will remove a critical usage/telemetry signal.

key_files.why: Entry point client component that implements the agent-specific chat UI, handles thread initialization/resume, message send/stream/stop, browser history updates, and composes all major chat UI primitives.
Layout wrapper that composes chat runtime providers for the agent thread; defines provider contracts and context used by page.tsx and child components.

construct_relationships: AgentChatPage (page.tsx) depends on provider contexts composed by AgentChatLayout (layout.tsx) to access thread lifecycle hooks and streaming facilities.
AgentChatPage uses thread runtime hooks (useThreadChat, useThreadStream, useThreadSettings) to initialize/resume threads, submit messages, control streaming, and load thread-scoped settings.
UI primitives (ChatBox, MessageList, InputBox, ThreadTitle, TodoList, TokenUsageIndicator, AgentWelcome) are composed by the page and rely on the layout-provided contexts for state and actions.
The page updates browser history on thread creation without triggering a full router remount, implying router state manipulation and preservation of React state across navigation.
notable_relationships: This folder relies on shared workspace hooks and UI components outside the folder (useThreadChat, useThreadStream, TokenUsageIndicator, ChatBox, MessageList, InputBox, etc.), so changes here can break contracts with those cross-folder modules.
Thread creation/navigation behavior interacts with Next.js routing and history; incorrect changes can cause remounts or loss of provider context for the thread.
Token usage display depends on telemetry/billing data sources or stores elsewhere in the frontend; removing or altering its consumption can affect billing/usage reporting UX.

Message streaming and stop behavior implicitly depend on backend streaming endpoints and SSE/WebSocket semantics; changes must respect the expected streaming lifecycle and error handling patterns used elsewhere in the app.
recommendations: Preserve the contract between layout.tsx providers and page.tsx: do not change provider names, context shapes, or expected lifecycle callbacks without coordinating the consumer hooks.

When editing message streaming or stop logic, validate the full streaming lifecycle (start, incremental updates, stop, error) and verify that UI stops cleanly and that backend stream cleanup is triggered.

Any changes touching settings must continue to use thread-scoped settings hooks/providers; confirm per-thread model overrides apply by testing model selection/temperature overrides at the thread level.
When altering routing or history update logic, ensure thread creation updates the URL without causing a full router remount and that in-page React state persists across the update.

Run the frontend unit/integration tests and manually verify: streaming responses, stop behavior, thread creation & navigation without remount, per-thread settings application, token usage indicator visibility and updates, and notification-on-completion when the page is unfocused.

frontend/src/app/workspace/agents/[agent.description: This folder implements the agent-scoped chat UI for a single conversational thread inside the workspace. It composes thread runtime providers (layout) and a client-side page that manages thread lifecycle, message streaming, per-thread settings, token usage, and in-page notifications — making it the UI surface where agent runs are started, streamed, controlled, and inspected.
key_behaviors: Thread lifecycle management: initialize or resume a Thread when the page opens and reflect thread creation in browser history without causing a route remount.
Message submission and streaming: accept user input, submit messages to the thread stream with agent context, render streaming responses, and provide a stop control to abort streaming.
Per-thread settings and model overrides: load and apply thread-scoped settings (model selection and overrides) distinct from global/local settings.
Runtime composition: wrap the page with chat runtime providers that establish thread-scoped state (workspace, uploads, sandbox bindings) and expose hooks used by the page.
Usage and notifications: display token usage in the chat header and show notifications when a conversation finishes while the page is not focused.

insights.title: Per-thread settings must be used instead of global/local hook
insights.problem: Agent chat pages previously used a global local settings hook, which prevented per-thread model overrides from taking effect for agent-specific threads.
insights.root_cause: A settings hook with application- or workspace-level scope was used in a per-thread context, creating a mismatch between UI expectations (thread overrides) and the settings source.
insights.solution: Ensure the page and providers read thread-scoped settings (from thread state/providers) rather than global settings. When editing, preserve or integrate the thread settings hook usage and propagate model selection/overrides from the thread state into the agent run context. Risk: changing the source of settings can alter which model/config applies to an in-progress thread.

insights.title: Token usage indicator must be integrated into agent chat header
insights.problem: Token usage indicator was not shown on agent-specific chat pages.
insights.root_cause: The token usage UI element was not included in this page's header composition or provider tree despite being expected for threaded agent runs.
insights.solution: When editing, preserve or add the TokenUsageIndicator into the chat header composition and ensure it reads usage data from the same thread runtime/source used by streaming logic. Risk: mismatched data sources between stream and indicator will report inconsistent token counts.

insights.title: Updating browser history without remounting must be preserved
insights.problem: Thread creation/navigation must update the browser history so the URL reflects the active thread, but it must do so without remounting the Next.js route (to preserve runtime providers and streaming state).
insights.root_cause: A naive navigation or router push could cause a Next.js remount which tears down provider state and active streaming sessions.
insights.solution: When modifying submit/creation flows, use the existing history manipulation pattern (shallow route replace/push or replaceState) that updates the URL but preserves the mounted React tree. Risk: switching to a full navigation will reset thread providers and stop streams unexpectedly.

insights.title: Stop control and finish notifications need consistent lifecycle handling
insights.problem: Stopping an active stream and signaling conversation completion (including visibility-based notifications) require consistent coordination between the streaming hook and page controls.
insights.root_cause: Streaming is managed by runtime hooks and provider state; the page must call the correct stop API and react to the stream-complete events emitted from the provider.
insights.solution: Respect the streaming hook's contract for stop/start and ensure page-level handlers call those APIs and subscribe to completion events. Preserve logic that triggers notifications when the page is not focused. Risk: calling the wrong API or unmounting providers will leave background subagents running or lose completion signals.

insights.title: Layout composes runtime providers required by page hooks
insights.problem: The page expects certain providers (thread runtime, streaming, settings) to be present and to expose specific hooks/APIs; layout composes these providers.
insights.root_cause: Tight coupling between page hooks and the provider interface; changes to provider APIs or composition order will break page behavior.
insights.solution: When editing layout or page, preserve provider composition, keep client-only semantics (Next.js 'use client' expectations), and do not change provider method names/semantics without co-editing all consumers. Risk: altering composition or client/server directives can break hook consumption and lifecycle guarantees.

key_files.why: Client page implementing the agent-scoped chat UI: wires thread lifecycle, message submission/streaming, stop control, notifications, and composes UI primitives used during an agent run.
Layout that composes the chat runtime providers for this thread; establishes the provider contracts that page hooks depend on.

construct_relationships: AgentChatPage (page.tsx) calls into thread runtime hooks (useThreadChat, useThreadStream, useThreadSettings) to initialize/resume threads, submit messages, and control streaming.

AgentChatPage renders UI primitives (ChatBox, MessageList, InputBox, ThreadTitle, TodoList, TokenUsageIndicator, AgentWelcome) that depend on data surfaced by the thread providers composed in AgentChatLayout (layout.tsx).
AgentChatLayout composes provider instances that expose the hook contracts consumed by AgentChatPage; page and layout must remain aligned on names and lifecycle semantics.

Message submission flow: handleSubmit initializes or resumes a thread, updates browser history (shallow) to reflect thread_id, then delegates to the stream API; handleStop invokes the stream stop API exposed by the provider.
notable_relationships: Depends on shared UI components and thread hooks elsewhere in the codebase (ChatBox, MessageList, TokenUsageIndicator, and hooks like useThreadStream). Changes here may require corresponding contract compatibility with those modules.
Interacts with routing/history APIs (Next.js router or window.history) to update URLs without remounting; changes affect global navigation behavior and deep linking.
Relies on a client-side-only execution model (Next.js client component expectations). Server-side rendering or removing 'use client' could break streaming and provider state.
Token usage and streaming state likely reflect backend telemetry/streaming systems; edits must account for integration with the thread's streaming backend and token accounting mechanisms.
recommendations: Preserve provider composition and the hook contracts used by page.tsx when making edits to layout.tsx or page.tsx; co-change both sides if provider API changes are necessary.
When modifying thread creation or navigation, ensure the URL update uses shallow navigation or history.replaceState to avoid remounting the route and losing provider state/active streams.
Keep per-thread settings consumption (thread-scoped) separate from any global/local settings hooks; validate that model overrides flow from thread settings into the agent run context.
Ensure TokenUsageIndicator reads from the same thread-scoped source as the streaming hook so token counts remain consistent with the active run.

Maintain the streaming lifecycle contract: start, stream events, stop, and completion notifications (including unfocused-page notifications). Run integration tests that exercise streaming start/stop, thread resume, URL updates, and token usage UI after edits.
Respect Next.js client component constraints (imports, 'use client', browser-only APIs) and run frontend type/lint/build checks (pnpm build/pnpm lint/pnpm format) and relevant UI tests after changes.

backend/packages/harness/deerflow/agents.description: This directory implements the set of agent-side middleware used by DeerFlow's lead agent and subagents to prepare runtime context, enforce safety/limits, sanitize and augment messages, and queue asynchronous side-effects (memory, auditing, subagents). These middleware components run in a defined order per model turn and directly affect the message stream, tool binding, sandbox interactions, and downstream systems (memory updater, subagent executor, sandbox providers). Changes here are high-impact because middleware mutate messages, build runtime middleware stacks, and interoperate with external services and LangGraph runtime semantics.
key_behaviors: Prepare per-thread filesystem paths and surface uploaded artifacts into the last HumanMessage so tools and sandboxes can access files.

Sanitize and augment the message stream before and after model calls: inject clarifications, view-image content, todo reminders, generated titles, token-usage observations, and placeholder ToolMessages for interrupted tool calls.
Enforce runtime safety and resource constraints: audit and block dangerous bash commands, detect repetitive tool-call loops (warn or hard-stop), and cap concurrent subagent spawns per response.
Filter what the LLM sees: remove deferred/MCP tool schemas from model binding and strip tool-calls when loop hard-limits are reached.

Intercept and convert failures into recoverable signals: classify and retry transient LLM errors, convert tool exceptions to error ToolMessages, and assemble ordered middleware stacks for lead and subagent runtimes.
Queue asynchronous side effects: prepare and enqueue filtered conversation slices to the memory update pipeline with correction/reinforcement flags and emit structured audit/usage records.

insights.title: Handle non-string AIMessage.content and list-type content blocks
insights.problem: Several middleware assumed AIMessage.content is a plain string and used string concatenation, causing TypeError when provider modes produced list/object content blocks.
insights.root_cause: Different model/provider adapters return message content as str or as lists/typed-blocks; code performed naive concatenation or returned arrays of strings where an API expects a string or typed blocks.
insights.solution: Middleware must treat message content as either string or structured blocks: normalize/safely append text by handling both types and return content in the provider-expected shape.

insights.title: Stable hashing to detect tool-call loops
insights.problem: Semantically identical tool calls escaped loop detection due to noisy argument representations (stringified JSON, non-dict types, minor numeric or slice differences).
insights.root_cause: Inconsistent normalization of tool-call args and reliance on raw string serialization caused equivalent calls to produce different keys.
insights.solution: Normalize tool-call arguments defensively into canonical forms (handle non-dict payloads, canonical JSON ordering, normalize ranges) before deriving stable keys and maintain a bounded LRU per-thread sliding history.

insights.title: Null/missing runtime.context and thread_id dependency
insights.problem: Uploads and thread-path resolution code assumed runtime.context and runtime.context.thread_id always exist, producing attribute errors and skipping uploads injection.
insights.root_cause: Some invocation paths from LangGraph may pass None for runtime.context or omit thread_id.
insights.solution: Null-check runtime.context and thread_id; implement graceful fallbacks when thread_id is absent and avoid NPEs.

insights.title: Avoid prints; use structured logging
insights.problem: Multiple middleware used print() for runtime notices (thread dir creation, clarification interception, memory skipping, image injection), polluting stdout and making structured logs and testing harder.
insights.root_cause: Quick debugging/initial implementations used print() instead of logger calls.
insights.solution: Replace prints with logger.debug/info/warn as appropriate to make output testable and configurable.

insights.title: Insert placeholder ToolMessages for dangling tool calls
insights.problem: Interrupted or cancelled tool calls left AI messages referencing tools without corresponding ToolMessages, confusing the model and causing formatting errors.
insights.root_cause: Tool execution may be interrupted, cancelled, or omitted from history; the model still sees AI text describing tool results.
insights.solution: Detect AI messages that reference missing tool outputs and inject synthetic error ToolMessages immediately after the AIMessage to preserve expected message ordering and semantics.

insights.title: Runtime middleware stack composition matters
insights.problem: Security and guardrail middleware may be omitted or applied in the wrong order for lead vs. subagent runtimes; some patches (e.g., DanglingToolCallMiddleware) were disabled for subagents.
insights.root_cause: Multiple places assemble middleware stacks (lead vs subagent) and conditional inclusion based on config led to inconsistent behavior.
insights.solution: Build middleware stacks centrally and ensure conditional inclusion respects security and message-consistency middleware for both lead and subagent runtimes.

insights.title: Deferred/MCP tools must remain executable but not bound to model prompt
insights.problem: Sending deferred tool schemas to the LLM wastes tokens and exposes tools that should be discovered at runtime.
insights.root_cause: Model binding code included all executable tools in ModelRequest.tools rather than filtering deferred/MCP tools.
insights.solution: Filter deferred tool schemas from ModelRequest.tools while preserving their runtime executability and discovery via tool_search.

insights.title: Audit and split compound bash commands
insights.problem: Dangerous commands were hidden inside compound commands (&&, ||, ;), pipelines, or obfuscations and escaped naive regex detection.
insights.root_cause: Initial regex-based classification missed compound splitting and several bypass patterns.
insights.solution: Parse/split compound commands with shlex-aware logic, apply layered classification (block/warn/pass), and produce structured audit logs and hard blocks for high-risk patterns.

insights.title: Filter ephemeral artifacts and detect reinforcement signals for memory
insights.problem: Upload bookkeeping and transient tool-call chatter leaked into persisted memory; correction/reinforcement signals were not consistently detected.
insights.root_cause: Memory pipeline received unfiltered conversation slices and lacked detection for positive reinforcement patterns.
insights.solution: Filter out upload blocks and tool messages before enqueueing memory updates and detect both corrections and positive reinforcement to forward as flags to the memory updater.

key_files.why: Central assembly point for runtime middleware stacks (lead and subagent) and the component that translates tool exceptions into error ToolMessages; editing affects which middleware run and error propagation semantics.
Implements safety logic that detects repetitive tool-call loops and modifies the message stream (warnings or hard-stop); changes here affect tool-call visibility and run termination behavior.
Computes and (optionally) creates per-thread filesystem paths consumed by uploads/sandbox middleware; modifications impact sandbox access and per-turn isolation.

Injects uploaded-file blocks into the last HumanMessage and resolves uploads_dir; changes influence what the LLM sees for file-based tasks and interact with memory filtering and thread path resolution.
Performs security classification of bash tool calls and can block or warn; edits here affect host safety and audit logs for shell execution.

construct_relationships: ThreadDataMiddleware computes thread-specific paths which UploadsMiddleware uses to validate and discover historical uploads and which sandboxes/providers mount as workspace/uploads/outputs.

UploadsMiddleware prepends an <uploaded_files> block to the last HumanMessage; MemoryMiddleware subsequently filters those messages to prevent upload bookkeeping from being persisted to long-term memory.

LoopDetectionMiddleware observes the set of tool_calls emitted by the model (after model responses) and can inject HumanMessage warnings or strip tool_calls; this affects DanglingToolCallMiddleware which expects consistent ToolMessage sequences.

ToolErrorHandlingMiddleware wraps tool execution and assembles runtime middleware stacks; it must include or order SandboxAuditMiddleware, DanglingToolCallMiddleware, DeferredToolFilterMiddleware, and LoopDetectionMiddleware appropriately to preserve security and message consistency.

DeferredToolFilterMiddleware removes deferred/MCP tool schemas from ModelRequest.tools during model binding while keeping those tools executable when invoked; this interacts with tool discovery mechanisms (tool_search) and the model's available tool-set.

ViewImageMiddleware inspects completed view_image tool outputs and injects base64 image content into the conversation before the next LLM call; TokenUsageMiddleware then records usage metadata for that model invocation.

SubagentLimitMiddleware truncates excess 'task' tool calls from the last AI message to enforce concurrency caps; Subagent executor (outside this folder) must be compatible with the truncated tool_calls and respect the configured per-response limits.
notable_relationships: These middleware operate within LangGraph/LangChain runtime semantics: runtime.context may be None and model provider adapters can return different message content shapes; middleware must interoperate with upstream LangGraph calls and provider-specific message formats.

MemoryMiddleware enqueues data to an asynchronous MemoryUpdater component (outside this folder) and relies on consistent flagged metadata (correction/reinforcement) for downstream summarisation behavior.

SandboxAuditMiddleware and Uploads/ThreadData middleware directly affect interactions with sandbox providers (backend/src/sandbox/*). Changes to paths or command auditing semantics can affect sandbox execution safety and tests that use LocalSandboxProvider or AioSandboxProvider.

ToolErrorHandlingMiddleware's assembled stacks are used both by the lead agent and subagent runtimes; divergence between lead and subagent middleware composition (e.g., omitting DanglingToolCallMiddleware for subagents) has observable behavioral and safety differences downstream (subagent executor and SSE reporting).

DeferredToolFilterMiddleware reduces token usage and changes model binding; this influences LLM prompt size and could affect token-usage logging, retry/backoff heuristics, and any logic that depends on the set of bound tools.
recommendations: Preserve message format compatibility: handle AIMessage.content as either a string or structured content blocks; when appending or returning content, emit the provider-expected shape to avoid BadRequest errors.
Always null-check runtime.context and runtime.context.thread_id before resolving thread paths or uploads; assume LangGraph may pass None.

When changing middleware ordering or the middleware assembler (build_lead_runtime_middlewares / build_subagent_runtime_middlewares), ensure downstream assumptions (DanglingToolCall, SandboxAudit, DeferredToolFilter) remain satisfied for both lead and subagent runtimes.
Avoid prints; use structured logging so tests and CI capture diagnostics consistently.

If modifying loop-detection, normalization, or LRU sizing, add unit tests covering semantically equivalent but syntactically different tool-call args (stringified JSON, list payloads, file-range variants) and exercise warn vs hard-limit paths.

If editing sandbox/audit or thread path code, run sandbox-related integration tests (preferably in Docker dev mode) because Local vs Aio sandbox behaviors and path mounts affect security and command execution.
When altering memory filtering or reinforcement/correction detection, run memory pipeline integration tests to validate enqueued flags and that upload bookkeeping is excluded from persisted memory.

Run backend unit tests, linting (ruff), and full CI matrix (including any LangGraph provider mocks) after changes; changes to middleware can alter model input/outputs, so validate token usage logging and retry behavior as well.

backend/packages/harness/deerflow/tools/.description: This folder implements the builtin LangChain/agent tools that the DeerFlow lead agent exposes at runtime. It contains tools for delegating background subagents (task), presenting artifacts and images, invoking external ACP agents, requesting clarifications, provisioning on-disk agent metadata, and a deferred tool discovery system. These tools mediate between the agent runtime (thread/workspace/sandbox), the Subagent execution subsystem, and the run state persisted by middleware, so they must respect sandbox policy, runtime metadata availability, and middleware expectations.
key_behaviors: Delegate complex work to background subagents: create SubagentExecutor, start execute_async, stream lifecycle events, poll background status, and coordinate cooperative cancellation.
Expose filesystem-oriented artifact operations: normalize/validate paths under virtual thread directories and update run state with artifacts or viewed images (base64 + MIME).
Provide runtime tool discovery: maintain a per-request deferred tool registry and promote tools to active schemas on demand so LLMs can call them.

Invoke external agent processes (ACP): spawn isolated subprocesses with per-agent workdirs, collect streamed output, handle missing binaries and permission prompts, and apply configured environment substitutions.

Surface operator interactions: tools are thin callables that return structured Commands/ToolMessages which middleware (e.g., ClarificationMiddleware, ThreadDataMiddleware, SandboxMiddleware) interprets to interrupt or update a run.

insights.title: Cooperative cancellation of background subagents
insights.problem: When the task polling coroutine is cancelled (asyncio.CancelledError), the background thread running the subagent continued processing because it could not be forcefully cancelled.
insights.root_cause: Background subagent runs in separate threads/tasks that are not force-cancelled when the polling coroutine is cancelled; the polling coroutine only manages status observation and event emission.
insights.solution: Task tool must request cooperative cancellation and schedule deferred cleanup rather than assuming immediate termination; callers should expect background tasks to continue briefly after polling cancellation and emit lifecycle events accordingly (and avoid assuming synchronous teardown).

insights.title: Thread/run identifiers may be missing from runtime.context
insights.problem: Tools assumed runtime.context and thread_id exist and crashed in environments where runtime.context was None or thread_id was absent.
insights.root_cause: LangGraph server sometimes supplies thread_id via config.configurable rather than runtime.context, and some runtime environments omit runtime.context entirely.
insights.solution: Guard all runtime.context accesses (null-safe), and fallback to config.configurable for thread_id where applicable; tools that manipulate thread-scoped paths must handle missing thread_id gracefully and return informative tool errors when necessary.

insights.title: Host-bash subagent must be explicitly disallowed
insights.problem: A bash-type subagent could be exposed in local mode even when host bash is unsafe, and task tool allowed such requests.
insights.root_cause: Task tool did not consistently validate requested subagent types against the sandbox configuration.
insights.solution: Task tool validates and rejects disallowed subagent types based on sandbox policy and returns clear messages when host bash is disabled; callers must expect explicit rejection and not try to construct unsafe subagents.

insights.title: Avoid top-level imports that cause circular dependencies
insights.problem: Top-level import of sandbox.tools in view_image_tool produced circular import errors during test collection.
insights.root_cause: Module-level imports referenced other runtime/tool modules that import this module (or modules that import it), creating import cycles at import time.
insights.solution: Move imports into function scope to break import cycles; maintain awareness that runtime import ordering can affect test collection and startup.

insights.title: Deferred tools must be promoted into active bindable set
insights.problem: Deferred tools remained filtered out after tool_search returned a tool's schema: the LLM obtained a schema but middleware kept stripping the tool from bind_tools so it could not be invoked.
insights.root_cause: Promotion step did not update the runtime/middleware-visible tool binding state; returning a schema alone is insufficient for middleware to allow runtime invocation.
insights.solution: tool_search must promote matched deferred tools into the per-request registry/state that middleware reads so the tool becomes callable during the same run.

insights.title: Per-agent environment variable resolution for ACP invocation
insights.problem: ACP agent invocation did not resolve configured environment substitutions; $VAR references in config were not passed/resolved to spawn_agent_process.
insights.root_cause: Per-agent env map from config was not processed (variable expansion) before spawning the ACP subprocess.
insights.solution: Resolve $VAR references in the per-agent env map and pass the resulting env to the spawned process; ensure substitution semantics are documented and consistent with other environment handling in the system.

key_files.why: Primary entry for delegating work to background subagents: validates subagent types, constructs SubagentExecutor, streams lifecycle events, and implements polling/cancellation behavior.
Implements the deferred per-request tool registry and promotion logic used to expose OpenAI-function-style tool schemas to the LLM and transition tools into callable state.
Handles normalization/validation of presented artifact paths under virtual outputs and updates run state with artifacts; touches thread/workspace path logic and run-state schema.
Reads and base64-encodes image files and writes viewed_images into run state; demonstrates import-on-demand to avoid circular dependencies and enforces virtual path validation and MIME detection.
Responsible for invoking external ACP-compatible agents in isolated workdirs, streaming output, handling missing binaries/permissions, and applying per-agent environment settings.

construct_relationships: task_tool constructs a SubagentExecutor (using parent run metadata and available tools) and uses the runtime/tool_call_id as the background task_id; it polls the SubagentExecutor and emits structured task lifecycle events via the run's stream writer.

present_file_tool and view_image_tool both map user-provided paths to virtual thread-scoped directories (/mnt/user-data/{outputs,uploads,workspace}), validate those paths, and return Commands that mutate the run state (artifacts, viewed_images) consumed by middleware and the Gateway.

tool_search maintains a per-request DeferredToolRegistry (ContextVar) and when a tool is matched it must promote the DeferredToolEntry into the runtime-visible bindable tool set so middleware and agent tooling will allow invocation.

invoke_acp_agent_tool spawns external processes in isolated workdirs and returns streamed text; it depends on config-provided per-agent environment mappings and permission-auto-approval logic to decide subprocess behavior.

clarification_tool exposes an API surface that is implemented by ClarificationMiddleware: calling this tool triggers middleware-driven interruption and a user clarification flow rather than internal tool logic.
notable_relationships: These tools rely on ThreadDataMiddleware and SandboxMiddleware to provision thread-scoped directories, sandbox providers, and the run state shape (thread_id, workspace/uploads/outputs virtual prefixes).

task_tool behavior is tightly coupled to the subagent executor implementation (subagents/executor.py) and expects a background executor that supports execute_async, cooperative cancellation, and status polling; changes to executor semantics affect task_tool behavior.
tool_search promotion must align with the lead_agent/tool binding process in make_lead_agent and any middleware that filters bind_tools; otherwise tools with returned schemas may remain uncallable.

present_file_tool and view_image_tool enforce virtual path restrictions consistent with LocalSandboxProvider/AioSandboxProvider virtual mount semantics; edits must respect the same virtual prefix invariants used across sandboxes.
invoke_acp_agent_tool interacts with OS subprocess spawning and permission prompts; its correctness depends on platform/compose environments and available ACP binaries in deployment images.
recommendations: Always null-check runtime.context and provide a fallback for thread_id (e.g., config.configurable) before deriving thread-scoped paths or updating run state.
Respect sandbox policy: validate requested subagent types against configuration and refuse unsafe types (host bash) with clear tool messages.
When modifying tools that update run state, preserve the exact run-state keys and shapes (artifacts, viewed_images, task lifecycle events) so middleware and downstream consumers can parse them.
Avoid module-level imports that reference sandbox/tools or other modules that may import this package; prefer function-scoped imports to prevent circular import issues during startup/tests.

If changing task lifecycle or polling behavior, maintain cooperative cancellation semantics (request cancellation and schedule cleanup) and keep polling/backoff behavior observable through emitted lifecycle events.
When adjusting deferred tool promotion or schema exposure, ensure the promotion updates the per-request registry/middleware-visible binding so tools become callable in the same run.
For ACP invocation changes, resolve and pass per-agent env substitutions before spawning subprocesses, and handle missing binaries/permission prompts explicitly to produce informative tool messages.
Run the test suite that covers middleware, sandbox, and subagent behaviors after edits (sandbox-related tests are sensitive to environment and may require Docker dev environment to pass reliably).

backend/packages/harness/deerflow/agents.description: This folder implements the agent memory subsystem: extraction prompt templates, a debounced queue that batches conversation contexts, an LLM-driven updater that validates/parses/merges facts, and configurable persistent storage (file-backed by default). It matters because memory facts and summaries are injected into agent prompts and persisted across runs; correctness here affects what the agent remembers, deduplication, concurrency, and downstream behavior of the lead agent/middleware chain.
key_behaviors: Accept conversation contexts (ConversationContext) through a per-thread, debounced MemoryUpdateQueue that batches replaces and later invokes the MemoryUpdater.
Format conversation + existing memory into token-bounded prompts (prompt module) and instruct an LLM to produce structured memory updates.

Parse LLM JSON responses, validate and coerce confidence values, deduplicate and merge new facts against existing facts (case-insensitive), sanitize session-scoped uploads, and persist changes via an abstract MemoryStorage.
Provide manual CRUD helpers (create, update, delete) for facts that validate and persist via the storage provider.
Abstract persistence through a MemoryStorage factory with a FileMemoryStorage default that caches reads and performs atomic saves while updating lastUpdated metadata.

insights.title: Case-insensitive fact deduplication
insights.problem: Duplicate facts differing only by case were stored as separate entries.
insights.root_cause: Comparison/deduplication used simple string matching without normalization.
insights.solution: Normalize keys using casefold/case-insensitive keys when deduplicating new facts against existing memory; be aware this changes identity semantics for facts and may collapse previously distinct entries.

insights.title: Reinforcement and correction detection can be lost by queue replaces
insights.problem: reinforcement_detected and correction_detected flags were sometimes overwritten when later queue.add() replaced earlier per-thread entries.
insights.root_cause: Queue.add replaced pending ConversationContext entries without merging these boolean flags.
insights.solution: Queue producers/consumers must preserve/merge reinforcement_detected and correction_detected when replacing or batching per-thread entries to avoid losing important signals.

insights.title: Long-term background and correction guidance missing from prompt injection
insights.problem: longTermBackground and explicit correction/reflection guidance were omitted from prompt injection, making the LLM unaware of long-term user context and correction handling.
insights.root_cause: format_memory_for_injection excluded longTermBackground and prompt templates did not instruct LLM about corrections/sourceError.
insights.solution: When building prompts for memory extraction/updates, ensure long-term background is available within token constraints and include structured guidance to surface corrections and sourceError where applicable.

insights.title: Storage abstraction extracted from updater
insights.problem: Memory persistence logic had been embedded in the updater, making alternate backends and testing harder.
insights.root_cause: Updater module directly performed file operations previously.
insights.solution: All I/O is delegated to a MemoryStorage interface with a configurable factory (get_memory_storage). Maintain this separation: core update logic must remain I/O-agnostic and use the storage abstraction.

insights.title: LLM parsing may drop sourceError and correction hints
insights.problem: Incoming facts from the LLM lost sourceError and correction hints during parsing/merging.
insights.root_cause: The response parsing pipeline did not surface or preserve optional fields from the LLM responses.
insights.solution: Parsing should tolerate and preserve optional structured fields (e.g., sourceError, correction hints); validators should coerce missing confidence values but retain any provided error/source metadata.

insights.title: Session-scoped uploads must be removed before persisting
insights.problem: Memory could persist references to session-scoped uploads that are invalid out of session.
insights.root_cause: No consistent sanitization step to strip session-scoped upload mentions from facts prior to saving.
insights.solution: Normalize/sanitize facts to remove session-only upload references before saving to long-term storage so persisted memory remains valid across sessions.

insights.title: Queue used print() for lifecycle/errors
insights.problem: print() calls were used for queue lifecycle and error messages instead of structured logging.
insights.root_cause: Quick debugging outputs remained in production code.
insights.solution: Use the project's logging facilities for lifecycle and error reporting to ensure consistency with monitoring and tests.

key_files.why: Central LLM-driven memory update pipeline, CRUD helpers, parsing, deduplication and validation logic; delegates persistence to MemoryStorage.
Defines MemoryStorage interface, FileMemoryStorage implementation (caching + atomic save), and factory (get_memory_storage) used by the updater and other callers.
Prompt templates and formatting utilities used to generate token-bounded prompts for LLM-based fact extraction and memory injection.
Thread-safe, debounced queue that batches conversation contexts by thread and triggers MemoryUpdater; must preserve reinforcement/correction signals and concurrency semantics.
Public surface for the memory subsystem; re-exports prompts, queue, storage, and updater so other modules import a single API.

construct_relationships: MemoryUpdateQueue holds ConversationContext entries and, after debounce, calls MemoryUpdater to process a batched update.
MemoryUpdater uses prompt.format_conversation_for_update and MEMORY_UPDATE_PROMPT to build LLM inputs and then parses the LLM JSON outputs into structured facts.
MemoryUpdater delegates all persistence operations to MemoryStorage obtained via get_memory_storage; FileMemoryStorage implements that interface and provides caching and atomic save behavior.
prompt.format_memory_for_injection formats existing facts (from MemoryStorage) into token-bounded text that MemoryUpdater injects into system prompts.

__init__.py re-exports MEMORY_UPDATE_PROMPT, FACT_EXTRACTION_PROMPT, MemoryUpdateQueue, MemoryStorage, and MemoryUpdater so other parts of the system import these constructs from a single module path.
notable_relationships: Lead agent/middleware: MemoryMiddleware in the agent middleware chain (lead_agent factory) expects memory injection/extraction behaviors from these modules — changes here affect system prompts and agent behavior.
ThreadDataMiddleware and upload handling: the memory updater/sanitizer must align with ThreadDataMiddleware virtual paths and upload semantics to correctly remove session-scoped upload references.

Subagents and concurrency: subagent executor and other concurrent components can produce reinforcement/correction signals and conversation contexts that are queued; the queue's thread-safety and flag merging behavior impacts downstream persistence.

Gateway/API and UI: CRUD helpers (create_memory_fact, update_memory_fact, delete_memory_fact) are surfaced to the Gateway and UI for manual edits — persistence and validation here affect what users see.

Storage configuration and deployment: get_memory_storage may instantiate file-backed storage or alternate providers; file-backed storage uses host FS semantics, atomic saves, and caching — configuration mistakes can lead to persistence inconsistencies in Docker vs local environments.
recommendations: Preserve the storage abstraction: keep I/O delegated to MemoryStorage and use get_memory_storage to instantiate backends; any changes to persistence must not reintroduce direct file I/O in the updater.
Respect case-insensitive dedup semantics: modifications to dedup or keying must maintain casefold normalization or explicitly account for changes to fact identity.
Maintain sanitization of session-scoped uploads and ensure ThreadDataMiddleware path conventions are honored so persisted facts do not reference ephemeral uploads.

When altering queue behavior, ensure thread-safety, debounce semantics, and merging of reinforcement_detected and correction_detected flags are preserved; update tests that rely on flush/reset behavior.

Keep LLM parsing tolerant: preserve optional fields such as sourceError and correction hints, coerce/validate confidence values, and ensure prompt templates include longTermBackground and correction/reflection guidance within token budgets.
Preserve FileMemoryStorage atomic save and caching semantics; any change to persistence must consider atomicity, lastUpdated metadata, and race conditions in concurrent updates.

Replace any print() usages with the project's logging facility and run the backend test suite (make test / uv run pytest) plus queue/unit tests after edits; run CI linting/formatting to satisfy project invariants.

frontend/src/app/workspace/chats/[thread.description: This folder contains the chat workspace route for a single thread: a client-side page that composes the thread UI (header, message list, todo list, input) and a layout that provides workspace-scoped React context providers (subtasks, artifacts, prompt input). It is the glue layer between thread-scoped runtime hooks/streams, browser-only behavior (notifications, input focus), and workspace-level context, and is sensitive to server-vs-client rendering and lifecycle concerns.
key_behaviors: Mount and manage a thread-scoped streaming lifecycle: initialize useThreadStream on mount, listen for run events, and handle cleanup.
Render the chat UI header and controls (thread title, token usage indicator, export/artifact triggers) tied to thread settings and telemetry.
Defer browser-dependent components (InputBox) until client mount to avoid server/client hydration mismatches.
Provide workspace contexts (SubtasksProvider, ArtifactsProvider, PromptInputProvider) to child pages so message list, todo list, and input can access shared state.
Navigate seamlessly when a thread is created/renamed by replacing history state and issuing desktop notifications when runs finish while the page is unfocused.

insights.title: Client-only InputBox to avoid hydration mismatch
insights.problem: Hydration mismatches and UI overlays occurred when InputBox (which uses browser-only APIs or mutable state) was rendered during server-side render.
insights.root_cause: InputBox depends on browser-only behavior/state that differs between server and client render (Turbopack/Next SSR vs client mount).
insights.solution: Defer rendering of InputBox until client mount; treat InputBox as a client-only component and ensure server render does not include it to avoid markup/behavior differences that break hydration.

insights.title: Respect thread-scoped settings over global defaults
insights.problem: Chat page previously used global settings and ignored per-thread overrides, causing incorrect behavior or UI state for threads with custom settings.
insights.root_cause: Thread pages were not reading or applying useThreadSettings (or equivalent thread-scoped configuration) when composing header/UI behavior.
insights.solution: Ensure thread-scoped settings are retrieved and applied at page level before rendering controls and initializing stream listeners so thread overrides take precedence.

insights.title: Token usage and run completion UX tied to thread lifecycle
insights.problem: Token usage indicator and run-finish notifications were not integrated into the thread header or lifecycle, reducing visibility for users.
insights.root_cause: Header composition and stream lifecycle hooks were not wired to token usage telemetry and document focus/run-finish notifications.
insights.solution: Wire TokenUsageIndicator into the chat header and use thread stream lifecycle events to trigger desktop notifications when a run completes while the page is unfocused; preserve focus checks and replaceState navigation semantics.

key_files.why: Primary client-only chat page: mounts thread stream, composes header/controls/message list/todo list, defers InputBox until client mount, and handles navigation/notifications.
Route layout that provides SubtasksProvider, ArtifactsProvider, and PromptInputProvider; must remain in place and ordered so child pages receive expected context.

construct_relationships: ChatPage initializes and subscribes to useThreadStream to receive streaming run events and uses those events to update message list, show notifications, and drive header state.
ChatPage reads thread-scoped configuration via useThreadSettings and applies those settings to header controls, input behavior, and stream initialization.

ChatLayout wraps ChatPage with SubtasksProvider, ArtifactsProvider, and PromptInputProvider so ChatPage and its children consume those contexts for todo lists, artifact triggers, and prompt input state.
ChatPage defers mounting of InputBox (a browser-dependent construct) until client mount to avoid SSR/client mismatch; message list and header render on server while InputBox is client-only.
Header components (ThreadTitle, TokenUsageIndicator, export/artifact triggers) are composed within ChatPage and rely on thread stream and settings for live state.
notable_relationships: Relies on external hooks/providers (useThreadStream, useThreadSettings, SubtasksProvider, ArtifactsProvider, PromptInputProvider) defined elsewhere in the codebase; changes here must honor those contracts.
Interacts with browser APIs (history.replaceState, Notification/document focus) and Next.js SSR/CSR boundaries — editing client/server rendering behavior will affect hydration and user-visible flows.
Artifact triggers and export controls likely call backend Gateway APIs or artifact services; layout providers expose context that mediates these interactions.
Token usage indicator consumes telemetry/state that may be produced by stream events or separate usage APIs; header wiring influences billing/telemetry visibility downstream.
recommendations: Preserve the client-only deferral for InputBox (or replicate equivalent guard) to avoid server/client hydration mismatches; verify changes under Next.js dev/build and with Turbopack.
Do not remove or reorder the SubtasksProvider, ArtifactsProvider, and PromptInputProvider in layout; children expect these contexts with their existing contracts.
When modifying stream initialization/cleanup, ensure useThreadStream lifecycle handling (subscribe/unsubscribe) remains correct and that desktop notification logic still checks document focus.

Any edits to header controls must continue to read thread-scoped settings (useThreadSettings) and update TokenUsageIndicator integration; confirm behavior for newly created threads (history.replaceState usage).

Run client-side integration and SSR/hydration checks after edits (next build / dev), and smoke-test thread lifecycle flows: stream delivery, run-complete notifications, input focus, and artifact/export triggers.

backend/packages/harness/deerflow/commun.description: This package provides a community AIO (agent-infra) sandbox integration: a client (AioSandbox) that talks to a sandbox HTTP API for executing commands and manipulating files, a pool-and-lifecycle manager (AioSandboxProvider) that provisions and reuses sandbox containers, pluggable backends for local Docker-based or remote-provisioner sandboxes, and a SandboxInfo dataclass for cross-process discovery/serialization. It matters because it bridges the runtime agent middleware to isolated execution environments (containers), handling mounts, readiness, concurrency, platform differences, and cross-process coordination required for safe sandboxed execution.
key_behaviors: Provisioning and discovery: create/adopt sandboxes via LocalContainerBackend or RemoteSandboxBackend and expose SandboxInfo for cross-process reuse.
Pooling and lifecycle: maintain a warm pool, deterministic per-thread sandbox assignment, idle eviction, and graceful shutdown/signal handling.
Execution & file ops: provide an HTTP-backed client to execute shell commands and perform file reads/writes/globs with retries and error normalization.
Mount and environment mapping: compute and map host thread directories, uploads, outputs, and skills into container mounts with platform-aware path resolution.
Health and readiness: poll sandbox health endpoints (wait_for_sandbox_ready) before use and adopt existing containers when name/port conflicts occur.

insights.title: Serialize exec_command calls to avoid corrupted persistent shell session
insights.problem: Concurrent execute_command calls against a single persistent AIO sandbox shell could corrupt the shell session, producing 'ErrorObservation' outputs and cascading failures.
insights.root_cause: The AIO sandbox exposes a single persistent shell session; concurrent writes/commands interleave and corrupt session state.
insights.solution: Serialize execution calls at the client boundary and implement retry logic on ErrorObservation; any edits must preserve single-session serialization and retry normalization.

insights.title: Host-side mount sources must be Windows-aware
insights.problem: Docker bind mounts on Windows required Windows-style host paths; converting Paths to POSIX strings broke mounts on Windows.
insights.root_cause: Path conversion code assumed POSIX-style strings; Windows Docker requires drive-letter / backslash-preserved host paths.
insights.solution: Use appropriate Path helpers and preserve Windows-style host paths when computing mounts; maintain conditional logic that produces platform-correct mount sources.

insights.title: Exclusive file locking must be platform-conditional and robust
insights.problem: File locking used fcntl unconditionally and attempted to unlock even when lock acquisition failed, causing failures on Windows and potential race conditions.
insights.root_cause: Implementation assumed POSIX fcntl support and did not branch for Windows semantics or verify lock acquisition before unlocking.
insights.solution: Ensure locking is platform-agnostic: acquire conditionally, only unlock if held, and use appropriate Windows-compatible primitives where needed; preserve cross-process discovery guarantees.

insights.title: Deterministic naming, port allocation, and adopt-on-conflict behavior
insights.problem: Name conflicts or port collisions occur when starting containers; adopting existing containers is part of robust behavior but must be deterministic.
insights.root_cause: Containers are created with deterministic names/ports; startup races or leftover containers can cause conflicts unless adoption/retry logic is resilient.
insights.solution: Keep deterministic naming/port allocation and resilient adoption/retry semantics (inspect, adopt, or retry) and ensure edits keep those behaviors and associated retries/timeouts intact.

insights.title: SandboxInfo serialization is a contract across processes
insights.problem: Sandbox metadata is serialized and shared across processes; incompatible changes can break discovery and reuse.
insights.root_cause: Multiple processes rely on to_dict/from_dict shapes to discover running sandboxes and reuse warm pool entries.
insights.solution: Preserve backwards-compatible serialization for SandboxInfo, and treat its schema changes as potentially breaking for cross-process discovery; maintain helpers that support older formats where present.

insights.title: Readiness polling and error normalization are essential for reliable provisioning
insights.problem: Consumers expect a sandbox to be ready before use; missing or brittle polling can yield transient failures at runtime.
insights.root_cause: Provisioning can be asynchronous; clients must wait for a health endpoint and treat transient errors with retries/timeouts.
insights.solution: Retain wait_for_sandbox_ready polling semantics, configured timeouts, and normalized health/error handling between backends and provider to avoid races.

key_files.why: Client-facing HTTP-backed sandbox implementation performing command execution, file operations, exec serialization, retries and error normalization.
Lifecycle and pooling manager that composes backends, computes mounts, implements deterministic IDs, warm-pool reuse, eviction, and cross-process discovery with locks.
Local container backend that actually starts/stops/adopts Docker containers, handles mounts, port allocation and deterministic container naming.
Remote provisioner backend that delegates create/destroy/discover/health to an external API and translates responses into SandboxInfo.
Schema/serialization for sandbox metadata used for cross-process discovery and persistence; a compatibility contract.

construct_relationships: AioSandbox (aio_sandbox.py) issues HTTP calls to the AIO sandbox API to run commands and perform file ops; it relies on serialization and retry behavior to keep a persistent shell session healthy.

AioSandboxProvider (aio_sandbox_provider.py) uses a SandboxBackend implementation (LocalContainerBackend or RemoteSandboxBackend) to provision/discover sandboxes and returns SandboxInfo records for callers.
LocalContainerBackend (local_backend.py) implements the SandboxBackend interface (backend.py) to start/inspect Docker containers, allocate host ports, and produce SandboxInfo used by the provider.
RemoteSandboxBackend (remote_backend.py) implements the same SandboxBackend contract but calls an external provisioner HTTP API and converts provisioning responses into SandboxInfo.

SandboxInfo (sandbox_info.py) is produced/consumed by provider and backends for persistence and cross-process lookup; provider uses file locks and host-path computations to coordinate shared sandbox records.
notable_relationships: Cross-package: This community package plugs into the broader sandbox selection logic elsewhere in the repo (the main aio_provider / sandbox layer). Changes here affect middleware (SandboxMiddleware, ThreadDataMiddleware) that expect mounts at host-side thread directories (/mnt/user-data/{workspace,uploads,outputs}) and skills mounting.

Platform interop: Mount path handling and file locking interact with host OS semantics (Windows vs POSIX). Host environment, Docker engine, and Path utilities elsewhere must align with conversions done here.

Process coordination: SandboxInfo serialization and file-locking are used for cross-process discovery; other processes (e.g., gateway, agent workers) will read the same records and expect backward-compatible shapes.

Runtime safety: Warm-pool eviction, idle timeouts and graceful shutdown/signal handling affect availability and resource cleanup of sandboxes consumed by agent runs; other subsystems assume predictable lifecycle behavior.
recommendations: Preserve exec serialization and retry/error-normalization logic around AioSandbox.execute_command; concurrent execution semantics are safety-critical.
When changing mount/path logic, ensure platform-aware (Windows/Posix) behavior and use existing Path helpers to compute host-side mount sources exactly as other code expects.
Do not alter SandboxInfo serialization shape without coordinating cross-process consumers — maintain backward-compatible to_dict/from_dict behavior or add explicit versioning.
Any changes to file locking must remain conditional on platform support and only attempt unlocks when a lock was successfully acquired to avoid Windows errors; add or run platform-specific tests.
Maintain deterministic sandbox ID/naming, port allocation and adopt-on-conflict semantics; updates can affect container adoption and must be tested for startup races.

When editing provisioning/eviction/timeouts or health-polling, run integration tests that exercise: provisioning (local + remote), warm-pool reuse, eviction under idle load, readiness polling, and command execution including concurrent attempts and ErrorObservation retry paths.

backend/packages/harness/deerflow/agents.description: This folder builds and configures the lead LangChain/LangGraph agent used for DeerFlow runs. It resolves the runtime model, constructs the system prompt (including a cached, refreshable skills section), assembles the ordered middleware chain (including optional token-usage middleware and summarization/todo-list middleware), and exposes the make_lead_agent factory used by the runtime. Its responsibilities are central to correctness of runtime behavior (model choice, prompt content, middleware ordering) and to performance (non-blocking skill cache refresh, avoiding synchronous disk I/O on request paths).
key_behaviors: Resolve the runtime model for each run with safe fallbacks and pass resolved model objects to middleware and summarization components.
Build and return a fully-wired lead agent: model, tools, system prompt, ordered middleware chain, and per-run state schema.
Render the system prompt with dynamic sections (enabled skills, mounts, subagent rules) using a background-refreshable cache to avoid blocking I/O.
Assemble middleware in a deterministic order while conditionally inserting optional middlewares (e.g., TokenUsageMiddleware) based on configuration flags.
Expose synchronous and asynchronous helpers to warm, invalidate, and read the enabled-skills cache to support both coroutine and blocking call sites.

insights.title: Summarization middleware must receive resolved model objects
insights.problem: Summarization middleware was constructed using raw config.model_name strings (or aliases) instead of resolved model objects, causing incorrect model selection for summarization tasks.
insights.root_cause: Code passed model name strings (including un-mapped aliases) instead of creating a chat model via the project model factory/resolution path.
insights.solution: Resolve model aliases through the central create/resolution path before constructing middleware so all middleware receives the actual model object instance.

insights.title: Skills loading can block request paths and destabilize prompt construction
insights.problem: Loading enabled skills touches disk and was performed synchronously during prompt rendering, slowing startup and potentially causing failures or long latencies on request paths.
insights.root_cause: Prompt builder reloaded skills repeatedly and did not use a background refresh or robust caching; skill I/O occurred inline.
insights.solution: Keep a background refresh worker plus sync-friendly helpers; ensure prompt building reads the cached skills and that cache warm/invalidate functions exist and handle I/O errors gracefully.

insights.title: Skill-evolution toggles and prompt memoization require stable cache invalidation
insights.problem: Toggling features like the skill-evolution section caused stale or incorrect prompt sections because the cache key/signature wasn't robust and cache invalidation semantics were missing.
insights.root_cause: Prompt section memoization lacked a signature that included toggle state and did not expose deterministic invalidation paths.
insights.solution: Use a cache signature that includes toggle parameters (e.g., skill-evolution flag) and expose explicit invalidation and warm helpers used by both blocking and async code paths.

insights.title: Optional middlewares must be inserted deterministically and respect config flags
insights.problem: TokenUsageMiddleware and other optional middlewares were sometimes inserted without respect to feature flags, causing unexpected telemetry or ordering issues.
insights.root_cause: Middleware assembly logic did not consistently check configuration before insertion and/or did not centralize ordering assumptions.
insights.solution: Centralize middleware assembly that respects config-driven opt-ins and preserves strict ordering required by downstream expectations (e.g., ThreadData before Sandbox, Summarization after Memory).

insights.title: Background skills refresh must support coroutine and sync callers safely
insights.problem: Background refresh worker and synchronous helpers need to interoperate with async call sites and avoid race conditions or event-loop blocking.
insights.root_cause: Cache helpers exposed both sync and async entry points but changes elsewhere assumed one model, risking deadlocks or blocking the event loop.
insights.solution: Provide non-blocking background refresh; expose both sync and async read/invalidate/warm helpers that coordinate via thread-safe primitives, and ensure callers in async contexts use the async helpers.

key_files.why: Factory that resolves the runtime model, builds summarization/todo-list middleware, assembles ordered middleware chain (including optional TokenUsageMiddleware), and returns the fully-wired lead agent used by the runtime.
Constructs the agent system prompt and manages the background cache of enabled skills (warm/invalidate helpers, cache worker), which affects prompt contents and runtime performance.
Exports the make_lead_agent factory for import by the surrounding LangGraph/Gateway runtime; changes here affect import surface and consumers.

construct_relationships: make_lead_agent (agent.py) calls the prompt builder in prompt.py to obtain the rendered system prompt and may use cache warm/invalidate helpers to prime skills before building the agent.
agent.py resolves model names via a central resolution path before creating summarization middleware; summarization middleware factory expects an actual model object rather than a raw name/alias.

The middleware assembly in agent.py conditionally inserts TokenUsageMiddleware and other middlewares; ordering decisions in agent.py must align with external middleware consumers (ThreadData, Sandbox, Memory, etc.).

prompt.py's background refresh worker updates the enabled-skills cache that prompt rendering reads; both sync and async helpers in prompt.py must coordinate so agent.py can safely access prompt data during agent construction.
notable_relationships: Depends on global model factory/resolution (outside this folder) to map model aliases to concrete model objects used by summarization and other middleware.

Interacts with filesystem-backed skills storage (skills directory) and must avoid blocking disk I/O on hot code paths; changes propagate to skill installation and runtime skill filtering logic in other parts of the system.
Middleware ordering and presence are observable by downstream components (LangGraph run lifecycle, subagents, sandbox tools) and affect runtime behavior such as memory injection and sandbox access.

Prompt caching and background threads interact with the runtime event loop and process lifecycle (startup/shutdown); any change must respect how the Gateway/LangGraph host manages worker threads and async tasks.
recommendations: When editing make_lead_agent or middleware assembly: preserve deterministic middleware ordering and honor configuration flags for optional middlewares; validate via tests that middleware order matches runtime expectations.

When touching model resolution or summarization middleware creation: always resolve model names/aliases to concrete model instances via the central model factory before constructing middleware or passing models to components.

When editing prompt cache or skills loading: avoid synchronous disk I/O on request paths. Use the provided background refresh/warm/invalidate helpers and ensure thread-safety between sync and async callers; add guards so skill-loading errors do not throw during prompt construction.

When modifying the enabled-skills cache shape or its signatures: update cache keys to include any toggle state (e.g., skill-evolution) and ensure invalidation routines are reachable from both sync and async code paths.

Run or add tests that cover: model alias resolution for summarization, middleware chain composition and ordering, prompt rendering under cache warm/invalidate scenarios, and background refresh worker lifecycle and concurrency (sync vs async access).
Remember make_lead_agent is exported via __init__.py: keep public API stable or ensure all import sites are updated when changing the factory signature.

backend/packages/harness/deerflow/sandbo.description: This folder implements a host-backed local sandbox that presents a container-style filesystem view by mapping virtual/container paths to real host paths. It centralizes bidirectional path translation, enforces read-only mounts, and provides listing and provider wiring so sandboxed tools and skills can run locally while appearing containerized to callers.
key_behaviors: Translate incoming container-style paths to host filesystem paths before performing filesystem operations or executing commands.
Mask/translate any host paths produced in command outputs or API responses back to their corresponding container-style paths.
Enforce read-only mounts at the API level and raise standard filesystem errors (errno.EROFS) when writes are attempted on read-only mappings.
Provide a singleton LocalSandbox instance via a LocalSandboxProvider constructed from application config, including skills and custom mounts validation.
Recursively enumerate directory contents with configurable depth and ignore patterns for sandbox UIs and tooling.

insights.title: Bidirectional path translation is required to avoid leaking host layout
insights.problem: Host filesystem paths were previously leaked in outputs and interactive command results, causing confusion and breaking the illusion of a containerized workspace.
insights.root_cause: Commands and filesystem APIs operated directly on host paths and returned host paths without reverse-mapping to the virtual/container view.
insights.solution: All code paths that accept container-style inputs must translate to host paths before acting; all outputs that could contain host paths must be reverse-resolved to the container namespace before returning to callers.

insights.title: Read-only mounts must be enforced consistently
insights.problem: The earlier local sandbox lacked consistent enforcement of read-only mounts, allowing accidental writes to host paths that should be immutable from the sandbox view.
insights.root_cause: Path mapping logic did not carry mount-level metadata (read-only flags) through resolution and write operations.
insights.solution: Attach mount metadata to mappings and check _is_read_only_path prior to any write/update operation; on violation, raise errno.EROFS so callers receive a standard filesystem error.

insights.title: Support for multiple custom mounts and reserved prefixes
insights.problem: Initial implementations supported only a single skills mapping and did not validate or warn about invalid user-configured mounts or reserved virtual prefixes.
insights.root_cause: Provider construction logic did not iterate or validate configured mounts and merge them robustly with built-in mappings.
insights.solution: Provider must construct a list of PathMapping entries from config, validate against reserved/container prefixes, log warnings for invalid mounts, and expose the resulting mapping list to the LocalSandbox singleton.

insights.title: Command invocation must be OS-aware and sanitize paths
insights.problem: Executing shell commands on the host using container paths produced surprising failures and returned host paths in stdout/stderr.
insights.root_cause: Commands were executed without translating embedded container paths and without platform-specific invocation choices.
insights.solution: Before executing a command, resolve contained paths to host equivalents (with OS-aware invocation); after execution, reverse-resolve any output tokens that match host paths back to container paths.

key_files.why: Core implementation of LocalSandbox: path resolution (forward and reverse), read-only enforcement, and command execution logic — the behavioral heart of the folder.
Constructs and validates path mappings from app config and provides the singleton LocalSandbox instance that other code will obtain.
Directory listing implementation used for file browsing and sandbox tooling that must respect ignore patterns and max depth.
Public export surface for the local sandbox provider used by higher-level code (keeps consumer import paths stable).

construct_relationships: LocalSandboxProvider reads application config and builds a list of PathMapping entries (mount points, read-only flags) and returns a singleton LocalSandbox initialized with those mappings.

LocalSandbox exposes path resolution helpers (_resolve_path, _reverse_resolve_path) used by its filesystem methods (write_file, update_file) and by command execution helpers (_resolve_paths_in_command, _reverse_resolve_paths_in_output).
list_dir is consumed by sandbox listing code paths in LocalSandbox to perform recursive directory enumeration while honoring ignore patterns and max depth.
__init__.py exposes LocalSandboxProvider so external modules import the provider rather than internal implementation files.
notable_relationships: This local sandbox implements the same conceptual contract as the abstract Sandbox interface in backend/src/sandbox/sandbox.py — callers (SandoxMiddleware, sandbox tools) expect container-style paths and sandbox behavior consistent with the AioSandboxProvider.

SandboxMiddleware and sandbox-related builtin tools assume consistent path translation semantics and rely on the provider to supply a skills mapping (virtual /mnt/skills) so skills run identically between local and container sandboxes.

Configuration-driven mounts originate from application config (gateway/agent config); mount validation here affects higher level behaviors and can emit warnings that operators must see when starting the service.
Subsystems that parse command stdout/stderr or file listing outputs (UI, tools, skills) depend on reverse-mapping to avoid exposing host paths and to maintain identical UX across sandbox providers.
recommendations: Preserve public method signatures and observable behaviors: forward-/reverse-path translation, errno.EROFS on blocked writes, and singleton provider semantics.
When changing resolution logic, ensure every code path that accepts container paths translates to host paths and that outputs are reverse-resolved before being returned to callers.
Keep mount metadata (read-only flags and virtual prefixes) associated with mappings and consult it on all write/update operations.
Validate and log warnings for invalid or reserved mount configurations during provider setup; do not silently accept mounts that collide with reserved prefixes (e.g., /mnt/skills).
Be mindful of OS-specific command invocation and quoting; any edits to execute_command must preserve the OS-aware invocation behavior and output sanitization.
Run and/or add unit tests that cover: multiple custom mounts, read-only enforcement (errno.EROFS), reverse path masking in command outputs, and list_dir behavior with ignore patterns and max depth.
Avoid exposing host filesystem structure in outputs — changes must maintain the masking guarantees expected by callers (UI, skills, tools).

backend/packages/harness/deerflow/runtim.description: This folder implements run lifecycle management for in-process agent runs: a concurrency-safe in-memory registry, canonical run status and disconnect semantics, and the background worker that executes agent graphs and streams LangGraph events as SSE-like outputs. It provides the public API surface used by the rest of the runtime and the gateway to create/list/cancel runs and to stream run progress to clients.
key_behaviors: Create and register runs with unique IDs and maintain RunRecord lifecycle state under asyncio locks to allow concurrent operations safely.
Manage run state transitions (running, completed, cancelled, errored, rollback) and expose conflict/strategy errors when concurrent run operations collide.
Execute agent graphs in background asyncio tasks and stream multi-mode LangGraph output to an SSE-like consumer (StreamBridge), translating LangGraph stream modes to runtime events.
Handle consumer disconnect semantics via DisconnectMode, including interrupt/rollback behavior and final cleanup/update of RunStatus.
Provide a compact public import surface so other modules can create runs, query status, and drive worker execution consistently with the LangGraph Platform API.

insights.title: Deterministic newest-first listing relies on insertion order
insights.problem: list_by_thread returns runs newest-first using insertion order; when runs share identical timestamps this can produce an unstable or surprising order.
insights.root_cause: Sorting uses insertion order along with timestamps; timestamp ties are not further disambiguated.
insights.solution: When modifying listing behavior, preserve the deterministic insertion-order semantics or add an explicit tie-breaker (e.g., monotonic counter or UUID ordering). Consider the impact on callers that rely on newest-first behavior.

insights.title: Thread identification may be missing from run config.context
insights.problem: Middleware that relies on a thread_id in config.context may not find it if callers supply a context without thread_id, causing middleware to miss thread-scoped resources (workspaces, memory).
insights.root_cause: Clients can provide an arbitrary context object in run config; LangGraph >= 0.6.0 expects a thread_id but callers may omit it.
insights.solution: Ensure worker and manager behavior tolerates missing thread_id (e.g., propagate or synthesize it) or preserve existing behavior by keeping checks that assume thread_id is present. Any change must remain compatible with middleware expectations and document required shape of config.context.

insights.title: RunManager exposes multitask conflict strategies and errors
insights.problem: Concurrent create/cancel/interrupt operations can conflict; consumers depend on specific error types and strategy semantics (reject/interrupt/rollback).
insights.root_cause: RunManager enforces policies via asyncio locks and explicit conflict/error classes (e.g., ConflictError) and strategy enums.
insights.solution: When editing lifecycle logic or exported errors/types, preserve the surface and semantics of conflict handling or update all consumers (including gateway, tests, and any external callers). Ensure locks remain correct and that cancellation/interrupt ordering is deterministic under concurrency.

key_files.why: Public API export point — controls which run constructs (RunManager, RunRecord, RunStatus, DisconnectMode, run_agent) are visible to the rest of the codebase.
Core in-memory registry and concurrency-safe lifecycle manager — responsible for creating, listing, cancelling, and cleaning up runs and for exposing conflict/strategy behavior.
Canonical enums for RunStatus and DisconnectMode used across the runtime and relied upon by APIs and middleware to interpret run state and disconnect semantics.
Background worker that runs agents, maps LangGraph stream modes to SSE events, handles interrupts/rollback, and updates RunManager — the runtime's streaming execution core.

construct_relationships: RunManager maintains RunRecord instances and enforces lifecycle transitions defined by RunStatus and DisconnectMode enums.
