worker.run_agent executes agent graphs and consults/upserts RunRecord state via RunManager to reflect running, completion, error, or interrupted statuses.
worker translates LangGraph stream modes into SSE-like events consumed by StreamBridge; these events are tied to RunRecord identifiers so clients can correlate streams with run metadata.
manager's conflict/strategy logic (reject/interrupt/rollback) is surfaced to callers via explicit error types (e.g., ConflictError) that other runtime components depend on for control flow.
__init__.py re-exports RunManager, RunRecord, RunStatus, DisconnectMode, and run_agent so that external modules import a stable public surface rather than internal module paths.
notable_relationships: Upstream dependency on LangGraph semantics and versions: worker assumes certain stream mode names and that config.context may contain thread_id (LangGraph >= 0.6.0). Changes here may require coordination with LangGraph client usage elsewhere.

Middlewares (e.g., ThreadDataMiddleware) expect thread identification to be present for workspace and upload mounting; run creation and worker execution must preserve the context shape these middlewares need.

Gateway/API and any SSE consumers rely on the SSE-like events emitted by worker.run_agent and on RunStatus/DisconnectMode enum semantics — any change to event format or enum values is a breaking API change.
Concurrency semantics interact with higher-level request handling: callers from Gateway or other modules may concurrently create/cancel runs and rely on manager's locking and conflict behavior.
recommendations: Preserve the public exports in __init__.py or update all import sites if changing the exported names/locations.

When touching manager concurrency or listing code, respect asyncio locking semantics and deterministic ordering guarantees; run unit tests that exercise concurrent create/cancel scenarios and list_by_thread ordering.
If modifying worker streaming behavior or event shapes, ensure backward compatibility with current SSE consumers and update any integration tests that assert event formats and multi-mode mappings.

Any change that affects RunStatus or DisconnectMode values or semantics must be coordinated with middleware and API consumers; run integration tests that involve ThreadDataMiddleware, sandbox mounting, and memory middleware.

Because missing thread_id in config.context is a known source of issues, edits that affect context propagation or validation should check for thread_id presence and consider impacts on middleware that rely on it.

Run the backend unit test suite and any end-to-end tests involving LangGraph streaming and SSE consumers after edits; include concurrency-heavy tests (cancellation, interrupts, rollback) to validate semantics.

backend/packages/harness/deerflow/runtim.description: This package defines the StreamBridge protocol and provides the default in-memory implementation plus an async factory for creating a shared instance for the server lifespan. It decouples agent-run event producers from SSE/streaming consumers by standardising event objects, sentinel semantics (heartbeat/end), replay offsets (Last-Event-ID) and per-run buffer lifecycle so background agents and SSE endpoints can exchange run events without an external broker.
key_behaviors: Produce and publish run-scoped StreamEvent objects (including heartbeat and end sentinels) from agent workers or background tasks.
Subscribe and replay run event streams from a given offset (Last-Event-ID), supporting new subscribers and reconnects.
Provide a server-lifespan shared StreamBridge instance via an async context manager so SSE endpoints and background agents share the same bridge.
Maintain bounded, per-run in-memory buffers that permit replay, heartbeat emission and explicit cleanup when runs end.

insights.title: END sentinel must always reach subscribers
insights.problem: When the per-run buffer/queue filled up, publish_end() could fail to deliver the END sentinel, causing subscribers to wait indefinitely and resources to leak.
insights.root_cause: Use of a non-replayable bounded asyncio.Queue where timeouts/drop semantics could discard the END event when the buffer was full.
insights.solution: Switch to a bounded in-memory event log with replay semantics and explicit handling to guarantee delivery of END sentinel even under saturation; ensure publish_end appends the sentinel to the log and subscribers can read it via replay.

insights.title: Subscribers need deterministic replay from Last-Event-ID
insights.problem: Queues without replay semantics cannot serve reconnecting clients or Last-Event-ID-based resumption reliably.
insights.root_cause: Per-run ephemeral queues drop historical events; new subscribers or reconnects cannot see past events.
insights.solution: Maintain an append-only per-run event log with monotonically increasing IDs and bounded retention so subscribers can request events from a specific ID and replay missed events.

insights.title: Per-run buffers can leak memory if not cleaned
insights.problem: Long-lived or finished runs may retain per-run buffers causing memory growth.
insights.root_cause: Buffers retained after runs finish or subscribers never trigger cleanup; reliance on queue consumer drain semantics is insufficient.
insights.solution: Provide explicit cleanup semantics for per-run buffers, bounded retention policies, and ensure publish_end and subscriber disconnects participate in buffer lifecycle to release memory.

insights.title: Multi-producer/multi-consumer ordering and monotonic IDs
insights.problem: Concurrent publishers and subscribers require stable event ordering and unique, monotonically increasing IDs for reliable replay and Last-Event-ID comparisons.
insights.root_cause: Concurrent append operations without a central ID generator or atomic append semantics can produce inconsistent offsets for replay.
insights.solution: Use a single _next_id generator or otherwise atomic mechanism to assign monotonic IDs at append time and ensure append/replay operations are concurrency-safe.

insights.title: Shared instance lifetime needs explicit management
insights.problem: SSE endpoints and background agents must share the same bridge instance for coordination; creating multiple bridge instances could cause inconsistent views or missed events.
insights.root_cause: Per-request instantiation or lack of server-lifespan context manager leads to multiple, disconnected bridges.
insights.solution: Provide an async context manager factory (make_stream_bridge) intended for server lifespan use; default to the in-memory implementation when no external provider is configured.

key_files.why: Defines StreamEvent, sentinel semantics (heartbeat/end) and the abstract StreamBridge protocol that drives publish/subscribe contracts and expectations across implementations.
Implements the default in-memory StreamBridge, per-run event logs, replay semantics, heartbeat and END handling, and buffer cleanup — behavior consumers rely on.
Provides the async factory/context manager (make_stream_bridge) used to create and share the bridge instance for the server lifespan; determines fallback to memory implementation.
Re-exports the protocol, sentinel constants and the default implementation so callers import a stable public API (affects all consumers).

construct_relationships: StreamEvent and sentinel constants (base.py) are the canonical event model consumed and produced by MemoryStreamBridge (memory.py).
StreamBridge (abstract in base.py) declares publish/subscribe/cleanup contracts implemented by MemoryStreamBridge (memory.py).

async_provider.make_stream_bridge returns/creates a StreamBridge implementation (MemoryStreamBridge by default) and is intended to be used as a server-lifespan async context manager so SSE endpoints and background agents share the same instance.
The _next_id mechanism in memory.py assigns monotonic IDs to StreamEvent instances so subscribers can replay from Last-Event-ID deterministically.
notable_relationships: This bridge is positioned between agent producers (background agent runners/subagents) and SSE/streaming consumers (SSE endpoints in the server); both sides depend on stable sentinel behavior and replay semantics.

The async factory is designed to be used at server startup/shutdown (lifespan) so other server modules that expose SSE endpoints must consume the same shared instance rather than create per-request instances.

There is an implicit contract between configuration and runtime: when no external provider is configured, the memory implementation is the fallback — changes must preserve compatibility with consumers expecting the default behavior.

Downstream modules rely on stable event IDs, heartbeat semantics to keep SSE alive, and guaranteed end sentinel delivery to close streams and free per-run buffers; breaking these will affect SSE clients and resource cleanup.
recommendations: When editing API shapes or re-exported names in __init__.py, verify and update all consumers that import the public symbols to avoid import/runtime errors.
Preserve StreamEvent structure and sentinel constants (HEARTBEAT/END) or ensure consumers are updated simultaneously; consumers (SSE endpoints) depend on exact sentinel semantics to close streams.
Maintain monotonic ID assignment and replay behavior: test Last-Event-ID replay, reconnection scenarios and ordering under concurrent publishers.
Ensure publish_end always appends/delivers the END sentinel even under buffer saturation; include tests that simulate full buffers and verify subscribers receive END.
Retain or explicitly manage per-run buffer cleanup semantics to avoid memory leaks; run memory/stress tests that create many short-lived runs and verify memory retention is bounded.
Respect concurrency safety: use atomic/locked append and ID generation and test multi-producer/multi-consumer interactions (including heartbeat emission) under load.
When changing lifecycle behavior of the async factory, validate server-lifespan usage by SSE endpoints and background agents so a single shared instance is maintained in production and tests.

backend/packages/harness/deerflow/agents.description: This folder implements the agent checkpointer subsystem: factories and context managers (sync and async) that provide a configured Checkpointer backend (in-memory, SQLite, or Postgres) for agent runs. It centralizes public imports for other modules and encapsulates resource initialization and teardown semantics that matter for both synchronous processes and async server lifecycles.
key_behaviors: Provide a process-global, synchronous checkpointer singleton (get_checkpointer) and a synchronous one-shot context manager (checkpointer_context) for callers that expect blocking APIs.
Provide an async context manager factory (make_checkpointer) that yields an async Checkpointer implementation suitable for async servers, handling backend selection and lifecycle (init/cleanup).
Select among backends (memory, sqlite, postgres) based on configuration, prepare required resources (e.g. create SQLite parent directories), and log the chosen backend.
Expose reset semantics for tests/reconfiguration to allow replacement of the global singleton without process restart.
Act as the public API surface for checkpointer utilities via the package __init__ exports.

insights.title: Filesystem setup executed on event loop
insights.problem: Synchronous filesystem operations (mkdir for SQLite parent dir) are performed on the async event loop during async provider setup, which may block it.
insights.root_cause: Filesystem helper (ensure_sqlite_parent_dir) invoked directly inside the async context manager without offloading to a thread or moving to sync startup code.
insights.solution: Avoid synchronous blocking calls on the event loop: perform filesystem creation off the loop (thread executor) or consolidate setup into synchronous startup paths shared by both providers to prevent event-loop stalls; when editing async provider code, respect non-blocking semantics.

insights.title: SQLite parent-directory logic duplicated across providers
insights.problem: Multiple provider implementations duplicate logic to create parent directories for SQLite connection strings.
insights.root_cause: Each provider independently handles connection-string-specific filesystem setup rather than using a shared runtime/store utility.
insights.solution: Centralize SQLite filesystem helpers in a shared runtime/store utility so both sync and async factories can reuse the same behavior and invariants; maintain a single canonical behavior for path creation and permissions when modifying provider code.

insights.title: Context manager semantics define resource lifecycle
insights.problem: Backend resources (connections, files) must be initialized and torn down in the expected enter/exit semantics for both sync and async contexts; mismatched lifecycles lead to leaked handles or premature teardown.
insights.root_cause: Distinct sync and async factories manage resources separately (global singleton vs per-context), creating subtle differences in when resources are created, reused, or released.
insights.solution: Preserve context manager enter/exit behavior and ensure resets/tests can reliably replace singletons; any changes must maintain explicit initialization and cleanup boundaries for both sync and async paths.

insights.title: Resettable singleton used by tests and reconfiguration
insights.problem: Tests and dynamic reconfiguration rely on the ability to reset the process-global checkpointer; accidental removal or behavioral change can break test isolation.
insights.root_cause: Global get_checkpointer and reset_checkpointer semantics are part of the public API and are used to control in-process state across tests and runtime transitions.
insights.solution: Keep reset semantics intact and ensure that test utilities still can reinitialize state; when editing code, validate via unit/integration tests that reset clears/tears down resources and new instances start with a clean state.

key_files.why: Public package entry: re-exports make_checkpointer, context helpers, and accessors — changing exports alters the API surface consumed by other modules.
Async checkpointer factory for server lifecycles: allocates async Checkpointer backends and handles async-safe initialization/teardown; edits here affect async startup and event-loop safety.
Synchronous factories and process-global singleton implementation: controls get_checkpointer, checkpointer_context, and reset behaviors used by tests and synchronous entrypoints.

construct_relationships: make_checkpointer (async_provider.py) yields an async Checkpointer implementation configured for memory/sqlite/postgres and is intended to be used as an async context manager by async servers.

get_checkpointer / checkpointer_context (provider.py) expose the synchronous singleton or one-shot context manager for blocking code paths; reset_checkpointer allows replacing the singleton for tests or reconfiguration.

Both async and sync providers perform backend selection (memory/sqlite/postgres) and invoke SQLite filesystem helpers (ensure_sqlite_parent_dir), creating a cross-cutting dependency on filesystem setup logic.
__init__.py re-exports factories and accessors so callers import the public API from the package root rather than the individual modules.
notable_relationships: Other modules (agents, harnesses, tests) import make_checkpointer, get_checkpointer, and reset_checkpointer from this package; changing signatures or export names will have wide impact.
SQLite backend usage depends on filesystem access and correct directory creation; changes affect persistent storage across process restarts and Docker/container deployments.
Postgres backend depends on external network/datastore availability and connection configuration; provider factories must not assume availability and must surface appropriate initialization failures.

Async provider changes may impact server startup paths (e.g., LangGraph or FastAPI lifecycles) that rely on non-blocking initialization; synchronous provider changes affect CLI, tests, and process-local consumers.
recommendations: Preserve public API names and context-manager semantics exported from __init__.py to avoid breaking imports across the codebase.
When modifying async_provider.py, avoid introducing synchronous blocking filesystem calls on the event loop; ensure any filesystem setup is offloaded or shared with sync startup code.
Do not change singleton/reset semantics without verifying unit and integration tests that depend on reset_checkpointer; tests assume reset provides a clean reinitialization path.

If touching SQLite helper logic, be aware it is duplicated across providers; update or centralize helpers so both sync and async factories maintain identical behavior for path creation and permissions.

Run the backend unit tests and any integration tests that exercise agent startup, checkpointer persistence, and async server lifecycles after edits (including tests that rely on reset_checkpointer and SQLite/Postgres backends).

backend/packages/harness/deerflow/subage.description: This folder provides the built-in subagent configuration objects used by the subagent subsystem. It defines concrete SubagentConfig instances (e.g., general-purpose and bash specialists) and exposes a registry mapping names to those configurations so the rest of the system can instantiate and constrain delegated subagents. These configs encode system prompts, allowed tools, and runtime limits that directly affect subagent behavior and sandboxing/security.
key_behaviors: Expose named SubagentConfig objects (e.g., 'general-purpose', 'bash') for lookup by the subagent registry/creator.
Embed policy and behavioral constraints in configs: system prompt, expected output format, allowed/disallowed tools, max_turns and other runtime limits.
Serve as the authoritative source for which capabilities a spawned subagent may use (toolset → sandbox/tool implementations), affecting security and runtime safety.
Centralize built-in subagent types so consumers (executor, registry, lead_agent/task tool) can create appropriate subagents by name.

insights.title: Registry must remain in sync with exported config names
insights.problem: If a config is renamed, added, or removed but not correctly registered, consumers that look up built-in subagents by name will fail to instantiate them or will get stale references.
insights.root_cause: Simple in-process registration relies on manual exports and a shared BUILTIN_SUBAGENTS mapping; there is no indirection or dynamic discovery.
insights.solution: Treat the __init__ registry as canonical: maintain matching keys and values, and ensure any rename/add/remove is propagated to consumers and tests that assert registry contents.

insights.title: Allowed tool lists in configs determine runtime surface area
insights.problem: Misalignment between a config's allowed tools and actual tool implementations (or sandbox provider capabilities) can lead to runtime errors or unexpected privilege exposure (e.g., enabling bash without a Docker-isolated sandbox).
insights.root_cause: Configs statically declare allowed tools but do not themselves enforce sandbox provider selection or runtime availability.
insights.solution: When editing tool allowances or config defaults, verify the corresponding tools exist and that the runtime sandbox mode (Local vs Aio) supports them; ensure callers select appropriate sandbox providers for configs that require shell access.

insights.title: Config limits (max_turns, etc.) must align with executor constraints
insights.problem: Divergent values between a subagent config's declared limits and Executor/global limits (e.g., concurrency, timeout) create confusing behavior and possible failure modes (tasks aborted unexpectedly or overrunning intended caps).
insights.root_cause: Limits are defined in multiple places (per-config and in executor/global settings) without an enforced canonical source of truth.
insights.solution: Respect per-config values but ensure edits are cross-checked with the subagent executor and system-wide policy; update tests that validate behavior under limits when changing values.

insights.title: System prompts and expected output formats are contract points
insights.problem: Downstream code (parsers, UI, or aggregators) may rely on specific output formats or markers the system prompts request; changing prompts/formats can break parsing and presentation logic.
insights.root_cause: Prompts embed expectations (e.g., JSON response schema, status markers) that are implicit contracts rather than strictly typed interfaces.
insights.solution: Treat prompt wording and declared output formats as breaking-change surfaces: when modifying, audit all consumers that parse or display subagent output and update tests that assert output shape.

key_files.why: Central registry and export point mapping built-in names to SubagentConfig objects — the canonical lookup used by subagent registry/creator.
Defines the default general-purpose SubagentConfig including prompts, allowed/disallowed tools, and limits used for generic delegated tasks.
Defines the bash-specialist SubagentConfig which explicitly controls shell-related permissions and behavior; changes here have direct security and sandboxing implications.

construct_relationships: BUILTIN_SUBAGENTS (in __init__.py) aggregates and exposes GENERAL_PURPOSE_CONFIG and BASH_AGENT_CONFIG for name-based lookup.
Each SubagentConfig object encodes allowed_tools and limits which are expected to be enforced/consumed by the SubagentExecutor and the subagent registry when spawning tasks.
Allowed tool names declared in configs reference tool implementations defined elsewhere (sandbox tools and builtin tools); mismatch leads to runtime lookup errors.
System prompts and expected output formats inside configs are inputs to the language model runs performed by lead_agent / subagent executor and are implicitly relied on by downstream parsers/UI.
notable_relationships: Subagent registry and executor (backend/src/subagents/registry.py and backend/src/subagents/executor.py) consume these configurations by name — changing keys or semantics requires updating those consumers or ensuring backward compatibility.

Sandbox providers and builtin tools (backend/src/sandbox/* and backend/src/tools/builtins/*) must support the toolset declared by these configs; enabling bash in a config implies a runtime that permits shell execution (AioSandboxProvider) or risk if LocalSandboxProvider has bash disabled.

Lead agent middleware and the 'task' tool rely on these built-in configs to spawn subagents; prompt/format changes can affect how tasks are presented and how results are integrated back into a parent run.
Front-end or external callers that may reference subagent types by name (if surfaced) must remain synchronized with registry keys.
recommendations: Do not change registry keys or exported names without updating all consumers that look up built-in subagents by name (registry, executor, any tests, and any code that calls task(tool='name')).
When altering allowed_tools in a config, verify the corresponding tool implementations exist and that the configured sandbox provider supports them (check Local vs Aio sandbox capabilities).

When editing system prompts or declared output formats, audit downstream parsers and UI components that parse or display subagent outputs; update unit/integration tests that assert output shapes or behavior.

Ensure any changes to per-config runtime limits (max_turns, timeouts) are consistent with subagent executor/global limits; run the subagent executor tests and integration tests that exercise delegation and timeouts.

Run the repository's backend tests (make test / uv run pytest) and any sandbox-related integration tests after edits, and confirm CI linting/formatting passes, since these configs are treated as part of runtime contracts.

backend/packages/harness/deerflow/runtim.description: This folder provides factories and helpers to construct runtime persistence stores for DeerFlow's embedded and server runtimes. It normalizes SQLite connection strings and parent-dir creation, and exposes parallel sync and async store construction patterns (singleton for CLI/embedded, context-manager for server lifecycles) so other runtime components can obtain a configured langgraph Store consistently.
key_behaviors: Normalize and validate configured checkpointer/backing-store settings (memory, sqlite, postgres) and produce a Store instance matching that backend.
Provide an async context manager (server usage) that sets up backend resources, runs DB init steps, and tears them down on exit.
Provide a sync global singleton for CLI/embedded client usage and a one-shot context manager for tests or ephemeral runs, with ability to reset the singleton.

Handle SQLite-specific filesystem semantics: accept special tokens (:memory:, file: URIs) unchanged, convert plain paths to absolute paths, and ensure parent directories exist before opening DB files.
Surface helpful errors and fallbacks when optional DB driver packages are missing (avoid cryptic failures at runtime).

insights.title: Mirror async/sync factory semantics
insights.problem: Server code expects an async context-managed store while CLI/embedded code expects a sync singleton/context manager; mixing them up can cause deadlocks or misuse.
insights.root_cause: Two parallel factory implementations that provide the same conceptual functionality but different lifecycles (async vs sync) and different usage patterns.
insights.solution: When modifying factories or the re-export API, preserve both async and sync entrypoints and their signatures (make_store for async, get_store/store_context for sync) and keep explicit docs so callers choose the correct one for their runtime.

insights.title: SQLite file path normalization and parent-dir creation
insights.problem: Creating SQLite DB files on the fly can fail if parent directories are missing or if special SQLite URIs are mishandled.
insights.root_cause: Plain path values in config may be relative and not pre-created; naive absolute resolution can accidentally alter special connection strings like :memory: or file: URIs.
insights.solution: Retain path normalization logic that preserves special tokens and ensures parent directories exist before opening files; any change to path handling must continue to respect :memory: and file: URIs and create necessary directories.

insights.title: Graceful handling of missing DB drivers
insights.problem: Missing optional async/sync DB driver packages (e.g., aiosqlite, asyncpg, psycopg2) can cause unclear failures during store creation.
insights.root_cause: Provider chooses implementation based on config but relies on optional third-party packages that may not be installed in all environments.
insights.solution: Keep and maintain explicit, actionable error messages and fallback behavior when optional packages are missing; any refactor of provider selection must preserve those checks and the user-facing diagnostics.

insights.title: Global singleton impacts test isolation
insights.problem: A process-global sync store singleton can leak state across tests or runs if not reset, causing flakiness.
insights.root_cause: Provider caches a global store instance for CLI convenience without automatic per-test isolation.
insights.solution: Preserve the ability to reset the cached store from tests and ensure context managers yield ephemeral stores; changes must not remove reset hooks or make singletons implicit/unreachable from test code.

insights.title: Async setup/teardown for DB-backed stores
insights.problem: Improper setup or teardown of async DB stores can leave connections open, migrations unfinished, or cause connection pool leaks in long-running servers.
insights.root_cause: Async provider is responsible for initialization and teardown and must coordinate with server lifespan hooks.
insights.solution: Keep explicit async setup/teardown steps in the async factory and ensure the provider yields control only after initialization; preserve correct exception handling so teardown runs on error paths.

key_files.why: Creates and yields the async Store for server lifecycles; responsible for async DB init and teardown and for surfacing driver/package errors.
Provides the sync singleton and context manager used by CLI/embedded clients and tests; includes reset logic important for test isolation.
Encapsulates SQLite connection-string normalization and parent-directory creation logic that other providers rely on; changes here affect filesystem-backed DB behavior.
Re-exports the async and sync factory APIs so callers import a consistent module; changing exports will alter the public API surface.

construct_relationships: async_provider.make_store constructs an async langgraph Store implementation based on the configured checkpointer and performs initialization and teardown.
provider.get_store and provider.store_context mirror the same backend selection logic synchronously and optionally share sqlite normalization helpers.
_sqlite_utils.resolve_sqlite_conn_str and ensure_sqlite_parent_dir are used by both async_provider and provider to normalize paths and prepare filesystem state for sqlite-backed stores.
__init__.py re-exports async_provider.make_store and provider.get_store/store_context to present a unified import surface to callers.
notable_relationships: This subtree depends on external langgraph Store implementations and optional DB driver packages (aiosqlite, asyncpg, psycopg or equivalents) — missing packages or mismatched versions are a cross-cutting runtime concern.
Configuration for the 'checkpointer' backend originates outside this folder (runtime or app config); providers read that config to choose memory/sqlite/postgres backends.
Filesystem behaviors (creating parent dirs for SQLite files) have implications for containerized deployments and mounting (Docker/Compose), where volume permissions and paths differ from local dev.
Consumers of the exported API include server lifecycle code (async) and CLI/embedded client code (sync); changes here can impact both long-running servers and short-lived scripts/tests.
recommendations: Preserve the public API names and semantics: async make_store (context-managed) and sync get_store/store_context (singleton and context manager).

When modifying sqlite path logic, continue to treat :memory: and file: URIs as special-cased and ensure parent directories are created for filesystem paths; verify behavior in containerized environments.
Retain explicit checks and user-facing error messages for missing optional DB drivers; do not defer raising until obscure runtime errors.
Maintain the sync singleton reset hook so tests can reliably isolate store state; changes that remove or hide reset behavior will break existing tests.
Ensure async provider continues to run initialization/teardown in all control-flow paths (normal and exceptional); run integration tests that exercise async lifecycle and DB backends after edits.
Run both sync and async test suites (including DB-backed tests) and validate in Docker-style environments where filesystem mounts and permissions differ from local dev.

frontend/src/app/workspace/agents/new.description: This folder implements the workspace UI flow for creating a new agent. It handles name validation, an interactive bootstrap chat via the thread streaming abstraction, and a two-step create + explicit-save lifecycle that tolerates backend eventual consistency. Its behavior bridges client-side optimistic UI, persisted agent records via backend APIs, and an asynchronous thread-based bootstrap that issues a hidden save command.
key_behaviors: Client-side and server-side agent name validation (local regex + remote API check) with network-aware error mapping.
Create agent API call followed by optimistic UI bootstrapping of a short chat using the thread streaming hook.
Hidden 'setup_agent' command sent within the bootstrap thread to persist/read agent configuration, plus an explicit Save button for the user.
Read-after-write retry loop when fetching the newly created agent to tolerate backend eventual consistency.
Local persistence of transient UI hints (SAVE_HINT_STORAGE_KEY) and keyboard handling for name confirmation.

insights.title: Read-after-write eventual consistency requires retry
insights.problem: Immediately reading the agent record after create can fail due to backend eventual consistency, producing confusing UI states or errors.
insights.root_cause: The backend persists the agent record asynchronously or is subject to propagation delays; the client attempted a read immediately after the create call.
insights.solution: Retry reads with increasing delays (AGENT_READ_RETRY_DELAYS_MS) and surface a clear UX path (explicit Save) while retrying; treat transient read failures as expected rather than fatal.

insights.title: Bootstrap chat needs an explicit save plus a hidden-save side-effect
insights.problem: Implicit save flows caused UI prompts and surfaced internal artifacts; without an explicit Save the client could misrepresent persistence state.
insights.root_cause: Bootstrapping the agent relies on a message-based hidden 'setup_agent' command to persist configuration, but users expect an explicit save action and clear confirmation.
insights.solution: Provide an explicit Save action in the UI while also sending the hidden 'setup_agent' command in the background; keep both flows coordinated and expose the explicit Save as the primary confirmation to users.

insights.title: Network/CORS errors should map to distinct UI messages
insights.problem: Name-check failures due to network or CORS errors presented misleading validation messages to users.
insights.root_cause: The name-check code conflated remote validation failures with network issues and did not surface network-specific diagnostics.
insights.solution: Detect and surface AgentNameCheckError (or equivalent) to show a network-specific message distinct from name-invalidation feedback; ensure the UI does not treat network failures as deterministic validation failures.

insights.title: Thread streaming bootstrap requires coordination with agent lifecycle
insights.problem: Bootstrapping the initial chat and issuing hidden commands while the agent creation is still resolving can create race conditions and inconsistent UI experience.
insights.root_cause: The thread streaming hook is used optimistically before the agent read is confirmed; hidden commands in the stream modify server-side agent state asynchronously.
insights.solution: Coordinate the bootstrap flow with create + get-with-retry semantics and ensure streaming/hidden-command delivery is idempotent or tolerant of retries/duplicate runs.

key_files.why: Single entry point for the new-agent creation UI; orchestrates name validation, createAgent API calls, thread streaming bootstrap, hidden 'setup_agent' command, explicit Save flow, and read-after-write retry logic.

construct_relationships: NewAgentPage performs local name validation using NAME_RE and then calls the checkAgentName remote API for uniqueness.
On create, NewAgentPage calls createAgent API and then uses getAgentWithRetry (backed by AGENT_READ_RETRY_DELAYS_MS) to read the persisted agent record until it becomes available.

NewAgentPage initializes a short bootstrap chat via the useThreadStream hook; that stream sends a hidden 'setup_agent' command to persist configuration on the server while the UI also exposes an explicit Save action.
SAVE_HINT_STORAGE_KEY is used by the page to persist transient UI hint state (client-side storage) between steps or reloads.
getCreateAgentErrorMessage and AgentNameCheckError handling map network/CORS failures to distinct UI messages separate from validation errors.
notable_relationships: Relies on backend gateway APIs: createAgent, checkAgentName, getAgent; backend eventual-consistency characteristics affect client retries and UX.
Depends on a thread-streaming abstraction/hook elsewhere in the frontend (useThreadStream) to bootstrap conversations and deliver hidden commands.
Interacts with client-side storage (localStorage) via SAVE_HINT_STORAGE_KEY; other UI components may read/write the same key.
Behavior is coupled to server-side handling of the 'setup_agent' command: server must accept hidden stream commands and persist agent configuration.
recommendations: When editing, preserve the create -> get-with-retry ordering and delay schedule (AGENT_READ_RETRY_DELAYS_MS) to avoid surfacing transient read failures.
Keep the explicit Save UX and the background hidden 'setup_agent' command semantics coordinated; ensure hidden commands remain idempotent or handle duplicates.
Maintain clear separation of validation vs network errors (AgentNameCheckError mapping) so UI messaging remains accurate.
Run integration tests that exercise createAgent + getAgent eventual consistency, thread-stream bootstrap (useThreadStream), and localStorage hint behavior when changing this page.
Respect optimistic UI flow with thread streaming — changes must not break the stream delivery or the assumptions that bootstrap messages can be sent before final confirmation of persisted state.

backend/packages/harness/deerflow/commun.description: This folder provides a community integration for the InfoQuest web/search API: an HTTP client (InfoQuestClient) that wraps fetch/search/image endpoints and a set of LangChain-style tool wrappers that construct the client from the app configuration and present normalized, LLM-friendly results (web_search, web_fetch, image_search). It matters because agents use these tools to retrieve web content and images at runtime, so correctness of header/auth handling, timeouts, result normalization, and output formatting directly affects agent behavior and downstream prompts.
key_behaviors: Construct an InfoQuestClient from application configuration (timeouts, API key, search parameters).
Perform web searches and image searches against InfoQuest, normalize and clean results into simplified lists.
Fetch webpage content and run a readability extraction to produce condensed article text for LLM consumption.
Truncate/format tool outputs to be prompt-friendly and safe for agent consumption.
Handle API errors and inconsistent response shapes via fallback parsing and explicit error propagation.

insights.title: Robust response parsing and fallback handling
insights.problem: InfoQuest responses can be inconsistent or include non-JSON payloads; naive parsing leads to crashes or lost error context.
insights.root_cause: Upstream API sometimes returns mixed content or unexpected shapes; network errors and non-200 responses need special handling.
insights.solution: Client implements parsing fallbacks and explicit error handling. Any edits must preserve fallback parsing, ensure errors are propagated clearly to tools, and avoid swallowing or over-normalizing original error details.

insights.title: Timeouts and search parameters must flow from app config into the client and tools
insights.problem: Tool behavior (time_range, size, request timeout) depends on runtime configuration; inconsistent propagation causes different behaviors in dev vs production or missing timeouts.
insights.root_cause: Client is constructed per-call from app configuration; tools rely on that construction to enforce defaults and limits.
insights.solution: Changes must retain the pattern of building the client from app config, honoring configured timeouts/search params, and providing sane defaults when config entries are absent.

insights.title: Readability extraction and truncation produce LLM-friendly outputs
insights.problem: Raw HTML or long web content is too noisy for prompts and can overwhelm token limits.
insights.root_cause: Fetch returns full page content; downstream agents expect concise, cleaned text to include in prompts.
insights.solution: The tools use a readability_extractor and truncate/format results for LLM consumption. Edits must preserve extraction and truncation behavior and output shapes expected by agents and prompts.

insights.title: API key and header management is centralized in the client
insights.problem: Incorrect header construction or accidental logging of keys can leak credentials or fail authentication.
insights.root_cause: InfoQuestClient assembles auth headers per request.
insights.solution: Any changes must avoid exposing API keys in logs, preserve header construction semantics, and maintain safe defaults for missing credentials.

insights.title: Image search is a newly exposed capability requiring config and normalization
insights.problem: Image results need size/time_range config and normalized URLs; remote image content may introduce access issues or privacy considerations.
insights.root_cause: Image search support was added to the client/tools; image results differ from web text results and have different downstream requirements.
insights.solution: Ensure image_search tool continues to read config options, normalize URLs consistently, and return a stable, LLM-friendly representation while leaving access/security decisions to consuming layers.

key_files.why: Core HTTP client: constructs headers/auth, issues fetch/search/image requests, implements JSON fallback parsing and result normalization used by tools.
Tool wrappers that build the client from app config, run readability extraction for web_fetch, enforce timeouts/params, and present truncated/LLM-friendly outputs to the agent tool registry.

construct_relationships: tools._get_infoquest_client reads application config and constructs an InfoQuestClient instance.
web_search_tool calls InfoQuestClient.web_search, then uses client clean/normalize routines to produce simplified result lists for LLMs.
web_fetch_tool calls InfoQuestClient.fetch, then passes raw page text through readability_extractor and truncation logic before returning to agents.
image_search_tool calls InfoQuestClient.image_search and relies on the client to normalize image result shapes and URLs.
InfoQuestClient centralizes header/auth construction and error/fallback parsing; tools depend on it for consistent semantics and output shapes.
notable_relationships: These tools are expected to be registered into the agent toolset (LangChain/LangGraph tool registry) so changes here affect agent capabilities and prompts across the system.
Tools rely on the global application configuration (timeout, API keys, search params). Changes may have runtime differences between local/dev and Docker/production if config handling differs.
Network calls made by this folder mean unit/integration tests must mock HTTP responses; CI may need network isolation to avoid flakiness.
Downstream prompt templates and middleware (e.g., Summarization/Memory) assume the output shapes (truncated text, list items, image URL lists) produced by these tools.
recommendations: When editing, preserve the pattern of building the client from the app config and honor configured timeouts, search params, and API keys.
Do not remove or weaken JSON fallback parsing and explicit error propagation in the client; tests should cover non-JSON and error responses.
Keep the readability_extractor and truncation/formatting behaviors intact (or ensure equivalent output shapes) because agents expect concise LLM-ready strings.
Avoid logging or exposing API keys or sensitive headers; review any logging changes for credential leakage.
Add or update unit tests that mock InfoQuest HTTP responses for success, error, and malformed payloads; include tests for image_search parameter propagation and normalized output shapes.
Confirm whether the client and tools are synchronous or asynchronous and maintain that contract to avoid breaking the tool registration/agent execution path.

backend/packages/harness/deerflow/commun.description: This folder contains a community connector that exposes Jina AI's r.jina.ai reader/crawl API as an async web-fetch tool for agents. It provides an asynchronous HTTP client wrapper (JinaClient) and a LangChain-compatible tool (web_fetch) that runs a crawl, extracts readable article content, converts it to markdown, and returns a truncated result while honoring application-level timeouts and API-key configuration.
key_behaviors: Asynchronously POST a crawl request to Jina's reader API and return raw text or an error message.
Apply a readability extraction step to crawl output and produce Markdown-formatted article text.
Enforce application-configured timeout and truncate output to a fixed maximum length before returning to the caller.
Log and gracefully handle missing API key and HTTP/network errors, returning user-readable error strings.
Expose the functionality as a LangChain-style async tool for use by agents in the wider system.

insights.title: Non-blocking network calls are required
insights.problem: Blocking/synchronous HTTP calls prevented concurrent agent activity and caused the web_fetch tool to block the async runtime.
insights.root_cause: Initial use of synchronous HTTP requests within agent-facing code paths.
insights.solution: Use an async HTTP client (httpx.AsyncClient) and async function signatures throughout the connector to avoid blocking the event loop; ensure callers and tool interfaces remain async.

insights.title: Error propagation must preserve types and not feed error strings into processors
insights.problem: Error strings were passed into the ReadabilityExtractor, producing misleading results instead of clear failures.
insights.root_cause: Returning human-readable error strings as successful crawl outputs, rather than raising or signalling errors separately.
insights.solution: Distinctly separate successful content results from error cases — return structured errors or raise exceptions that callers/transformers can handle. Avoid passing error strings into downstream extractors that expect page content.

insights.title: Tool behavior depends on application config (timeouts, API key)
insights.problem: Timeouts and API-key handling are driven by external app config; changes to config shape or missing values change runtime behavior (e.g., silent fallback or warnings).
insights.root_cause: Tool reads timeouts and API key from the shared app configuration at runtime.
insights.solution: Respect and validate config values at call time: enforce timeout limits, log/emit clear warnings when API key absent, and ensure the tool uses configured timeout values consistently.

insights.title: Truncation and formatting are enforced at tool boundary
insights.problem: Large page content must be limited to prevent downstream overload, but truncation may cut off HTML/markdown in the middle.
insights.root_cause: Tool truncates the final Markdown string to a fixed character limit before returning.
insights.solution: Maintain the truncation cap at the tool boundary and ensure returned content is valid Markdown/UTF-8; callers depend on the fixed-size contract.

key_files.why: Implements the async JinaClient.crawl HTTP integration and controls API-key header, network error wrapping, and use of httpx.AsyncClient — central to all network I/O in this connector.
Defines the LangChain async web_fetch tool, orchestrates calling JinaClient, applying ReadabilityExtractor, enforcing timeouts and truncation, and shapes the tool's public contract used by agents.

construct_relationships: web_fetch tool in tools.py calls JinaClient.crawl in jina_client.py to fetch raw page text.
web_fetch uses a ReadabilityExtractor (imported/constructed within tools.py) to convert raw crawl output to article Markdown.
web_fetch reads timeout and possibly API-key presence from the application config and applies the timeout to its operations.
jina_client.py creates and uses an httpx.AsyncClient — its lifecycle and async behavior must align with the async web_fetch tool's expectations.
The final Markdown is truncated to a configured maximum length inside the web_fetch tool before returning to the caller.
notable_relationships: Relies on app-wide configuration for timeout and API key values (external config file or injection) — changes to config schema or environment variables affect behavior.
Exposes an async LangChain-compatible tool used by agent middleware or tool tables elsewhere in the backend; callers expect an async interface and the type/shape of returned content.
Depends on a ReadabilityExtractor implementation that is external to this folder (could be a shared utility elsewhere) — contracts of that extractor (input/output expectations) must be respected.
Performs network I/O and thus interacts with runtime concerns such as sandboxing, rate limits, and agent concurrency policies enforced elsewhere in the system.
recommendations: Preserve async function signatures and use of httpx.AsyncClient; ensure any changes maintain non-blocking behavior and proper client lifecycle (creation/closing or reuse).

Do not conflate error strings with successful content; maintain a clear, documented contract for success vs failure (exceptions, error objects, or explicit status) to avoid passing errors to the ReadabilityExtractor.
Respect and validate application config inputs (timeout, API key) at runtime; maintain current timeout enforcement and logging behavior for missing keys.
Ensure the returned Markdown is truncated to the established limit and remains valid/UTF-8; callers depend on the fixed-size contract.

When modifying behavior, run async unit/integration tests that exercise: successful crawl + extraction, API-key-missing path, network failures/timeouts, and concurrency scenarios to ensure changes don't block the event loop or leak resources.

backend/packages/harness/deerflow/commun.description: This folder implements a lightweight DuckDuckGo-based web search tool intended to be consumed by agent tooling. It exposes a LangChain-style tool (web_search_tool) that performs searches via the ddgs library, normalizes results to JSON, and is exported for import into the community tool registry so agents can call it without requiring API keys.
key_behaviors: Provide a callable LangChain-style tool object (web_search_tool) that agents can invoke to perform text searches.
Execute DuckDuckGo searches via the ddgs library (if available) and return results normalized into a consistent JSON structure.
Read optional configuration overrides to alter search behavior (timeouts, result limits, etc.) when available.
Gracefully handle missing ddgs dependency or runtime errors and return structured results/errors instead of raising import failures.
Be importable by name from the package entrypoint so community/tool registries can discover and register it.

insights.title: Tool must tolerate missing optional ddgs dependency
insights.problem: The ddgs library is an optional runtime dependency and may not be installed in all environments, which would crash import or usage if not handled.
insights.root_cause: Direct reliance on ddgs without guards causes ImportError or runtime failures when ddgs is absent.
insights.solution: Design the tool to detect the ddgs dependency at import or call time and return a structured, graceful result or error message; avoid hard import-time failures so the package remains importable as a community tool even when ddgs is not present.

insights.title: Normalized JSON return shape must be consistent
insights.problem: Downstream tools and agent logic expect a predictable JSON schema from web search results; changes to the format can break consumers.
insights.root_cause: Tool implementation decisions (fields, nesting, error representation) can diverge over time if not documented or enforced.
insights.solution: Keep a documented, consistent normalized JSON structure for success and error cases; when evolving the schema, ensure backward compatibility and communicate changes to any registry/consumers.

insights.title: Tool must be exportable by package entrypoint
insights.problem: Community tooling and registries locate tools by import path/name; failing to re-export the tool from the package prevents discovery and registration.
insights.root_cause: Missing or incorrect exports in __init__ prevent external code from importing web_search_tool by the expected module path.
insights.solution: Expose web_search_tool at the package-level import (via __init__.py) so callers can import the tool by the canonical path; ensure packaging preserves that export.

key_files.why: Defines the web_search_tool and helper _search_text that implement search execution, normalization, config override handling, and graceful dependency/error handling.
Exports web_search_tool at package level so other parts of the system (tool registry/lead agent) can import it by name for registration and use.

construct_relationships: tools.py:_search_text is the internal helper used by tools.py:web_search_tool to perform the actual DuckDuckGo query and prepare results.
tools.py:web_search_tool is implemented as a LangChain-style tool object (decorator/metadata) so agent runtimes can call it like other tools.
__init__.py re-exports tools.py:web_search_tool to present a stable package-level import path for discovery and registration.
notable_relationships: This tool depends on an optional external library (ddgs). Upstream packaging or runtime environments may or may not include ddgs, so callers must handle both cases.
Downstream agent components expect the tool to be importable by name from the community package; failing to export it breaks tool registration in the lead agent/tool registry.
Consumers expect a normalized JSON result format; any change to the returned schema can affect memory extraction, tool chaining, or UIs that display search results.
recommendations: Preserve the public symbol web_search_tool and its import path so community/tool discovery remains stable.
Retain graceful handling for missing ddgs dependency and keep error/success JSON shapes stable; tests should cover both 'ddgs present' and 'ddgs absent' scenarios.
When modifying result normalization, verify downstream consumers (agent tool registry, memory extractors, and any UI code) still work with the JSON shape.
If adding configuration overrides, ensure they are optional and documented; configuration parsing should not cause import-time failures.
Run unit tests that exercise search success, search error handling, and import-time behavior in environments without ddgs before merging edits.

backend/packages/harness/deerflow/commun.description: This folder provides a community-contributed image search tool that queries DuckDuckGo for images and exposes a normalized image_search_tool for use by agents. It matters because agents rely on a predictable tool contract (JSON payload, usage hints, error semantics) and optional external dependencies (ddgs) and configuration overrides when performing image lookups during runs.
key_behaviors: Accepts image search requests and performs DuckDuckGo queries via a helper (_search_images) producing a normalized JSON payload of image URLs and metadata.
Applies optional filters and configuration-driven overrides to the query and results (e.g., limiting results, restricting file types, or altering search parameters).
Handles missing external dependency (ddgs) or empty/failed search results gracefully and returns tool-friendly error/usability hints rather than raw exceptions.
Exposes the tool symbol (image_search_tool) via package export for registration in the agent tool table.

insights.title: Optional external dependency (ddgs) must be gracefully handled
insights.problem: The tool can fail at runtime if the ddgs library is not installed or importable, which would break agent tool executions.
insights.root_cause: Direct reliance on an external package for search without guaranteed availability in all deployments.
insights.solution: Maintain and preserve the existing graceful fallback behavior: detect absence of ddgs and return a clear, tool-level message/error rather than raising an unhandled ImportError. Any edits must preserve that conditional import/fallback logic.

insights.title: Agents depend on a consistent normalized JSON result and usage hints
insights.problem: Upstream code expects a consistent JSON payload structure (URLs, metadata, hints). Changing keys, shapes, or error formats will break consumers.
insights.root_cause: Tool consumers (agent prompts, downstream middleware) assume stable result schema and hint text.
insights.solution: Preserve the normalized payload shape and the presence of usage hints / metadata keys. If extending fields, keep backward-compatible additions and do not remove existing keys.

insights.title: Configuration-driven overrides and filters affect search behavior
insights.problem: Search results and behavior can vary depending on configuration; tests and runtime behavior must account for config overrides (result limits, filters).
insights.root_cause: Tool uses configurable parameters and optional filters that can change returned results and side-effects.
insights.solution: When modifying the tool, respect and thread configuration overrides and filter parameters through to _search_images. Ensure any default values remain consistent and document any changes to limits or filter semantics.

key_files.why: Implements the core search logic and the tool wrapper; contains _search_images and the image_search_tool behavior that agents will invoke.
Exports image_search_tool for discovery/registration by the broader agent tooling system; changing exports affects tool availability.

construct_relationships: image_search_tool (exported in __init__.py) calls _search_images (defined in tools.py) to perform DuckDuckGo queries and then transforms results into a normalized JSON payload with usage hints.
_search_images depends on the ddgs client/library when available; tools.py contains conditional handling around that dependency.
The tool reads and applies configuration overrides and optional filters passed in through tool parameters and/or global config, and returns results constrained by those parameters.
notable_relationships: This community tool is consumed by the agent tool table (lead_agent factory) and must conform to the tool invocation contract expected by LangGraph/LangChain tooling and middleware (input args, return type, error handling).
Runtime behavior depends on environment/network availability and external DuckDuckGo service; sandboxing/network policies and timeouts in the broader system can affect calls.
Presence (or absence) of ddgs in the deployment environment (uv workspace / Docker image) is an upstream packaging concern that affects this tool's runtime path.
recommendations: Preserve the public tool API: input parameters, normalized JSON output shape, metadata keys, and usage hint presence. Changes that remove or rename keys will break consumers.
Keep the current strategy for handling missing ddgs (detect and return a clear tool-friendly error/hint) rather than allowing ImportError to propagate.
Respect configuration-driven overrides and filter arguments; thread them into _search_images consistently and ensure defaults remain unchanged unless explicitly coordinated with consumers.

When editing, account for external network calls: ensure adequate timeouts and error wrapping; run unit tests for the tool and integration tests that exercise tool registration and expected payload shape.
If adding new behavior or fields, maintain backward compatibility and update any tests and the tool export in __init__.py so the tool remains discoverable by the agent runtime.

backend/packages/harness/deerflow/commun.description: This folder provides a community integration that exposes Firecrawl-backed web search and page-fetch tools to agents. It supplies @tool-decorated functions that obtain a Firecrawl client from configuration, perform remote searches and scrapes, and return normalized, truncated, agent-safe results consumed by the lead agent tool table and downstream workflows.
key_behaviors: Obtain Firecrawl API client from application configuration and environment
Perform remote web search queries and page fetch (scrape) requests against Firecrawl
Normalize and format fetched content into JSON/markdown snippets suitable for inclusion in agent responses and prompts
Enforce truncation/size limits and safe formatting to avoid overwhelming prompts or violating token budgets
Surface errors and metadata back to the calling agent tool framework

insights.title: Dependency on Firecrawl API key and config
insights.problem: Tools require Firecrawl credentials and configuration to instantiate the client; absent or misconfigured credentials will cause runtime failures.
insights.root_cause: The client factory reads API keys from the shared project configuration/environment rather than being fully decoupled or mocked.
insights.solution: When editing, preserve configuration lookup semantics and avoid hardcoding values; ensure any refactors keep a clear, testable client factory path and do not leak credentials in logs or exceptions.

insights.title: External network calls introduce latency and failures
insights.problem: Search and fetch calls can fail, time out, or be slow, which will impact agent responsiveness and may cascade into long-running runs or timeouts.
insights.root_cause: Tools directly call a third-party HTTP service without guaranteed retry/backoff or well-defined timeouts.
insights.solution: Maintain explicit timeouts, surface transient errors clearly to callers, and keep calls cancellable/mocked in tests. Any changes must preserve timeout and error propagation semantics expected by agent middleware and subagent executor.

insights.title: Fetched page content may exceed token/prompt budgets
insights.problem: Large scrapes can exceed downstream model token limits or cause prompt bloat if not truncated and formatted consistently.
insights.root_cause: Raw fetch results include full page bodies and metadata; truncation/formatting is applied at the tool layer to mitigate this risk.
insights.solution: Keep existing truncation and normalization rules intact or ensure any adjustments continue to enforce size limits and consistent formatting so the lead agent and middleware receive predictable payloads.

insights.title: Agents expect normalized JSON/markdown outputs
insights.problem: Consumers (lead agent, memory extraction, or UI) rely on predictable keys/structures; changing output shapes risks breaking downstream parsers or memory ingestion.
insights.root_cause: Tools transform Firecrawl responses into a specific schema and text format intended for immediate use by agents and storage systems.
insights.solution: Preserve stable output keys/types and document any schema changes. Ensure serialization remains JSON-serializable and compatible with memory middleware and the lead agent's tool contract.

insights.title: Integration touches external service and must be testable
insights.problem: Direct changes that introduce unmocked network behavior will break CI or make local tests flaky.
insights.root_cause: Tools are thin wrappers over an external client and do real HTTP requests in typical operation.
insights.solution: Ensure the client factory and tool functions remain mockable; edits should not remove injection points used by tests. New network-related behaviors must include unit tests with mocked FirecrawlApp and integration tests guarded or configurable.

key_files.why: Defines the Firecrawl client factory and the two @tool functions (web_search_tool, web_fetch_tool) that agents call for search and scraping; changing this file affects agent tool behavior directly.

construct_relationships: _get_firecrawl_client provides a FirecrawlApp client instance that both web_search_tool and web_fetch_tool use to perform network operations

web_search_tool and web_fetch_tool produce normalized outputs (JSON/markdown) that are intended to be consumed by the lead agent's tool table and downstream middleware (e.g., memory extraction, prompt construction)
Tools rely on the project configuration/environment for API keys and settings; the client factory is the single place these are read for the two tools
notable_relationships: These tools are part of the agent toolset assembled by make_lead_agent and therefore affect agent runs, prompt content, and streaming responses served by the LangGraph server

Output from these tools may be passed into MemoryMiddleware (facts extraction) or written into thread-local sandboxes/workspaces via sandbox tools, so their shape and size affect memory persistence and workspace artifacts

Since they call a third-party service, the tools' runtime requires outbound network access and valid credentials in containerized or deployed environments (Docker Compose, CI), and may be subject to rate limits or service-specific quotas
recommendations: Preserve the public function signatures and @tool metadata/return shapes so the lead agent tooling table and downstream consumers remain compatible
Do not expose or log raw API keys; any changes must continue secure configuration access patterns used across the repo
Ensure any edits keep explicit timeouts, error propagation, and safe truncation/formatting behaviors to avoid blocking runs or overflowing prompts

When modifying network behavior, update or add unit tests that mock the Firecrawl client and ensure CI remains deterministic; integration tests that require live Firecrawl credentials should be optional/guarded
Run backend unit tests and linting (make test, make lint) and validate end-to-end agent flows that consume these tools (lead_agent runs, memory extraction paths) after making changes

frontend/src/components/workspace/messag.description: Components that render conversation messages and related UI in the workspace chat view. This folder centralizes how single messages, grouped assistant reasoning (chain-of-thought), subtasks, markdown content, artifacts and loading states are presented and how special runtime artifacts (virtual paths, tool call results, citations, external links) are rendered and linked into the rest of the frontend.
key_behaviors: Render individual chat messages (human, assistant, tool) with rich previews for images, files, upload state, streaming, and copy-to-clipboard functionality.
Group message sequences into logical assistant flows (chain-of-thought steps and tool-call steps) and render them as an ordered CoT UI with per-step presentation.
Render markdown message payloads with custom handling for internal 'citation:' anchors and safe external link attributes (target/rel) in a client-only renderer.
Detect and render subtask/tool-call results (collapsible SubtaskCard) only for appropriate tool IDs (task), including live status/animations and expanded markdown results.
Show conversation loading skeletons and streaming indicators while thread state is initializing or receiving streamed content.

insights.title: Specialized treatment of citation: anchors and external links
insights.problem: Plain markdown links with citation:... and external http(s) links need distinct behaviors (internal navigation vs open new tab), and incorrect handling can break internal citation navigation or create unsafe external links.
insights.root_cause: Markdown rendering pipeline needed to map specific anchor patterns to internal components and apply safe attributes for external hrefs; default markdown renderers treat all anchors the same.
insights.solution: Centralize anchor handling in the markdown renderer: convert citation:... hrefs into internal CitationLink components and mark http(s) links as external with target/rel attributes. Ensure this client-only component stays client-rendered and returns null for falsy content to avoid SSR mismatch.

insights.title: Task vs non-task tool call handling causes runtime errors
insights.problem: Rendering SubtaskCard or accessing task.status for non-task tool_call IDs caused TypeError or broken UI when an unrelated tool_call result was present.
insights.root_cause: Earlier code attempted to treat all tool_call entries uniformly and accessed task-specific fields without verifying the tool ID or shape.
insights.solution: Only map and render SubtaskCard for tool_call entries that correspond to the 'task' tool and guard accesses to task-specific fields. Components that consume grouped messages should check tool ID/shape before reading task properties.

insights.title: Copy-to-clipboard includes or strips citation blocks unexpectedly
insights.problem: Copying message content either included citation blocks/links that yield messy clipboard text or stripped citations leading to incomplete copied content.
insights.root_cause: Copy logic is applied to rendered markdown and may either remove citation nodes or include link markup without cleaning for plain-text copying.
insights.solution: Be aware that copy behavior is coupled to markdown rendering. When editing copy behavior, preserve consistent policy: decide whether citations should be present in clipboard and implement deterministic cleaning/serialization of rendered message content to plain text.

insights.title: Tool result content overflows tooltips and may be unusable
insights.problem: Long tool outputs (e.g., ls, read_file results) can overflow tooltip containers and not wrap properly, making content unreadable or causing layout issues.
insights.root_cause: Tool result previews are rendered inside constrained tooltip/hover containers without word-splitting or overflow handling.
insights.solution: Treat long tool outputs as first-class content: ensure previews can wrap or be truncated with a clear affordance (open full view). When editing tooltip/popup styles or rendering logic, respect wrapping and overflow behaviors and consider providing click-through to full artifact views.

insights.title: Virtual /mnt artifact paths require mapping to public URLs
insights.problem: UI components resolve virtual workspace paths (e.g., /mnt/*) to public artifact URLs for previews and downloads; incorrect mapping breaks file display or causes broken links.
insights.root_cause: The frontend renders artifacts created by sandboxes or skills using virtual paths that are not directly web-accessible and must be converted via Gateway routes.
insights.solution: Preserve the mapping logic from virtual paths to public artifact URLs and keep components that build artifact links in sync with backend artifact-serving endpoints. Any edits to file-list rendering must preserve this mapping and handle missing or stale artifacts gracefully.

key_files.why: Client-only markdown renderer with custom anchor/citation handling and external-link safety — central to how markdown messages and subtask results are presented.
Transforms raw messages into chain-of-thought and tool-call steps; orchestrates per-step UI and is key to showing assistant reasoning and tool interactions.
Renders a single message with file/image previews, upload and streaming states, copy behavior and artifact URL resolution — the per-message rendering core.
Top-level message rendering controller: groups messages, maps group types to UI components (present-files, subtasks, clarifications), and controls skeleton/loading/follow behavior.
Displays subtask prompts, live status, and markdown results in a collapsible card — used for rendering 'task' tool_call results with live UI affordances.

construct_relationships: MessageList consumes the thread stream and uses groupMessages to map flat Message arrays into groups which are rendered by MessageListItem, MarkdownContent, ArtifactFileList, SubtaskCard, and MessageGroup.

MessageGroup converts grouped messages into chain-of-thought steps and delegates rendering of individual steps to ChainOfThought primitives; it recognizes tool_call entries and emits CoT tool steps consumed by ChainOfThought components.
MarkdownContent is used by MessageListItem and SubtaskCard to render markdown results; it performs anchor transformation (citation: -> CitationLink) and external link attribute injection.
MessageListItem resolves virtual artifact paths (e.g., /mnt/*) into public artifact URLs for previews and download links and exposes CopyButton semantics tied to the message's rendered content.
SubtaskCard reads the latest message(s) for a subtask group to render status, last tool call explanation, and markdown result — it expects the tool call to conform to the 'task' tool shape.
notable_relationships: Frontend artifact URL resolution depends on the Gateway artifact serving endpoints and the runtime virtual path convention (/mnt/…), so frontend path-to-URL logic must stay consistent with backend artifact routing.

Tool-call naming and payload shapes (tool IDs like 'task', 'write_file', 'str_replace', 'bash', 'ls', 'read_file') are implied contracts shared with the backend agent tooling; UI components assume specific fields for status and results and will break if backend tool payload shapes change.

Streaming and thread lifecycle semantics (thread.isLoading, streaming indicators, skeleton) are tied to the thread stream abstraction used across the workspace; changes here affect perceived responsiveness and live update UX.
Client-only constraints: markdown-content.tsx is explicitly 'use client', so it cannot be used in SSR contexts; any changes that affect SSR/CSR boundaries must respect this.
recommendations: Preserve the special-case behaviors: citation: anchor conversion, external-link target/rel attributes, and guarded rendering for 'task' tool calls to avoid runtime errors.

When modifying copy-to-clipboard logic, verify plain-text serialization semantics for citations and links to avoid confusing clipboard results; test with messages containing citation blocks and links.
Maintain the virtual-path -> public-URL mapping assumptions; validate artifact previews/downloads across saved runs and ensure missing artifacts are handled without breaking the message render.
Test long tool output rendering: check tooltip overflow/wrapping and that long outputs have a discoverable full-view affordance to avoid unreadable previews.

Run UI-level tests and smoke checks when editing these components: message grouping behaviors, SubtaskCard expansion/collapse, streaming/skeleton states, markdown rendering (including citations and external links), artifact URL resolution, and copy/paste behavior.

backend/packages/harness/deerflow/config.description: This folder centralizes all typed configuration models, loaders, and runtime accessors used by the DeerFlow harness. It provides Pydantic schemas for subsystems (sandbox, memory, models, skills, subagents, summarization, guardrails, stream bridge, etc.), deterministic path utilities for per-thread sandboxes, and module-level cached singletons/getters that the rest of the service consumes at runtime. These files matter because long‑running services (agents, middleware, sandbox providers, subagent executor and the Gateway) rely on stable, reloadable config objects and path resolution semantics for correctness, security and cross-process isolation.
key_behaviors: Deterministic config discovery and loading from filesystem (config.yaml, extensions_config.json/legacy) with environment-variable placeholder resolution.
Validation and typing of subsystem configuration using Pydantic, producing authoritative AppConfig and many subsystem-specific models.
Module-level caching and singleton-style accessors (get/set/load/reload/reset) for subsystem configs so callers read in-memory instances.
Automatic refresh/reload semantics driven by file mtime or explicit reload calls that propagate into global subsystem getters.
Virtual-to-host path resolution and per-thread sandbox directory creation/cleanup used by sandbox providers and tools (ensuring correct mounts and permissions).
Per-subsystem loader functions that take raw dicts (from AppConfig) and populate module-level runtime config objects consumed by middleware and factories.

insights.title: Config path resolution depends on CWD and global candidates
insights.problem: Config discovery relied on the process current working directory and module global candidates, causing nondeterministic selection when services are started from different directories or in concurrent contexts.
insights.root_cause: resolve_config_path and _default_config_candidates used cwd and global variables rather than explicit, injected paths for all callers.
insights.solution: Treat config path resolution as deterministic by honoring explicit overrides and documenting that callers may supply paths; operations that rely on discovery must handle differing invocation contexts and validate the resolved path before using cached config.

insights.title: Module-level cached singletons are widespread
insights.problem: Many modules maintain module-level cached config instances (get_xxx_config / set_xxx_config) which can lead to stale state, race conditions, or nondeterministic behavior in multithreaded or multi-process contexts.
insights.root_cause: Global mutable state with lazy loading and mtime-based reloads without centralized synchronization semantics.
insights.solution: Any change must respect existing getter/setter/reload/reset APIs; callers expect immediate visibility of set_* and reload calls and rely on mtime checks for automatic reloads in long-running processes.

insights.title: Host/virtual path resolution and Windows preservation
insights.problem: Path joining and normalization risk changing Windows-style host paths or mis-resolving virtual-to-host mappings, affecting sandbox mounts and file access.
insights.root_cause: Use of generic Path operations without explicit preservation of host-native path syntax; multiple places validate thread_id and join paths.
insights.solution: Maintain existing semantics that preserve Windows-style host paths, validate thread_id strictly, and use the centralized Paths utilities for all sandbox path operations. Thread directory creation/deletion behavior and permission settings are relied upon by sandboxes and tests.

insights.title: Thread directory creation and cleanup
insights.problem: Thread-specific sandbox directories are created with permissive permissions and until now there was no canonical delete operation across the codebase.
insights.root_cause: Paths provided creation utilities but lacked a canonical delete_thread_dir; cleanup was handled inconsistently elsewhere.
insights.solution: Respect the centralized thread directory semantics (creation, permission model, and the canonical delete functionality) when modifying code that creates or removes thread directories; ensure callers use Paths.delete_thread_dir (or equivalent) if present.

insights.title: ModelConfig as authoritative source for provider capabilities
insights.problem: Model/provider configuration lacked explicit flags to opt providers into newer APIs (e.g., /v1/responses) and structured versioning, causing ambiguity for providers that support multiple endpoints.
insights.root_cause: ModelConfig originally omitted fields like use_responses_api and output_version.
insights.solution: Treat ModelConfig as the single source of truth for provider capabilities. If adding or changing fields ensure consumers (tool loaders, model factories) read those fields and that configs in AppConfig include them.

insights.title: Some runtime toggles were previously missing from AppConfig tree
insights.problem: Flag-based features (example: token usage tracking) were not structured in the central AppConfig and therefore could not be toggled consistently by external config files.
insights.root_cause: TokenUsageConfig and similar toggles were added at folder level but required explicit inclusion in the top-level AppConfig.
