insights.solution: When editing AppConfig or its consumers, preserve the presence and propagation of small toggle configs so middleware components can rely on get_* accessors for runtime checks.

insights.title: Per-ACP-agent environment injection missing
insights.problem: ACP agent subprocesses had no configured per-agent environment injection mechanism, limiting isolated per-agent env settings.
insights.root_cause: ACPAgentConfig model and loader existed but lacked explicit env injection fields or the runtime plumbing to apply them to subprocess invocations.
insights.solution: Assume ACPAgentConfig may be augmented with per-agent env settings; callers that spawn ACP subprocesses expect to be able to query ACP agent configs for env injection.

insights.title: Extensions config needs recursive env resolution and backward-compat
insights.problem: Extensions_config.json (and legacy mcp_config.json) contain values that may reference environment variables and must be loaded consistently and cached.
insights.root_cause: Multiple config formats and the need for $ENV resolution across nested structures.
insights.solution: Use centralized resolve_env_variables behavior when loading extensions configs and preserve cached accessors with reload/reset semantics used by MCP tooling and skill toggles.

key_files.why: Central entrypoint that discovers/loads config.yaml into the authoritative AppConfig, resolves $ENV placeholders, caches the config, and triggers per-subsystem loader functions; changes here affect all subsystem configs and reload behavior.
Implements virtual-to-host path resolution, thread directory lifecycle, and host path joining semantics relied on by sandbox providers and tools; path correctness and thread cleanup logic live here.
Loads MCP/OAuth and extension toggles (extensions_config.json / legacy) with recursive env resolution and cached accessors used by MCP tooling and skill discovery.
Authoritative model/provider schema used by model factories and tool loaders; contains capability flags (thinking/vision/use_responses_api) that affect runtime behavior of model integrations.
Defines per-subagent timeout and turn overrides consumed by the subagent executor and logging; changes here directly affect subagent lifecycle and concurrency behavior.

construct_relationships: app_config.AppConfig is the root loader that reads config.yaml, resolves env placeholders, and calls subsystem-specific load_* functions (e.g., load_memory_config_from_dict, load_summarization_config_from_dict, load_subagents_config_from_dict) to populate module-level singletons.

Paths utilities (Paths.resolve_path, create/delete thread dirs) are used by sandbox configuration (SandboxConfig mounts) and by Local/Aio sandbox providers to map virtual sandbox mounts to host filesystem paths.
ExtensionsConfig loader resolves env placeholders and its cached get_extensions_config is consumed by MCP/tool loader code and by skills mounting logic to enable/disable MCP features.

ModelConfig values are embedded in tool and provider definitions (ToolConfig/model_config) and are read by model/provider factories to decide API endpoints and capability flags (thinking/vision/use_responses_api).

SubagentsAppConfig (from subagents_config) is referenced by the subagent executor to determine per-agent timeouts and max-turn values; changes propagate to subagent execution and observability/logging.
notable_relationships: The lead_agent middleware chain and factory functions depend on many of these getters (get_memory_config, get_summarization_config, get_title_config, get_guardrails_config, get_stream_bridge_config, etc.) so any change to config shapes or getter semantics impacts agent startup and runtime middleware behavior.

Sandbox providers (backend/src/sandbox/*) depend on Paths for thread directory structure and on SandboxConfig for mount/env settings; containerized vs local sandbox behavior depends on these configs and on preserved host path semantics.
Subagent executor and registry (backend/src/subagents/*) depend on subagents_config; changes to defaults or schema must be compatible with how subagents are spawned and timed out in executor.
MCP/tool loading and skill mounting (skills/ and tool_config/tool_search) depend on extensions_config, skills_config, and tool_search_config for deferred-loading toggles and mount paths.

Long‑running services and LangGraph server rely on mtime-based reload semantics in app_config and extension config caches; Docker/dev vs local invocation contexts can produce different resolved paths—caller code expects consistent behavior across deployment modes.
recommendations: Respect and use the central AppConfig loader (app_config.py) and its resolve_config_path precedence instead of ad-hoc file reads; if supplying an explicit config path, ensure callers pass it through rather than relying on cwd.
Do not bypass module-level get/set/load/reset accessor patterns when altering subsystem configs; updates should use the existing APIs so cached singletons remain consistent across the process.
When touching path handling, use Paths utilities for virtual-to-host resolution and thread directory lifecycle; preserve Windows host-path semantics and thread_id validation logic.

Be aware of the mtime-based cache/reload behavior: edits that change when or how reloads are detected require you to consider long-running services and concurrent threads/processes that expect automatic reloads or immediate visibility of set_* calls.

If you add, rename or change fields in Pydantic models (AppConfig or subsystem models), update the corresponding loaders and ensure callers that read the old fields are updated; run backend unit tests, sandbox-related tests and middleware integration tests to catch propagation errors.

Any change that affects ModelConfig or ToolConfig semantics may impact model/provider factories and tool loaders—verify provider capabilities (e.g., use_responses_api) are respected by downstream code paths.
Before modifying ACP or ACPAgentConfig, confirm where ACP subprocesses are created and that any new per-agent env fields will be applied when spawning subprocesses.

After modifications, run the backend test suite (make test / uv run pytest), sandbox tests (preferably under Docker dev to exercise AioSandboxProvider), and CI linting/formatting steps to ensure compatibility with existing deployment and CI expectations.

backend/packages/harness/deerflow/sandbo.description: This subtree implements the sandbox abstraction and a host-backed LocalSandbox for DeerFlow. It provides bidirectional container↔host path mapping, read/write semantics (including read-only mount enforcement), command execution with output masking, search/listing utilities, provider lifecycle and middleware integration so agent tools can operate against a consistent virtual filesystem view whether running locally or in a container.
key_behaviors: Translate container-style virtual paths to host filesystem paths before any filesystem or shell operation, and reverse-translate host paths back to virtual paths in outputs.
Execute shell commands and filesystem operations under a sandbox interface while enforcing read-only mounts and serializing concurrent file mutations with per-path locks.
Provide builtin sandbox-aware tools (bash, glob, grep, read/write/ls) that validate paths, apply ignore/size/binary safeguards, and truncate large outputs to protect LLM context windows.
Manage a singleton SandboxProvider that constructs LocalSandbox path mappings from app config and supplies instances to the runtime via middleware (acquire/reuse/release per agent/thread).
Gate unsafe features (host bash) via centralized security policy helpers and surface structured sandbox-specific exceptions for callers to handle consistently.

insights.title: Bidirectional virtual↔host path mapping must preserve separators and trailing slashes
insights.problem: Path translation could lose trailing separators or mix separator styles, leading to incorrect concatenations and leaking host layout in outputs.
insights.root_cause: Naive string replacements and path joins that don't preserve virtual path semantics across platforms and command outputs.
insights.solution: Always preserve virtual path separator style and trailing slashes during forward and reverse resolution; mask host paths in all command/tool outputs to present only virtual paths and prevent host-layout leakage.

insights.title: Writes to read-only mounts must be blocked with standard filesystem errors
insights.problem: Write operations could succeed against mounts that are intended to be read-only, or produce non-standard errors that callers cannot interpret.
insights.root_cause: Insufficient enforcement at the API boundary and lack of consistent error mapping to standard errno values.
insights.solution: Enforce read-only flags at the sandbox API level and raise errno.EROFS-style errors (and/or SandboxFileError carrying that errno) so callers and tooling can handle denied writes consistently.

insights.title: Code assumes non-null runtime.context in middleware and helpers
insights.problem: Accessing runtime.context when it's None (LangGraph default) causes TypeError/AttributeError during lazy initialization paths.
insights.root_cause: Direct indexing/attribute access of runtime.context without null checks in middleware and sandbox helper writers.
insights.solution: Guard all runtime.context accesses in middleware and sandbox helpers (lazy init paths) and fail gracefully or skip sandbox actions when context is absent to avoid uncaught exceptions.

insights.title: Grep/ls/glob can return very large results or scan binaries
insights.problem: Unbounded directory listings or naive grep can produce multi-megabyte outputs, scan binary files, or overwhelm LLM context windows.
insights.root_cause: Lack of strict size limits, binary detection, ignore pattern consistency and output truncation across search/listing tools.
insights.solution: Apply ignore patterns, binary/size detection, max-result / max-file-size limits and truncate outputs consistently from the search/listing tools to prevent context overflow and expensive I/O.

insights.title: File operations require per-path serialization
insights.problem: Concurrent writes/updates to the same sandbox path from different threads can race and corrupt files or produce inconsistent results.
insights.root_cause: Multiple threads or subagents may operate on shared host-backed paths without serialization.
insights.solution: Use the per-sandbox per-path locking utilities to serialize write/update operations. Ensure lock creation is guard-protected to avoid races when creating locks.

insights.title: Local provider constructs path mappings from config and must validate mounts
insights.problem: Invalid or reserved mount prefixes can be introduced via config, or the provider may not expose configured custom mounts to the LocalSandbox.
insights.root_cause: Mount configuration parsing and validation not being centralized or enforced at provider initialization.
insights.solution: Have the provider validate mount configuration against reserved prefixes, warn on invalid mounts, and supply a consistent mapping object to the LocalSandbox so tools and reverse-resolution work reliably.

key_files.why: Implements bidirectional path resolution, read-only enforcement, command execution and output masking—the core local sandbox behavior relied on by tools and middleware.
Provides sandbox-aware filesystem and shell helper functions and the builtin sandbox tools (bash, ls, glob, grep, read/write) that agents call.
Creates the LocalSandbox singleton and constructs path mappings from app config (custom mounts, skills path) used throughout the local sandbox implementation.
Allocates/releases sandboxes for agent runs; integrates the sandbox provider with the LangGraph runtime lifecycle and must handle runtime.context nullability.
Implements glob/grep utilities with ignore patterns, binary/size safeguards and result structures used by search-related tools.

construct_relationships: LocalSandbox (_local/local_sandbox.py) uses path mappings supplied by LocalSandboxProvider to translate virtual paths to host paths before performing filesystem and command operations.
tools.py helpers call LocalSandbox path resolution functions (replace_virtual_path, replace_virtual_paths_in_command) and rely on LocalSandbox to mask host paths when returning tool outputs.

SandboxMiddleware acquires a Sandbox instance from the singleton SandboxProvider and attaches it to thread state so agent tools can access sandbox APIs during runs; after_agent releases that acquisition.
File operation functions in LocalSandbox (write/update) obtain locks from file_operation_lock.get_file_operation_lock to serialize operations for a given sandbox and path.

search.py functions (find_glob_matches, find_grep_matches) are invoked by tools.py glob/grep tools and must respect the same ignore patterns and size/binary limits used by listing tools to provide consistent results.
notable_relationships: Agents and middleware outside this subtree (lead_agent and agent middleware chain) depend on SandboxMiddleware to provide a sandbox in runtime.context—changes here affect agent tool availability and subagent behavior.

AioSandboxProvider (not in this folder) is an alternate provider; provider selection is config-driven via sandbox_provider.py and security.py gating (host bash policy) must remain consistent across provider implementations.

Skills, uploads and thread workspace directories (configured virtual mounts like /mnt/skills, /mnt/user-data, /mnt/acp-workspace) are mounted via provider mappings and are used by skills and builtin tools elsewhere in the codebase.
Structured sandbox exceptions are consumed by callers across the codebase; any change to exception shapes or errno mapping will affect error handling in higher-level components (agents, Gateway, UI).
recommendations: When editing path translation logic, preserve virtual path separator style and trailing slashes and ensure reverse-mapping is applied to all user-visible outputs (command stdout/stderr, tool responses).

Always enforce read-only mount semantics at the sandbox API level and surface errors in a standard errno-like form (e.g., errno.EROFS) or via SandboxFileError so callers can distinguish permission failures.
Guard all accesses to runtime.context (middleware and sandbox helpers) for None and handle lazy-initialization flows gracefully to avoid runtime exceptions during agent startup.
Use file_operation_lock for any code path that mutates host-backed files to avoid races; ensure the guard lock pattern for lock creation is maintained.
Respect ignore patterns, binary detection, max-size and result truncation in listing/search tools; changing these limits requires verifying downstream LLM/agent behavior to avoid context overflow.

When modifying provider lifecycle or singleton behavior, run tests that cover provider reset/shutdown and middleware acquire/release flows, and ensure mount validation still warns for invalid configs.

Run the backend test suite (including sandbox-related unit tests) and sandbox integration scenarios (agent runs that execute tools, subagent tasks, and file I/O) after any change in this subtree to catch cross-module regressions.

backend/packages/harness/deerflow/models.description: This folder implements provider-specific chat model adapters, credential discovery, and a factory that constructs configured chat model instances used by the runtime. It normalizes provider differences (thinking/reasoning toggles, streaming, tool-call payloads), patches SDK behaviors to preserve non-standard reasoning/tool metadata, and provides credential loading for CLI/OAuth-authenticated LLM endpoints — all enabling multi-provider, multi-turn reasoning and tool-calling flows.
key_behaviors: Construct configured chat model instances from runtime configuration and attach tracing callbacks
Discover and load provider credentials (Claude Code, Codex CLI) from environment or filesystem
Adapt and normalize provider-specific request/response payloads so multi-turn reasoning and tool-calls preserve provider metadata (reasoning, thought_signature, reasoning_details)
Stream and reassemble SSE responses while preserving intermediate assistant content, tool call metadata, and reasoning deltas
Apply retries/backoff, billing headers, and payload augmentation (prompt caching, thinking budgets) for specific providers

insights.title: Claude OAuth needs billing-identification metadata and bearer usage
insights.problem: Requests using Claude Code OAuth tokens (sk-ant-oat*) failed with generic 400 errors when billing-identification blocks were missing.
insights.root_cause: Anthropic endpoints require an explicit billing header/metadata.user_id block for OAuth Bearer tokens; SDKs and default payloads omitted this.
insights.solution: Detect OAuth bearer usage, inject required billing header and metadata.user_id into payloads, and patch Anthropic client usage when necessary.

insights.title: Credential file/FD handling is environment-sensitive and can fail
insights.problem: Reading credentials from paths or file descriptors can be non-portable and crash on unexpected directory/JSON content.
insights.root_cause: Use of Path.home(), reliance on /dev/fd/<n>, and lack of robust JSON/dir checks make the loader brittle across OS/containers.
insights.solution: Auto-discover credentials across env vars and file inputs with explicit validation, expiry checks, and defensive JSON/path handling; log source and failure modes.

insights.title: Thinking/reasoning flags differ between providers
insights.problem: Different providers expect thinking/reasoning toggles in different locations (top-level, extra_body, chat_template_kwargs), causing incorrect enablement or missing flags.
insights.root_cause: Provider SDKs use inconsistent field names and nesting for thinking-related flags; factory mappings previously missed some provider styles (e.g., vLLM).
insights.solution: Normalize thinking flags per-provider when constructing model instances and merge extra_body safely (support vLLM/chat_template kwargs mapping).

insights.title: Codex SSE streams can end with empty final payloads
insights.problem: Final SSE 'completed' event sometimes contains an empty completed.response.output, which prior logic treated as an empty final answer and discarded earlier streamed output.
insights.root_cause: Some Codex streams emit assistant output in intermediate events and an empty final output, so relying solely on the final 'completed' payload loses content.
insights.solution: Accumulate streamed SSE items and preserve earlier assistant output even if the final completed.response.output is empty; ensure assembled result includes all streamed content.

insights.title: Tool-call arguments from providers may be malformed
insights.problem: Malformed or non-JSON 'arguments' fields from provider responses caused parsing crashes and broke tool-calling flows.
insights.root_cause: Providers can emit non-JSON or unexpected formats; parsers assumed well-formed JSON.
insights.solution: Gracefully handle malformed tool-call arguments with defensive parsing and fallbacks so tool-call extraction does not crash the flow.

insights.title: Non-standard reasoning/think metadata is dropped by default SDK serialization
insights.problem: LangChain/OpenAI/other adapters dropped fields such as vLLM 'reasoning', MiniMax 'reasoning_details' or Gemini 'thought_signature', breaking multi-turn reasoning and tool-call flows.
insights.root_cause: Standard model wrappers serialize only canonical fields; provider-specific fields live in additional metadata that is not re-injected into subsequent payloads.
insights.solution: Patch/adapt model wrappers to preserve and copy provider-specific reasoning and thought_signature metadata into outgoing requests and message chunks so downstream gateways accept and continue thinking-enabled dialogues.

insights.title: Provider requests need tuned retries for transient errors
insights.problem: Transient server errors and rate limits can produce recoverable failures that need retry/backoff handling.
insights.root_cause: Default SDK retry behavior may be insufficient or missing for some provider endpoints and OAuth flows.
insights.solution: Implement provider-aware retry/backoff policies for rate limits and server errors and instrument logging to surface retry events.

key_files.why: Central factory that instantiates and normalizes chat model objects from configuration and attaches tracing; it orchestrates provider-specific mappings used across the runtime.
Provider adapter that enables Claude OAuth bearer auth, injects required billing metadata, prompt caching, thinking budgets, and retry/backoff logic.
Encapsulates discovery and validation logic for CLI/OAuth credentials (Claude Code, Codex CLI) relied on by provider adapters.
Adapter for ChatGPT Codex Responses endpoint: message conversion, SSE streaming assembly, tool-call parsing, and CLI auth handling—critical for Codex-based flows.
Adapter to preserve vLLM reasoning fields and convert streaming deltas into LangChain message chunks while retaining reasoning/tool metadata.

construct_relationships: create_chat_model (factory.py) instantiates provider-specific classes (ClaudeChatModel, CodexChatModel, VllmChatModel, PatchedChatOpenAI/MiniMax/DeepSeek) and merges per-provider config (thinking flags, extra_body).
credential_loader functions are consumed by provider adapters (claude_provider.py, openai_codex_provider.py) to supply OAuth or CLI credentials at construction/run time.

Provider adapters patch or subclass upstream SDK/LangChain classes to inject or restore provider-specific metadata (thought_signature, reasoning, reasoning_details) before serializing outgoing payloads.
CodexChatModel (_stream_response) accumulates SSE events and passes parsed assistant content and tool-call structures into higher-level message results used by the runtime.
Patched provider adapters ensure that reasoning/tool metadata flows from incoming AI messages through to subsequent request payloads so multi-turn reasoning and tool-calls remain valid.
notable_relationships: lead_agent (agents/lead_agent) builds model instances via the factory.create_chat_model and depends on these adapters to correctly enable thinking/tool-call flows in the agent runtime.

Middleware that toggles thinking, memory extraction, and tool invocation expects provider adapters to preserve/propagate reasoning and tool metadata across turns; breaking that will surface as invalid argument or broken tool calls upstream.

Subagents and builtin tools that rely on streamed output and tool-call parsing (task, bash, sandbox tools) depend on correct SSE assembly and resilient parsing in openai_codex_provider and vllm_provider.
Gateway configuration and runtime model configs feed into the factory; changes to config merging or extra_body normalization affect how providers receive thinking flags and tracing settings.
Tracing/instrumentation integration attaches callbacks at model instantiation time in the factory; adapters must expose or accept callback hooks for tracing to function.
recommendations: When editing any adapter or the factory, preserve non-standard provider fields (reasoning, reasoning_details, thought_signature) across request/response serialization and ensure they are re-injected into outgoing payloads for subsequent turns.
Do not alter credential discovery semantics without accounting for portability (Path.home(), /dev/fd) and explicit expiry/validation; validate credential sources and log provenance.

Maintain streaming assembly behavior: accumulate intermediate SSE items so final 'completed' events that lack output do not discard earlier assistant content; include tests that stream intermediate events then a terminal empty completed event.
Retain existing retry/backoff and billing header injection behavior for providers that require them (Claude OAuth, Codex CLI) and ensure HTTP retry semantics are preserved in changes.
Handle malformed tool-call arguments defensively; changes must avoid raising unhandled exceptions on non-JSON or unexpected argument formats.

Run unit and integration tests that exercise: multi-turn thinking flows, tool-call round-trips, SSE streaming edge cases, credential loading in container and host environments, and factory tracing callback attachment. Also run CI linting/formatting to keep compatibility with repository standards.

Be mindful of downstream consumers: lead_agent construction, middleware ordering (thinking/memory), and subagent executor flows depend on the contracts implemented here — coordinate any changes that alter message or payload shapes.

frontend/src/components/workspace/artifa.description: This folder contains the workspace artifact UI: provider/state for artifacts, a trigger button, a file list, and a file detail/preview pane. It centralizes selection state, preview/download/install actions, and artifact-specific rendering rules (code vs markdown vs HTML vs remote resources), and therefore mediates user interactions that require backend artifact URLs and install APIs.
key_behaviors: Maintain and expose artifact list and selection state to workspace UI (open/close panel, select/deselect files).

Render artifact previews: choose between code editor, markdown rendering, live HTML iframe, or external-resource preview via backend URL depending on artifact metadata and whether the artifact is a write-file virtual artifact.

Download / copy / open artifact content: provide clipboard copy, direct-download, and open-in-new-tab flows using either raw content loaded locally or backend-provided artifact URLs for non-write files.
Install .skill artifacts by calling backend install API and show loading / toast feedback; respect environment flags that can disable install in demo/static builds.
Expose a UI entry point (trigger) that conditionally renders based on artifacts presence and toggles the artifact panel via provider/context integration with the global sidebar.

insights.title: Non-write artifacts may require backend URLs for preview
insights.problem: Artifact content loader returns raw text only for some artifacts, which prevents previewing binary or resource artifacts that must be displayed via URL (e.g., images, remote-served HTML).
insights.root_cause: Separation between raw text content retrieval and backend-served artifact resource URLs; write-file virtual artifacts are available as text but other artifacts are expected to be fetched/embedded via backend endpoints.
insights.solution: When implementing preview logic or changing artifact fetching, preserve the distinction: use raw text for code/markdown/write-file artifacts and use backend-provided artifact URLs for resource previews. Ensure URL generation/injection remains intact and that components can handle both modes.

insights.title: Skill install action must be environment-aware
insights.problem: Attempting to install .skill files in static/demo builds or without server backing leads to confusing failures or silent errors for users.
insights.root_cause: Install action requires server-side support; demo/static builds do not provide backend install endpoints or are intentionally disabled.
insights.solution: Preserve the environment check and disabled UI state (tooltip/disabled action) for install flows. Any edits must keep the guard so installs are only enabled when backend APIs exist and are reachable.

insights.title: getFileIcon signature/usage is fragile across callsites
insights.problem: Icon helper signature changed to accept className; callers must pass className to preserve size and styling, otherwise UI sizing breaks.
insights.root_cause: Centralized icon helper changed its API without keeping a backward-compatible call signature for consumers.
insights.solution: When editing components that render file icons, ensure the new signature is used consistently and propagate className where required. If modifying icon usage, check all artifact list/detail callsites together to avoid inconsistent sizes.

insights.title: Artifact selection has side effects on global sidebar
insights.problem: Selecting an artifact left the sidebar open in some modes causing extra clicks for users on dynamic deployments.
insights.root_cause: Selection handler did not coordinate with global sidebar state or ignored environment-specific behavior.
insights.solution: Maintain existing behavior where selection toggles/affects global sidebar (e.g., close sidebar on select except in static/demo mode). Any changes to selection should respect the integration with global sidebar state and the environment mode checks.

insights.title: Copied/downloaded artifact content may include inconsistent citation blocks or badges
insights.problem: Copy/download operations may include citation text or external link badges inconsistently, leading to unexpected content being pasted or saved.
insights.root_cause: Artifact preview and content export paths differ (preview injects citation/badge UI while raw content API returns text), and copy/download uses content without harmonizing citation handling.
insights.solution: When touching copy/download flows, preserve the existing normalization: copy/download should use the same canonical content path (typically raw text without injected UI decorations) and the UI should still surface citation links via unified CitationLink components rather than embedding them into exported content.

key_files.why: Central provider for artifact list, selection state, open/close behavior, and integration point with global sidebar and environment flags; edits here affect all artifact consumers.
Presents artifact listing and initiates downloads and .skill install API calls; changes affect selection, icon rendering, and install feedback flows.
Implements preview logic, decides between code editor/markdown/iframe, handles copy/download/open actions and skill-install triggers; central to artifact display behavior.
Entry-point UI button that conditionally renders and toggles the artifacts panel via context; small but affects discoverability and panel opening behavior.
Re-exports artifact components; important for import paths and build consumers outside the folder.

construct_relationships: ArtifactsProvider (context.tsx) stores artifacts list, selectedArtifact and open state and exposes select/deselect/toggle helpers used by ArtifactFileList and ArtifactTrigger.

ArtifactFileList reads artifacts from ArtifactsProvider, renders file icons via getFileIcon, and calls provider.selectArtifact to show details; it also initiates backend install/download API calls and surfaces toast/loading states.

ArtifactFileDetail uses provider.selectedArtifact and useArtifactContent to obtain raw text when available; it chooses rendering mode (CodeEditor, Streamdown markdown, iframe for HTML) or uses backend artifact URL for resource previews when the artifact is not a write-file.

ArtifactFileDetail triggers skill install flows for .skill files by calling the same backend install API used by ArtifactFileList and relies on environment flags from context or process env to disable the action in demo builds.
ArtifactTrigger consumes ArtifactsProvider to decide visibility and to call open/close helpers that ultimately modify the global sidebar state via provider integration.
notable_relationships: Backend artifact download/preview endpoints: previewing non-write artifacts depends on backend-generated artifact URLs and must be reachable and stable; install action depends on a backend install API endpoint.
Global sidebar state: ArtifactsProvider coordinates with the global sidebar (outside this folder) to open/close the artifacts panel; changes here must align with that external state contract.
Environment/static-demo flags: Several behaviors (skill install, sidebar auto-close) are conditioned on environment or build-time flags that come from higher-level configuration or build environment.

Shared UI primitives: components rely on external UI/code components (CodeEditor, Streamdown, CitationLink, toast system) and icon helpers whose APIs must be respected when changing rendering or props.
recommendations: Do not change or remove the provider's public shape (selectedArtifact, artifacts list, select/deselect/toggle helpers) without updating all consumers (list, detail, trigger).
Preserve the distinction between write-file (raw-content) artifacts and non-write/resource artifacts that require backend artifact URLs; ensure preview paths remain available.
Keep environment-aware guards for skill install and sidebar behavior (static/demo builds); confirm any change still reads the same env/config flags used elsewhere.
If changing icon usage, update getFileIcon callsites to match its current signature (pass className) to avoid styling regressions.
When modifying copy/download/install flows, preserve existing loading/toast UX and canonicalization of exported content (avoid injecting UI decorations into copied/downloaded data).

Run the frontend component/unit tests and the integration flows that mock backend artifact URL and install endpoints after edits; verify behavior in both dynamic (server-backed) and static/demo build modes.

frontend/src/components/workspace/settin.description: This folder implements the Workspace Settings UI: a Settings dialog shell plus page-level React components for Appearance, Memory, Skills, Tools, Notifications and About content. It matters because it mediates user-facing configuration flows that call backend APIs, manipulate local browser capabilities (theme, locale, file I/O, notifications), and trigger navigation or global-app state changes (e.g., creating a skill chat or closing the settings dialog).
key_behaviors: Render a modal/settings dialog with side navigation and dynamic content panes for multiple settings categories.
Client-side interactive flows that change global app state: switching theme/locale, toggling skills via API, and navigating to a new skill chat while closing the dialog.
Memory management flows: display memory summaries and facts, search/filter, import/export JSON with validation, and CRUD operations that call memory APIs.
Tool/MCP server management surfaced in UI and persisted via backend/API hooks.
About content streamed to the client using a streaming Markdown renderer and static markdown content packaged with the component set.

insights.title: Streaming/DOM-dependent rendering
insights.problem: Some pages/components (e.g., about page using Streamdown) require browser-only APIs and must execute as client components.
insights.root_cause: Use of DOM or streaming renderer libraries that are not compatible with server-side rendering.
insights.solution: Ensure components that depend on the DOM are declared as client components ("use client") and remain inside the client component tree; keep server components from importing these client-only modules.

insights.title: Memory JSON import/export and validation
insights.problem: Memory settings implement import/export flows that accept external JSON and perform destructive operations (clear all, bulk create), which can lead to data loss if validation or confirmation is inadequate.
insights.root_cause: Direct file import/export and bulk mutation UI without strict validation or clear confirmation UX.
insights.solution: Import/export flows centralize validation and confirmation logic; any edits must preserve existing validation, confirm dialogs, and non-destructive defaults to avoid accidental data loss.

insights.title: Creating a skill triggers navigation and dialog close
insights.problem: The Create Skill action navigates to a new skill chat and must close the settings dialog; failing to close the dialog or correctly navigate can leave inconsistent UI state.
insights.root_cause: Settings dialog is a separate modal layer and create-skill action needs to affect parent navigation and modal state.
insights.solution: Component changes must preserve the navigation callback and modal-close interaction contract so the parent workspace correctly routes to the new chat and the dialog is dismissed.

insights.title: Button variant and icon styling expectation
insights.problem: Some buttons had a variant that conflicted with icon-only styling (e.g., variant='outline' prevented desired icon appearance).
insights.root_cause: Button variant API and icon styling expectations were misaligned in initial implementations.
insights.solution: Any edits that change button variants or styles must respect the existing visual contract for icon-only buttons and ensure consistent appearance across the settings pages.

key_files.why: Entry shell for the settings UI: provides modal, side navigation and mounts per-category content panes; changes here affect dialog layout, navigation, and lifecycle (open/close).
Exports the SettingsDialog; used by other parts of the app to open settings—maintains public API surface for this subtree.
Largest and most complex page: implements memory CRUD, import/export, search/filter, and confirmation dialogs that interact with backend memory APIs; high-risk area for data integrity.
Controls global theme and locale via next-themes and i18n hooks; changes affect app-wide appearance and locale behavior.
Lists and toggles skills via API mutations and initiates create-skill navigation flows that must close the settings dialog—impacts runtime behavior and navigation.

construct_relationships: SettingsDialog composes SettingsSection and mounts per-category page components (AppearanceSettingsPage, MemorySettingsPage, SkillSettingsPage, ToolSettingsPage, NotificationSettingsPage, AboutSettingsPage).
SettingsSection is a generic wrapper used by individual settings pages to present consistent layout, headings and action regions.
AboutSettingsPage consumes aboutMarkdown from about-content and renders it using Streamdown (a streaming markdown-to-React renderer), requiring client execution.
MemorySettingsPage calls core memory API hooks to list, create, update, delete facts and performs import/export formatting/validation (summariesToMarkdown, buildMemorySectionGroups).

SkillSettingsPage uses API mutation hooks to toggle skill enabled state and triggers navigation to create a new skill chat; it relies on parent dialog controls to close the settings modal after navigation.
AppearanceSettingsPage interacts with next-themes and i18n hooks to update global theme and locale providers affecting the entire app.
notable_relationships: These components depend on backend APIs (memory, skills, MCP/tool configuration) and must preserve the contract of the hooks/mutations they call; changing request/response shapes will require corresponding backend or hook updates.
Appearance settings depend on the next-themes provider and global locale/i18n provider; changes must respect the providers configured at the app root.
About page rendering depends on a streaming markdown renderer (Streamdown) and static markdown content; this ties to client-only rendering constraints and any shared UI library exports.
Import/export flows rely on browser file APIs (download/upload) and input elements—edits should account for browser capability differences and keep 'use client' where required.
Skill creation flow integrates with app routing/navigation and modal state; other parts of the app that open/close the settings dialog or consume navigation state must remain coherent.
recommendations: Preserve 'use client' declarations for components that require DOM, streaming rendering, file input, or notification APIs; do not move client-only modules into server components.
When changing shapes of API calls or hooks used by memory/skills/tools pages, update all callers and associated backend endpoints or hook signatures to avoid runtime errors.
Maintain the SettingsDialog navigation keys and modal open/close contract — verify that create-skill and other actions still dismiss the dialog and perform navigation as expected.
Ensure import/export validation and confirmation dialogs in MemorySettingsPage remain intact; test destructive flows (clear all, bulk import) manually and via automated UI tests.

Run integration UI checks after edits: theme switching, locale change, skill toggle and create flow (dialog closed + navigation), memory import/export roundtrip, and About rendering in a browser environment.

backend/packages/harness/deerflow/skills.description: This folder implements the skill management surface for DeerFlow: discovery, parsing, validation, safe installation, on-disk management (atomic writes/history) and an AI-driven security gate for user-provided .skill archives and SKILL.md content. It centralizes the domain rules and filesystem semantics that other runtime components (Gateway, installer, sandbox mounts) rely on to load and mount skills safely into agent sandboxes.
key_behaviors: Discover skills by scanning public and custom skill directories, parse SKILL.md frontmatter into Skill objects, and derive enabled/disabled state from extensions/config.

Safely install .skill ZIP archives: validate archive contents, protect against zip-slip, symlinks and zip-bombs, prevent overwrites of existing custom skills, and place files into the custom skills directory.
Validate SKILL.md frontmatter and skill names against project naming/length rules and allowed properties before persisting.
Parse SKILL.md YAML frontmatter (including multiline values) into structured Skill dataclasses that expose filesystem and container mount paths.

Manage safe on-disk writes and edit history (atomic writes + JSONL history append), and gate writes via an AI security scanner that returns allow/warn/block decisions and falls back conservatively on failures.

insights.title: Robust ZIP extraction is required
insights.problem: Archive extraction historically allowed zip-slip, symlinks and zip-bomb vectors and had inconsistent traversal checks between client and gateway implementations.
insights.root_cause: Multiple extraction codepaths evolved independently and lacked a single hardened extraction routine with unified error types and defenses.
insights.solution: Use the provided safe extraction utilities and SkillAlreadyExistsError semantics; preserve explicit checks for zip-slip, symlink handling, and archive size/entry count limits when modifying installer logic.

insights.title: Custom skill symlinks must be followed when scanning
insights.problem: os.walk() did not descend into symlinked skill installs, causing SKILL.md files under symlinked directories to be missed and skills to silently fail to load.
insights.root_cause: Default directory traversal did not explicitly follow symlinks for custom skills.
insights.solution: Loader scanning must follow symlinks for custom skills directories (or explicitly resolve symlink targets) and continue to respect config-provided alternate paths.

insights.title: SKILL.md frontmatter needs multiline YAML support and proper error logging
insights.problem: A simple line-split parser failed on folded (>) and literal (|) multiline YAML strings and printed parsing errors to stdout.
insights.root_cause: An ad-hoc parser was used without handling YAML multiline constructs and used print()/stdout for diagnostics.
insights.solution: Maintain multiline frontmatter parsing behavior and use logger.error for parse errors so callers (loaders, installers) can react programmatically to parsing failures.

insights.title: Atomic writes, validation and edit history are coupled
insights.problem: Skill writes must simultaneously validate frontmatter, enforce name/path rules, write files atomically, and append history in JSONL; breaking any part can leave inconsistent state or lost history.
insights.root_cause: Multiple responsibilities (validation, path safety, atomic file operations, history logging) are implemented together and consumers expect all to succeed as a unit.
insights.solution: Respect validate_skill_markdown_content, ensure_safe_support_path and atomic_write semantics when changing manager utilities; preserve the JSONL history format and append semantics.

insights.title: AI-driven security scanner must default to conservative blocking
insights.problem: Model calls can fail or return unparseable output; without a conservative fallback, unsafe content could be allowed.
insights.root_cause: Dependence on an external chat model and JSON extraction from free-text responses is inherently brittle.
insights.solution: Keep the scanner's allow/warn/block contract and the conservative block fallback on call failure or malformed output; any caller of scan_skill_content should handle ScanResult semantics explicitly.

insights.title: Consistent logging and unified error types are important for API consumers
insights.problem: Some modules used print-based warnings while others use logger; installer/loader semantics differed across client and gateway implementations.
insights.root_cause: Inconsistent diagnostic/output patterns across implementations.
insights.solution: Preserve or migrate to logger-based diagnostics and the documented exceptions (e.g., SkillAlreadyExistsError) so upstream callers (Gateway API, CLI) can rely on stable behaviors.

key_files.why: Primary implementation of safe .skill archive validation and installation; enforcement point for zip-slip, symlinks, zip-bomb defenses and overwrite prevention.
Skill discovery entrypoint used at runtime to enumerate and parse public/custom skills and compute enabled state; must correctly follow symlinks and respect config paths.
Provides validation, atomic write, safe path checks and edit history append/read operations consumed by higher-level APIs and by installers/edit endpoints.
Parses SKILL.md frontmatter into Skill objects used by discovery and validation; supports multiline YAML handling.
Gates skill content writes with model-driven allow/warn/block decisions and contains the fallback behavior on failures that callers must respect.

construct_relationships: parser.parse_skill_file -> types.Skill: parser returns Skill dataclass instances that include filesystem and container path helpers.
loader.load_skills -> parser.parse_skill_file + types.Skill: loader scans directories, uses parser to produce Skill objects and determines enabled state from config.

installer.install_skill_from_archive -> installer.safe_extract_skill_archive + manager.validate_skill_markdown_content: installer extracts archive safely, then validates skill frontmatter before committing to disk.
manager.atomic_write & append_history -> loader/consumers: manager writes and records edits atomically so loaders and the runtime see consistent skill files and a JSONL edit history.

security_scanner.scan_skill_content -> manager/installer: security_scanner is invoked before persisting skill content; callers must interpret ScanResult (allow/warn/block) and obey conservative fallback behavior.
types.Skill.get_container_path -> runtime sandbox mounting: Skill exposes container path helpers that must align with sandbox providers' mount points when skills are mounted into agent sandboxes.
notable_relationships: Sandbox providers and agent runtime (outside this folder) mount skills into running containers at configured paths (project-wide convention /mnt/skills); changes to Skill.get_container_path must remain consistent with backend/src/sandbox providers and Docker compose mounts.
Gateway API and CLI components call into installer/manager/loader for skill installation, editing and listing; these callers rely on consistent exceptions, logger messages and return structures.
Skill discovery and enabling uses extensions/config objects external to this folder; loader warnings when extension config load fails should be logged (not printed) so monitoring/CI can pick them up.

Security scanner depends on configured chat/model providers (external model keys/config); its conservative fallback behavior affects API flows that allow agent-driven skill edits—breaking or changing that contract will impact Gateway endpoints and any automated skill write paths.
recommendations: When editing installer.py preserve zip-slip, symlink, and zip-bomb defenses; keep SkillAlreadyExistsError semantics and ensure unit tests cover malicious archive patterns and overwrite prevention.

When editing loader.py ensure directory traversal follows symlinks for custom skills and continue to support alternate skills root paths from config; add tests for symlinked skills and for config-load failure logging (use logger assertions).

When editing parser.py maintain multiline YAML frontmatter parsing behavior and switch any print() diagnostics to logger.error; add unit tests exercising folded (>) and literal (|) multiline fields and malformed frontmatter.

When editing manager.py preserve validate_skill_markdown_content, ensure_safe_support_path and atomic_write semantics and keep the JSONL history format; tests should assert atomicity, path safety, and history append/read correctness.

When editing security_scanner.py preserve the ScanResult allow/warn/block contract and the conservative-block fallback on model failures or parse errors; tests should cover model success, malformed model output, and model call failures.

Any change that affects Skill.get_container_path or container-file-path helpers must be validated against sandbox mounting behavior and Docker/compose mount points (ensure runtime mounts align); run integration tests that exercise agent sandbox mounts.
Avoid introducing stdout prints for errors or warnings; use the module logger so Gateway/CLI consumers can programmatically detect and aggregate failures.

Run and extend unit tests that cover: safe archive extraction (zip-slip/symlink/zip-bomb), symlinked-skill discovery, multiline frontmatter parsing, validation rules (naming/allowed properties/length limits), atomic writes and history format, and security scanner fallback behaviors.

backend/packages/harness/deerflow/mcp.description: This package owns integration with Multi-Client Provider (MCP) servers: discovering and loading remote LangChain tools, constructing transport-specific server configs, and managing OAuth tokens and cached tool instances. It matters because it exposes remote tools into DeerFlow runtime (including synchronous call sites), must operate correctly across sync/async contexts and processes, and secures/maintains long-lived connections to MCP servers (HTTP/SSE).
key_behaviors: Read MCP extensions configuration and build transport-specific server parameter mappings for client construction.
Lazy, safe initialization and module-level caching of MCP tool instances with staleness detection based on extensions config mtime and explicit reset semantics for tests.
Obtain, cache, and refresh OAuth tokens (with expiration skew) and populate Authorization headers for both SSE and HTTP MCP transports.
Load tools from MCP servers, inject initial OAuth headers/interceptors, and wrap coroutine-only tools with synchronous wrappers to support sync callers.
Coordinate background resources (global thread pool executor) and lifecycle (clean shutdown) to avoid nested event loop issues and resource leaks.

insights.title: Module-level lazy cache with staleness detection
insights.problem: MCP tools need to be cached for reuse but must reflect updates to extensions config or external changes; naive caching can serve stale tools across processes or tests.
insights.root_cause: Cache is module-scoped and long-lived; extensions config changes are external and may occur without process restart.
insights.solution: Maintain a cache keyed by config mtime, detect config file modification times to invalidate the cache, and expose a reset function for tests and orchestrated cross-process updates.

insights.title: Safe initialization across sync and async contexts
insights.problem: Initializing MCP tools may run inside an existing event loop or be called from synchronous code paths, causing runtime errors if not handled correctly.
insights.root_cause: Tool loading uses async adapters but DeerFlow must support sync entry points (e.g., DeerFlowClient) and evloop nesting is not allowed.
insights.solution: Provide helpers that detect running event loops and route initialization accordingly; use a dedicated thread-pool executor to run coroutine-only operations when invoked synchronously.

insights.title: Token lifecycle and header injection
insights.problem: MCP server requests (HTTP/SSE) require valid Authorization headers that must be refreshed before expiry to avoid connection failures.
insights.root_cause: Tokens expire and multiple concurrent requests may race to refresh them.
insights.solution: Centralize token acquisition and caching in an OAuthTokenManager with locking and expiration skew handling; expose helpers to build initial headers and interceptors that inject Authorization for outgoing MCP requests.

insights.title: Async-only MCP tools used by synchronous callers
insights.problem: Some MCP-loaded tools expose only async coroutines; sync callers (e.g., embedded DeerFlowClient or other sync runtime code) can trigger NotImplementedError or event loop errors.
insights.root_cause: LangChain/adapter tools are coroutine-native and DeerFlow must support sync invocation sites.
insights.solution: Wrap async-only tools with synchronous wrappers that run them in a thread executor, and ensure that the executor lifecycle is managed and shut down at process exit.

insights.title: Transport parameter validation
insights.problem: Different MCP transports require different fields; misconfiguration can produce runtime failures when building server params.
insights.root_cause: Server parameters are transport-specific and may be incomplete or malformed in extensions config.
insights.solution: Perform explicit validation per transport type and log or raise clear errors during servers config assembly so failures are detected early.

key_files.why: Core cache and initialization semantics for MCP tools (lazy load, mtime-based invalidation, sync/async-safe init) that other modules rely on.
Discovers and loads remote MCP tools, applies OAuth header injection and produces synchronous wrappers for async-only tools; directly impacts runtime callability of MCP tools.
Centralized OAuth token manager and header/interceptor builders used to authenticate SSE/HTTP MCP transports.
Builds transport-specific server parameters and aggregate server config from ExtensionsConfig; used by initialization and tool-loading flows.
Public API surface that re-exports initialization, cache accessors and config builders relied on by other DeerFlow subsystems (tool injection, tests).

construct_relationships: build_servers_config (client.py) reads ExtensionsConfig and produces a servers mapping that initialize_mcp_tools (cache.py) consumes to load tools.
initialize_mcp_tools / get_cached_mcp_tools (cache.py) call get_mcp_tools (tools.py) to actually fetch and wrap remote tools before placing instances into the module cache.
OAuthTokenManager (oauth.py) provides get_initial_oauth_headers and an interceptor builder which tools.py injects into MCP tool requests to populate Authorization headers for HTTP/SSE transports.

_make_sync_tool_wrapper and _SYNC_TOOL_EXECUTOR (tools.py) are used to wrap coroutine-only tools so synchronous callers (like DeerFlowClient) can invoke them without causing event loop nesting errors.
reset_mcp_tools_cache (cache.py) is a test-facing hook that clears the module-level cache and must coordinate with the lifecycle of the thread executor used by tools.py.
notable_relationships: This package depends on external langchain-mcp-adapters and transport libraries; misalignment in adapter APIs can surface as async/sync incompatibilities here.

Changes here affect LangGraph/lead_agent tool injection and any runtime that expects MCP tools to be callable synchronously (embedded DeerFlowClient) — downstream systems will see immediate runtime/auth failures if headers, wrapping, or cached instances break.

Server config assembly relies on an ExtensionsConfig file—mtime checks and staleness detection assume a filesystem-accessible config; cross-process updates (e.g., in containerized deployments) are handled by mtime-based invalidation but editors must respect that behavior.
OAuth flows are used for long-lived SSE connections as well as short HTTP requests; token refresh behavior and lock contention can impact connection stability for streaming endpoints.
recommendations: When editing, preserve safe sync/async initialization paths: detect running event loops and use the provided thread executor wrappers rather than calling async coroutines directly from sync contexts.
Do not remove or weaken cache invalidation (mtime-based) or the reset_mcp_tools_cache hook — tests and cross-process config reloads rely on them.

Any changes to OAuthTokenManager must maintain locking, expiration skew handling, and the ability to produce initial headers for SSE connections; ensure token refresh is race-free under concurrent callers.
If altering the sync wrapper or global executor, ensure executor lifecycle and process-exit shutdown remain intact to avoid leaked threads or inability to call wrapped tools synchronously.

Run unit and integration tests that cover: tool loading under both sync and async callers, cache invalidation after config changes, OAuth token acquisition/refresh under concurrent calls, and server-params validation for all supported transports.

backend/packages/harness/deerflow/subage.description: This subtree defines the subagent subsystem used to spawn, configure, and run autonomous worker agents. It centralizes SubagentConfig definitions (built-in types), a registry for exposing and filtering available subagents, and an executor that schedules and runs subagent tasks (sync or background) with streaming, timeouts, and cancellation semantics. These components control capability exposure (tools, sandbox access), runtime limits, and concurrency — making them critical for security, observability, and user-facing task delegation.
key_behaviors: Declare and expose canonical SubagentConfig objects that encode system prompts, allowed/disallowed tools, and runtime limits for each subagent type.
Register and surface built-in subagent names and configurations, applying runtime overrides and filtering availability based on environment (e.g., host bash availability).
Filter the runtime toolset for a spawned subagent according to its SubagentConfig before execution.
Schedule and run subagents either synchronously or as background tasks, stream partial AI outputs, collect final results, and manage a task registry with status tracking and concurrency limits.
Enforce timeouts and cooperative cancellation for running subagents, protecting status updates across threads.

insights.title: Cooperative cancellation and thread lifecycle
insights.problem: Canceling the parent asyncio.Task did not stop work executing in subagent threads; threads continued LLM calls and file writes after the user requested cancellation.
insights.root_cause: Subagents run in ThreadPoolExecutor threads with their own event loops and lacked a reliable, shared cooperative cancellation mechanism; parent asyncio task cancellation does not automatically terminate spawned threads.
insights.solution: When editing executor logic, preserve or improve cooperative cancellation hooks that threads check (e.g., cancellation flags/events) and ensure status transitions (including CANCELLED) are applied atomically and respected by running thread logic. Assume threads may continue briefly and protect shared state with locks.

insights.title: Racing status updates across threads
insights.problem: Timeout handlers and cancellation flows could overwrite each other's status updates, leading to incorrect final states.
insights.root_cause: Multiple threads and timers update SubagentStatus without adequate synchronization.
insights.solution: Ensure status updates are protected by synchronization (locks) and that status transitions respect a priority order (e.g., CANCELLED should be durable). Any change to status handling must maintain locking and ordering guarantees to avoid inconsistent task state.

insights.title: Runtime exposure of bash subagent when host bash is disabled
insights.problem: The registry exposed the bash subagent as available even when host bash execution was disabled, misleading consumers and enabling selection of an unusable capability.
insights.root_cause: Listing logic did not account for sandbox/runtime constraints when enumerating available subagents.
insights.solution: When altering registry or built-in configs, ensure availability checks consult runtime sandbox capabilities (host bash flag) and that listing functions only advertise subagents that can actually run in the current environment. Treat availability filtering as a first-class, runtime-dependent behavior.

insights.title: Centralized defaults and overrides
insights.problem: Subagent runtime behavior depends on central defaults (e.g., 15-minute timeout, disallowed tools) which can be overridden by config.yaml at runtime.
insights.root_cause: Defaults are encoded in SubagentConfig dataclass and registry applies overrides; inconsistent handling could lead to mismatch between advertised and enforced limits.
insights.solution: Preserve the dataclass defaults and the registry override pipeline semantics. Any code changes that touch configuration application should keep override precedence clear and log applied overrides as the registry currently does.

insights.title: Public package API re-exporting
insights.problem: Consumers import subagent constructs via the package public API; mismatches between exports and actual modules can break callers.
insights.root_cause: __init__ provides re-exports of key types and helpers, so changes to module locations/names must be mirrored in package exports.
insights.solution: When renaming/moving constructs, update package exports to preserve the stable public API surface; run dependent imports/tests to validate compatibility.

key_files.why: Defines the SubagentConfig dataclass and default runtime/tool restrictions that drive how subagents are constrained and validated at spawn time.
Implements the SubagentExecutor, background scheduling, streaming collection, cancellation, timeouts, and the task registry — central to runtime behavior and concurrency semantics.
Provides lookup and availability filtering for subagent configurations and applies runtime overrides; controls which subagents are advertised and instantiated.
Registers and exposes the built-in SubagentConfig objects to the registry and the public package API.
Houses the bash-specialist SubagentConfig whose availability is sensitive to sandbox host-bash settings and thus is a focal point for capability filtering and security.

construct_relationships: SubagentConfig (config.py) is the authoritative configuration object consumed by the registry and by SubagentExecutor when spawning a subagent.
The builtins package constructs named SubagentConfig instances (general-purpose, bash) and registers them into the in-process registry exported by builtins.__init__.
registry.get_subagent_config fetches a SubagentConfig (possibly with config.yaml overrides) and is used by callers (e.g., task-spawning code) to validate and filter tools before execution.
SubagentExecutor calls a local _filter_tools helper using the SubagentConfig to limit the toolset exposed to the running subagent.
Executor maintains a background task registry and SubagentStatus/SubagentResult lifecycle; status updates must be synchronized to avoid races between timeout handlers and cancellation paths.
__init__.py re-exports key types and helpers so external consumers import subagent constructs from deerflow.subagents rather than internal module paths.
notable_relationships: Sandbox providers and middleware upstream (e.g., SandboxMiddleware, LocalSandboxProvider/AioSandboxProvider) determine runtime capabilities (host bash allowed) that registry functions must consult when listing available subagents.

Lead agent / tooling layers (task tool invoked by agents created in lead_agent) depend on the executor and registry to spawn and report on subagents; changes here affect agent delegation and SSE/status streaming behaviors.

Subagents invoke sandbox tools and may perform file writes and LLM calls; executor cancellation and timeout behavior directly influences whether in-flight external effects (LLM requests, file IO) continue after user cancellation.
Configuration overrides (config.yaml) applied by registry affect runtime timeouts and turn limits; CI and runtime tests expect default values (e.g., 15-minute timeout) unless explicitly overridden.
recommendations: Preserve and respect SubagentConfig defaults and the registry override/application order when modifying configuration or registry code; log any applied overrides consistently.

Any edits to executor must maintain thread-safety: protect status updates with locks, preserve CANCELLED semantics, and keep cooperative cancellation hooks available to threads (assume threads may continue briefly).
When changing how availability is listed, ensure registry consults runtime sandbox capability flags (host bash) so advertised subagents are actually runnable in the current environment.

Validate tool-filtering logic against SubagentConfig allowed/disallowed lists; ensure changes do not widen the toolset exposed to subagents without accounting for sandbox/tool implementation boundaries.

Run the repository backend test suite (make test or uv run pytest) and sandbox-related tests; when modifying sandbox-sensitive behavior, exercise tests in the Docker dev environment to reflect realistic sandbox constraints and avoid false positives on a host machine.
Keep public API exports (package __init__.py) stable or update them in tandem with any refactors to avoid breaking imports used by other modules.

frontend/src/components/landing/sections.description: This folder contains React presentation components that compose the landing page sections for the frontend (case studies, community CTA, what's new, sandbox, and skills). These components are thin UI compositions that rely on shared design primitives (Section, Button, MagicBento, AuroraText, image assets) and encode navigation/interaction patterns (external GitHub CTA, links to example thread pages, feature cards). They matter because they define the public-facing marketing surface and must remain consistent, accessible, and compatible with Next.js build/runtime constraints.
key_behaviors: Compose shared Section/wrapper UI to build distinct landing page areas (hero-like content, card grids, CTA).
Render lists/grids of feature or case-study cards using static data defined locally in the component.
Create navigation interactions: internal example thread links (with mock query params) that open in a new tab; external links to GitHub for community contributions.
Reference background/static image assets for visual cards and rely on shared UI components (MagicBento, Button, AuroraText) for styling/behavior.
Client-side rendering assumptions for interactive sections (components expect to run in the browser and use client-only primitives).

insights.title: Case study links open example threads via mock query params
insights.problem: Components generate links to mock thread pages that open in a new tab; the exact query format and destination routing must match the gateway/next routes expected by the rest of the app.
insights.root_cause: Links are created locally in the component using a predefined URL pattern without centralized route constants.
insights.solution: When editing these components ensure link URLs follow the app's routing conventions and preserve target behavior (open new tab) and any query parameter format used by consumers; verify behavior in Next.js dev server and that link targets include appropriate rel attributes for security.

insights.title: Community CTA points to GitHub
insights.problem: External anchor link leads out of the app; missing link security attributes or analytics hooks could cause security or tracking inconsistencies.
insights.root_cause: Simple anchor/Button composition may omit rel="noopener noreferrer" and consistent telemetry tracking.
insights.solution: Keep external links identifiable, include appropriate rel attributes, and maintain any existing analytics calls; check that styling and behavior match shared Button/AuroraText semantics.

insights.title: Components depend on shared Section and MagicBento primitives
insights.problem: These landing sections assume stable APIs and styling contracts from shared components (Section, MagicBento, Button). Changes to those primitives can break layout or behavior here.
insights.root_cause: Local composition tightly couples to the shape and props of upstream UI components instead of encapsulating transformations.
insights.solution: Respect existing prop shapes and layout expectations of shared components; when modifying props or composition, review the referenced shared components and the larger landing page layout to avoid regressions.

insights.title: Card visuals reference static background images
insights.problem: Image asset paths and lazy-loading semantics are important for build correctness and performance; incorrect paths or missing alt text can break builds or impair accessibility.
insights.root_cause: Images are referenced by path in component-local data; Next.js/public asset conventions and image optimization must be considered.
insights.solution: When modifying image usage keep paths consistent with the app's asset/static folder conventions, preserve alt text, and verify Next.js build (pnpm build) to ensure assets resolve. Consider lazy-loading semantics already implied by the UI primitives.

insights.title: Components declared as client-side and use browser-only features
insights.problem: Client-only components may break SSR expectations or cause hydration mismatches if server-rendered wrappers expect different markup.
insights.root_cause: Interactive/visual sections use client-side only primitives or browser APIs and are rendered exclusively on the client.
insights.solution: Preserve client-side declaration where present (use client) and ensure parent pages/wrappers that render these sections are compatible with client-only children; validate in dev server and during build to catch hydration warnings.

insights.title: Card grids and CTA must remain accessible and responsive
insights.problem: Visual-only changes can inadvertently remove keyboard focusability, meaningful alt text, or responsive breakpoints.
insights.root_cause: The components are focused on visual composition and rely on CSS/grid utilities provided by shared primitives.
insights.solution: Maintain accessible attributes (aria-labels, alt text, logical heading order) and existing responsive breakpoints; test at multiple viewport sizes and via keyboard navigation after edits.

key_files.why: Renders example agent runs (case-study cards) and constructs links to mock thread pages — changing this affects example navigation and the visual showcase on the landing page.
Defines the feature cards and data for the 'What's New' area using MagicBento; edits here change the product messaging and rely on shared card/grid primitives.
Contains the community CTA linking to GitHub and composes shared Button/AuroraText primitives — impacts external linking and CTA behavior.
Describes the runtime/sandbox environment messaging; changes affect technical messaging and any dependencies on icons/assets or layout used by the landing page.
Showcases skills animation and interactive visuals; edits can affect client-side behavior and animation primitives.

construct_relationships: Each section component composes a shared Section wrapper component to provide consistent spacing and headings across the landing page.
CaseStudySection creates card items that reference static image assets and generate links to thread pages; those links interact with the app's routing layer.
WhatsNewSection supplies a local features array to MagicBento (a shared card/grid UI) which handles layout and presentation.
CommunitySection composes Button and AuroraText shared UI components to render the CTA and external link to GitHub.
SkillsSection and SandboxSection rely on the same styling primitives and likely re-use CSS grid/bento utilities used by WhatsNewSection for consistent visuals.
notable_relationships: These components depend on shared UI primitives located elsewhere in the codebase (Section, Button, MagicBento, AuroraText); changes here may require coordinated changes to those primitives or their props.
Links to mock thread pages imply a dependency on Next.js route definitions and any runtime that consumes the query param format; edits should preserve the contract used by the rest of the app.
Static image references must match the project's public/static asset organization (Next.js public folder); broken paths will surface during pnpm build.
External links (GitHub) are outbound dependencies; ensure link security attributes and any analytics/telemetry instrumentation used by the app remain intact.
recommendations: Preserve existing client-side declaration (use client) and verify no SSR/hydration warnings occur after edits.
When modifying links, keep target behavior (open in new tab) and include rel="noopener noreferrer" for external targets; ensure internal route query formats remain compatible.
Keep prop shapes and layout assumptions of shared components (Section, MagicBento, Button, AuroraText); if changing composition, check the shared components for required prop changes.
Verify static asset paths and alt text; run the Next.js build (pnpm build) locally to catch missing asset errors.
Run frontend lints, typechecks and the build pipeline (pnpm lint, pnpm typecheck, pnpm build) and visually test responsive layouts and keyboard navigation for accessibility after edits.
Check that any analytics or telemetry hooks tied to CTA clicks remain functional if link/button markup is modified.

frontend/src/content/en/application.description: This folder contains the English-language documentation pages for the "Application" section of the frontend site and the navigation metadata that exposes them to the site router. It is purely content (MDX) plus a small meta descriptor used by the Next.js content/navigation system; changes here affect site routing, navigation, localization, and static site builds.
key_behaviors: Provides static MDX content consumed by the Next.js content layer and rendered as documentation pages at predictable routes.
Exports navigation metadata via _meta.ts which the documentation/site navigation system reads to build sidebars and route entries.
Interacts with localization/internationalization (this folder is the English locale) so content is part of a multi-locale site structure.
Links and cross-references inside MDX pages create dependencies with other docs and frontend components (internal anchors, images, examples).
Participates in the static build pipeline: any syntax/metadata errors or broken internal links can fail or degrade the production build.

insights.title: _meta.ts drives navigation entries
insights.problem: If _meta.ts structure or exported names change, the site navigation or sidebar may break or pages may be omitted.
insights.root_cause: Navigation system expects a specific metadata shape and export name(s) for collecting site structure.
insights.solution: Maintain the export shape and keys used by the content/navigation system when editing _meta.ts; any change requires updating consumers and verifying the build and sidebar render correctly.

insights.title: MDX pages are compiled at build-time
insights.problem: Syntax or frontmatter errors in MDX can cause build-time failures or runtime rendering issues.
insights.root_cause: MDX is parsed and statically compiled into React during the Next.js build; malformed MDX or missing required frontmatter break compilation.
insights.solution: Ensure MDX files are syntactically valid, include expected frontmatter (if any), and run the frontend typecheck/build to catch compile-time errors before committing.

insights.title: Folder is locale-scoped (English)
insights.problem: Adding or renaming pages here without coordinating with other locale folders can lead to inconsistent content across locales or broken locale routing.
insights.root_cause: The documentation structure is mirrored per locale; changes must be reflected across locale-specific directories when required.
insights.solution: Respect locale boundaries: keep English content here and, when changing slugs or adding pages that should be localized, ensure corresponding updates or placeholders exist in other locale directories and verify locale routing.

insights.title: Internal links create cross-file coupling
insights.problem: Relative links, internal anchors, or imports referenced by MDX pages can break if target files or slugs are renamed or moved.
insights.root_cause: MDX content often contains hard-coded paths and anchors that assume stable file paths and metadata.
insights.solution: When changing file names, exports, or slugs, update all internal references and validate via site build/link checkers to avoid broken navigation.

insights.title: Content changes affect static site builds
insights.problem: Content edits that violate tooling expectations (formatting, types, missing exports) will fail CI or cause the site to render incorrectly.
insights.root_cause: CI and production builds statically compile and validate frontend code and content; they enforce formatting/type rules and successful builds.
insights.solution: Run local lint/format/typecheck and a production build (pnpm build) locally or in a dev container after edits to ensure compatibility with CI checks.

key_files.why: Exports the navigation metadata used to register these MDX pages in the site sidebar and route structure; editing it changes how pages appear in navigation.
Top-level overview page for the Application section; commonly the landing doc that links to the other pages and can affect discoverability and parent routing.
Quick Start guide is often surfaced prominently; changes here impact first-time user experience and should preserve expected anchors/links referenced elsewhere.
Deployment instructions are operationally sensitive; broken or outdated content here can mislead users and cause setup problems.
Workspace usage docs are likely to include commands, paths, or examples that must remain consistent with backend behavior and project conventions.

construct_relationships: _meta.ts → MDX pages: _meta.ts provides the navigation metadata (labels, order, slugs) consumed by the documentation/navigation system to expose these MDX pages in the site UI.
MDX pages → Next.js content layer: each .mdx file is processed by the frontend build pipeline to produce React pages; frontmatter and filenames influence route generation.
MDX pages ↔ other docs: MDX files contain internal links/anchors that reference routes or headings in other documentation files across the site and must remain consistent with those targets.
This locale folder ↔ other locale folders: the English content here corresponds to mirrored structures in other locale directories used by the i18n/routing subsystem.
notable_relationships: Frontend build/CI: changes here feed into pnpm build/typecheck/lint steps; CI will compile MDX into the site and fail on syntax or export mismatches.
Site navigation system: global navigation collector aggregates per-section _meta.ts files — changing metadata affects cross-folder nav and sidebar composition.
