Localization/internationalization: this folder is part of a locale-specific tree; cross-locale consistency matters for translated builds and routing.
Search/indexing: content here is indexed by any site search or static site indexer; renaming or removing pages impacts search results and referenced anchors.
recommendations: Do not change the exported shape or expected keys in _meta.ts without confirming the navigation collector expectations; verify the site sidebar after edits.
Ensure every modified or new MDX file is syntactically valid MDX and, if applicable, contains required frontmatter fields; run the frontend build (pnpm build) and typecheck locally.
When renaming paths or slugs, update all internal links and anchors in MDX files and verify with a link checker or by building the site to catch broken references.
Remember this folder is English-only; if content changes require localization, coordinate equivalent updates in other locale folders to keep routing consistent.
Run formatting/linting (pnpm format, pnpm lint) and the CI build flow locally where possible to catch issues that would fail automated checks or the production build.

backend/packages/harness/deerflow/tools.description: This folder implements the runtime tools the DeerFlow lead agent exposes to LangGraph/agents: built-in LangChain StructuredTools (delegation to subagents, artifact presentation, image viewing, clarification, skill management, ACP invocation), a deferred in-request tool discovery layer, and the tool-loading logic that decides which tools are exposed based on config and sandbox policy. These tools act as the boundary between agent code, the sandbox/subagent subsystem, the request-scoped runtime state (thread/workspace/uploads/outputs), and skill files on disk, so they must align with middleware expectations, sandbox policy, and runtime metadata availability.
key_behaviors: Deferred discovery and promotion of tools per-request so LLMs can discover and then invoke tool schemas (tool_search + DeferredToolRegistry).
Delegation of long-running work to background subagents with asynchronous start/polling, lifecycle event streaming, and cooperative cancellation (task_tool).
Validation and normalization of filesystem artifacts and thread-scoped virtual paths; publishing artifacts and viewed images into run state (present_file_tool, view_image_tool).
Provisioning and mutation of on-disk custom skills with concurrency control, security scanning, history logging, and prompt cache refresh triggers (skill_manage_tool).
Conditional exposure/selection of tools at runtime driven by configuration, model capabilities and sandbox policy (tools.get_available_tools).

insights.title: Background subagent polling must be cooperatively cancellable and cleaned up
insights.problem: When the task_tool polling coroutine is cancelled (asyncio.CancelledError), the background subagent thread continued to run because threads cannot be forcefully cancelled from the coroutine that started them.
insights.root_cause: Background execution is launched asynchronously (execute_async) and polling/cancellation logic only cancelled the polling coroutine without signalling/ensuring subagent cleanup in all paths.
insights.solution: Request cooperative cancellation from the background executor and schedule deferred/guaranteed cleanup operations; ensure cancellation paths send a shutdown signal or schedule executor cleanup so background tasks do not orphan.

insights.title: Code assumes runtime.context and thread_id are always present
insights.problem: Multiple tools (present_file_tool, setup_agent_tool, task_tool) failed in some runtimes/tests when runtime.context was None or lacked thread_id.
insights.root_cause: Tool implementations accessed runtime.context/get('thread_id') without null-safety; LangGraph or some runtime environments provide thread_id via config.configurable instead of runtime.context.
insights.solution: Treat runtime.context and thread-scoped identifiers as optional: null-check before use, fallback to config.configurable when appropriate, and emit clear error/tool messages if required metadata is missing.

insights.title: Host-bash subagent/tools can be exposed even when unsafe
insights.problem: Host bash tools or bash subagents were exposed in local-mode even when LocalSandboxProvider disabled host bash, creating a security risk.
insights.root_cause: Tool selection logic did not consistently consult the active sandbox provider or config flags before including host-bash-related tools.
insights.solution: Tool-loading and subagent-creation code must consult sandbox policy/config at decision time and return clear rejection messages when a requested tool/subagent is disallowed.

insights.title: Promoted deferred tools remained filtered out by later middleware
insights.problem: After tool_search returned a tool schema (promoting a deferred tool), middleware later stripped the promoted tool from runtime bind_tools, making the tool non-invocable by the agent despite having its schema.
insights.root_cause: Deferred tool promotion and middleware binding/stripping operate on different registries or ordering assumptions; promotion was not persisted into the bind_tools list accessible to middleware.
insights.solution: Ensure deferred tool promotion updates the same request-scoped registry that middleware reads for bindable tools (or perform promotion before bind_tools filtering) so schema discovery and invocation remain consistent.

insights.title: Top-level imports can create circular import errors
insights.problem: view_image_tool caused circular import failures during test collection due to module-level imports of sandbox.tools.
insights.root_cause: Eager/top-level import of modules that themselves import tool modules produced cycles when test collection or other imports occurred.
insights.solution: Delay imports that cause cycles into function/local scope (lazy import) to avoid circular import during module import time; preserve this pattern when adding new cross-referencing modules.

insights.title: ACP agent environment substitutions were not resolved when spawning subprocesses
insights.problem: Invoke ACP agent tool did not pass configured environment substitutions ($VAR references) to the spawned agent process resulting in misconfigured agent runs.
insights.root_cause: Per-agent env map from configuration was not resolved for $VAR placeholders before being handed to spawn_agent_process.
insights.solution: Resolve environment variable references from config into concrete values prior to spawning external agent processes and pass the resolved environment map into the subprocess invocation.

key_files.why: Primary implementation of the 'task' tool that launches and polls background subagents, streams lifecycle events, and must respect sandbox policy and cooperative cancellation.
Central tool discovery/loader that decides which tools are exposed at runtime based on config, sandbox provider, and model capabilities; controls exposure of host-bash and conditional tool inclusion.

Implements creation, mutation and deletion of custom skills on disk with security scanning, locking, and prompt-cache refresh impacts — touches persistent skill storage and prompt indexing subsystems.
Implements deferred tool registry and search/promotion flow used to surface tool schemas to LLMs; interacts with request-scoped registries and middleware binding logic.
Validates and publishes run artifacts (outputs) using virtual thread paths; must align with ThreadDataMiddleware path mapping and artifact ingestion semantics.

construct_relationships: tools.get_available_tools includes builtin tools exported from tools.builtins (clarification, present_file, task, view_image, setup_agent) and filters them by sandbox policy and config flags.

task_tool constructs a SubagentExecutor (background runner) using the current run's runtime metadata and available tools, starts execution via execute_async, polls the task status and emits structured stream events via the run's stream writer.

present_file_tool and view_image_tool resolve thread-scoped virtual paths (outputs directory) created by ThreadDataMiddleware and update the run state with artifacts/viewed_images so middleware and UI can surface them.

tool_search manages a ContextVar-scoped DeferredToolRegistry per request; when a tool is matched it promotes the DeferredToolEntry so the tool schema is available to the LLM and should be reflected in the runtime bind_tools used by middleware.
skill_manage_tool writes/patches/deletes files under skills/custom, uses per-skill locking and triggers prompt-cache refreshes (upstream prompt/indexing components) after mutations.
notable_relationships: Middleware: these tools assume middleware (ThreadDataMiddleware, UploadsMiddleware, SandboxMiddleware, etc.) has provisioned runtime context, virtual paths and stream writers. Changes must account for middleware ordering and guaranteed state shape.
Sandbox providers and subagent subsystem: task_tool and host-bash tool exposure depend on LocalSandboxProvider vs AioSandboxProvider and global config flags (host bash allowed/disallowed).

Subagent executor/service: task_tool interacts with the SubagentExecutor and background task lifecycle (start, poll, cancel) and must interoperate with the executor's concurrency and timeout policies.
Skills & prompt cache: skill_manage_tool mutates on-disk skill artifacts under skills/custom and triggers prompt cache/refresh mechanisms which are consumed by agent tooling and indexing subsystems.

External integrations: invoke_acp_agent_tool depends on external binaries and spawn semantics (env resolution, permission prompts); MCP/ACP plugin exposure depends on get_available_tools and runtime config.
recommendations: Treat runtime.context, thread_id and other request-scoped metadata as optional: null-check, provide clear error messages, and fallback to config.configurable where documented.
When changing tool exposure or selection, consult/get and respect the active sandbox provider/config flags so host-bash tools or unsafe subagents are not exposed in disallowed modes.
Avoid top-level imports that reference sandbox/tools or other modules that could create circular imports; prefer function-local lazy imports for cross-module references used at runtime.
Preserve cooperative cancellation and ensure task_tool schedules/requests executor cleanup on asyncio.CancelledError paths so background subagents are not orphaned.
Ensure deferred tool promotion updates the same request-scoped registry that middleware reads (or occurs before middleware filtering) so discovered schemas remain invocable.
When editing skill management code, maintain per-skill locking, security scanning hooks, history append behavior and trigger prompt-cache refresh paths; consider filesystem error cleanup semantics.

Run the backend unit and integration tests (backend make test / uv run pytest) and test with both LocalSandboxProvider and AioSandboxProvider configurations, as well as the LangGraph-related middleware flows, after making changes.

frontend/src/components/workspace/chats.description: UI components and hooks for the workspace chat area: rendering the main chat panel, an optional artifacts sidebar with detail view, and URL-driven chat/thread mode selection. This subtree coordinates UI state (selection, thread context) and a resizable panel layout whose persistence is route-aware, so it is central to how chat sessions and artifact inspection behave across navigation.
key_behaviors: Render main chat content alongside an optional, resizable artifacts sidebar with artifact list and detail views.
Manage artifact selection state and keep that selection consistent or reset when the active thread changes.
Derive chat mode and active thread from URL/route parameters and update UI behavior accordingly.
Persist and restore resizable panel layout per-route using stable identifiers to avoid cross-route layout corruption.
Expose components and hooks through an index for consumption by other workspace UI code.

insights.title: Stable IDs required for per-route resizable panel state
insights.problem: Resizable panel state (size/position) was inconsistenly restored when navigating between routes because panel instances had non-unique or non-stable identifiers.
insights.root_cause: Resizable panels used duplicate or transient IDs that did not map deterministically to a route or chat context.
insights.solution: Use stable, route-aware IDs for resizable panels (persisted per-route) so layout restore and user expectations for panel sizing remain consistent across navigation. Any edits must preserve or migrate these IDs.

insights.title: Artifact selection must be thread-aware
insights.problem: Artifact selection could leak across different chat threads or be lost unexpectedly when switching threads.
insights.root_cause: Selection state was not scoped to the active thread context; thread switches did not consistently update or reset artifact selection.
insights.solution: Keep artifact selection keyed by thread (or clear it explicitly on thread change). When modifying selection logic, ensure thread lifecycle transitions update selection state deterministically.

insights.title: Hooks depend on URL params for mode and thread
insights.problem: Chat mode and active thread are derived from route/query params; changing param names or route shape will break the hooks and the UI behavior.
insights.root_cause: use-chat-mode and use-thread-chat interpret URL parameters directly as authoritative source of mode/thread state.
insights.solution: Respect the current URL contract: when editing hooks or consumers, keep parameter names and semantics consistent or update all consumers and routing mappings together. Tests should cover common route transitions.

insights.title: Index aggregator is the single import surface
insights.problem: Multiple consumers import chat components/hooks; inconsistent export changes can break many import sites.
insights.root_cause: index.ts centralizes exports; changing names or signatures without updating imports elsewhere introduces breakage.
insights.solution: Maintain stable exported identifiers and signatures in index.ts or coordinate bulk updates across consumers when changing exports.

insights.title: UI rendering influenced by environment/feature flags
insights.problem: Artifacts sidebar rendering and resizable behavior are conditional on environment flags; modifying logic without checking flags can produce unexpected UI states in different deployments.
insights.root_cause: ChatBox uses environment flags to decide whether to render the artifacts panel or use certain layout behaviors.
insights.solution: When editing rendering or layout decisions, preserve respect for the environment/feature flags. Verify behavior under both flag states (enabled/disabled).

key_files.why: Primary UI component that composes the chat area and artifacts sidebar, manages selection state, and controls the resizable panel lifecycle and IDs.
Hook that interprets URL parameters into a chat mode; consumers rely on this to toggle UI behavior.
Hook that maps route/context to the active thread and provides thread-scoped state used by chat-box and artifact selection.
Central export point for chat components and hooks; changes here affect all imports across the workspace UI.

construct_relationships: ChatBox calls/use useThreadChat to obtain the active thread context and to scope artifact selection to the current thread.
ChatBox calls/use useChatMode to decide whether to show artifacts sidebar or alter UI behavior based on URL-derived mode.
ChatBox manages resizable panel instances whose persistent IDs are derived from the current route/chat context so that layout is restored per-route.
index.ts re-exports chat-box and the hooks so external consumers import them from a single module boundary.
notable_relationships: Routing layer (URL params/query) is upstream: changes to routes or param names will directly affect use-chat-mode and use-thread-chat behavior.
Global environment or feature-flag configuration influences ChatBox rendering decisions (artifacts panel and layout behavior).
Artifact data and thread metadata are likely provided by higher-level workspace state or backend APIs; ChatBox and hooks expect those external data contracts to be stable.
Layout persistence may interact with a broader layout/storage mechanism (e.g., localStorage or a layout manager) used elsewhere in the app; migrations or key changes can have cross-cutting effects.
recommendations: Preserve stable, route-aware IDs for resizable panels; if IDs must change, implement a migration strategy so users do not lose layout state.
Treat artifact selection as thread-scoped state: when altering selection logic, ensure deterministic updates on thread switches (clear, persist, or remap as current UX requires).
Do not rename or repurpose URL parameters consumed by use-chat-mode/use-thread-chat without coordinating updates to routing and all consumers; add/update tests that exercise route transitions.
When editing ChatBox rendering conditions, check environment/feature-flag permutations to avoid surprising differences between deployments.

If changing exports in index.ts, update all import sites or keep backward-compatible re-exports; run the frontend build/typecheck to catch broken imports and run component-level tests that cover chat navigation and layout persistence.

backend/packages/harness/deerflow/agents.description: This subtree implements the DeerFlow agent surface: the lead-agent factory and prompt composition, the middleware suite that mutates and protects the model message stream, the long-term memory pipeline (prompts, debounced queue, LLM-driven updater, pluggable storage), and checkpointer factories. It matters because these components determine runtime model selection, prompt content (skills/subagents/mounts), per-turn sanitization and safety, background persistence of facts, and lifecycle/resource initialization used by LangGraph runs.
key_behaviors: Resolve runtime model and assemble a fully-wired lead agent: model object, system prompt (with cached enabled-skills section), ordered middleware chain, tools and per-run state.

Mutate and sanitize message streams via ordered middlewares: inject uploads metadata, filter deferred tools, detect loops and dangling tool calls, request clarifications, queue memory updates, and handle LLM errors and retries.

Debounce and batch conversation contexts per-thread, format token-bounded memory extraction prompts, call an LLM to produce structured facts, validate/deduplicate/merge facts, and persist via configurable MemoryStorage implementations.
Provide checkpointer factories (sync + async) that initialize chosen backend (memory/sqlite/postgres) and expose process/global or scoped contexts for agent runs.
Maintain background caches and non-blocking helpers (enabled-skills cache, prompt warmers) to avoid synchronous disk I/O on request paths.

insights.title: Resolved model objects must be passed to middleware
insights.problem: Summarization middleware received raw model name strings or unresolved aliases, causing incorrect model objects to be used by middleware that expects provider/model methods.
insights.root_cause: Middleware creation used the config.model_name raw string instead of resolving through the model factory (create_chat_model/alias mapping).
insights.solution: Always resolve model aliases to concrete model objects before creating middleware that depends on model behavior or token accounting; treat model resolution as part of agent assembly.

insights.title: Prompt construction must avoid blocking disk I/O
insights.problem: Loading enabled skills synchronously during prompt build can slow startup and block request paths; repeated prompt rendering reloaded skills unnecessarily.
insights.root_cause: Skill discovery touches disk and was previously performed synchronously or on every prompt render without robust caching/invalidation.
insights.solution: Use a background refresh worker with synchronous warm/invalidate helpers and an LRU/signature memoization for prompt sections; ensure import-time priming avoids first-request stalls and support per-agent filtering.

insights.title: Memory updates need case-insensitive dedupe and reinforcement/correction flags
insights.problem: Duplicate facts differing only by case were persisted; reinforcement (positive) signals were not propagated to the updater; correction hints and sourceError fields were sometimes dropped.
insights.root_cause: Deduplication used case-sensitive keys and the queue/updater did not preserve or surface reinforcement/correction metadata from MemoryMiddleware.
insights.solution: Apply casefold-based keys during dedupe, preserve reinforcement_detected and correction_detected flags in the MemoryUpdateQueue when replacing/merging entries, and ensure sourceError and correction hints flow through prompts and persisted records.

insights.title: Message and argument normalization must handle varied runtime shapes
insights.problem: Loop detection and hard-stop injection failed or raised exceptions when tool args or AIMessage.content had unexpected shapes (e.g., stringified JSON, non-dict args, or content as list blocks).
insights.root_cause: Normalizers and mutators assumed narrow types (str content, dict args) and concatenated strings without guarding structured message bodies.
insights.solution: Defensively normalize tool-call args (stable JSON/string conversions, handle lists), derive order-independent stable keys for hashing, and handle AIMessage.content that may be structured lists when appending warnings or hard-stop text.

insights.title: Guard against missing runtime.context and replace prints with logging
insights.problem: Some middleware assumed runtime.context existed and used print() for diagnostics, leading to NPEs and noisy stdout in server environments.
insights.root_cause: Inconsistent invocation paths in LangGraph can pass None for runtime.context; debugging used prints instead of structured logging.
insights.solution: Null-check runtime.context before accessing thread_id/uploads_dir and use structured logging at appropriate levels (debug/info/warn) instead of print() so servers and tests can control verbosity.

insights.title: Async checkpointer setup must not block the event loop
insights.problem: ensure_sqlite_parent_dir ran synchronously on the event loop during async checkpointer initialization, risking loop blocking.
insights.root_cause: Filesystem setup logic was inlined in async startup code without offloading to threads or shared runtime helpers.
insights.solution: Move filesystem initialization to synchronous startup helpers or offload to an executor; centralize SQLite parent-dir helpers for reuse between sync and async factories.

key_files.why: Central factory that resolves runtime model, composes summarization/todo middlewares, assembles the ordered middleware chain and returns the fully-configured lead agent used by runs.
Builds the dynamic system prompt and manages the background-enabled-skills cache used to inject skills and subagent rules into the agent prompt.
Core LLM-driven memory update logic: parses LLM responses, validates/merges/deduplicates facts, and delegates persistence to MemoryStorage implementations.
Surfaces uploaded artifacts into the conversation by prepending a structured <uploaded_files> block, impacting agent understanding of attachments and downstream memory/sandbox access.
Safety middleware that detects repetitive tool-call loops and injects warnings or hard-stops; sensitive to tool-arg normalization and message shapes.

construct_relationships: make_lead_agent (lead_agent/agent.py) calls prompt builders (lead_agent/prompt.py) to render the system prompt and relies on the enabled-skills cache to avoid blocking I/O.

make_lead_agent assembles an ordered middleware chain (factory.create_deerflow_agent, features) that includes uploads, clarification, dangling-tool-call, deferred-tool-filter, loop-detection, memory, and summarization middlewares; order and Next/Prev anchors must be respected.
UploadsMiddleware prepends an <uploaded_files> message to the last HumanMessage; subsequent middlewares (memory_middleware, summarization middleware, tools) consume that modified history.

MemoryMiddleware extracts filtered conversation and queues ConversationContext into MemoryUpdateQueue; MemoryUpdateQueue debounces and calls MemoryUpdater, which uses MemoryStorage (memory/storage.py) to read/merge/persist facts.

LoopDetectionMiddleware inspects tool-call history and hashes normalized tool-call arguments; its normalization must align with how tools are serialized/produced by the tool subsystem to effectively detect repetition.
Checkpointer async/sync providers expose make_checkpointer/get_checkpointer used across agent lifecycle code; sqlite parent-dir logic is shared concern between async and sync providers.
notable_relationships: This agents subtree tightly integrates with LangGraph/LangChain runtime semantics (AgentMiddleware hooks, model binding, ToolMessage/AIMessage shapes). Changes must respect LangGraph message and tool-binding contracts.

Memory storage uses file-backed defaults and is referenced by updater and queue; modifications affect persisted facts consumed by future prompts and the Gateway UI. File paths and atomic save behavior can have cross-process implications.

Skill loading touches disk and interacts with skills packaging/mounting in the broader system (skills/public, sandbox mounts). Prompt cache priming at import time reduces first-request IO but couples agent package import with filesystem availability.

Sandbox and tool subsystems (outside this folder) produce tool-call arguments and file uploads that middlewares normalize and filter; mismatches across these subsystems can lead to missed loop detection or incorrect memory filtering.

Checkpointer backends (sqlite/postgres) affect persistence and must be initialized correctly for both synchronous test runners and async server lifecycles; event-loop blocking risks appear when filesystem operations run inline.
recommendations: Preserve middleware ordering semantics and respect Next/Prev anchors when modifying assembly logic; many behaviors depend on strict order (uploads → memory → deferred-tool-filter → loop-detection → summarization).

When touching prompt or skills code, maintain non-blocking behavior: background refresh worker, import-time priming, and synchronous warm/invalidate helpers must remain available to avoid first-request latency.
If touching model wiring, always resolve model names/aliases to concrete model objects before creating middleware or passing models to summarization/token-usage components.

Guard for varied runtime shapes: runtime.context may be None, AIMessage.content can be list or str, tool-call args may be non-dict/stringified JSON; normalize defensively and null-check before mutation.

Maintain propagation of memory metadata: reinforcement_detected and correction_detected flags must be preserved across MemoryMiddleware → MemoryUpdateQueue → MemoryUpdater → storage to avoid lost signals.
Replace or retain structured logging rather than prints in middleware, memory queue, and checkpointer initialization so server logs remain manageable; check existing log levels used elsewhere.
Ensure async checkpointer initialization does not perform blocking filesystem operations on the event loop; use shared helpers or offload to executor where needed.

Run backend unit tests and integration scenarios that exercise: middleware ordering effects, uploads injection, loop detection behavior on similar tool calls, memory queue debounce/merge semantics, and checkpointer startup (sqlite/postgres) after edits.

backend/packages/harness/deerflow/utils.description: This utils subtree provides small, focused helpers used by the DeerFlow harness for content ingestion and runtime orchestration: converting various document formats and web HTML into normalized Markdown/messages, and managing ephemeral TCP port allocation in concurrent environments. These utilities matter because they sit at the boundary between external artifacts (uploaded files, scraped HTML, runtime ports) and core services (agents, sandboxes, subagents) and therefore must preserve robust fallback, thread-safety, and predictable output shape.
key_behaviors: Convert uploaded documents (PDFs, Office formats) into Markdown with heuristics for image-based PDFs and async offloading for large files.
Extract normalized outlines/headings from generated Markdown while cleaning up artifacts (e.g., split or adjacent bold spans) to produce reliable document outlines.
Extract readable article content from raw HTML (title + body), convert it to Markdown or a structured message list that preserves images and content pieces.
Atomically allocate and reserve TCP ports in multi-threaded/concurrent contexts, exposing context-managed allocation and explicit release to avoid races and binding conflicts.

insights.title: Split/adjacent bold-span artifacts corrupt outline extraction
insights.problem: Markdown produced by a preferred PDF library (pymupdf4llm) sometimes emits adjacent bold spans or split-bold headings (e.g., "**A** **B**" or "**1** **Introduction**"), which pollute or break outline extraction.
insights.root_cause: The upstream conversion engine emits bold spans around subcomponents (page fragments, numbering, or layout pieces) rather than a single heading token.
insights.solution: Normalize and coalesce adjacent/split bold spans during heading extraction so outline generation tolerates fragmented markup; preserve this heuristic and its assumptions when editing conversion logic.

insights.title: Multiple conversion fallbacks and async offload for robustness
insights.problem: Some PDFs are image-only or too large/complex for the primary converter; synchronous conversion can block event loops or threads.
insights.root_cause: Document diversity (image-based PDFs, large multi-page files) and optional availability of third-party converters require layered handling.
insights.solution: Maintain the layered conversion approach: prefer pymupdf4llm when available, detect image-only PDFs and switch to OCR-friendly conversion, fall back to MarkItDown when necessary, and offload heavy work to threads/async executors to avoid blocking the caller.

insights.title: Avoiding race conditions when reserving ephemeral ports
insights.problem: Concurrent components can attempt to bind the same TCP port, and naive 'check+bind' logic leads to race conditions or incorrect assumptions about address semantics.
insights.root_cause: A free-port probe that does not atomically reserve the port or that checks the wrong bind address (e.g., localhost vs 0.0.0.0) can produce conflicts at bind time.
insights.solution: Use an atomic PortAllocator that reserves ports (mirrors Docker semantics by checking 0.0.0.0), expose context-managed allocation and explicit release, and preserve thread-safety primitives when modifying this module.

insights.title: Robust HTML-to-article extraction with graceful fallbacks
insights.problem: Native readability extraction can fail in malformed HTML or when optional extractors are absent, and missing titles/images must not break downstream consumers.
insights.root_cause: HTML variability, optional dependency availability (readabilipy vs readability.js), and differing content representations (embedded images, missing titles).
insights.solution: Keep a fallback chain and non-fatal error handling: attempt the native extractor, log and fall back to a JS-based extractor when needed, and produce consistent Article objects (with safe defaults for title/body/images) that downstream converters expect.

key_files.why: Core document-to-Markdown conversion and outline extraction; contains heuristics and fallbacks that downstream ingestion depends on.
Provides the thread-safe PortAllocator and module-level helpers used wherever ephemeral TCP ports are needed (sandboxes, local servers, tests).
Encapsulates HTML article extraction and conversion to Markdown/messages; used for web-content ingestion and must produce stable message shapes for downstream processing.

construct_relationships: convert_file_to_markdown (file_conversion.py) → extract_outline (file_conversion.py): convert_file_to_markdown produces Markdown that extract_outline parses and normalizes to produce a document outline.

ReadabilityExtractor/Article (readability.py) → Article.to_markdown / to_message: the extractor returns Article instances which convert HTML content to the same Markdown/message shapes that document conversion produces, enabling a common downstream consumption model.

PortAllocator (network.py) → runtime components (sandboxes, subagent processes): callers allocate ports via get_free_port/release_port (or context-managed allocation) to avoid binding conflicts; allocator must mirror Docker/host semantics (0.0.0.0) so consumers can rely on the reserved port being usable.

All converters (file_conversion + readability) → downstream ingestion/middleware: both conversion outputs are expected to be stable Markdown or structured messages consumed by middleware (UploadsMiddleware/ThreadDataMiddleware) and persistence layers.
notable_relationships: These utilities are upstream of several backend subsystems: UploadsMiddleware and ThreadDataMiddleware will feed file_conversion outputs into agent context and sandbox mounts; Summarization/MCP and memory extraction may consume normalized Markdown produced here.

PortAllocator semantics must be compatible with sandbox providers (local and dockerized) and with Docker Compose networking — port reservation must avoid conflicts with services like Gateway, LangGraph, nginx, and other dev/test helpers.

Readability fallbacks may require optional dependencies (readabilipy, readability.js) and possibly Node/JS toolchains if a JS fallback is used; changes here affect web-content ingestion reliability and may surface in integration tests.
These modules must remain resilient to missing optional third-party libraries (pymupdf4llm, MarkItDown, readabilipy); callers expect graceful fallbacks rather than hard failures.
recommendations: Preserve existing fallback chains and non-fatal error handling: optional third-party import failures must continue to be handled gracefully and logged rather than raising uncaught exceptions.
When editing conversion heuristics, ensure normalization for split/adjacent bold spans remains intact because downstream outline extraction and title detection depend on it.

Any changes to PortAllocator must maintain atomic reservation, 0.0.0.0 bind semantics, and context-manager/release semantics; validate concurrency via tests that simulate concurrent allocations/releases.

Retain async offload behavior for heavy/large-file conversions to avoid blocking the event loop; if changing synchronous code paths, ensure callers still get non-blocking semantics or document blocking impacts.

Run the backend unit tests and sandbox-related integration tests after modifications; when touching readability fallbacks, also ensure environments without optional native libs (CI and Docker dev) still pass and that Node-based fallbacks are exercised where expected.

backend/packages/harness/deerflow/guardr.description: This folder implements a small pluggable guardrails subsystem used to authorize agent tool calls before execution. It provides a protocol and dataclasses describing guardrail requests/decisions, a middleware that enforces decisions synchronously and asynchronously, and a built-in zero-dependency allowlist/denylist provider as a default. The components are re-exported for convenient consumption by the agent construction code.
key_behaviors: Define a runtime contract (dataclasses + Protocol) for pre-tool-call authorization providers.
Translate agent tool-call intent into GuardrailRequest objects and consult a GuardrailProvider prior to executing tools.
Support both synchronous and asynchronous evaluation paths (evaluate / aevaluate) and corresponding middleware wrappers (wrap/awrap).
Enforce allow/deny decisions by returning structured ToolMessage errors for denied calls and by handling provider failures with configurable fail-open/fail-closed behavior.
Provide a built-in, zero-dependency AllowlistProvider implementation to serve as a default provider when no external provider is configured.

insights.title: Zero-dependency built-in allow/deny provider
insights.problem: Consumers need a safe, out-of-the-box guardrail provider that does not introduce dependencies or external network calls.
insights.root_cause: No standard or default provider existed; many environments require a dependency-free baseline.
insights.solution: Provide a simple AllowlistProvider implementation that synchronously and asynchronously evaluates tool names against configured allow/deny lists; treat it as the default, lightweight option.

insights.title: Pre-tool-call authorization and failure semantics
insights.problem: Provider errors and control-flow exceptions (e.g., GraphBubbleUp) can alter tool execution flow in surprising ways if not explicitly handled.
insights.root_cause: Middleware must mediate between agent tool invocation and provider responses, including exceptions and transient errors, while maintaining expected agent control flow.
insights.solution: Middleware implements explicit handling for provider errors (supporting fail-closed vs fail-open/warn modes) and preserves passthrough behaviors for control-flow exceptions; callers rely on these semantics when invoking tools.

insights.title: Handle missing or empty tool names and empty deny reasons
insights.problem: Providers or agent requests may present empty tool names or return deny decisions without explanatory reasons, which can lead to unclear agent responses or errors.
insights.root_cause: Tool-call metadata originates from agents and external providers and may be malformed or underspecified.
insights.solution: Middleware and providers defensively handle empty tool names and empty reason strings, returning structured deny messages or fallback explanations to preserve stability and clarity.

insights.title: Stable provider API required for interoperable implementations
insights.problem: Without a clear protocol, alternate guardrail implementations may be incompatible with middleware expectations (sync vs async behavior, returned shape).
insights.root_cause: Dynamic typing and lack of a runtime-checkable contract lead to subtle mismatches between implementations and consumers.
insights.solution: Expose a runtime_checkable GuardrailProvider Protocol and concrete decision dataclasses to establish a stable contract that middleware relies upon across sync/async boundaries.

key_files.why: Defines the GuardrailRequest/GuardrailDecision datatypes and the GuardrailProvider Protocol that other components must implement and follow.
Implements the AgentMiddleware that intercepts tool calls, builds GuardrailRequests, consults providers, enforces allow/deny decisions, and encodes fail-open/fail-closed behavior.
Provides the built-in AllowlistProvider used as the default, dependency-free provider for simple allow/deny rules.
Exports the package's public API (providers, middleware, and provider types) and is imported elsewhere in the codebase as the guardrails entrypoint.

construct_relationships: GuardrailMiddleware constructs GuardrailRequest objects (provider.py types) from agent tool-call metadata and calls GuardrailProvider.evaluate or GuardrailProvider.aevaluate depending on sync/async path.

GuardrailProvider implementations (e.g., AllowlistProvider in builtin.py) return GuardrailDecision dataclasses that middleware inspects to decide to allow execution or to synthesize a denial ToolMessage.
__init__.py re-exports GuardrailProvider, GuardrailDecision, AllowlistProvider and GuardrailMiddleware so other packages import the contract and the default provider from a single module.
Middleware enforces fail-closed/fail-open semantics based on provider errors and encodes denial reasons (which may be empty) into the ToolMessage returned to agents.
notable_relationships: Middleware is intended to be part of the agent run pipeline (lead_agent factory / middleware chain) and therefore directly influences whether sandbox and builtin tools (sandbox tools, task/subagent spawns, etc.) are ever invoked.

Changes here affect both synchronous and asynchronous agent execution paths and thus will impact any code that expects evaluate/aevaluate behavior (including subagent spawning and streaming execution flows).
The exported Protocol and datatypes are consumed across the codebase; renaming or changing their shapes will require coordinated updates in modules that import them via the package entrypoint.

Denial messages produced by middleware propagate back into agent responses and may be surfaced to external clients (API/frontend), so textual and structural changes to denial formatting have downstream UX and API effects.
recommendations: Preserve the GuardrailProvider Protocol signatures (evaluate / aevaluate) and the shapes of GuardrailRequest/GuardrailDecision dataclasses; any signature changes require updates to middleware and all provider implementations.
When editing middleware, respect existing fail-closed vs fail-open semantics and GraphBubbleUp/control-flow passthrough handling to avoid changing runtime authorization behavior unexpectedly.
Maintain both synchronous and asynchronous wrapper implementations (wrap_tool_call / awrap_tool_call) because agent runtime code exercises both paths; update unit and integration tests for both.
Ensure __init__.py continues to export the same public names or update all import sites accordingly; changes to exports are breaking for other modules that import the guardrails package.

Add or run tests that cover: provider error paths, deny decisions with empty reasons, missing/empty tool-name inputs, and interplay with agent tool invocation flow (including sandbox and subagent tools) to validate end-to-end behavior.
Avoid introducing new heavy runtime dependencies into builtin.py without updating packaging and CI; the built-in provider is relied on for zero-dependency default deployments.

frontend/src/content/en/reference.description: This folder contains the English reference documentation pages and the navigation metadata used by the frontend documentation site. It provides routeable MDX pages under the /reference section and a _meta.ts file that the docs system uses to assemble navigation, ordering and visibility for those pages. These files directly influence site routing, static generation, and how reference content is presented in the frontend.
key_behaviors: Serve static/documentation routes: each MDX file becomes a routeable documentation page under the /reference section.
Navigation assembly: _meta.ts exports metadata consumed by the docs layer to build navigation, ordering, visibility and possibly sidebar grouping.
Localization scoping: files are in an 'en' locale folder implying parallel localized content elsewhere and a locale-aware docs pipeline.
Build-time compilation: MDX is compiled at build/dev time (Next.js static/SSR): changes require MDX/TS/typing to remain valid to avoid build errors.
Content fallback handling: placeholder MDX pages may exist; presence/absence of frontmatter or exports affects rendering, SEO, and linking.

insights.title: _meta.ts determines nav structure and must match page slugs
insights.problem: Modifying navigation metadata or page filenames without keeping them in sync breaks docs navigation, ordering, and published routes.
insights.root_cause: _meta.ts is the authoritative mapping used by the docs router to expose pages; filename/slug mismatches are not automatically reconciled.
insights.solution: Any edit that renames or moves an MDX page must be accompanied by corresponding updates to _meta.ts entries (and vice versa). Validate after changes that the navigation shows expected links and no 404s occur.

insights.title: Placeholder/empty MDX pages can still affect UX and build
insights.problem: Pages that are placeholders or lack required frontmatter may still compile but produce empty content or missing metadata (title, description), affecting navigation labels, SEO, and previews.
insights.root_cause: The docs pipeline depends on frontmatter and component-level exports inside MDX; placeholders may omit these intentionally or inadvertently.
insights.solution: Treat each MDX file as a contract: ensure minimal required frontmatter and exports remain present. After edits, verify the resulting page title/description and navigation label render correctly in dev/build.

insights.title: Locale-specific folder structure implies mirrored changes
insights.problem: Editing reference content under 'en' without considering other locales can lead to inconsistent translations and broken assumptions elsewhere in the site that expect parallel structure.
insights.root_cause: Docs are organized by locale directories; other tooling or build steps may expect matching paths across locales.
insights.solution: When moving/renaming pages or changing slugs, check whether the change must be mirrored in other locale folders or whether redirects / fallbacks are required; ensure i18n configuration still references the correct paths.

insights.title: MDX and _meta.ts edits are subject to frontend build/type checks
insights.problem: Incorrect TypeScript types, invalid MDX syntax, or malformed metadata will cause dev/build failures and CI failures.
insights.root_cause: Next.js/MDX compilation and the project's CI enforce typechecking and linting during build; _meta.ts exports are TypeScript and must compile.
insights.solution: Run the project's local frontend checks (format, lint, typecheck, dev build) after changes. Ensure any imports used in MDX components resolve and that _meta.ts export shapes remain consistent with the docs system's expected type.

key_files.why: Provides navigation/metadata for all /reference pages; authoritative for ordering, visibility and route mapping in the docs system.
Routeable MDX page for API/Gateway reference content; changes affect the /reference API documentation route.
Configuration reference page under /reference; likely referenced from other docs or external links and used by developers for config guidance.
Documents runtime flags and modes; content edits can affect operational guidance shown in the UI and must preserve accuracy.
Source map documentation page; part of the technical reference set and contributes to the overall completeness of the reference section.

construct_relationships: _meta.ts exports a metadata object that the docs rendering/router layer reads to build the /reference navigation and to map slugs to MDX files.
Each MDX file is compiled into a route and may depend on frontmatter keys (title, description, slug) that _meta.ts or the docs layout expects.
Locale folder ('en') placement ties these files into the i18n system; the docs router resolves routes with locale + reference slug.
The docs build pipeline (Next.js + MDX) consumes both _meta.ts and the MDX files together; type errors in _meta.ts will block compilation even if MDX is valid.
notable_relationships: Changes here affect the frontend site build and the public documentation routes — downstream consumers include the live Next.js site, CI pipelines, and external links from README or marketing pages.
Other locale folders (e.g., frontend/src/content/<other-locale>/reference) are expected to mirror structure; editing en without coordinating may create localization/translation drift.

Docs layout components and navigation UI elsewhere in the frontend codebase consume the exported metadata shape from _meta.ts; edits to metadata shape or keys must remain compatible with those components.
CI (frontend lint/typecheck/build) will run on changes to these files; broken MDX or TypeScript metadata will be surfaced in CI.
recommendations: When editing an MDX page, preserve or update frontmatter (title/description/slug) consistently with _meta.ts so navigation labels and routes remain valid.
If renaming/moving files, update _meta.ts and any cross-links; verify via local dev server and a production build to catch route and static-generation issues.
Run frontend checks after edits: pnpm format, pnpm lint, pnpm typecheck, and a dev or production build to ensure MDX compiles and TypeScript types in _meta.ts are valid.
Consider locale implications: check for mirrored pages in other locale directories and update translation workflows if slugs/paths change.
Validate navigation UI and links in the site after changes (sidebar, breadcrumbs, direct routes) to avoid broken links or missing entries in the /reference section.

frontend/src/content/en/tutorials.description: This folder contains the English tutorial content for the frontend site: a small collection of MDX tutorial pages and a metadata module that drives navigation/ordering for the tutorials section. It is documentation-only source used by the static site/content pipeline to generate the /tutorials routes and related navigation UI.
key_behaviors: Provide rendered tutorial pages (MDX -> HTML) for the site’s tutorials section
Expose navigation/ordering metadata via _meta.ts that the content layer or sidebar components consume
Integrate with the frontend build/runtime to produce routes and links (e.g., /tutorials/<slug>)
Carry localized content in the English docs tree (this folder is en/tutorials) that other i18n trees may mirror

insights.title: _meta.ts controls tutorials navigation
insights.problem: If filenames or slugs for MDX files change but _meta.ts is not updated, the tutorials navigation, ordering, or links can break or become inconsistent.
insights.root_cause: Navigation metadata and page files are separate artifacts; the content pipeline expects _meta.ts to match available MDX slugs.
insights.solution: When renaming, adding, or removing MDX tutorial files, update _meta.ts to keep metadata (title, slug, order) in sync so the navigation UI and route generation remain correct.

insights.title: MDX content affects static site build
insights.problem: Invalid MDX (syntax errors, missing frontmatter where expected, or broken imports/assets) can cause the frontend build or content runtime to fail.
insights.root_cause: The content pipeline parses MDX at build time and resolves imported assets and components referenced inside MDX.
insights.solution: Ensure MDX pages maintain expected frontmatter shape and valid MDX syntax; verify any component imports and referenced image/asset paths are present and resolvable by the build.

insights.title: Folder path determines locale and route
insights.problem: Moving or duplicating files without preserving locale folder structure (en/) may cause incorrect routing or missing localized variants.
insights.root_cause: This tutorials folder is under content/en; the content system maps folder placement to locale and route structure.
insights.solution: Respect the en/ path semantics when editing or relocating files; if adding translations, follow the same structure in parallel locale folders.

insights.title: Inter-document links and assets must resolve
insights.problem: Relative links from tutorials to other docs, images, or assets can become broken after edits or moves.
insights.root_cause: MDX uses relative paths and the site renderer expects those to exist at runtime/build time.
insights.solution: When editing MDX, verify all relative links and image references still point to valid targets; run the site build/link checks to detect broken links.

insights.title: Content consumers expect specific metadata shape
insights.problem: Consumers (sidebar, toc, search) may rely on fields provided by _meta.ts (title, description, order); changing the metadata shape or exports can break UI components.
insights.root_cause: The frontend code that renders navigation and search indexes depends on a stable metadata contract.
insights.solution: Preserve the exported structure and field names in _meta.ts; if the shape must change, update consumers in lockstep and run the frontend typechecks/build.

key_files.why: Central navigation/metadata module for ordering and exposing tutorial metadata to the content layer and UI; changes here affect sidebar and routing
Core onboarding tutorial likely referenced prominently in the tutorials index and linked from other docs
Deployment-focused tutorial that may contain environment-specific instructions and asset references; high risk for build/link issues
Tutorial describing runtime behaviors and integrations; may reference components, examples, or interactive embeds used by the site
Content that documents persistent features (memory) which could link to backend docs or external resources; verifying links and accuracy is important

construct_relationships: _meta.ts exports metadata that maps to the MDX filenames/slugs; the content renderer uses that to build tutorial navigation and ordering
Each MDX file is a content node consumed by the site’s MDX/markdown pipeline and rendered into a route under the tutorials section
MDX pages may import or reference shared UI components or assets from the frontend app; those imports must resolve at build time
The tutorials folder’s placement under content/en ties files to the English locale; the content system maps folder structure to routing and i18n behavior
notable_relationships: Upstream: The site content pipeline and sidebar/search components consume _meta.ts and MDX outputs; edits here will affect rendered navigation, search indices, and build artifacts
Downstream: Links inside tutorials may target other docs, public assets, or backend-related instructions — those upstream or external targets must remain available for the tutorials to be accurate
Cross-folder i18n: This en/tutorials folder is one locale variant — parallel locale folders may mirror structure and require synchronized updates when tutorial content changes
recommendations: When renaming, adding, or removing MDX files, update _meta.ts to keep navigation metadata in sync and maintain expected export shape
Validate MDX syntax, frontmatter, and component imports locally and run the frontend build (pnpm build) to catch parsing or import errors
Run linters/typechecks (pnpm lint / typecheck) and any site link-checking tooling to detect broken links or missing assets after edits
Preserve the folder locale (content/en) semantics; if adding translations, create corresponding files in the matching locale folder
Avoid changing the exported structure or field names in _meta.ts without updating all consumers (navigation, sidebar, search) and re-running the build/typechecks

frontend/src/components/workspace/citati.description: UI components that render and manage citation-style links inside the workspace. These components map markdown artifact links (including citation: prefixed links) to safe external anchors and compact citation badges with hover previews; they are small, interactive pieces that must integrate with parent selection/markdown rendering and Next.js client behavior.
key_behaviors: Translate artifact and citation-style markdown links into rendered anchors or badge components.
Detect external URLs and open them in new tabs with safe rel/target attributes.
Render compact citation badges that show a normalized label (domain or text) and reveal a hover popover with the full href and a 'Visit source' action.
Stop click event propagation from citation links to avoid interfering with parent workspace selection/interaction handlers.

insights.title: External links must use safe rel/target attributes
insights.problem: Links that open in a new tab can expose the app to tabnabbing or other cross-window attacks if rel/target are not set correctly.
insights.root_cause: Anchor tags that open external URLs via target="_blank" without rel="noopener noreferrer" allow the opened page to access window.opener.
insights.solution: Maintain explicit setting of target and rel for external links; any edits must preserve or reintroduce noopener/noreferrer semantics and avoid removing these attributes.

insights.title: Clicks on citation links must not trigger parent selection handlers
insights.problem: Clicking a link inside the workspace can inadvertently trigger parent-level selection, drag, or other click-based handlers, producing confusing UX or breaking navigation.
insights.root_cause: Parent workspace components implement global click/selection handlers that rely on normal event propagation; inline link clicks need to stop propagation.
insights.solution: Ensure click handlers on citation badges and artifact links continue to stop propagation (and prevent default where appropriate); maintain that behavior when changing markup or event wiring, and cover it with integration tests.

insights.title: Domain extraction and hover UI assume a browser runtime
insights.problem: Code that normalizes display text (e.g., extractDomain) or relies on hover/popover behavior may assume window/document APIs and break under SSR.
insights.root_cause: Next.js/React apps render on the server during builds or SSR, so DOM-dependent operations must either be guarded or run client-side only.
insights.solution: Treat domain extraction and popover rendering as client-centric behaviors; when modifying these components, keep SSR-safe code paths or guard DOM access behind client-only checks and validate in the Next.js build/dev environment.

insights.title: Hover-only popovers can be inaccessible
insights.problem: A popover exposed only on mouse hover may be inaccessible to keyboard and screen-reader users.
insights.root_cause: Interaction is described as hover-driven; without focus and ARIA support, keyboard users cannot access popover contents or the 'Visit source' action.
insights.solution: When interacting with the popover logic, preserve keyboard-focus visibility and aria attributes (or ensure that existing keyboard/focus handlers remain intact), and validate keyboard navigation and screen-reader behavior after edits.

key_files.why: Entry point for rendering artifact and citation-marked links; decides when to render CitationLink vs. a regular anchor and enforces external-link attributes and styling.
Implements the compact citation badge, domain/text normalization, hover popover UI, and click-event suppression used by artifact-link and other consumers.

construct_relationships: artifact-link.tsx maps children starting with 'citation:' to CitationLink (citation-link.tsx) instead of rendering a normal anchor.
artifact-link.tsx uses an isExternalUrl predicate to determine whether to set target="_blank" and rel attributes on anchors.
citation-link.tsx uses extractDomain to normalize badge text and composes a hover popover that exposes the href and a 'Visit source' action.
CitationLink prevents click event propagation so parent workspace selection/drag handlers do not receive link clicks; artifact-link preserves that behavior for citation-prefixed links.
notable_relationships: Parent workspace components implement global selection/drag/click handlers — changes here can break or alter selection behavior if event propagation is modified.
Markdown rendering or a higher-level link parser supplies 'citation:'-prefixed link children that artifact-link.tsx expects; changing the mapping semantics impacts how markdown links are rendered.
Global app styling, z-index, and overlay layers (popover placement) come from the broader frontend theme/CSS and can affect visibility/positioning of the citation popover.
Next.js SSR/build pipeline can exercise these components during server render; DOM assumptions must be guarded to avoid build/runtime errors.
recommendations: Preserve safe external-link attributes (target and rel) when modifying anchor rendering; verify behavior in a browser.
Keep the stopPropagation/preventDefault behavior on citation click handlers to avoid interfering with parent selection; validate with integration tests that include selection interactions.
Be mindful of SSR: avoid unguarded use of window/document or run popover logic client-side only; run Next.js build and dev checks after changes.
Validate accessibility: ensure popover remains reachable by keyboard (focusable) and includes appropriate ARIA semantics; test with keyboard navigation and a screen reader if UI is changed.
Run frontend lint/typecheck/build (pnpm lint, pnpm typecheck, pnpm build) and any UI or snapshot tests after edits; visually verify popover placement/z-index in the workspace context.

frontend/src/content/en/introduction.description: This folder contains the English 'Introduction' documentation pages for the frontend site: a small set of MDX content pages (why DeerFlow, core concepts, harness vs app) and a companion _meta.ts that supplies navigation/metadata. These files are consumed by the static site/MDX pipeline and the documentation router to render the introduction section and to build the docs navigation for the English locale.
key_behaviors: Provides static MDX content that is compiled into the site during Next.js/MDX build and rendered as pages in the docs section.
Supplies navigation and metadata via _meta.ts which the docs site loader uses to order, title, and expose the Introduction section in the English locale.
Acts as a localized content bundle — filenames, paths, and exported metadata determine routing, slug generation, and inclusion in the docs navigation.
MDX pages can include inline JSX, imports, links to assets, and frontmatter which the MDX compiler and site theme consume.

insights.title: _meta.ts is authoritative for this section's docs navigation
insights.problem: Changes to this file (export shape, keys, or path) will break the docs navigation or hide pages in the UI.
insights.root_cause: _meta.ts exports metadata consumed by the docs router / content loader to assemble menus and ordering for the Introduction section.
insights.solution: When modifying _meta.ts preserve the exported structure (names and types) expected by the docs loader, and update any dependent references or tests that assert navigation entries.

insights.title: MDX pages are static assets compiled into the frontend build
insights.problem: Editing MDX without preserving expected frontmatter keys or relative imports can cause build-time errors or missing props in the docs pages.
insights.root_cause: The MDX pipeline and site templates expect specific fields (titles, slugs, optional frontmatter) and valid import paths for images/components used inside MDX.
insights.solution: Ensure MDX frontmatter and any imported assets/components remain valid; verify relative paths and that component imports are exported from the expected locations before running the build.

insights.title: Files here are part of the English locale content tree
insights.problem: Renaming or moving files can change routes and break external links or cross-doc anchors, and may cause inconsistencies with other locale directories or site search index.
insights.root_cause: Docs routing and link resolution are path- and filename-sensitive; the site uses folder structure to determine locale and route slugs.
insights.solution: When renaming/moving files, update any internal links, URL references, i18n counterparts, and ensure the docs index/search index is rebuilt to reflect the new routes.

insights.title: MDX and meta changes affect static site build
insights.problem: Unvalidated changes can cause type, lint, or build failures in the frontend CI pipeline.
insights.root_cause: The frontend enforces formatting/type checks and compiles MDX to React during pnpm build; missing props or bad imports cause the build to fail.
insights.solution: Any edit should be validated locally with the same commands used in CI (format, lint, typecheck, build) to ensure compatibility with the production build pipeline.

key_files.why: Provides the navigation metadata and exported shape used by the docs loader to include and order this Introduction section in the English docs.
Primary landing/positioning doc explaining project goals; changing it affects user-facing messaging and potentially the section title shown in navigation.
Communicates core architecture and mental model — frequently cross-referenced by other docs and important for accuracy in other technical pages.
Explains conceptual separation used by the product; edits can impact how other docs interpret the harness/app boundary or link to these concepts.

construct_relationships: _meta.ts exports metadata that the docs router/content loader reads to list and order the three MDX pages for the Introduction section.
Each MDX file is routed as a documentation page by the MDX/Next.js content pipeline; the router associates route slugs and page props with the metadata from _meta.ts.
MDX pages may import shared React components or assets from other frontend folders (theme components, images); those imports are resolved at build time by the site bundler.
notable_relationships: This folder is part of the en locale tree; there are parallel locale directories (e.g., /content/xx/...) that should remain consistent when adding/removing pages or changing slugs.

The docs loader, site navigation components, and any search/indexing utilities consume the metadata and content here — changes can ripple into the global docs index, search results, and navigation UIs.
Frontend CI and static site build steps (pnpm build, typecheck, lint) run over these files; broken imports, invalid JSX, or missing frontmatter will surface as build failures.
recommendations: Preserve the export shape and keys in _meta.ts; if editing, ensure the docs loader's expected types are honored.
When editing MDX files, keep frontmatter keys (title, slug, etc.) consistent and validate relative import paths for images/components used inside MDX.
If renaming or moving files, update internal cross-doc links and confirm corresponding entries in other locales (if present) to avoid broken routes.
Run the frontend checks used in CI after edits: format/lint, typecheck, and a full pnpm build to catch MDX/JSX/import errors before committing.
Be mindful that edits affect static site generation and search/indexing; verify the docs navigation and any cross-references in the running development server or a local production build.

frontend/src/core/i18n/locales.description: This folder contains the application's locale definitions and the translation type schema for the frontend i18n system. It provides English and Chinese translation objects and a central type that enforces the shape of translation entries; keeping these files consistent is required for successful TypeScript builds and correct UI text rendering.
key_behaviors: Define a typed Translations schema that translation modules (en-US, zh-CN) must conform to.
Provide concrete localized strings for UI features (pages, tooltips, error messages, token usage, shortcuts, memory import/export, suggestions).
Be exported centrally (index.ts) so the i18n loader/consumer elsewhere in the frontend can import available locales.

insights.title: Translations shape enforced by types.ts
insights.problem: New UI strings were added but the Translations type did not include them, causing TypeScript/build errors.
insights.root_cause: Translation keys introduced in UI were not mirrored by type definitions in locales/types.ts.
insights.solution: Keep Translations type in sync with UI-required keys; when adding new UI strings, extend the types first and then implement translations in each locale.

insights.title: Parallel updates required across locales
insights.problem: English locale received new strings (network error, memory import/export, token usage, shortcuts, etc.) while other locales lacked corresponding entries.
insights.root_cause: Localization updates were implemented for the primary locale only, not the full set of supported locales.
insights.solution: Ensure each new translation key is added to every supported locale (at least en-US and zh-CN) to avoid missing-text fallbacks or runtime i18n lookups returning undefined.

insights.title: Locale availability via index export
insights.problem: Locale modules must be exported centrally for downstream import; omissions or renames can break i18n loader imports.
insights.root_cause: Index file (locales/index.ts) is the single place that exposes locales to the rest of the app and can become stale if files are renamed or not exported.
insights.solution: When adding/removing/renaming locale modules, update the index export so consumers import the correct locale objects.

insights.title: New UI features require matching i18n keys
insights.problem: New UI flows (token usage display, command palette/shortcuts, memory import/export, suggestions, create-skill prompt) reference keys that may not exist in locales.
insights.root_cause: UI implementation and i18n resource updates were not coordinated.
insights.solution: Coordinate changes: when a UI feature adds text keys, ensure the Translations type and all locale files include those keys; validate runtime usage and interpolation/placeholder formats.

key_files.why: Defines the Translations schema and LucideIcon type; the rest of the locale files must conform to it and TypeScript build errors originate here when keys diverge.
Primary English translation object that currently includes the newest UI strings; changes here reflect the canonical text used in development.
Chinese translation object that must mirror en-US keys; missing or mismatched entries here cause untranslated text or runtime undefined values.
Central re-export that exposes available locales to the i18n loader; must be kept accurate for consumers to import locales correctly.

construct_relationships: types.ts exports a Translations type and LucideIcon type that en-US.ts and zh-CN.ts implement; those locale objects must match the shape declared in types.ts.
index.ts imports and re-exports the locale objects (enUS, zhCN) so the broader i18n subsystem imports available locales from a single entry.
UI components elsewhere reference translation keys expected by Translations; mismatches between Translations and locale objects propagate to runtime lookup failures or TypeScript errors.
notable_relationships: This folder is consumed by the frontend i18n loader and UI components across the app; any change here affects text rendering in multiple pages and features (command palette, token usage displays, memory import/export, suggestions, create-skill flows).
Type changes here directly influence the TypeScript build/CI pipeline — mismatched keys or missing type entries will fail typecheck and block CI.
Localization changes must remain synchronized with component code that references these keys; otherwise runtime fallbacks or undefined strings will surface in the UI and tests.
recommendations: When editing translations add or remove keys, update types.ts first (Translations) and then implement the same keys in every locale file (en-US.ts, zh-CN.ts) to avoid type errors and missing UI text.
Keep index.ts exports in sync with locale filenames and newly added locales so the i18n loader and imports do not break.
Run TypeScript typecheck/build (pnpm typecheck or frontend build), lint, and frontend tests after any change to translations or types; CI enforces these checks.
Verify that interpolation/placeholders and any icon-related typings (LucideIcon) match the expectations of UI components that consume those strings.

When introducing UI strings for new flows (network errors, memory import/export, token usage, shortcuts, suggestions), audit all supported locales to ensure coverage and avoid runtime undefined lookups.

backend/packages/harness/deerflow/upload.description: This folder contains pure business-logic utilities that manage thread-scoped uploaded files: validation, safe filesystem ops, listing/enrichment, unique filename claiming, and artifact URL construction. It centralizes upload behavior used by both the HTTP Gateway and the embedded DeerFlow client so that file handling, path validation and URL semantics remain consistent across runtime modes.
key_behaviors: Validate and normalize thread IDs and uploaded filenames to enforce allowed namespaces and prevent path traversal.
Ensure and return per-thread uploads directories (get/ensure uploads dir) and provide safe file-creation semantics (claiming unique filenames).
List and enrich uploaded file metadata (normalize names, compute virtual paths or artifact URLs) for consumption by APIs/clients.
Delete files safely with path checks and error signaling (PathTraversalError) to avoid accidental or malicious filesystem access.
Construct virtual/artifact URLs for uploaded files so callers (Gateway, client) can reference or serve artifacts consistently.

insights.title: Path traversal prevention is central
insights.problem: File and thread identifiers supplied by clients can be used to traverse outside of permitted directories or access sensitive files if not normalized and checked.
insights.root_cause: Filesystem operations use input-derived paths and must be validated against allowed base upload directories; naive joins can be exploited with ../ sequences or absolute paths.
insights.solution: Keep and exercise strict validation/normalization functions (validate_thread_id, normalize_filename) and raise/propagate PathTraversalError; ensure all functions that read/write/delete use the same base-dir resolution logic.

insights.title: Single exported surface used by multiple callers
insights.problem: Both the Gateway and the embedded DeerFlow client import from this package; renames or signature changes will break callers across the codebase.
insights.root_cause: __init__.py re-exports manager utilities and exceptions to provide a stable import surface.
insights.solution: Preserve exported names and signatures or provide backward-compatible shims; update __init__.py consistently when altering manager functions.

insights.title: Race conditions when claiming unique filenames
insights.problem: Multiple concurrent uploads for the same thread could attempt to create the same filename, leading to collisions or lost uploads if uniqueness is not atomic.
insights.root_cause: Claim-unique operations implemented at the filesystem level without coordination can be subject to TOCTOU (time-of-check-to-time-of-use) races.
insights.solution: Callers must assume potential races and either rely on atomic filesystem semantics used by claim_unique_filename or coordinate externally; tests should exercise concurrent uploads to validate behavior.

insights.title: URL generation must match serving layer
insights.problem: Artifact URLs generated here must match the Gateway/nginx routing and any reverse-proxy base paths; mismatches will make uploaded artifacts unreachable.
insights.root_cause: upload_artifact_url composes virtual paths that are later served via Gateway endpoints and/or reverse proxy routing.
insights.solution: When modifying URL construction, verify alignment with Gateway route patterns and nginx/frontend expectations; ensure generated URLs are stable across dev/docker/prod deployments.

key_files.why: Contains the core upload business logic: validation, safe reads/writes/deletes, listing and artifact URL composition used by both Gateway and embedded client.
Re-exports the public API (utilities and PathTraversalError) forming the stable import surface consumed by other packages; changes here affect all callers.

construct_relationships: backend/packages/harness/deerflow/uploads/__init__.py re-exports functions and PathTraversalError from manager.py to create a stable public API.

validate_thread_id and normalize_filename (manager.py) are prerequisites called by list_files_in_dir, delete_file_safe and claim_unique_filename to ensure safe path resolution before performing filesystem operations.
claim_unique_filename coordinates with get_uploads_dir/ensure_uploads_dir to produce a non-conflicting file path within a thread-scoped uploads directory.
enrich_file_listing uses normalized filenames and upload_artifact_url to produce metadata that API layers present to clients.
notable_relationships: This upload utility is consumed by the Gateway HTTP layer and the embedded DeerFlowClient; changes affect both in-process and over-the-wire clients.

Thread-scoped uploads must align with ThreadDataMiddleware and the agent runtime's per-turn directories (workspace/uploads/outputs); thread ID validation logic here must match how ThreadDataMiddleware generates/assigns thread IDs.

Artifact URLs emitted by upload_artifact_url must be consistent with Gateway endpoints and nginx reverse-proxy routes so downstream consumers (frontend, external clients) can retrieve uploaded artifacts.
recommendations: Preserve exported names and function signatures in __init__.py to avoid breaking imports across the codebase; if renaming, provide compatibility shims.
Never bypass validate_thread_id/normalize_filename when adding new file operations; ensure PathTraversalError is raised and handled by callers.
When changing filesystem behavior (claiming or deleting), consider concurrent upload scenarios and run tests that simulate parallel uploads to the same thread.
After edits that affect URL formation, validate artifact URLs end-to-end against the Gateway/nginx routing in local Docker and dev setups.
Run backend unit tests and any integration tests that exercise upload workflows, and run lint/format tasks (uv/ruff) before committing changes.

backend/packages/harness/deerflow/runtim.description: This subtree implements the in-process runtime surface that orchestrates agent runs, streaming of run events, and checkpointer-backed stores for DeerFlow's embedded and server runtimes. It provides concurrency-safe run lifecycle management, canonical serialization for SSE/REST payloads, an in-memory StreamBridge with replay semantics for SSE consumers, and factories for sync/async persistence stores — all exported as a compact runtime public API used by the Gateway, LangGraph workers, and embedded clients.
key_behaviors: Create, register, manage and mutate RunRecord lifecycle state under asyncio locks (create, list, cancel/interrupt, cleanup) with support for multi-task conflict strategies.
Execute agent graphs in background asyncio workers, translate LangGraph streaming modes into SSE-like events, and push event items into a shared StreamBridge.
Publish and subscribe to per-run event logs with replay semantics (Last-Event-ID), heartbeat and explicit end sentinels to support reconnecting SSE clients.
Serialize LangChain/LangGraph/Pydantic objects into JSON-safe payloads and strip internal runtime keys before exposing state to clients.
Construct store instances (async and sync) driven by configuration (memory/sqlite/postgres), normalizing sqlite URIs and provisioning parent directories when needed.

insights.title: Deterministic run listing and multi-task strategies
insights.problem: Concurrent mutations to run registry can cause race conditions and ambiguous selection when multiple runs have tied timestamps or when conflicting run creation/cancellation strategies are used.
insights.root_cause: Shared in-memory registry mutated by concurrent coroutines without robust tie-breaking beyond insertion order; different strategies (reject/interrupt/rollback) must be coordinated atomically.
insights.solution: Keep asyncio locks around registry mutations, rely on insertion-order deterministic listing (newest-first) and propagate ConflictError/strategy errors to callers so higher layers can handle conflicts. Tests should exercise tied timestamps and concurrent create/cancel flows.
