insights.title: Replayable in-memory event logs with guaranteed END delivery
insights.problem: Previous queue-based approach could drop END sentinel when per-run buffers were full, leaving subscribers waiting indefinitely and leaking resources.
insights.root_cause: Per-run asyncio.Queue lacked replay semantics and could drop terminal sentinel events when bounded and full.
insights.solution: Use a bounded in-memory event log per run that supports replay from offsets, bounded retention, heartbeat items, and guarantees END sentinel delivery even under backpressure. Any change must preserve replay offsets (Last-Event-ID) and explicit sentinel semantics for client reconnects.

insights.title: Stable JSON serialization for streaming and REST
insights.problem: LangChain/LangGraph objects and Pydantic models include types and runtime-internal keys that are not directly JSON-safe and may leak internal state.
insights.root_cause: Heterogeneous object graphs returned by the agent graph; internal LangGraph state keys and custom objects need normalized representation for SSE/REST consumers.
insights.solution: Provide canonical serializers (messages, channel values, state dicts) that convert to JSON-serialisable Python primitives and strip internal LangGraph keys. Ensure the serializer behavior is stable across LangGraph versions and used consistently by workers and HTTP endpoints.

insights.title: Checkpointer-backed store factory must normalize SQLite and provision directories
insights.problem: Plain sqlite file paths may not have parent directories created which causes store initialization errors; special SQLite URIs (:memory:, file:) must be preserved.
insights.root_cause: Consumers may supply simple paths as connection strings and expect the runtime to handle filesystem provisioning; naive absolute resolving can corrupt special URIs.
insights.solution: Normalize sqlite connection strings while leaving special values untouched and ensure parent directories are created when a filesystem-backed sqlite file is used. Async provider must perform DB setup/teardown and surface helpful errors when optional DB drivers are missing.

insights.title: LangGraph context requirements (thread_id)
insights.problem: Middleware and run bookkeeping rely on thread identification in run config.context; missing thread_id in client-supplied context can break middleware that expects it.
insights.root_cause: Changes in LangGraph versions or client usage can omit thread_id insertion into context, or clients may provide their own context that lacks thread_id.
insights.solution: Worker and middleware assume thread_id exists; when modifying worker or run entrypoints, ensure thread_id is present or documented as required. Tests should simulate runs with and without client-supplied context to validate behavior.

key_files.why: Public runtime API aggregator — re-exports run management, serialization and stream bridge factories that other packages import from deerflow.runtime.
Concurrency-safe in-memory RunManager and RunRecord lifecycle operations — central to creating, cancelling and listing runs and enforcing multitask strategies.
Background worker that executes agent graphs, maps LangGraph modes to SSE events, updates run status and pushes events into the StreamBridge — core to run execution and streaming.
Default in-memory StreamBridge implementation providing per-run replayable event logs, heartbeat and guaranteed END sentinel delivery used by workers and SSE endpoints.
Async store factory used for server lifecycles — constructs and initializes langgraph async Stores according to configuration (memory/sqlite/postgres) and is relied upon by server startup code.

construct_relationships: RunManager in runs/manager.py holds RunRecord objects that runs/worker.py updates as background tasks progress (status transitions, cancellations).

Worker (runs/worker.py) serializes agent output via serialization.serialize* helpers and publishes StreamEvent objects to the StreamBridge interface defined in stream_bridge/base.py and implemented by stream_bridge/memory.py.

StreamBridge memory implementation provides replay offsets consumed by SSE endpoints and is created via stream_bridge/async_provider.make_stream_bridge for a server lifespan; worker and SSE handlers must share the same bridge instance.

Async store factory (store/async_provider.py) and sync provider (store/provider.py) present parallel factory patterns but must normalize sqlite URIs via _sqlite_utils.resolve_sqlite_conn_str and ensure parent directories using ensure_sqlite_parent_dir.
runtime/__init__.py re-exports RunManager, RunStatus, make_stream_bridge and serialize helpers so external modules (Gateway, embedded client) depend on these exact public symbols.
notable_relationships: Gateway API and HTTP SSE endpoints depend on the StreamBridge API and memory implementation to serve run progress to clients; any change to sentinel semantics or replay offsets affects SSE consumers.
LangGraph server and agent middleware rely on thread_id presence in run config.context; mismatches across LangGraph versions (>=0.6.0) can cause middleware to miss thread identification.

Server startup lifecycles must instantiate the async StreamBridge and async Store via their context managers so background workers and HTTP handlers share the same instances; changing these factories impacts server-wide state.
Front-end SSE reconnect and Last-Event-ID logic expects stable event IDs and guaranteed delivery of END sentinel — changes to event numbering/retention affect client reconnect behavior.
Tests and CLI/embedded flows use the sync store singleton and reset helper (store/provider.py); changing singleton semantics or API will impact test harnesses and embedded clients.
recommendations: Preserve public re-exports in runtime/__init__.py to avoid breaking imports across the codebase.

When editing RunManager or worker code, maintain asyncio locking discipline and the semantics of multitask strategies (reject/interrupt/rollback); add or run concurrent create/cancel tests to validate behavior.
Do not change StreamEvent sentinel meanings or replay semantics without updating all consumers — ensure END sentinel delivery is guaranteed and Last-Event-ID replay works under backpressure.

Keep serialization behavior stable: strip internal LangGraph keys and produce JSON-safe primitives for messages/state; run unit tests that assert serialized payload shapes used by SSE and REST endpoints.

If altering store factories, preserve sqlite connection string normalization and parent-dir creation; ensure async provider runs DB initialization and teardown steps and that missing DB drivers surface clear errors.
Ensure worker code continues to require or inject thread_id into config.context for LangGraph compatibility; test runs with client-supplied context lacking thread_id to catch regressions.
Run integration tests exercising SSE streaming, reconnect (Last-Event-ID), and END sentinel delivery plus store-backed checkpointer initialization (sqlite file path cases) after edits.

backend/packages/harness/deerflow/tracin.description: This folder provides a small factory that constructs tracing callback/handler objects for instrumenting LangChain/LangGraph agents with external tracing providers (currently LangSmith and Langfuse). It centralizes provider validation, provider-specific initialization, and returns a list of callbacks that the lead agent attaches to its runs. This factory matters because tracing integrations are optional, may require credentials and SDKs, and must not break agent startup when tracing is misconfigured or unavailable.
key_behaviors: Read tracing configuration and determine which tracing providers are enabled
Validate provider configuration and credentials before initializing provider-specific clients
Initialize provider-specific tracer/handler instances (LangSmith, Langfuse) with provider SDKs and settings
Return a list of callback/handler objects conforming to the agent/callback interface for attachment to LangChain agents

insights.title: Factory composes multiple tracing handlers
insights.problem: Tracing stack needs to support more than one provider concurrently (e.g., LangSmith + Langfuse).
insights.root_cause: Previously code only handled a single provider and lacked a central factory to create and compose multiple callbacks.
insights.solution: Keep a single entrypoint that validates configured providers, initializes each provider-specific handler independently, and returns an ordered list of handlers so multiple tracing backends can be attached simultaneously.

insights.title: Misconfigured providers must not break agent startup
insights.problem: Missing credentials, invalid provider names, or SDK initialization failures could prevent the lead agent from being created if errors are not handled safely.
insights.root_cause: Provider initialization may throw or require environment variables; naive initialization during agent bootstrap can raise exceptions that propagate.
insights.solution: Validate and guard provider initialization so that absent or invalid tracing configs are handled gracefully (fail-fast with clear logs or skip provider initialization) and do not cause overall agent initialization to fail unintentionally.

insights.title: External SDKs and optional dependencies
insights.problem: Tracing providers rely on third-party SDKs that may not always be installed or desired in all deployments.
insights.root_cause: Importing provider SDKs at module import time or without optional dependency handling can cause import errors in environments where those SDKs are absent.
insights.solution: Defer imports or handle ImportError to enable optional tracing providers and ensure the factory can run in minimal environments; update packaging/requirements to reflect optional dependencies and ensure CI covers configurations with and without tracing SDKs.

insights.title: Callbacks must adhere to LangChain callback interface and lifecycle
insights.problem: Handlers returned by the factory are attached to agent runs; they must implement the expected callback contract and not retain inappropriate global state.
insights.root_cause: Provider-specific handlers may have different initialization semantics or thread-safety characteristics.
insights.solution: Ensure returned handlers conform to the expected callback methods and lifecycle (construction, attach/detach, thread-safety), and avoid long-lived side-effects that could leak resources across runs.

key_files.why: Primary implementation: builds and validates tracing provider integrations and returns callback/handler objects to attach to agents.
Package export: exposes build_tracing_callbacks for import by agent initialization code (e.g., lead agent factory).

construct_relationships: build_tracing_callbacks (factory.py) reads global tracing config and invokes provider-specific helpers (_create_langsmith_tracer, _create_langfuse_handler) to produce provider handlers.
_create_langsmith_tracer and _create_langfuse_handler encapsulate provider-specific SDK initialization and return objects that implement the agent callback interface.
__init__.py re-exports build_tracing_callbacks so upstream modules import a single symbol to acquire tracing handlers.
notable_relationships: Upstream: lead_agent factory (backend/src/agents/lead_agent) calls build_tracing_callbacks to attach tracing callbacks during agent creation; changes here affect agent startup behavior.

Configuration: factory reads tracing configuration from the application's config mechanism (config.yaml or env-backed settings elsewhere in the backend); edits must respect the config schema and keys expected by other parts of the system.

External SDKs: LangSmith and Langfuse SDKs (third-party packages) are required for provider initialization; packaging and runtime environments must include these or the factory must handle their absence.
Runtime/CI: The factory's behavior under different environments (no tracing, one provider, multiple providers) must be exercised by unit/integration tests to avoid regressions.
recommendations: Preserve the public API: build_tracing_callbacks is exported and consumed by lead_agent creation; keep its signature and return type (list of callback/handler objects) or coordinate co-changes where it is used.
Avoid import-time side-effects: do not import provider SDKs unguarded at module import time; handle ImportError and only initialize providers when build_tracing_callbacks is invoked.

Fail-safe behavior: ensure missing/invalid tracing configs or initialization errors do not raise unhandled exceptions that prevent agent initialization—handle errors and log or skip providers instead.

Respect callback contract and lifecycle: returned handlers must implement the callback interface expected by LangChain/LangGraph and should not hold cross-run mutable global state; verify thread-safety where applicable.

Dependency and CI checks: if adding or updating provider SDK usage, ensure packaging (optional deps) and CI run the relevant tests both with and without those SDKs installed; run backend unit tests and any agent initialization/integration tests after edits.

frontend/src/components/workspace/agents.description: This folder contains client-side React UI components that present and manage workspace agents: a gallery view listing agents and a card view for each agent with actions (start chat, delete). These components are responsible for fetching and rendering agent metadata, handling user interactions (navigation, create, delete), and surfacing feedback (confirmation dialogs and toasts), so they are the UI surface for agent lifecycle actions in the workspace.
key_behaviors: Fetch and render a list of agents in a responsive grid with loading and empty states.
Render per-agent metadata (name, model, description, tool group badges) and expose primary actions (start chat/navigation and delete).
Present a confirmation dialog on delete, call a delete API hook, and surface success/error via toast notifications.
Initiate navigation flows: create-new-agent and start-chat for a specific agent.

insights.title: Agent deletion must coordinate with agent listing
insights.problem: When an agent is deleted via the card UI, the gallery list must reflect the removal to avoid stale UI.
insights.root_cause: Delete action is performed by a hook separate from the fetch hook used by the gallery; without explicit invalidation or state update the gallery may not refresh.
insights.solution: Ensure delete hook triggers the same cache invalidation or state refresh mechanism used by the gallery's fetch hook (e.g., query invalidation, refetch, or local state update) so the UI remains consistent after deletion.

insights.title: Components perform router-based navigation
insights.problem: AgentCard and AgentGallery navigate to chat or new-agent routes; incorrect route paths or changed route params will break navigation.
insights.root_cause: Navigation targets are hard-coded or dependent on app routing conventions outside this folder.
insights.solution: When editing these components, respect existing route shapes and any shared route helpers; verify navigation targets continue to match global routing and client expectations.

insights.title: Delete confirmation and toast feedback are part of flow
insights.problem: Removing confirmation dialog or weakening toast usage could degrade UX and allow accidental destructive actions.
insights.root_cause: Delete is a destructive operation; UI currently protects via explicit confirmation and toasts.
insights.solution: Preserve confirmation dialog semantics and success/error toasts. If the delete flow changes, ensure equivalent safeguards and user feedback remain present and accessible.

insights.title: Components rely on client-side hooks and runtime
insights.problem: These components use client-side hooks (fetching, deletion, toasts, navigation) and must not be treated as server components.
insights.root_cause: They depend on browser-only APIs and interactive behavior.
insights.solution: Keep the components as client components (retain 'use client' if present) and ensure any refactors maintain client-side lifecycle and side-effect semantics.

insights.title: Gallery layout and card badges encode metadata
insights.problem: Changes to DOM structure, CSS classes, or layout utilities can break responsiveness or badge rendering.
insights.root_cause: Layout uses responsive grid and badges rely on predictable DOM and styles.
insights.solution: When changing markup or class names, ensure the responsive grid and badge visuals remain intact and run visual checks across breakpoints.

key_files.why: Entry component that fetches the agent list, handles loading/empty states, and renders AgentCard instances and the create-new-agent action.
Per-agent UI surface that displays metadata and implements primary actions (start chat navigation and delete with confirmation and toast feedback).

construct_relationships: AgentGallery uses a fetch hook to obtain the list of agents and maps over results to render AgentCard components.
AgentCard invokes a delete API hook and displays confirmation dialog and toast notifications; the delete result should influence the AgentGallery's data state.
Both components call navigation functions to start chat or open new-agent creation, implying reliance on global routing utilities or router hooks.
Both components rely on shared UI primitives (confirmation dialog, toast system, badges/grid layout) provided elsewhere in the app.
notable_relationships: These components depend on external hooks/providers: agent list fetch hook, agent delete hook, toast/notification provider, and the app router—changes to those cross-folder APIs will impact behavior here.

UI primitives (dialog, buttons, badges, grid styles) and shared CSS utilities used here are likely defined in a shared components or style layer; altering those shared primitives will affect this folder's rendering.
Routing conventions and route paths used by navigation here are consumed elsewhere (chat screens, new-agent creation pages); mismatch will break cross-page flows.
recommendations: Preserve client-only semantics and ensure 'use client' (or equivalent) remains if present; keep hooks and browser-side lifecycle intact.
When changing deletion behavior, ensure you also update or trigger the same cache invalidation/refetch used by the gallery so the agent list stays synchronized.
Maintain confirmation dialog and toast usage for delete flows; verify accessibility (keyboard/ARIA) and that toasts display both success and error states.
Respect existing routing targets and any route helpers; run navigation flows manually to confirm chat and create routes still work after edits.
Run frontend linting, typechecks, and a production build (pnpm build) and validate responsive layout visually across breakpoints after changes.

frontend/src/app/workspace/settings.description: This folder contains React UI components that implement the Workspace Settings UX surface in the frontend. It provides a dialog shell and section wrapper used to organize multiple settings pages, plus a notification-specific settings page that interacts with the browser Notification API and a test/send flow. These components mediate client-only browser APIs and compose into the workspace settings dialog used by higher-level workspace pages.
key_behaviors: Render a modal settings dialog that composes multiple settings sections and supports navigation between them.
Provide a reusable section wrapper that standardizes labels, descriptions and layout for individual settings pages.
Manage browser notification permission state, request permission from the user, and offer a test/preview notification flow.
Guard client-only/browser APIs from server-side rendering and coordinate async status updates in the UI.

insights.title: Components interact with browser-only APIs
insights.problem: NotificationSettingsPage must use window/Notification which are undefined during server-side rendering in Next.js.
insights.root_cause: Next.js pages and components may be rendered on the server; components calling browser APIs without guarding will crash SSR.
insights.solution: Ensure these components run only on the client (e.g., client components or runtime guards) and check for the existence of window/Notification before accessing them; anticipate hydration mismatches and render placeholder states until client runtime is available.

insights.title: Asynchronous permission request and test notification flow
insights.problem: Requesting notification permission and sending a test notification are async and can fail or be blocked by browser policies.
insights.root_cause: Browser permission prompts are user-driven, may be denied, and notifications may require service workers or secure contexts.
insights.solution: UI must surface clear states for allowed/denied/default, handle rejected promises, and gracefully degrade when notifications are unavailable; avoid assumptions about successful notification delivery and reflect API results in the UI.

insights.title: Settings dialog composes multiple sections
insights.problem: Changes to props or expected children API on SettingsDialog or SettingsSection will affect all contained pages.
insights.root_cause: SettingsDialog and SettingsSection are shared wrappers used by multiple settings pages, so their public prop shapes are effectively a contract.
insights.solution: Respect existing prop shapes and render slots; when modifying props or callbacks, update all consumers and tests together to avoid breaking UI composition.

insights.title: Settings persistence and integration points
insights.problem: Notification preference UI likely maps to persisted user preferences or client storage; inconsistencies between UI and storage/back-end can cause drift.
insights.root_cause: The folder provides only the UI; persistence may be implemented elsewhere (API client, context/provider), so UI changes must align with the persistence contract.
insights.solution: When updating UI behavior, ensure compatibility with the existing persistence layer (localStorage, user settings API, or workspace state) and consider side effects like reloading or service worker subscriptions.

insights.title: Modal/dialog and interactive controls require accessible behavior
insights.problem: Settings dialog and test notification flow include focusable elements and ephemeral permission prompts that can disrupt keyboard users or screen readers if not handled.
insights.root_cause: Modals and permission requests change focus and visibility; without ARIA attributes and focus management, keyboard navigation and screen reader context can break.
insights.solution: Maintain modal semantics (role, aria-modal), ensure focus is trapped and returned, and make permission/test actions reachable and announced appropriately.

key_files.why: Top-level dialog component that composes settings pages and navigation; edits here affect all workspace settings UX.
Reusable wrapper for individual settings sections; defines layout and prop contract consumed by settings pages.
Implements browser Notification permission handling and test flow; interacts with client-only APIs and user permission state.

construct_relationships: SettingsDialog composes SettingsSection instances to render distinct settings pages and manages navigation between them.
NotificationSettingsPage is intended to be rendered inside SettingsSection (or as a child of SettingsDialog) and relies on the wrapper for consistent layout and headings.
NotificationSettingsPage reads/writes runtime state (browser Notification API status) and likely calls higher-level persistence APIs or context exposed outside this folder to save preference changes.
notable_relationships: These components run in the frontend Next.js app and must respect server/client rendering boundaries—changes may affect other app pages under /app/workspace that import the dialog.

NotificationSettingsPage may interact indirectly with service worker registration, push subscription logic, or backend user settings endpoints living elsewhere in the codebase; edits should not assume those implementations.
Styling, theme tokens, and global modal behaviors are likely provided by shared UI primitives elsewhere in the frontend (e.g., design system components or providers) and must be preserved.
recommendations: Treat these files as client-side UI: guard or mark components to run only on the client and avoid referencing window/Notification during SSR.
When modifying props or structure of SettingsDialog/SettingsSection, update all consumers and verify layout across settings pages to maintain the public contract.
Handle Notification API promise rejections, unavailable APIs, and all permission states; test in browsers that block or require secure contexts.
Preserve modal accessibility (focus trapping, ARIA) and verify keyboard/screen-reader interactions after changes.

Run frontend checks after edits: pnpm lint, pnpm typecheck, pnpm build (Next.js), and local manual testing of the notification permission/test flow and modal interactions; update any UI tests or storybook snapshots that reference these components.

frontend/src/content/en/harness.description: This folder contains the English documentation content for the Harness subsection of the frontend docs site. It provides the navigation metadata used by the docs engine and the MDX page(s) that render the visible documentation pages under the /harness route; changes here affect site navigation, routing, and rendered documentation for English users.
key_behaviors: Expose navigation metadata that the docs site uses to build the Harness section (ordering, titles, route grouping).
Provide page content as MDX that the site compiles into the /en/harness documentation routes.
Serve as the canonical English-language source for Harness docs (affects localization and search indexing).
Integrate with the global docs build system: content is compiled, type-checked, formatted, and included in site navigation and search.

insights.title: Navigation metadata drives docs routing and menu entries
insights.problem: If the _meta.ts export shape or its properties are changed or removed, the docs generator may fail to include the Harness section or mis-order pages in the navigation.
insights.root_cause: The site build expects a specific metadata structure and export contract from *_meta.ts files to assemble the sidebar and route tree.
insights.solution: Preserve the expected meta export shape and keys (title, order, slug-like names) and ensure any changes remain compatible with the docs generator's contract.

insights.title: MDX page placeholders may break rendering or search if incomplete
insights.problem: An MDX file containing only placeholder content or incorrect frontmatter can result in an empty page at runtime or missing search indexing entries for the Harness section.
insights.root_cause: The build and search/indexing pipelines rely on MDX frontmatter and content to generate page metadata and indexable content.
insights.solution: Maintain valid MDX syntax and frontmatter; ensure pages provide meaningful content or explicit metadata required by the build/search pipelines.

insights.title: Folder location and filename determine locale route and path
insights.problem: Renaming or relocating files in this folder without updating references or other locale folders can break the /en/harness route or cause inconsistencies across locales.
insights.root_cause: The docs site maps filesystem paths to localized routes; metadata and filenames must remain consistent with that mapping.
insights.solution: Keep filenames and folder structure stable for the English locale and coordinate changes with parallel locale folders and any cross-file references.

key_files.why: Exports the section metadata that the docs engine uses to build the Harness navigation and to determine ordering and visibility of pages in this section.
Source MDX page for Harness configuration content; compiled into the /en/harness/configuration route and used by search/indexing and the rendered docs site.

construct_relationships: The meta export in _meta.ts is consumed by the docs generator to create the Harness sidebar entry and to register routes for MDX pages in this folder.
configuration.mdx is paired with the folder's metadata so the generator can surface the page under the Harness section with the ordering and title specified in _meta.ts.
File and folder paths determine the route namespace (/en/harness/*) and are expected to align with localization conventions used across other locale subfolders.
notable_relationships: Docs build system: Changes here affect static site generation, search indexing, and navigation across the entire frontend docs site.
Localization pipeline: This folder is the English source; any path/name changes need to be reconciled with other locale folders (if present) and with translation workflows.
CI and lint/format/typecheck: Renaming or editing these files can trigger frontend CI jobs (pnpm format, lint, build) and should satisfy the project's formatting and typing rules to pass CI.
recommendations: Preserve the exported shape and keys in _meta.ts (do not change export name or expected fields) because the docs generator depends on them.
Maintain valid MDX syntax and required frontmatter in configuration.mdx so the build and search indexer can parse the page.
Do not rename or relocate files without updating any cross-references and verifying corresponding locale folders; filesystem paths map directly to routes.
After edits, run the frontend docs build and lint/typecheck flow (pnpm format, pnpm lint, pnpm build / site dev preview) to confirm navigation and rendering behave as expected.
Be aware that changes here affect site navigation and search; verify the sidebar, route URLs, and search index entries in a dev preview before committing.

frontend/src/app/[lang]/docs.description: This folder implements the localized documentation pages for the Next.js app using Nextra MDX. It contains the page route that imports and renders MDX content for a selected language/path and a docs layout that adapts Nextra's layout to include language prefixes, localized routing, and a custom header — enabling static generation and per-page metadata/TOC injection for localized docs.
key_behaviors: At build time, enumerate available language + documentation paths (generateStaticParams) so Next.js can statically render localized MDX pages.
Generate per-page runtime metadata (generateMetadata) and construct a table-of-contents that is passed into MDX wrapper components.
Render localized MDX content inside a docs Layout that has language-aware route prefixes and a localized Header.
Transform/format the Nextra page map so routes include the language and docs base path before handing it to the Layout/navigation.

insights.title: Language-prefixed static routes
insights.problem: Localized docs require path and route transformations so static route entries match language-prefix expectations.
insights.root_cause: Nextra page map and MDX paths are language-neutral; the app folder needs to prepend language/docs prefixes for correct client/server routing.
insights.solution: Ensure generateStaticParams enumerates the same language set used by routing and that any consumer (Layout or page) receives routes with consistent language/docs prefixes. Treat the page map as a transformed view rather than source-of-truth for raw file locations; preserve the transformed route shape wherever navigation or linking is produced.

insights.title: Consistent metadata/TOC shape for MDX wrappers
insights.problem: MDX wrapper components expect a specific metadata and table-of-contents shape; mismatches cause runtime rendering or SEO inconsistencies.
insights.root_cause: generateMetadata and TOC construction logic live in the page-level component and must produce the exact structure Nextra wrapper components expect.
insights.solution: Keep the metadata and TOC assembly logic aligned with the Nextra MDX wrapper contract; any changes to keys, nesting, or types must be reflected in both generator and wrapper consumers (and validated during build).

insights.title: Layout depends on external i18n and header components
insights.problem: Layout composes a custom Header and i18n configuration that live outside this folder, so changes here can break header behavior or localization if the contract changes.
insights.root_cause: The docs Layout formats pageMap routes and passes i18n data into the Header and Nextra Layout; the header/i18n interfaces are implicit rather than defined locally.
insights.solution: When modifying layout code or the route-formatting function, preserve the expected props and shapes passed to Header and Nextra Layout. Be aware of upstream i18n configuration and test header rendering across locales.

insights.title: App-router conventions and server-side logic
insights.problem: This directory contains server-side generation hooks (generateStaticParams/generateMetadata) and server-rendered components; mixing client-only APIs or side-effectful browser logic will break SSR/SSG behavior.
insights.root_cause: Next.js app directory defaults to server components; build-time hooks must remain pure and not depend on browser-only globals or client-only modules.
insights.solution: Keep generation hooks and layout components free of client-only dependencies; if a client component is required, isolate it as a client-bound child. Validate during build that static generation runs without importing browser-only modules.

key_files.why: Entry point for localized MDX pages: enumerates static params, generates per-page metadata/TOC, and renders the MDX content into the wrapper.
Docs Layout that adapts Nextra's Layout for localized routing: formats the page map with language/docs prefixes and composes the localized Header/i18n config.

construct_relationships: page.tsx -> uses generateStaticParams to enumerate language + mdx paths that Next.js will statically render.
page.tsx -> constructs metadata and TOC and passes them to the MDX wrapper component that renders the page content.
layout.tsx -> receives a Nextra pageMap and runs formatPageRoute to insert language/docs prefixes before passing the map into Nextra's Layout and the custom Header.
page.tsx and layout.tsx -> share the same language set and route-prefixing expectations; layout-adjusted routes must match the routes generated by page.tsx's static params.
notable_relationships: Relies on Nextra MDX internals and the site's docs content tree (MDX files) which are outside this folder — changes here assume the shape of the pageMap and MDX metadata produced elsewhere.
Depends on global i18n configuration and Header component implementations that live elsewhere in the frontend; language list and localization strings are upstream inputs.
Interacts with Next.js app-router conventions (generateStaticParams/generateMetadata) and will affect static build output and SEO metadata consumed by the site root and external crawlers.
recommendations: Preserve the signatures and return shapes of generateStaticParams and generateMetadata; changes will affect Next.js SSG and routing.
When modifying route formatting, ensure produced routes exactly match the paths used by generateStaticParams and any client-side navigation links.
Do not import browser-only modules or use window/document in generation hooks or server components; isolate client components explicitly if needed.
Validate changes by running the frontend build (pnpm build) and type/lint checks; test multiple locales and several docs pages to ensure metadata and TOC shapes are preserved.
When touching layout or header integration, verify localized navigation, breadcrumbs, and links across languages to avoid broken links or incorrect prefixes.

frontend/src/app/workspace/agents.description: This subtree implements the workspace agent UX: listing agents, creating a new agent (with a bootstrap chat and explicit save), and per-agent chat threads. It composes higher-level chat primitives and thread runtime hooks to start/resume streaming conversations, persist agent records, and surface thread-scoped settings, token usage and artifacts — all while tolerating backend eventual consistency and maintaining navigation state.
key_behaviors: Render agent gallery as the workspace entry point for agents
Two-step agent creation: validate name (local + remote), create agent record, then bootstrap a short thread and persist configuration via a hidden save command plus an explicit Save action

Agent-scoped chat lifecycle: initialize or resume a Thread, start/stop streaming responses, update browser history on new thread creation without remounting the route, and show notifications when runs finish while the page is not focused
Per-thread scoping of runtime settings (models, overrides) and integration of runtime indicators (token usage, artifacts/exports, todo/title flows)
Layout composition: provide chat-related context/providers at the agent-thread route boundary so descendant pages/components use the same thread runtime hooks

insights.title: Thread-scoped settings required
insights.problem: Agent chat pages previously used a global local settings hook so per-thread model overrides were ignored.
insights.root_cause: A shared/global settings hook was used rather than wiring thread-specific settings into agent-scoped chat pages.
insights.solution: Use thread-scoped settings hooks/providers inside the agent chat layout so per-thread overrides (e.g., model selection) are respected by the chat components.

insights.title: Explicit save with hidden bootstrap save
insights.problem: Implicit save flows surfaced internal artifacts and confused users; agent creation relied on implicit persistence.
insights.root_cause: Bootstrap thread was used for setup without an explicit user-facing save; backend eventual consistency meant reads right after write could fail.
insights.solution: Provide an explicit Save action for users while also sending a hidden 'setup_agent' command in the bootstrap thread; implement read-after-write retries when fetching the newly created agent.

insights.title: Read-after-write eventual consistency
insights.problem: Immediate reads after agent creation could fail or return stale results.
insights.root_cause: Backend store exhibits eventual consistency; immediate GET after POST can return not-found.
insights.solution: Retry agent retrieval with increasing delays (AGENT_READ_RETRY_DELAYS_MS) and surface network-specific errors clearly to users.

insights.title: Network vs validation error mapping
insights.problem: Name-check UI showed misleading messages (e.g., CORS/network errors appeared as validation errors).
insights.root_cause: The error from the name-check API was not classified separately as network-level vs validation-level.
insights.solution: Map AgentNameCheckError and similar network-specific exceptions to distinct messages so the user sees an accurate error reason.

insights.title: Stream lifecycle and background notifications
insights.problem: Users could lose awareness of stream completion if the page was not focused; creating a new thread could cause full route remounts that lose local runtime state.
insights.root_cause: The page lifecycle did not distinguish between router pushes that should preserve in-memory runtime and full remounts; streaming stop/start signals and notification hooks were not consistently applied.
insights.solution: Update history without remount to preserve thread runtime; ensure streaming stop handlers and completion notifications run even when page is in background.

key_files.why: Top-level entry for the agents listing route — renders the AgentGallery and is the workspace entrypoint for navigating to agent flows.
Implements the new-agent creation flow: local/remote name validation, createAgent API call, bootstrap thread via useThreadStream, hidden 'setup_agent' save, and read-after-write retry logic.

Agent-specific chat page that wires thread runtime hooks (useThreadChat, useThreadStream, useThreadSettings) to chat UI primitives, manages start/stop streaming, token usage display, and notifications.
Layout wrapper that composes chat-related providers and contexts for an agent thread — ensures descendant pages/components share the same thread runtime and settings.

construct_relationships: NewAgentPage calls createAgent API, then uses useThreadStream to bootstrap a short thread and sends a hidden 'setup_agent' command to persist agent configuration; it then retries getAgent until the created record is visible.

AgentChatPage consumes thread runtime hooks (useThreadChat, useThreadStream, useThreadSettings) provided by AgentChatLayout to initialize/resume Threads, send messages, stop streaming, and surface UI primitives (ChatBox, MessageList, InputBox, ThreadTitle, TokenUsageIndicator).
AgentChatLayout composes the provider boundary so per-thread settings, upload/workspace directories and thread state are injected before AgentChatPage and children render.

Agent listing (AgentsPage → AgentGallery) links to agent-scoped routes that resolve into the layout + thread pages, relying on route navigation that must push history without forcing remount to preserve runtime state.
notable_relationships: This subtree depends on global thread runtime hooks and shared UI primitives elsewhere in the app (ChatBox, MessageList, InputBox, TokenUsageIndicator, TodoList, AgentWelcome). Changes here must respect those component contracts.

NewAgentPage integrates with backend APIs: checkAgentName, createAgent, getAgent; it relies on eventual-consistency tolerant retry constants (AGENT_READ_RETRY_DELAYS_MS) and network error classification (AgentNameCheckError).

Agent thread streaming behavior ties into the broader streaming/event system (useThreadStream/useThreadChat) and likely to SSE/websocket plumbing in higher app layers — edits must preserve start/stop semantics and notification hooks.
Routing behavior (updating browser history without remount) interacts with Next.js client router semantics; preserving non-remount navigation is necessary to keep in-memory thread runtime intact.
recommendations: Preserve and use thread-scoped settings providers/hooks (do not revert to global settings) when editing agent chat pages or layout.

When modifying agent creation flows, retain the explicit Save action and the hidden 'setup_agent' bootstrap save; keep read-after-write retry delays (AGENT_READ_RETRY_DELAYS_MS) and ensure API error classification (AgentNameCheckError) is respected.
Any changes touching streaming must preserve start/stop behavior, ensure completion notifications fire when page not focused, and avoid router operations that unmount the thread runtime.

Do not change the public props/contract of shared UI primitives (ChatBox, MessageList, InputBox, ThreadTitle, TokenUsageIndicator, TodoList, AgentWelcome) relied upon by the pages without coordinating consumer updates.
When altering navigation or history updates, verify that browser history is updated without a full route remount to avoid losing in-memory thread state; test in focused and background tab scenarios.

Run integration tests that cover: name validation (local + remote error mapping), createAgent + bootstrap thread + explicit save, read-after-write retrieval and retry behavior, streaming start/stop and background notifications, and token-usage indicator visibility in agent-scoped chats.

frontend/src/app/api/memory.description: This folder implements Next.js server route handlers that act as an HTTP proxy layer from the frontend to the backend "memory" endpoints. It exposes stable /api/memory/* endpoints to the UI and forwards incoming requests (including dynamic subpaths) to the backend memory service, preserving method, headers, body and status semantics. These proxy endpoints centralize URL building and request forwarding logic so the frontend does not need to know backend host details or direct routes.
key_behaviors: Resolve incoming frontend requests under /api/memory and dynamic /api/memory/* paths into corresponding backend memory URLs
Forward HTTP methods (GET, POST, DELETE, PATCH) and request bodies to the backend memory service
Preserve and forward relevant headers/cookies/authorization from the original request to backend, and propagate backend response status, headers and body back to the client
Centralize backend URL construction and proxy logic so both the root and dynamic routes use consistent mapping to memory endpoints
Return backend errors and status codes unchanged (proxy semantics) so the frontend receives native backend behavior

insights.title: Dynamic path mapping must preserve exact path segments and encoding
insights.problem: The dynamic route accepts arbitrary path segments. Incorrect joining or encoding could result in incorrect backend routes or resource mismatches.
insights.root_cause: Constructing backend URLs by naive string concatenation without proper encoding or normalization.
insights.solution: When modifying routing logic, ensure path segments are normalized and URL-encoded; maintain a single, tested URL-building utility used by both static and dynamic handlers.

insights.title: Auth and cookies must be forwarded for backend authorization
insights.problem: Frontend operations on memory endpoints often rely on session cookies or Authorization headers. If these are not forwarded, requests will fail or be treated as unauthenticated.
insights.root_cause: Proxy handlers may drop or alter headers/cookies when constructing outgoing requests.
insights.solution: Preserve Authorization headers and cookie headers from the incoming request when proxying; be mindful of SameSite/secure cookie behavior and environment differences (dev vs Docker vs production).

insights.title: Correct method/body handling and content-type preservation
insights.problem: POST/PATCH requests with JSON or multipart payloads need exact body forwarding and content-type headers; middleware or body-parsing by the route can change the payload.
insights.root_cause: Framework-level body parsing or reserialization can alter streams, change charsets, or strip required headers.
insights.solution: When editing handlers, avoid pre-parsing or reserializing request bodies unless necessary; forward content-type and use streaming/raw body forwarding to the backend to preserve semantics and large payload support.

insights.title: Maintain transparent error/status propagation
insights.problem: Transforming or masking backend error statuses and error bodies at the proxy layer can break frontend error handling and tests.
insights.root_cause: Proxy logic that maps all errors to generic responses or modifies status codes.
insights.solution: Proxy handlers should propagate backend HTTP status codes and error payloads unchanged, with only minimal wrapper metadata if required for logging. Keep tests that assert status propagation intact.

insights.title: Large or long-running requests need timeout and size handling
insights.problem: Proxy handlers may not enforce timeouts or streaming behavior, causing resource exhaustion or hanging requests for large memory operations.
insights.root_cause: Missing explicit timeout or streaming support and reliance on default runtime limits.
insights.solution: Be aware of environment-level timeouts and streaming behavior when changing handlers; preserve streaming-friendly patterns and avoid buffering large bodies in memory.

key_files.why: Primary proxy entry for /api/memory; contains backend URL builder and proxyRequest utility used to map root memory operations and to centralize forwarding logic.
Dynamic proxy handler for arbitrary subpaths under /api/memory/*; supports GET/POST/DELETE/PATCH and is responsible for mapping and forwarding variable path segments to backend memory endpoints.

construct_relationships: route.ts exposes buildBackendUrl and proxyRequest utilities that the folder's route handlers use to construct backend memory URLs and forward HTTP requests.

[...path]/route.ts implements handlers for multiple HTTP methods (GET, POST, DELETE, PATCH) and relies on the same proxy semantics and URL construction convention as the root route.ts to ensure consistent mapping.
Both route handlers must preserve headers/cookies and method/body semantics so that backend memory service behavior (auth, validation, error codes) is visible to the frontend without alteration.
notable_relationships: Upstream dependency: the backend memory service (Gateway/LangGraph memory endpoints). The proxy must construct backend URLs compatible with that service and honor its auth and API contract.

Deployment dependency: production reverse proxies (nginx) and Docker networking may change reachable backend hostnames; URL building likely depends on environment/config variables that differ between local dev and production.
Downstream dependency: frontend UI code that calls /api/memory/* expects preserved status codes and response shapes; tests and UI flows rely on transparent proxying.
Runtime constraints: Next.js server route runtime behavior (Node vs Edge) and framework body parsing semantics affect how bodies/streams and headers must be forwarded.
recommendations: When editing handlers, preserve HTTP methods, status codes, response headers and bodies exactly as received from the backend unless there is an explicit, tested reason to change them.
Ensure any URL construction handles path normalization and percent-encoding; do not assume simple string concatenation is safe for arbitrary user-supplied segments.
Always forward Authorization and Cookie headers from incoming requests to the backend; validate that changes do not strip these headers in dev/prod environments.
Avoid parsing and reserializing request bodies unless necessary; prefer streaming or raw forwarding to support large payloads and multipart content types.

Be mindful of runtime environment differences (local dev vs Docker vs production reverse proxy) — update or respect environment-based backend base URL configuration and run integration tests against the backend memory endpoints after changes.
Run frontend integration tests and any end-to-end tests that exercise memory endpoints to validate status propagation, auth behavior, and large payload handling after edits.

frontend/src/app/workspace/chats.description: This folder implements the chat workspace UI surfaces: a thread list page and a per-thread chat route composed of a layout that supplies workspace-level React contexts and a client-only chat page that mounts the runtime stream and browser-only controls. It is the glue between thread-scoped runtime hooks (streams, settings), workspace context providers (subtasks, artifacts, prompt input), and browser lifecycle concerns (hydration, notifications, navigation).
key_behaviors: Provide workspace-scoped React contexts (SubtasksProvider, ArtifactsProvider, PromptInputProvider) to all chat route children.
Render a per-thread chat UI that initializes and listens to a thread stream on client mount, reacts to run events, and cleans up on unmount.
Defer rendering of browser-dependent interactive elements (InputBox) until client mount to avoid server/client hydration mismatch.
Surface thread header controls (ThreadTitle, TokenUsageIndicator, export/artifact triggers) and trigger telemetry/navigation when threads are created or selected.
Show desktop notifications for run completion when the page is not focused and update history state for new threads.

insights.title: Avoid server/client hydration mismatches for browser-only components
insights.problem: Dynamic interactive components (InputBox) caused hydration mismatch overlays when rendered on the server and then different on the client.
insights.root_cause: Components that depend on browser APIs or client-only state were included in server-rendered markup, producing differing render output between server and client.
insights.solution: Defer rendering of browser-dependent components until after client mount (conditional render/useEffect based mount flag). Ensure server-rendered markup does not include client-only elements that change shape or behavior on mount.

insights.title: Respect thread-scoped settings over global defaults
insights.problem: Chat page previously used global settings and ignored per-thread overrides, causing mismatch between user expectations and UI behavior.
insights.root_cause: Thread-scoped settings were not read or applied when composing header and runtime behavior.
insights.solution: Read and apply thread-scoped settings within the thread page and header controls so UI and runtime actions reflect per-thread configuration.

insights.title: Run completion notifications require visibility checks
insights.problem: Users need to be notified when long-running runs finish while the page is not focused, but naive notification triggers may run at inappropriate times or without permission.
insights.root_cause: Run event handling does not check document visibility or Notification permission state prior to emitting desktop notifications.
insights.solution: Only dispatch desktop notifications for run completions when the document is hidden and when notification permission is granted; otherwise fall back to in-page indicators.

insights.title: Thread creation must update history without reload
insights.problem: Newly created threads need to replace current location so the URL and history reflect the new thread without adding duplicate history entries or causing reloads.
insights.root_cause: Thread creation flow requires client-side navigation that does not rely on a full page navigation.
insights.solution: Use history.replaceState or client-side navigation APIs to update the URL for newly created threads and ensure associated client state (stream hooks) re-initialize correctly.

key_files.why: Client-side per-thread chat page: initializes thread stream, manages lifecycle, header controls, defers InputBox, and handles notifications/history — primary place for thread-scoped runtime behavior.
Route layout that provides SubtasksProvider, ArtifactsProvider, and PromptInputProvider — must remain intact to supply context dependencies to the chat page and descendants.
Thread list page: entrypoint for selecting threads and linking to per-thread routes; also responsible for setting document.title for the chats list view.

construct_relationships: ChatLayout composes and provides SubtasksProvider, ArtifactsProvider, and PromptInputProvider which ChatPage and its children consume for workspace-scoped data and actions.
ChatPage uses useThreadStream (external hook) to initialize and listen to run events for the current thread; run events drive header indicators, todo list updates, and desktop notifications.
ChatPage defers rendering of InputBox until client mount (client-only render flag) to prevent server/client markup differences; InputBox interacts with PromptInputProvider supplied by layout.
Header controls in ChatPage (ThreadTitle, TokenUsageIndicator, export/artifact triggers) read thread settings and telemetry hooks to display per-thread state and report events.
ChatsPage lists threads and navigates to per-thread ChatPage routes; it must keep document.title and linking behavior compatible with client-side route transitions handled by ChatPage.
notable_relationships: useThreadStream and thread settings hooks are implemented outside this folder and depend on backend streaming/SSE APIs and thread-scoped persistence — changes here must respect their expected initialization/cleanup contract.

ArtifactsProvider interacts with artifacts storage and Gateway APIs; ChatPage header artifact triggers will surface artifacts created/stored by backend services, so UI changes can affect artifact lifecycle.

TokenUsageIndicator likely reads aggregate token usage data from shared state or APIs; integrating it into the header means changes can impact billing/telemetry visibility and must honor the underlying data contract.
Next.js routing and SSR/CSR boundary: these files must maintain explicit client-only behavior where needed (e.g., input, notifications) to avoid hydration and rendering issues with Turbopack/Next.
recommendations: Preserve the client-only deferment pattern for InputBox (render only after client mount) to avoid hydration mismatches; do not move browser-only API usage into server-rendered code.

When modifying ChatPage, ensure useThreadStream is initialized on mount and properly cleaned up on unmount; verify no duplicate stream subscriptions are created during history.replaceState navigation.
Respect per-thread settings: read and apply thread-scoped overrides for header controls and runtime behavior instead of falling back to global settings.
If adding or changing desktop notifications, check document.visibilityState and Notification.permission before showing notifications; ensure graceful fallback to in-page indicators.

After edits run the frontend build/typecheck/lint (pnpm build, pnpm typecheck, pnpm lint/format) and test the pages in dev mode to validate hydration behavior, especially with Turbopack/Next server/client rendering.

Be mindful that the layout's providers are required by child components — any change to provider shape or context value must be coordinated with consumers in this folder and with backend API contracts (artifacts, subtasks, prompt input).

backend/packages/harness/deerflow/commun.description: This directory contains community-contributed integrations: an AIO (agent-infra) sandbox client/provider/backends for Docker-isolated execution and a set of pluggable web/image search and fetch tools (DuckDuckGo, InfoQuest, Firecrawl, Jina, Tavily). It provides the runtime bridge between agent tooling and external services or isolated execution environments; correctness here affects safety of command execution, cross-process sandbox reuse, platform portability, and the shape/availability of search/fetch results returned to agents.
key_behaviors: Provision, discover, reuse, and evict containerized sandboxes with deterministic per-thread mapping and a warm pool (AioSandboxProvider + backends).
Execute shell commands and perform file operations against an HTTP-backed sandbox API with serialized execution, retries, and error normalization (AioSandbox client).
Expose LangChain-style @tool wrappers that call external web/search/image/fetch services, normalize responses and apply truncation/readability extraction for safe LLM consumption.
Provide HTTP client wrappers for external services (InfoQuest, Jina, Firecrawl, Tavily) that honor app configuration (timeouts, API keys) and convert provider responses into consistent tool payloads.
Surface community tools and sandbox components via package exports so the lead agent/tool registry can import them by name.

insights.title: Serialized exec_command to avoid corrupting persistent shell sessions
insights.problem: Concurrent exec_command calls to the same AIO sandbox corrupted a single persistent shell session and produced 'ErrorObservation' outputs that cascaded into failures.
insights.root_cause: The sandbox HTTP API backed by a single long-lived shell session could not safely handle concurrent command invocations without interleaving/serialization.
insights.solution: Serialize command execution and implement retry/error-normalization around ErrorObservation. When modifying sandbox client/provider code, preserve serialization and retry behavior to avoid session corruption.

insights.title: Platform-aware mount paths and file locking
insights.problem: Docker bind mounts and cross-process file locking behaved incorrectly on Windows: mount sources needed Windows-style host paths and file locking used POSIX-only fcntl semantics.
insights.root_cause: Path conversion assumed POSIX-style strings and locking assumed fcntl availability; operations attempted unlocking even when lock acquisition failed.
insights.solution: Use OS-aware path helpers for host-side mount sources and conditional locking/unlocking strategies that detect platform capabilities. Maintain conditional/unrolled code paths for Windows vs POSIX in provider code and avoid unconditional fcntl calls.

insights.title: Make network clients and tools asynchronous to avoid blocking agent concurrency
insights.problem: Synchronous HTTP clients blocked async flows; blocking web_fetch calls led to reduced concurrency and misleading downstream behavior when error strings were passed into extractors.
insights.root_cause: Clients (e.g., JinaClient) used synchronous network I/O while surrounding code expected async semantics.
insights.solution: Preserve async HTTP client usage (httpx.AsyncClient) and ensure tools expose async entry points or wrap blocking calls appropriately. When editing clients/tools, maintain async APIs and timeouts consistent with app config.

insights.title: Optional external dependencies and graceful degradation
insights.problem: Community tools rely on optional third-party libraries (ddgs, Firecrawl, Tavily). Missing dependencies or API keys must not raise unhandled exceptions in agent runs.
insights.root_cause: Tools directly import/assume optional libs or external credentials without runtime fallbacks.
insights.solution: Keep graceful error handling and structured fallback payloads when dependencies are absent or external services fail. Maintain consistent normalized JSON error/empty-result semantics so caller code can handle absence without exceptions.

insights.title: Stable normalized tool outputs and backward-compatible sandbox metadata
insights.problem: Agent code and tool chaining expect consistent output shapes (search result lists, image URL payloads) and persistent sandbox metadata for cross-process discovery.
insights.root_cause: Multiple providers and tools transform upstream provider responses differently; SandboxInfo persisted formats may evolve.
insights.solution: Preserve normalized response shapes and SandboxInfo to_dict/from_dict compatibility. When editing serialization, keep backward-compatible keys and conversion helpers.

key_files.why: HTTP-backed sandbox client that executes commands and file ops; its serialization, retry, and error-normalization behavior is central to safe remote sandbox use.

Manages sandbox lifecycle, deterministic thread sandbox assignment, warm-pool caching, cross-process locking and mount computation; changes here affect provisioning, reuse, eviction and platform behavior.
Local container backend that starts/adopts sandbox containers, handles mounts and port allocation; directly influences how sandboxes are realized on Docker/Apple Container runtimes.
HTTP client that normalizes search/fetch/image responses for InfoQuest; its header/auth/timeouts and result-cleaning behavior determine tool outputs consumed by agents.
Async wrapper for Jina crawl API; its async I/O behavior and error handling affect web_fetch tool responsiveness and concurrency.

construct_relationships: AioSandboxProvider composes a SandboxBackend implementation (LocalContainerBackend or RemoteSandboxBackend) to provision/destroy/discover sandboxes and returns SandboxInfo for cross-process discovery.
AioSandbox (client) communicates with the sandbox HTTP API exposed by backends; it relies on serialized execute_command and normalized error semantics to provide safe exec/file ops.
SandboxInfo is the serialization contract shared between provider, backends, and discovery logic (to_dict/from_dict used across processes).

InfoQuestClient, JinaClient, Firecrawl/Tavily clients are thin HTTP client layers used by corresponding tools modules (infoquest.tools, jina_ai.tools, firecrawl.tools, tavily.tools) to implement @tool functions consumed by the agent tool registry.

Tool modules (ddg_search, image_search, infoquest, firecrawl, tavily, jina_ai) expose LangChain-style tool callables that expect consistent normalized outputs and read configuration (API keys, timeouts, max results) from the application config.
notable_relationships: The community AioSandbox package is consumed by the Gateway/lead_agent sandbox middleware chain: SandboxMiddleware must map thread directories to provider mounts and relies on provider behavior for isolation and safety.

Sandbox provider/backends interact with host environment and container runtimes (Docker, Apple Container, remote provisioner) and therefore depend on environment variables, host path resolution, and OS semantics (notably Windows).
Community web/fetch tools depend on application configuration for API keys/timeouts and on optional third-party libraries; missing config or deps should not break the agent tool registry.

Async client choices (httpx.AsyncClient) affect the overall async event loop used by LangGraph/lead_agent; synchronous blocking in these modules can reduce concurrency and interfere with subagent execution.

SandboxInfo persistence and warm-pool semantics enable cross-process sandbox reuse — changes to serialization, naming, or discovery protocols can impact long-running workflows and tests that expect deterministic sandbox IDs.
recommendations: Preserve public package export names (module __all__ and tool exports) because the agent tool registry imports these by name.

When editing sandbox client/provider/backends, maintain serialized exec_command, retry/error normalization, and SandboxInfo serialization compatibility to avoid session corruption and cross-process discovery breaks.
Respect platform differences: use OS-aware path resolution for mount sources and conditional locking strategies that avoid POSIX-only fcntl calls on Windows.

Keep network clients asynchronous (httpx.AsyncClient) or surface async tool entry points; honor configured timeouts and propagate errors as structured error strings or normalized payloads, not raw exceptions.

Retain graceful handling for optional dependencies and absent API keys: tools should continue to return structured error/empty responses rather than raising, and tests should cover missing-dependency paths.

Run sandbox-related tests and integration scenarios under Docker (recommended) and validate warm-pool/eviction, mount behavior, and cross-process discovery; also run CI linting/formatting to satisfy repository invariants.

frontend/src/app/api/auth.description: This folder contains the Next.js catch-all API route that exposes the application's authentication endpoints under /api/auth/* by adapting requests to the better-auth handler. It is the single entry point in the frontend for sign-in, sign-out, callback, and session-related HTTP traffic and therefore central to the app's auth flows and cookie/session behavior.
key_behaviors: Accepts incoming HTTP requests under the /api/auth/* path (catch-all) and dispatches them to the better-auth handler.
Supports at least GET and POST methods and performs request/response adaptation between Next.js route semantics and the better-auth library via toNextJsHandler.
Acts as the canonical server-side endpoint for OAuth callbacks, session retrieval, and other auth lifecycle events used by client-side code and redirect flows.

insights.title: Single catch-all entry point for all auth endpoints
insights.problem: All authentication-related paths are routed through one catch-all handler. Any change here affects every auth flow (sign-in, sign-out, callback, session, etc.).
insights.root_cause: The Next.js [...all] route implementation funnels all subpaths under /api/auth/* to a single route module.
insights.solution: When modifying this file, treat it as a global auth surface — validate all auth flows (OAuth callbacks, redirects, session endpoints) and check that subpath forwarding semantics remain intact.

insights.title: Tight coupling to better-auth adapter and Next.js handler bridge
insights.problem: The route delegates to the better-auth handler using toNextJsHandler; mismatches in expected request/response shapes, method support, or adapter API changes can break authentication behavior.
insights.root_cause: The file translates Next.js HTTP objects to the better-auth adapter and relies on the adapter signature and behavior.
insights.solution: Ensure compatibility with the installed better-auth version and preserve the request/response adaptation contract (HTTP methods, headers, cookies, body). If the adapter API or toNextJsHandler semantics change, update this bridging logic accordingly and re-test end-to-end auth flows.

insights.title: Cookies, headers, and runtime environment implications
insights.problem: Authentication relies on precise cookie and header handling as well as the Next.js runtime (edge vs node). Improper handling or mismatched runtime assumptions can cause missing cookies, broken redirects, or insecure cookie flags.
insights.root_cause: Auth flows (OAuth redirects, session cookies, CSRF tokens) require consistent header/cookie propagation and may depend on runtime feature availability (e.g., Node-only APIs).
insights.solution: When editing, preserve cookie and header passthrough semantics and confirm the exported Next.js runtime matches better-auth expectations. Verify secure flags, SameSite, domain/path, and that redirects and callbacks function in the target deployment environment.

key_files.why: The single catch-all API route that adapts Next.js requests to the better-auth handler — the canonical surface for all frontend auth endpoints.

construct_relationships: The Next.js catch-all route ([...all]/route.ts) receives incoming GET/POST requests and delegates them to the better-auth handler via toNextJsHandler.
The route must correctly translate Next.js request/response objects, preserving headers, cookies, body, and HTTP method so that the better-auth library can perform OAuth flows and session management.
Because it is a catch-all, it implicitly handles all subpaths (e.g., /api/auth/signin, /api/auth/callback, /api/auth/session) and routes them through the same adapter logic.
notable_relationships: Client-side pages and components rely on the endpoints under /api/auth/* for sign-in, sign-out, session checks, and OAuth callbacks — changes here propagate to all client auth behavior.
The route depends on the better-auth library and its adapter (toNextJsHandler); upgrades or configuration changes to that package will affect this file.

Deployment/runtime choices (server Node vs Edge functions) and host environment (domain, HTTPS, cookie settings) influence how this route must behave — OAuth callback URLs and cookie semantics are environment-sensitive.
Other server routes, middleware, or rewrite rules (Next.js middleware, nginx rewrites) that touch /api or /api/auth can conflict with or alter the behavior of this catch-all route.
recommendations: Preserve the catch-all path behavior and ensure any edits continue to forward all subpaths to the auth handler.
Maintain correct HTTP method support (at least GET and POST) and preserve header/cookie/body passthrough semantics when adapting to better-auth.
Confirm compatibility with the installed better-auth package and toNextJsHandler signature before changing the adapter code.
Check and preserve Next.js runtime settings (edge vs node) expected by better-auth; verify behavior in the targeted deployment environment.

After making changes, run the frontend build (pnpm build) and exercise all auth flows (sign-in, sign-out, session retrieval, OAuth callback flows) in a running instance to validate cookies, redirects, and session persistence.
Be aware that changes here affect all client auth flows and may require coordinated updates to environment variables (OAuth callback URLs), reverse proxy rules, or backend auth expectations.

frontend/src/app/mock/api.description: This directory hosts Next.js API route handlers used as local mock implementations of backend endpoints for the frontend app. It provides deterministic responses for MCP config, available models, skills, thread operations (history, search), and thread artifacts so the UI can run and be tested without a live Gateway/LangGraph backend.
key_behaviors: Serve stable, deterministic JSON responses for UI components and tests (GET/POST handlers).
Expose dynamic route parameters for thread-scoped resources (thread_id, catch-all artifact_path) to emulate nested artifact retrieval.
Differentiate behavior by HTTP method (GET for resource reads; POST used to emulate queries/filters for history and search).
Act as a local substitute for upstream APIs during development and automated frontend tests.

insights.title: Mocks must match real Gateway response shapes
insights.problem: Frontend components and tests rely on specific JSON fields and structures; divergence will cause runtime UI errors or failing tests.
insights.root_cause: Mocks are separate code and may drift from backend schemas if updated independently.
insights.solution: Ensure mock responses replicate the real API payloads (field names, nested structures, status codes) and document any intentional deviations so edits preserve parity.

insights.title: Dynamic and catch-all route semantics are significant
insights.problem: Routes with dynamic params (thread_id) and catch-all segments (artifact_path) must preserve names and encoding behavior or callers will fail to resolve resources.
insights.root_cause: Next.js route filename conventions determine how request parameters are presented to handlers.
insights.solution: When modifying handlers or filenames, keep parameter names and catch-all versus optional-catch-all semantics intact and handle URL decoding/encoding consistently.

insights.title: Mocks are used by UI tests and local development
insights.problem: Changing mock responses, HTTP methods, or endpoint paths can break many tests and local developer workflows.
insights.root_cause: Tests and components assume fixed endpoints and behaviors provided by the mocks.
insights.solution: Any edits must preserve endpoint URLs, methods, and expected response semantics; update tests and test fixtures in lockstep if changing behavior.

insights.title: Artifact route may require binary/stream semantics or nested paths
insights.problem: Serving artifacts (files/videos) can involve non-JSON responses, range requests, or nested path resolution; naive changes risk incorrect content-type or path handling.
insights.root_cause: Artifact retrieval differs from simple JSON endpoints and relies on catch-all path processing and correct headers.
insights.solution: Maintain correct content-type, support nested paths and URL-encoded segments, and avoid introducing synchronous memory-heavy file reads that could degrade dev server performance.

insights.title: Mocks should not leak production behavior or secrets
insights.problem: Mock handlers could accidentally call real upstream services or require credentials, undermining isolation and reproducibility in tests.
insights.root_cause: Developers might add logic that reaches out to external APIs for data.
insights.solution: Keep mock handlers self-contained and deterministic; if external calls are necessary, gate them behind explicit flags and document dependency differences from production behavior.

key_files.why: Serves thread-scoped artifacts with optional nested paths — key for file/video preview flows and requires correct catch-all param handling.
Emulates POST-based thread history queries used by the UI to render past conversation/state; must match search/pagination shapes.
Simulates thread search functionality (POST) which many UI components rely on for listing and filtering threads.
Provides model list used by model selection UIs and feature gating — payload shape affects dropdowns and capability checks.
Exposes MCP server configuration used to drive UI behavior/configuration differences between environments.

construct_relationships: threads/* routes (history, search, artifacts) are logically grouped: search returns thread lists, history returns per-thread messages, artifacts maps to files referenced in history entries.
models/route.ts and skills/route.ts supply capability data consumed by UI components that also render thread content and tool availability.
mcp/config route provides environment/config flags that influence how frontend components interpret model/skill data returned by the other mock endpoints.
notable_relationships: These mock routes are a local substitute for backend Gateway/LangGraph APIs; production uses different endpoints (proxied via nginx) so path and response parity matters.
Frontend unit/integration tests and local developer workflows rely on these mocks to avoid depending on external model APIs or a running backend.
The artifacts route semantics must remain compatible with components that may expect streaming or binary responses; backend artifact storage behavior is upstream and may differ in content headers.
recommendations: Preserve HTTP methods, endpoint paths, and dynamic parameter names (thread_id, artifact_path) to maintain compatibility with tests and components.
When changing response shapes, update corresponding frontend tests and any shared TypeScript types; run the frontend test suite and build (pnpm test / pnpm build) after edits.
For the artifacts route, ensure correct handling of nested/catch-all segments and appropriate Content-Type/headers; validate with UI file/video preview behavior.
Do not add calls to real upstream services or require credentials in these mock handlers unless explicitly gated and documented; keep mocks deterministic.
Run linting and typechecks (pnpm lint/typecheck) and exercise dev server (pnpm dev) to ensure Next.js route handler changes behave as expected in hot-reload and production-build scenarios.

frontend/src/components/ai-elements.description: This folder contains reusable React UI primitives and composable building blocks used by the AI conversation and graph interfaces in the frontend. The components encapsulate input, messaging, attachments, streaming-aware displays, graph canvas primitives, and small feature UIs (model selector, context hovercard, suggestions) so higher-level pages can compose conversation UIs, flows, and artifact displays consistently.
key_behaviors: Collect and submit user prompts with optional attachments, drag/drop, paste and speech input while preventing accidental sends during IME composition.
Render chat messages, streaming assistant responses, branching alternatives, and attachments with navigation between branches and removal controls.
Provide composable UI primitives for graph/canvas editing (nodes, edges, connections, canvas defaults) and small interactive widgets (panels, controls, loaders, artifacts).
Expose provider/context patterns (PromptInputProvider, PlanContext, ContextContext) to share state (attachments, streaming flags, token/cost data) across multiple components.
Present ephemeral visual behaviors: staggered suggestion animations, shimmer placeholders during streaming, collapsible reasoning steps, and hovercards with computed model usage/cost.

insights.title: Respect IME composition state when submitting prompts
insights.problem: Enter key submission may fire while the user is in the middle of an IME composition, causing accidental or incomplete submissions.
insights.root_cause: Submit-on-Enter logic does not detect or gate on IME composition lifecycle (compositionstart/compositionend) across browser input events.
insights.solution: Guard Enter key submit behavior with composition state checks — treat composition-active states as non-submitting; developers must preserve or reapply this guard when touching PromptInput event handling.

insights.title: Prevent SpeechRecognition instance leaks and stale closures
