insights.problem: Speech recognition can create multiple instances or retain stale event callbacks, leading to duplicate transcripts, memory leaks, or callbacks referring to outdated refs/props.
insights.root_cause: Effects that initialize SpeechRecognition reference external props/refs without stable dependencies or correct cleanup; instances are not reliably stopped/removed on unmount or prop changes.
insights.solution: Ensure SpeechRecognition is created once per intended lifecycle, teardown (stop/remove handlers) on unmount/stop and avoid stale closures by wiring handlers to current refs/state; preserve these lifecycle semantics when editing or moving related code.

insights.title: Manage object URLs and attachment cleanup consistently
insights.problem: Attached files (images/generic) create object URLs that need revocation to avoid memory leaks and stale previews.
insights.root_cause: Multiple components accept attachments and manage preview URLs locally or via provider; missing or inconsistent revocation on removal/unmount leads to resource leakage.
insights.solution: When modifying attachment handling, respect the provider/local dual-mode, revoke created object URLs on removal/unmount, and maintain single source of truth for attachment lifecycle across PromptInput and MessageAttachment components.

insights.title: Search result bubbles and other inline chips can cause horizontal overflow
insights.problem: Collections of inline badges or suggestion chips may create horizontal overflow and break expected layout on narrow viewports.
insights.root_cause: Chip/bubble containers do not always constrain or wrap content; some components rely on horizontal scrolling rather than wrapping.
insights.solution: When altering ChainOfThoughtSearchResults, Suggestions or similar chip lists, preserve wrapping/overflow controls (e.g., clamp, wrapping or controlled scroll) to avoid layout breakage on small screens.

insights.title: Streaming flags must propagate consistently to child primitives
insights.problem: Streaming-aware shimmer placeholders and UI indicators must be synchronized across title/description and content areas; inconsistent propagation causes visual mismatch during streaming updates.
insights.root_cause: Streaming state is propagated via contexts (PlanContext, other streaming-aware contexts); changes to context shapes or provider usage can break child rendering assumptions.
insights.solution: Respect existing PlanContext and other streaming contexts when editing streaming UI components; maintain the expected context contract so children render shimmer placeholders correctly while content is streaming.

key_files.why: Primary prompt input component and provider - central to text, attachments, drag/drop, paste, IME and speech handling; changes here affect message submission and attachment lifecycle.
Core message rendering primitives (streaming, branching, attachments) used throughout chat interfaces; interacts with PromptInput attachments and streaming displays.
Code highlighting and copy-to-clipboard behavior used in assistant outputs; manages DOM injection for syntax HTML and clipboard interactions.
Collapsible reasoning UI with search result bubbles and state propagation — layout and overflow concerns originate here.
Suggestion chips and staggered animation primitives used for quick prompt templates; touches visual timing and scrollable chip containers.

construct_relationships: PromptInputProvider / LocalAttachmentsContext supply and coordinate attachment lists consumed by PromptInput and MessageAttachment components for preview and upload lifecycle.
PlanContext and other streaming contexts propagate streaming state from parent Plan/producer components to child pieces (PlanTitle, PlanDescription, shimmer placeholders).
Message components render streaming assistant content (Streamdown) and rely on CodeBlock for formatted code outputs and on Image/Artifact primitives for attachments.
Suggestions container supplies quick-prompt chips that act as input triggers for PromptInput (user clicks a Suggestion to populate the input).
ContextContext hovercards compute model token usage/cost and present info adjacent to conversation components that originate model calls (PromptInput submits → backend).
notable_relationships: Frontend prompt submission flows depend on backend Gateway/LangGraph APIs: PromptInput onSubmit ultimately triggers network flows and upload endpoints for attachments (object URL previews are client-only until uploaded).
Token/cost computations in Context components rely on tokenlens or other tokenization utilities; changes to ContextContext could affect displayed cost estimates across the UI.

Streaming UI behavior assumes a streaming-capable backend (SSE/websocket) and components (MessageResponse/Streamdown) expect incremental updates; breaking streaming contracts will affect many display primitives.
Graph/canvas primitives (Canvas, Node, Edge, Connection) fit into broader editor/flow UIs and may be consumed by pages relying on consistent ReactFlow/@xyflow/react behavior and styling conventions.
recommendations: When editing PromptInput or attachments: preserve IME composition gating for Enter submits; ensure object URLs are revoked on removal and on unmount to avoid leaks.

If modifying speech code paths: ensure SpeechRecognition instances are created/cleaned deterministically, handlers refer to current refs/state, and duplicate instances cannot persist across prop/effect changes.
When changing streaming or shimmer logic: maintain existing context contracts (PlanContext and other streaming contexts) so child components continue to render synchronized shimmer/stream states.
Any change that alters layout of chip/badge lists (Suggestions, ChainOfThought search results) must be validated across narrow viewports to prevent horizontal overflow; test with many items.

Run the frontend CI checks and local build/test: pnpm lint/format/typecheck and pnpm build; manually exercise interactive flows in a browser (IME composition, drag/drop/paste attachments, speech input, streaming responses) after edits.

frontend/src/components/ui.description: This folder contains the UI primitive components and small composed widgets used throughout the Next.js frontend. It centralizes accessible Radix wrappers, styling primitives (CVA-based variants), form and layout primitives, and several canvas/WebGL/animation-driven decorative components that underpin the app’s visual identity and interactive controls.
key_behaviors: Provide consistent, themed UI primitives (Button, Badge, Input, Card, Avatar) with centralized variant tokens to ensure uniform styling across the app.
Wrap and standardize Radix and other third-party primitives (Dialog, DropdownMenu, HoverCard, Avatar, Collapsible) to enforce project-level accessibility, animation and class conventions.
Compose higher-level interactions (ButtonGroup, InputGroup, Breadcrumb, CommandDialog) from primitives and propagate focus/keyboard/aria behavior.
Run browser-bound animations and visual effects (canvas/WebGL/gradient animations/confetti) with lifecycle management (resize, visibility, requestAnimationFrame) and performance guards.
Provide stateful interactive widgets (Carousel with embla, Command palette with cmdk) exposing keyboard navigation and programmatic controls.

insights.title: Browser-only APIs present in animated/interactive components
insights.problem: Components like Galaxy, FlickeringGrid, ConfettiButton and AuroraText use window, document, canvas and WebGL APIs that can break server-side rendering or static builds.
insights.root_cause: These components attach canvases, use requestAnimationFrame, ResizeObserver, IntersectionObserver, or rely on browser-only libs without guards.
insights.solution: Ensure DOM-bound initialization is gated behind runtime checks (e.g., typeof window !== 'undefined') and that cleanup/unmount paths remove observers/animation frames. Verify server builds don’t import side-effecting modules during SSR.

insights.title: Animation and observer lifecycle must be cleaned up
insights.problem: Long-running animations, RAF loops, ResizeObserver/IntersectionObserver, and WebGL contexts could leak or persist after unmount, causing memory and CPU issues.
insights.root_cause: Components create persistent browser resources (RAF, observers, GL contexts) without rigorous teardown.
insights.solution: When editing, respect existing cleanup logic: cancel RAFs, disconnect observers, and destroy GL contexts on unmount or when visibility changes. Changes that alter mounts must preserve teardown sequence.

insights.title: Centralized variant tokens (CVA) used across many primitives
insights.problem: Multiple components rely on cva-defined variant objects and shared class names; divergent edits can cause inconsistent appearance or broken polymorphism.
insights.root_cause: Styling is consolidated via class-variance-authority but is applied in many places; changing a variant shape or API affects all consumers.
insights.solution: Maintain stable variant prop names and class contracts. If altering variant shapes or naming, update all dependent components or usages and run visual regression checks.

insights.title: Radix wrappers expose ARIA and keyboard behavior
insights.problem: These components rely on Radix primitives to provide accessible semantics; changing markup or removing ARIA attributes could regress keyboard navigation or screen-reader behavior.
insights.root_cause: Wrappers add project classes but depend on underlying Radix semantics and specific DOM structure.
insights.solution: Preserve ARIA roles, tabIndex behavior and Radix structure. When adding props or altering structure, validate focus work-flows and screen-reader announcements.

insights.title: asChild / composition patterns are broadly used
insights.problem: Many components support 'asChild' or accept children elements to render as other tags; altering prop forwarding or className merge logic may break consumer compositions.
insights.root_cause: Polymorphic rendering relies on correct prop forwarding and class merging patterns.
insights.solution: Ensure edits keep prop forwarding semantics intact (including ref forwarding) and merge className/variant logic consistently so composed elements retain styling and behavior.

insights.title: External libs introduce build/runtime constraints
insights.problem: Components depend on external libraries (Radix, embla-carousel-react, cmdk, canvas-confetti, ogl) which can affect bundle size, SSR, and runtime availability.
insights.root_cause: Direct imports of browser-focused packages or heavy runtime libraries without lazy-loading or SSR guards.
insights.solution: When modifying imports or code paths, consider how/when these libraries are loaded at runtime and maintain existing import patterns (e.g., dynamic import guards) to avoid SSR/runtime failures.

key_files.why: Central button primitive providing sizes, variants and CVA tokens reused by many other components (ButtonGroup, ConfettiButton). Changes here affect global button appearance and behavior.
Project-level Dialog wrapper around Radix Dialog; used by higher-level widgets (CommandDialog) to supply consistent modal semantics, overlay and animation classes.
Embla-carousel integration that implements keyboard navigation, controls and stateful previous/next behavior — touches accessibility and complex third-party API usage.
Heavy WebGL background with shaders, per-frame uniform updates and mouse interaction; requires careful lifecycle and SSR handling.
Canvas-based animated background using ResizeObserver and IntersectionObserver; affects performance and visibility-driven animation behavior.

construct_relationships: Button (button.tsx) is the visual foundation used by ConfettiButton and grouped by ButtonGroup for layout and interaction consistency.
Dialog (dialog.tsx) is composed with CommandDialog (command.tsx) to present the command palette inside a modal overlay and relies on Radix Dialog primitives.
InputGroup (input-group.tsx) composes Input (input.tsx) and addon/button primitives to provide focus and click-to-focus behaviors.
Many UI wrappers (Dialog, DropdownMenu, HoverCard, Collapsible, Avatar) wrap Radix primitives to standardize classes, animation, and accessibility behaviors across components.
Carousel (carousel.tsx) integrates embla-carousel-react and exposes context and control components consumed by higher-level lists of CarouselItem components.

Animated/decorative components (Galaxy, FlickeringGrid, AuroraText, ConfettiButton) share common lifecycle concerns (requestAnimationFrame, observers, DOM bounds) and must coordinate cleanup semantics.
notable_relationships: Frontend components rely on global Tailwind/utility CSS and classnames produced by class-variance-authority; changes to variant tokens or global CSS may ripple across the app.

Several components import browser-only third-party libraries (ogl, canvas-confetti, embla-react, cmdk, @radix-ui/*) — these create SSR constraints and may require dynamic import or runtime guards when rendered in Next.js.

UI primitives are consumed across the frontend application and by storybook/visual tests; changing exported prop names, component APIs, or class contracts will require coordinated updates in pages, tests and stories.

Animation-heavy components depend on browser APIs (ResizeObserver, IntersectionObserver, WebGL contexts) that may behave differently in Docker/CI vs local browser; runtime assumptions must be preserved during edits.
recommendations: Guard any DOM/window usage with runtime checks to avoid SSR failures (typeof window !== 'undefined') and prefer lazy/dynamic imports for heavy browser-only libs.
When touching components that create observers, RAF loops or GL contexts, verify unmount cleanup and test in-browser to catch leaks or persisting CPU usage.
Preserve CVA variant prop names, class merging and ref forwarding semantics so polymorphic/asChild usage and global styling remain stable.
Validate accessibility (keyboard navigation, ARIA roles, focus management) after edits to Radix wrappers and composed controls; run keyboard navigation and screen-reader smoke tests where possible.

Run frontend build, lint, typecheck and the component visual/regression checks (pnpm build, pnpm lint, pnpm test / storybook snapshots) after changes — and test pages that use the edited primitives to detect styling regressions.

frontend/src/components/workspace.description: This folder contains the workspace-facing React UI: chat area, artifacts panel, message rendering and related small surfaces (command palette, settings, welcome, agent gallery). It centralizes user interactions for threads: rendering messages (including chain-of-thought and tool calls), presenting and previewing artifacts (code, markdown, HTML, remote resources), resizable artifacts sidebar and artifact lifecycle actions (download, copy, install). These components coordinate closely with thread context, artifact state/provider, and backend endpoints (artifact URLs, skill install, exports), so changes here have wide UI and API interaction impact.
key_behaviors: Render threaded conversation: group messages, render single message items, handle streaming state, images, files and chain-of-thought steps.
Present artifacts: list artifacts for a thread, allow selection, preview (code editor, markdown, iframe, external URL), download/copy, and initiate .skill installs.
Coordinate workspace layout: resizable chat/artifacts panel whose layout is persisted per-route and must preserve stable IDs across navigation.
Client-only interactions and keyboard UX: global command palette and platform-aware shortcuts registered on mount; settings and welcome flows that rely on client-only APIs.
Subtask/subagent UI: detect 'task' tool calls and render SubtaskCards with status, prompt and markdowned results.

insights.title: Avoid browser-only globals during SSR
insights.problem: Several components (e.g., CommandPalette) used navigator/userAgent or other browser-only APIs during render, causing server/client markup mismatches.
insights.root_cause: Client-only assumptions were used inside components that may be rendered on the server; hooks/values that inspect the environment were not gated by a mounted check.
insights.solution: Ensure browser globals are only accessed after mounting or mark components as client components; prefer mount gating for environment-dependent logic to avoid SSR mismatches.

insights.title: Differentiate write-file virtual artifacts vs remote-served artifacts
insights.problem: Non-write artifacts served remotely cannot be previewed by content-only loaders; some previews require a backend-provided artifact URL (iframe preview, external resources).
insights.root_cause: Artifact loaders returned raw text for local/write artifacts but remote/static artifacts need URL-based preview; UI implicitly relied on content presence for all previews.
insights.solution: Preserve and use artifact metadata (isWriteFile vs remote) and backend URLs when choosing preview mode; changes must respect the virtual path vs remote-served artifact distinction.

insights.title: Copied content must be consistent with in-UI display (citations)
insights.problem: Copying message or artifact content sometimes included citation blocks or external-link badges, producing noisy clipboard text.
insights.root_cause: Copy operations used raw message payloads without the same citation-stripping logic used for on-screen rendering.
insights.solution: Ensure clipboard/copy handlers apply the same citation-stripping/normalization rules as the display layer so copied text matches user expectation.

insights.title: Tool call result UI can overflow or lack affordance explanations
insights.problem: Large tool result content overflowed tooltips and clickable paths lacked tooltip explanations, reducing usability and causing layout issues.
insights.root_cause: Tool result rendering didn't constrain or wrap long content in compact affordances like tooltips and path badges.
insights.solution: When rendering tool-call summaries in compact UI (tooltips, badges), ensure wrapping/truncation and provide a clear hover/tooltip explaining the action and full value without breaking layout.

insights.title: Shared UI helpers have shifting signatures
insights.problem: Helper signature changes (e.g., getFileIcon now accepts className) required updating multiple callers.
insights.root_cause: Shared utilities used by many components changed shape without centralized compatibility shims.
insights.solution: When updating shared utility signatures, update all consuming components and guard callsites where necessary; treat these helpers as cross-cutting changes that affect artifact list/detail and other UI consumers.

key_files.why: Primary preview/detail pane: decides preview mode (code, markdown, iframe, remote URL), handles copy/download/open-in-new-tab and skill-install actions — central for artifact UX and backend artifact URL usage.
Artifacts provider and context: stores artifact list, selection state, open/close behavior and integrates with sidebar — edits here affect selection lifecycle and panel visibility across workspace.

Message grouping and render dispatch: transforms thread messages into grouped UIs and delegates to ArtifactFileList/SubtaskCard/MessageListItem; changing it will change which specialized UI is rendered for tool calls and artifacts.
Global command palette and keyboard shortcuts registration: affects global keyboard-driven flows (new chat, open settings, show shortcuts) and must be SSR-safe and platform-aware.
Chat area with resizable artifacts sidebar: orchestrates artifact selection across thread switches and persists resizable layout; critical for layout stability and artifact panel interactions.

construct_relationships: ArtifactsProvider (artifacts/context.tsx) supplies artifact list and selection state consumed by ArtifactFileList and ArtifactFileDetail for listing and preview operations.
MessageList (messages/message-list.tsx) groups threaded messages and passes artifact filenames to ArtifactFileList when a present-files group is rendered.
MessageListItem and MarkdownContent convert 'citation:' anchors into CitationLink and ArtifactLink components so CitationLink's hover/visit behavior is used across markdown and message rendering.
SubtaskCard is instantiated from MessageList for tool calls of type 'task' and consumes message-derived markdown for rendering subtask results and status.
ChatBox composes MessageList and the artifacts sidebar (ArtifactFileList + ArtifactFileDetail) and coordinates selection, resizable-panel IDs and per-route persistence for the workspace layout.
notable_relationships: Frontend artifact preview and install flows depend on backend-provided artifact URLs and install/export APIs — UI must handle absent or demo/static deployments which may disable install/export functionality.
Markdown and citation handling integrates with shared MessageResponse/Streamdown renderers (client-only) and must honor 'use client' constraints; server rendering must avoid these components.
Global keyboard shortcuts and command palette behavior affect navigation and settings dialog flows and rely on app-level routing and dialog components outside this folder.
Resizable panel layout persistence relies on stable IDs that correlate with route-aware keys; route changes elsewhere in app can affect persisted layout restoration.
Shared utility helpers (icons, getFileIcon, exporters) are used across components here; signature changes upstream require coordinated updates in this subtree.
recommendations: Preserve 'use client' markers and gate browser-global access behind mount checks to avoid SSR mismatches (run Next.js build to validate).

When modifying artifact preview logic, maintain the distinction between write-file (virtual/local) artifacts and remote/URL-backed artifacts; ensure preview selection uses artifact metadata and backend URLs where required.
Keep copy/download handlers aligned with on-screen rendering: apply the same citation-stripping/normalization so clipboard content matches displayed content.
If updating shared helpers (icons, getFileIcon) or render utilities, update all callsites in this folder; run frontend typecheck/lint/build to catch signature mismatches.
When changing resizable panel behavior or IDs, preserve stable identifiers per-route so layout persistence and restore continue to work across navigation.
Ensure components that render tool-call summaries or path badges implement wrapping/truncation/tooltip behaviors to avoid overflow and improve discoverability.
Respect environment/demo flags that disable server-backed flows (skill install, exports): verify UI shows appropriate tooltips/errors rather than triggering failing API calls.

Run the frontend build, lint, and TypeScript checks after edits and exercise: message rendering (including citation links), artifact preview/download/open, .skill install flow in both demo and normal modes, command palette keyboard shortcuts (mount gating), and resizable panel persistence across routes.

frontend/src/components/landing.description: This folder contains the React UI fragments that compose the public landing page: header/navigation, hero/CTA, multiple content sections (case studies, community, whats-new, sandbox, skills) plus the footer and an interactive demo. These components are primarily presentation-focused but include important runtime responsibilities (server-side header with optional API integration, client-side animations and autoplay behavior, links into the app and external repositories) that affect build/runtime behavior, routing, and assets.
key_behaviors: Render a fixed header with navigation and optional live GitHub star count (server-side component with environment-guarded data fetch).
Display a hero area with animated backgrounds, rotating headline words, an inline SVG badge, and a CTA that navigates to the workspace route.
Provide several section wrappers and content grids (whats-new, case studies, community, sandbox, skills) that compose the landing layout using shared UI primitives.
Run an interactive, multi-phase ProgressiveSkillsAnimation that auto-plays when scrolled into view, exposes play/pause controls, and incrementally updates a simulated file tree and chat timeline.
Render a simple footer that computes and shows the current year and license/copyright.

insights.title: Header performs server-side data access while other components rely on client-side runtime features
insights.problem: Mixing server-side fetching (header star count) and client-only browser APIs (animation autoplay, intersection observers) can introduce SSR/hydration issues if boundaries are not respected.
insights.root_cause: Some components are implemented to run during SSR (header) while others require browser APIs (progressive animation, hero effects), which must be designated as client components and avoid importing server-only modules.
insights.solution: Maintain explicit server/client component boundaries (keep header as server component and ensure any component using window/document or IntersectionObserver is a client component); guard any data fetch behind environment checks to avoid errors during static/SSR builds.

insights.title: Optional GitHub star fetch and environment gating
insights.problem: Header attempts to fetch GitHub star counts only when permitted by the runtime environment; unguarded fetches could fail during build or in constrained environments.
insights.root_cause: The star-count logic depends on runtime environment variables and/or outbound network access which may be unavailable in some deployment/build contexts.
insights.solution: Preserve the environment guard around star fetches; when editing header behaviour, ensure fetch code is conditional and fails safe (render without count) in builds or restricted runtimes.

insights.title: Progressive demo manages timed phases and auto-play on viewport entry
insights.problem: The animated demo drives staged UI updates and relies on timers and viewport observation which can lead to unexpected CPU usage or inconsistent state across mounts/hydration if uncontrolled.
insights.root_cause: The component uses timeouts/timers and intersection observation to auto-play; if lifecycle or cleanup is wrong, timers may leak or run during SSR/hydration.
insights.solution: Ensure the animation is strictly client-only, that timers and observers are cleaned up on unmount, and that state transitions are deterministic across remounts; when changing timing constants or autoplay triggers, consider their effect on CPU and perceived responsiveness.

insights.title: Multiple sections depend on common layout and UI primitives
insights.problem: Changes to Section/Button/MagicBento/AuroraText APIs or class names will have broad impact across landing sections.
insights.root_cause: Sections are thin wrappers that compose shared primitives for consistent styling and spacing.
insights.solution: When editing props, class names, or expected children shapes, verify all section consumers build and render correctly; update dependent imports or usages together to avoid type or runtime errors.

insights.title: Internal CTA and case-study links navigate to app routes while CTA/community links point to external GitHub
insights.problem: Link targets must respect Next.js routing expectations and use correct attributes (new tab behavior, rel attributes) to avoid security or navigation regressions.
insights.root_cause: Components open internal routes (workspace, threads) and external links (GitHub) with different navigation semantics.
insights.solution: Preserve link targets, ensure internal navigation uses the app's router/link primitives where required, and external links open in a new tab with rel="noopener noreferrer" to avoid security/hydration issues.

key_files.why: Entry for site navigation; server-side component that fetches GitHub star count and sets up header links—changes here affect SSR and environment-dependent behavior.
Prominent hero with animated background and CTA to workspace; changes affect first-impression visuals and internal navigation.
Complex client-side animation/demo that manages timed phases, autoplay on viewport entry, and simulates file/chat state—high risk for lifecycle/timer issues.
Shared section wrapper used by many landing subsections; modifications will propagate layout/typography changes widely.
Simple footer computing runtime year and license—affects copyright/license visibility and minimal runtime computation.

construct_relationships: Section is composed by WhatsNewSection, CommunitySection, SandboxSection, SkillsSection and CaseStudySection to provide consistent titles and spacing.
Hero contains a CTA which links to the workspace route; that navigation must align with app routing conventions.

ProgressiveSkillsAnimation controls internal FileItem and getFileTree constructs to update a simulated file tree and chat timeline over timed phases; it also exposes play/pause UI used by the landing page.
Header includes StarCounter logic that conditionally fetches GitHub repository stars based on environment/config; it also composes navigation links used across the landing sections.

CommunitySection and CaseStudySection produce external and internal links respectively (GitHub external CTA vs case-study links opening mock thread pages), relying on consistent link attributes and routing.
notable_relationships: All landing sections depend on shared UI primitives located elsewhere (Section, Button, MagicBento, AuroraText) — changes to those primitives will cascade into this folder.
Assets referenced by hero, case-study backgrounds, and badges likely live in public/static assets; edits must preserve correct import/asset paths expected by Next.js.
Header's optional runtime GitHub fetch depends on environment variables and network access; build or deployment environments without outbound access must still produce a valid server render.
Interactive client components (progressive animation, hero effects) depend on browser APIs (IntersectionObserver, requestAnimationFrame, window) and therefore must not be executed during SSR.
Internal navigation targets (workspace route, thread pages) are upstream/downstream concerns: changing those routes requires coordinating with the app router and pages that consume them.
recommendations: Respect server/client component boundaries: keep server-only logic (header star fetch, i18n resolution) in server components and mark any component using window/document or IntersectionObserver as client-side.
When editing header star-fetch logic, preserve environment guards so builds and restricted runtimes render safely without outbound network access.
For animation components, ensure timers and observers are registered only on the client, and are properly cleaned up on unmount to avoid leaks and inconsistent state.
If you change Section/Button/MagicBento/AuroraText interfaces or class names, update all consumers in this folder to avoid layout or type errors.

Run the frontend build and validation steps after edits: pnpm build (Next.js build), pnpm lint, and pnpm typecheck to catch SSR/hydration and type issues; also test client-side behaviors in a browser (animations, autoplay, navigation, external links opening in new tabs).
Preserve link semantics: internal navigation should use the app's router/Link primitives while external links should include target="_blank" and rel="noopener noreferrer".
Verify asset paths and image imports resolve at build time (Next.js public/static rules) to avoid broken visuals on the landing page.

backend/packages/harness/deerflow.description: The harness/deerflow package provides the in-process DeerFlow runtime surface: agent assembly (lead agent factory and middleware), prompt composition with a cached enabled-skills section, memory pipeline (queue + updater + storage), sandbox-local path mapping and sandbox-aware tools, checkpointer factories, and an embedded Python client that constructs and drives an in-process agent. These components glue config, sandbox policy, tools, subagents and memory together so edits here affect runtime behavior, safety boundaries, and in-process integration tests and clients.
key_behaviors: Assemble a fully-wired lead agent at runtime: resolve model -> build system prompt (with enabled-skills cache) -> attach ordered middleware chain -> expose sandbox-aware tools and subagent delegation.
Maintain and serve a background-cached skills prompt section for system prompt injection (non-blocking refresh + synchronous warm/invalidate helpers).
Filter and queue conversation context for debounced long-term memory extraction, dedupe/merge facts, and persist via pluggable storage while preserving correction/reinforcement signals.
Translate container-style virtual paths to host paths (and mask host paths back) and enforce read-only mounts for local sandboxed filesystem/command operations used by built-in tools.

Provide an embedded DeerFlowClient that lazily constructs and caches the in-process agent, streams run events, supports file uploads and memory import/export, and delegates long-running work to background subagents (task tool).

insights.title: Model alias resolution must be consistent when creating middleware and models
insights.problem: Summarization and other middleware were constructed using raw config model name strings instead of the resolved/aliased model object, producing incorrect model behaviour in middleware calls.
insights.root_cause: Middleware factories used the config.model_name string directly and bypassed the same model factory path used for runtime model resolution.
insights.solution: Always resolve model aliases through the same create_model/create_chat_model factory before passing a model to middleware; ensure factories share resolution logic so middleware and runtime model semantics match.

insights.title: Enabled-skills prompt composition must be background-cached and invalidatable
insights.problem: Loading enabled skills from disk during prompt build was blocking and caused slow imports/startup; repeated rebuilds produced unnecessary I/O and stale content when skills changed.
insights.root_cause: Prompt builder reloaded skills synchronously and exposed no background refresh or deterministic invalidation, and import-time warm runs could cause filesystem I/O during module import.
insights.solution: Keep a non-blocking background refresh worker with synchronous warm/invalidate helpers and memoization keys/signatures; preserve thread-safe accessors so prompt construction never performs heavy disk I/O on a request path.

insights.title: Facts must be deduplicated case-insensitively and preserve correction/reinforcement metadata
insights.problem: Memory storage could contain duplicate facts that differed only by case and memory extraction/updater lost correction and reinforcement signals and sourceError metadata.
insights.root_cause: Deduplication used case-sensitive keys and the pipeline did not propagate correction/reinforcement flags or sourceError through queue/updater.
insights.solution: Normalize keys casefold for dedupe, propagate correction_detected and reinforcement_detected flags through the queue to the updater, and preserve sourceError in incoming facts so downstream logic can act on provenance/corrections.

insights.title: Tool-call loop detection needs stable, order-independent hashing and robust argument normalization
insights.problem: Repetitive tool-call loop detection missed semantically equivalent calls due to noisy argument representations and could crash when messages had non-str content.
insights.root_cause: Argument normalization and key derivation were brittle: stringified JSON, non-dict args, and list-type AIMessage.content produced inconsistent keys or TypeErrors.
insights.solution: Harden normalization to produce stable, order-independent keys (normalize structures, coerce types, handle list/structured message content), and guard concatenation/append operations against AIMessage.content being non-string.

insights.title: Uploads middleware must tolerate missing runtime.context and absent thread_id
insights.problem: Runtime paths and uploads directory resolution failed when runtime.context was None or thread_id was provided in a different place, causing NPEs and missing uploads metadata injection.
insights.root_cause: Code assumed runtime.context and runtime.context.thread_id always present; different invocation paths (LangGraph modes) sometimes pass None or move thread_id into config.
insights.solution: Null-check runtime.context, fall back to config.configurable or other runtime sources for thread_id, and avoid throwing when context is absent so uploads injection gracefully degrades.

insights.title: Local sandbox must preserve virtual path form and enforce read-only mounts with reverse masking
insights.problem: Host paths leaked in outputs and path replacements could drop trailing separators or mix host/virtual separators (Windows), and multiple custom mounts or read-only semantics were unsupported.
insights.root_cause: Path mapping/replacement logic did not preserve trailing separators or separator styles and only supported simple single-mount mapping; reads/writes were not always enforcing read-only flags.
insights.solution: Map container↔host paths bidirectionally with preservation of trailing separators and separator style, support multiple mounts with per-mount read-only flags, and mask host paths in outputs to present the container view.

insights.title: Tool helpers must avoid leaking host layout, truncate large outputs and guard runtime.context writes
insights.problem: Tool outputs could expose multi-MB ls results or host filesystem separators; writers assumed runtime.context non-null and performed unchecked writes.
insights.root_cause: No output truncation policies and replace_virtual_path logic could alter separators; sandbox_from_runtime() wrote into runtime.context without null checks.
insights.solution: Enforce truncation limits on listing outputs, preserve virtual path separators when replacing paths, and null-check runtime.context before mutation to avoid TypeErrors in lazy-init runtimes.

insights.title: Background subagent execution must support cooperative cancellation and runtime thread_id fallbacks
insights.problem: When polling coroutines were cancelled, background subagent threads continued running; task creation failed when thread_id was missing from runtime.context.
insights.root_cause: Polling loop cancellation did not request cooperative cancellation of the SubagentExecutor, and task_tool assumed thread_id always found in runtime.context.
insights.solution: On asyncio.CancelledError request cooperative cancellation and schedule cleanup; fallback to config.configurable (or other runtime sources) for thread_id when runtime.context lacks it.

insights.title: Async checkpointer setup must avoid synchronous filesystem ops on the event loop
insights.problem: Sqlite parent directory creation was performed synchronously on the event loop, risking loop blocking.
insights.root_cause: Filesystem mkdir operations lived directly in async initialization paths instead of a background/safe context.
insights.solution: Move blocking filesystem helpers off the event loop (run in executor) and consolidate repeated sqlite helper logic into shared runtime/store utilities to avoid duplication and blocking.

insights.title: Embedded client must forward custom stream events and allow runtime skill filtering and memory import/export
insights.problem: DeerFlowClient.stream previously dropped custom stream-mode events and had no API to pass available_skills or import/export memory programmatically.
insights.root_cause: Client assumed a narrow agent.stream contract and lacked runtime filtering hooks and helpers for memory persistence operations.
insights.solution: Ensure stream forwards agent-stream events verbatim (including custom events), accept available_skills for runtime filtering when constructing the in-process agent, and expose import/export memory helpers for test/integration scenarios.

key_files.why: Central factory that resolves runtime model, constructs summarization/todo middlewares, assembles middleware chain and creates the lead agent used by runs.
Builds the system prompt with the enabled-skills section and manages the background cache used by make_lead_agent; edits here affect prompt content and startup I/O behavior.
Implements memory extraction/validation and persistence logic; coordinates with queue/storage and must preserve dedupe, correction/reinforcement and provenance semantics.
Implements container-style→host path mapping, read-only mount enforcement and masking of host paths; core to sandboxed file/command behavior and tool outputs.
Embedded in-process client used by tests and integrations; lazily builds and caches the agent, streams events, and exposes upload/memory convenience APIs.

construct_relationships: make_lead_agent (agents/lead_agent/agent.py) calls into prompt builder (agents/lead_agent/prompt.py) to obtain the system prompt and enabled-skills section before assembling the agent.

The middleware chain assembled by make_lead_agent includes UploadsMiddleware, SandboxMiddleware, MemoryMiddleware and LoopDetectionMiddleware; these middleware components communicate via per-run ThreadState/RuntimeState and expect runtime.context/thread_id and sandbox mounts to be present.

MemoryMiddleware filters conversation and enqueues ConversationContext into MemoryUpdateQueue; MemoryUpdateQueue debounces and forwards batched contexts to MemoryUpdater which persists via configured MemoryStorage (updater.py uses get_memory_storage abstraction).

LocalSandbox (sandbox/local/local_sandbox.py) exposes path translation functions used by sandbox tools (sandbox/tools.py) and built-in tools (tools/*) to validate paths, replace virtual paths and mask host paths in outputs.

task_tool (tools/builtins/task_tool.py) constructs and starts SubagentExecutor (subagents/executor) using runtime metadata (thread_id, available_skills, toolset) and emits lifecycle stream events consumed by the client/runtime stream path.
notable_relationships: This package depends on the config subsystem (config/app_config.py) for deterministic config discovery and runtime overrides; edits here must respect config singleton/reload semantics and cwd sensitivity.

Agents and middleware expect sandboxes and tools provided by the sandbox and tools subtrees; LocalSandbox and sandbox tools must remain aligned so virtual↔host path mappings and read-only policies are consistent.

Checkpointer factories touch persistent backends (sqlite/postgres) and can perform filesystem operations during initialization; async provider must not block the event loop and share helper logic with sync provider.

DeerFlowClient is used by tests and integrations as an alternative to the LangGraph server; changes to agent construction, middleware ordering, or available skill filtering affect both the embedded client and server-run agents.

Subagent and task lifecycle interacts with the runtime event streaming subsystem (runtime/StreamBridge) and background executors; cooperative cancellation, polling intervals and cleanup semantics affect resource leaks and test determinism.
recommendations: Preserve middleware ordering and anchoring semantics when modifying agent construction; test runs that exercise the full middleware chain (uploads → sandbox → memory → loop detection → summarization) must pass.
Avoid blocking filesystem I/O on async event loops: any mkdir/read that can block must be moved to an executor or performed in background threads; run async tests and check for event-loop warnings.

Guard all code paths against runtime.context being None and thread_id being absent; fall back to config.configurable or explicit parameters where appropriate so middleware and tools degrade gracefully.

When changing path-replacement logic, preserve virtual path separators and trailing slashes, and ensure reverse-mapping masks host paths in all tool outputs; run sandbox + tool tests (ls/grep/read/write) cross-platform.

Maintain propagation of memory flags (correction_detected, reinforcement_detected) through MemoryUpdateQueue -> MemoryUpdater -> storage; run memory unit tests that assert flag preservation and deduplication (casefold).

Ensure cooperative cancellation requests to SubagentExecutor when polling coroutines are cancelled and schedule deferred cleanup of background tasks; run subagent lifecycle tests including cancellation scenarios.
Replace any remaining print() diagnostic calls with logging at appropriate levels to avoid noisy test output and ensure logs are captured in CI.

Run the backend test suite (cd backend && make test or uv run pytest) and relevant integration tests (client in-process agent flows, sandbox operations, memory queue/updates, subagent tasks) after edits, and validate CI linting and formatting steps.

frontend/src/core/i18n.description: This folder implements the frontend internationalization (i18n) surface: typed translation schemas, concrete locale objects (en-US, zh-CN), runtime locale utilities, cookie-based persistence, and a React context + hook that provide translations and locale switching to the app. It is critical for correct UI text rendering, build-time type safety, and consistent locale behavior across client and server (SSR) codepaths.
key_behaviors: Detect active locale from environment: client cookies, server request cookies, or browser/preferences and normalize to supported locales.
Expose translations to UI via a React context provider and a useI18n hook so components can read localized strings and current locale state.
Persist and read locale selection through cookie utilities that have both client- and server-side variants.
Enforce a TypeScript Translations shape across locale modules; translation objects are registered in a translations map used by the provider and server utilities.
Keep server-side helpers aligned with client-side utilities to support SSR and initial render localization (detectLocaleServer / getI18n).

insights.title: Translations TS type must match locale objects
insights.problem: New UI strings (token usage, shortcuts, memory import/export, network error messages, suggestions, skill texts) were added but the Translations type did not include them, causing build/type errors and potential runtime omissions.
insights.root_cause: Translation type definition (locales/types.ts) lagged behind the evolving UI surface; locale modules (en-US/zh-CN) and the translations map can diverge from the Translations schema.
insights.solution: Ensure the Translations type is the authoritative schema and that every locale object conforms to it. When adding UI strings, update types first (or in lockstep) and add entries in every supported locale to avoid type errors and missing UI text.

insights.title: Locale objects must include new UI strings in all supported locales
insights.problem: English additions for network errors, memory import/export, token usage, and shortcuts lacked corresponding Chinese translations (and vice versa), resulting in untranslated UI or runtime gaps.
insights.root_cause: Feature development introduced new UI text without simultaneous localization updates for all supported locales.
insights.solution: Treat locale files as first-class artifacts: every new UI label/key must be added to every supported locale file and the central translations map. Omissions lead to inconsistent user experience and possible runtime fallbacks.

insights.title: Locale detection and cookie handling must be consistent across client and server
insights.problem: Client-side cookie utilities and server-side detection functions are separate constructs (cookies.ts vs server.ts); mismatches in cookie names, normalization logic, or supported-locale sets can produce different active locales between SSR and client hydration.
insights.root_cause: Separate implementations for client and server behaviors that reference the same conceptual data (locale name) but can drift independently.
insights.solution: Keep cookie names, normalization, supported-locale constants (DEFAULT_LOCALE, SUPPORTED_LOCALES), and detection logic synchronized across cookies.ts, server.ts, and locale utilities. Changes to locale normalization or cookie semantics must be applied consistently to avoid hydration mismatches.

insights.title: Provider/hook rely on translations map and locale utilities
insights.problem: I18nContext/I18nProvider, the useI18n hook, translations registration, and locale utilities are tightly coupled; inconsistent exports or renamed keys in translations.ts/locales/index.ts can break consumer code.
insights.root_cause: Multiple entry points and indirections (translations map, locale constants, provider, hook) create fragile coupling across files.
insights.solution: Maintain stable exports and names for the translations map, DEFAULT_LOCALE, SUPPORTED_LOCALES, and normalization/detection functions. When editing any of these artifacts, ensure the provider and hook still import expected identifiers and that translation keys remain available to consumers.

key_files.why: Defines I18nContext and the provider that wires translations, locale state, and change propagation to the React tree — the runtime entry point for frontend components.
Exposes the useI18n hook used by components to read translated strings and change locale; depends on context and translation shape.
Declares the Translations TypeScript interface/schema that all locale modules must conform to; a primary source of build-time checks.
Contains SUPPORTED_LOCALES, DEFAULT_LOCALE, normalization and detection helpers used by both client and server code to canonicalize and validate locales.
Implements server-side locale detection/getI18n for SSR paths and must align with client cookie utilities and the provider so initial renders use the same locale.

construct_relationships: I18nProvider (context.tsx) reads the translations map (translations.ts) and uses locale utilities from locale.ts to select which translation object to expose.
useI18n (hooks.ts) consumes I18nContext to provide translation lookup and locale-change functions to components; it relies on the Translations shape from locales/types.ts.
Locale modules (locales/en-US.ts, locales/zh-CN.ts) implement the Translations type and are assembled into the translations map exported by translations.ts and locales/index.ts.
cookies.ts provides client-side get/set cookie helpers that must use the same cookie key and normalization semantics as server.ts detectLocaleServer and setLocale.
server.ts's getI18n/detectLocaleServer call into locale normalization utilities and the translations map to return the correct translation object for SSR.
notable_relationships: This i18n subtree is consumed widely by frontend components across the app; changes here can affect any UI text rendering and must preserve shape/exports for imports used elsewhere.
Server-side rendering paths (Next.js / server components) depend on server.ts detect/getI18n for initial locale; mismatch with client cookie handling causes hydration inconsistencies.
TypeScript build and CI enforce the Translations type across locales; adding UI strings without updating types and all locale objects will cause CI failures.
Locale persistence via cookies interacts with routing and SSR: cookie name/format changes may impact user-selected locale persistence and must remain compatible with client and server helpers.
recommendations: Keep Translations type and all locale objects (en-US, zh-CN) synchronized — updating types implies updating every locale file and the translations map to avoid type/build failures.

When altering locale constants or normalization (DEFAULT_LOCALE, SUPPORTED_LOCALES, normalizeLocale, detectLocale), update both client cookies utilities and server-side detection to maintain SSR/client parity.

Do not change exported identifiers from this folder (provider, hook, translations map, locale utilities) without updating all consumers across the frontend; run a full TypeScript build to catch breakages.
If adding new translation keys, ensure entries exist in every supported locale to avoid runtime fallbacks or missing text; verify via app UI or automated snapshot tests if present.
After edits, run frontend typechecking/lint/build (pnpm typecheck / pnpm build) and exercise SSR paths to confirm no hydration or cookie-driven locale mismatches occur.

frontend/src/core/uploads.description: Client-side uploads subsystem for the frontend: provides API wrappers to the backend upload endpoints, client validation for special-case uploads (notably macOS app bundles), utilities to convert prompt input file parts into browser File objects, and React hooks that expose upload/list/delete flows scoped to a conversation/thread. This folder centralizes the vertical flow from user-selected/formed file inputs through validation and conversion, to network calls and reactive UI state used by components across the app.
key_behaviors: Convert prompt-provided file parts into browser File objects usable by the web upload API.
Validate files before upload to detect unsupported or dangerous patterns (macOS .app bundles) and split or flag them for user messaging.
Perform network operations: upload multiple files, list uploaded files for a thread, and delete individual uploaded files via typed API wrappers.
Expose upload/list/delete flows and state to React components through hooks (start/upload progress, success/failure, and refresh of the thread-scoped file list).
Unit-tested validation and conversion logic (tests exist as .mjs files) to ensure cross-platform semantics for file handling are preserved.

insights.title: Special-case detection for macOS app bundles
insights.problem: macOS app bundles packaged as directories or with specific content-types are unacceptable for upload in many contexts and must be detected client-side so users receive a helpful message.
insights.root_cause: macOS app bundles are often directories or have compound content types that browsers may not surface consistently; the backend may reject them or treat them differently.
insights.solution: Client code attempts to detect likely macOS bundles using content-type heuristics and file metadata and splits unsupported files out of the upload set while surfacing MACOS_APP_BUNDLE_UPLOAD_MESSAGE to the user; maintain parity between this heuristic and server-side expectations and keep tests to validate detection logic.

insights.title: Tight coupling to backend upload response shapes and endpoints
insights.problem: Frontend API wrappers expose and depend on typed response shapes (UploadedFileInfo, UploadResponse, ListFilesResponse). Changes to backend contract will break consumers.
insights.root_cause: The upload/list/delete wrappers serialize/deserialize specific fields and the React hooks assume those shapes to update UI state.
insights.solution: Treat the types in api.ts as a contract; when editing these files or their consumers, ensure compatibility with the gateway/upload endpoints, update types/tests together, and verify listing/deletion behavior in integration tests.

insights.title: Prompt input parts must reliably map to File objects
insights.problem: Prompt-input file parts (PromptInputFilePart) are an intermediate representation used by prompt components; incorrect conversion can lead to corrupted uploads or mismatched mime/names.
insights.root_cause: Differences between how prompt components construct file parts (base64, blobs, streaming parts) and how the browser File API is expected by the upload endpoint.
insights.solution: Keep promptInputFilePartToFile logic deterministic and covered by tests; when changing its shape, update callers and tests to ensure resulting File objects carry correct name, type, and binary content.

key_files.why: Defines the network contract and typed wrappers for upload, list, and delete operations — the primary boundary to the backend.
Encapsulates client-side heuristics for detecting unsupported macOS app bundle uploads and splitting unsupported files; tests accompany it.
Converts internal prompt input file parts into browser File objects consumed by the upload API; a key data-shape transformer.
Exposes upload/list/delete behaviors to React components and orchestrates state, side effects, and refresh semantics for thread-scoped files.
Contains unit tests validating macOS bundle detection heuristics — important to keep green when changing validation logic.

construct_relationships: hooks.ts calls functions from api.ts to perform upload/list/delete network operations and exposes the results/state to UI components.
hooks.ts uses file-validation.ts before or during the upload flow to split unsupported files and surface messages (MACOS_APP_BUNDLE_UPLOAD_MESSAGE) to the caller.
prompt-input-files.ts produces File objects that are passed to api.uploadFiles (directly or via hooks) for multipart/form-data uploads.
file-validation.test.mjs and prompt-input-files.test.mjs exercise the heuristic and conversion logic respectively, ensuring runtime behavior expected by hooks and APIs.
index.ts re-exports the module surface so consuming components import hooks, validators, and converters from the single entrypoint.
notable_relationships: This subtree is tightly coupled to backend upload endpoints and response shapes — changes here may require coordinated backend updates (contract compatibility).
UI components outside this folder (prompt components, thread views, upload dialogs) depend on the hook signatures and the exported utilities; consumers expect stable names and behaviors.
Unit tests are ES module .mjs files — the frontend test runner and build tooling expect ESM-compatible exports and may enforce particular bundling/compat settings.

Validation heuristics are a client-side complement to any server-side checks; mismatches between client and server logic can lead to confusing UX where the client permits an upload the server rejects or vice versa.
recommendations: Preserve the exported type and function names (UploadedFileInfo, UploadResponse, uploadFiles, listUploadedFiles, deleteUploadedFile, PromptInputFilePart, promptInputFilePartToFile, isLikelyMacOSAppBundle, splitUnsupportedUploadFiles, hooks) or update all consumers and tests in the same change.

When changing API shapes or endpoint semantics in api.ts, coordinate with backend endpoints and update ListFilesResponse/UploadResponse types; run integration tests that exercise uploads against the backend.
If modifying validation heuristics, update the accompanying .mjs unit tests (file-validation.test.mjs) to keep behavior documented and regressions caught.
Any edits to prompt-input-files.ts must retain the produced File object's name, type, and binary content fidelity; run frontend unit tests and file upload integration flows in the app.

Run the frontend test suite (including the .mjs tests), typecheck/lint (pnpm typecheck, pnpm lint), and a local dev build after edits to ensure ESM test compatibility and Next.js bundling expectations are preserved.
Be mindful of thread-scoped behavior in hooks.ts — changes that affect cache keys, refresh triggers, or returned state shapes will impact many UI components and should be verified in the UI.

frontend/src/core/tasks.description: This folder provides the frontend React state layer for managing Subtask objects that represent subagent work in the UI. It centralizes ephemeral, in-memory subtask records and exposes hooks for reading and updating them so multiple UI components can observe and mutate the same subtask state consistently.
key_behaviors: Create and expose a SubtaskContext provider that holds the authoritative in-memory store of subtasks for the UI session.
Allow components to read subtask records via a read hook and to update individual records via an update hook that performs shallow/partial merges.
Re-export task-related types for use across the frontend so the context, hooks, and other modules share a single Subtask type definition.

insights.title: In-memory ephemeral store
insights.problem: Subtask state is kept only in React context memory and is not persisted across page reloads or process restarts.
insights.root_cause: SubtasksProvider uses local React state/context without bridging to any persistence layer or backend synchronization in this folder.
insights.solution: When modifying or relying on this context, treat it as ephemeral UI state; any feature expecting persistence or cross-client synchronization must integrate with backend APIs or other persistence outside this folder. Tests/consumers must not assume durability across reloads.

insights.title: Partial (shallow) merges for updates
insights.problem: Updates to subtasks are described as partial merges; shallow merges can overwrite nested structures or lose fields if callers assume deep-merge or immutable updates.
insights.root_cause: Update hook merges partial objects into existing subtask records rather than performing deep merges or immutable nested-updates.
insights.solution: Consumers and maintainers must construct update payloads mindful of shallow-merge semantics (provide full nested objects when replacing nested state, or read/merge client-side before calling). When changing update logic, ensure backward compatibility for existing callers and verify nested-object cases.

insights.title: Risk of lost updates from concurrent UI actors
insights.problem: Multiple components may attempt to update the same subtask concurrently, which can lead to race conditions and lost changes if updates are not serialized or applied using functional updates.
insights.root_cause: Shared in-memory context with non-atomic partial-merge updates across multiple React components.
insights.solution: Edits must preserve atomic update semantics (use functional state updaters or other conflict resolution strategies). Verify that update hooks use React functional-setState patterns or otherwise guarantee last-write-wins behavior is acceptable for the product.

key_files.why: Implements the SubtasksProvider, SubtaskContext, and the hooks used by the entire UI to read and update subtask state — the primary entrypoint for consumers.
Defines the Subtask type used by the context and hooks; changing it impacts typing and runtime shape expectations across the UI.
Re-exports task-related types/identifiers; used as the public surface for importing subtask types/hooks from elsewhere in the frontend.

construct_relationships: SubtasksProvider (context.tsx) holds the Map/record of Subtask objects typed by Subtask (types.ts) and exposes hooks useSubtaskContext and useUpdateSubtask to consumers.
useSubtaskContext hook reads the provider state and returns subtask records to components; useUpdateSubtask performs partial merges into the provider store.
index.ts re-exports the Subtask type and possibly other task exports so external modules import types and hooks from a single path.
notable_relationships: Frontend UI components outside this folder depend on the provider and hooks; any change to hook signatures or context behavior will require updates to all consumers.

Subtask type must remain compatible with backend subagent/task shapes used elsewhere in the project (Gateway API, SSE updates, and any streaming/subagent UI). Type mismatches between backend payloads and this type will cause runtime or typing errors in components.
Next.js client/server boundaries: context usage is client-side UI state — edits need to respect Next.js 'use client' conventions and avoid relying on server-only code or lifecycle.
recommendations: Maintain the Subtask type shape compatibility with backend payloads; update all consumers if types are changed.
Preserve the provider API surface (hook names, signature, and partial-merge semantics) or update all imports/usages and run full frontend typecheck and build.
When editing update logic, ensure functional setState semantics (avoid lost updates) and add tests that simulate concurrent component updates.
Validate changes via frontend linting, TypeScript typecheck, unit tests, and a full pnpm build (Next.js) to catch client/server boundary or hydration issues.
Verify the provider remains mounted at the application root where existing UI expects it; changing export paths requires updating import sites across the frontend.

frontend/src/core/threads.description: This folder implements client-side thread primitives for agent-driven conversations: types and small utilities, a streaming hook that powers real-time thread state and reconnection, and export utilities that serialize threads to Markdown/JSON and trigger file downloads. These pieces are critical to keeping frontend thread state consistent with backend (LangGraph/Gateway) runs, enabling optimistic UI behavior, and letting users persist or share conversation data.
key_behaviors: Maintain canonical thread identity and metadata (path/title/text extraction, sanitized filenames) used across the UI and export flows
Stream and reconcile agent run/channel events into in-memory thread state with reconnect-on-mount semantics and normalization of persisted run identifiers
Support optimistic message sending with ability to attach extra kwargs (e.g., hide_from_ui, files) so UI can show intermediate/hidden tool calls consistently
Serialize and download threads in Markdown or JSON formats while preserving message content, tool calls, and safe filenames

insights.title: Run ID normalization for reliable reconnect/resume
insights.problem: Session-stored run IDs sometimes contained query parameters or suffixes and failed to match active LangGraph run IDs on reconnect, causing lost streaming/resume behavior.
insights.root_cause: Run ID values persisted to sessionStorage were not normalized before later comparison/usage; external systems (LangGraph/Gateway) can append extra data to run-related identifiers.
insights.solution: Normalize/extract canonical run id when reading stored values and when reattaching to streams; ensure reconnect logic tolerates extra suffixes so mount-time resume works reliably.

insights.title: Supporting hidden/augmented optimistic messages
insights.problem: There was no consistent way to mark outgoing messages (especially tool-invoking messages) as hidden from the UI or attach extra kwargs for optimistic state, leading to mismatches between what was shown and what was saved to backend.
insights.root_cause: sendMessage and the thread streaming hook lacked hooks/parameters for attaching arbitrary per-message kwargs (hide_from_ui, files) that must be honored both locally and when syncing to the backend.
insights.solution: Allow sendMessage to accept and persist additionalKwargs and ensure streaming/hydration paths preserve those flags so optimistic messages and hidden tool calls remain consistent across UI and server state.

insights.title: Prune local thread data when backend thread removed
insights.problem: Frontend could retain DeerFlow-managed local thread metadata after the corresponding LangGraph thread was deleted, producing stale local state and confusing UI listings.
insights.root_cause: Deletion of LangGraph threads was not fully propagated to DeerFlow-managed local storage/metadata on the frontend.
insights.solution: Trigger frontend cleanup of local thread data when Gateway confirms LangGraph deletion; ensure hooks that reconcile runs respect deletion events so local disk/session state does not persist orphaned threads.

insights.title: Safe and usable thread exports
insights.problem: Users needed a way to export threads; exported filenames and content could be unsafe or poorly formatted for re-use.
insights.root_cause: No centralized formatting/sanitization utilities for building Markdown/JSON exports and filenames.
insights.solution: Provide formatting functions for messages and tool calls, sanitize filenames, and a single download path that consistently produces Markdown and JSON exports that match UI message rendering semantics.

key_files.why: Primary runtime behavior: handles real-time thread streaming, reconnect-on-mount, optimistic sendMessage semantics, and integration points for hidden tool call kwargs.
Canonical types for AgentThread and AgentThreadState consumed by the hook, utilities, and export formatting; changing these affects the whole subtree.
Provides thread-to-Markdown/JSON serialization, filename sanitization, and file download helpers — the only centralized export surface used by UI export actions.
Small, widely-used helpers (pathOfThread, textOfMessage, titleOfThread) that determine thread identifiers and display titles; used by UI lists and exports.
Re-exports the folder’s public surface (types/utilities/hooks/exports); changes here affect import paths across the codebase.

construct_relationships: useThreadStream (hooks.ts) consumes AgentThreadState and AgentThread types from types.ts to shape runtime state and streaming events
export formatting functions (export.ts) call textOfMessage and titleOfThread from utils.ts to produce human-readable export content and filenames
sendMessage behavior in hooks.ts must preserve additionalKwargs (e.g., hide_from_ui, files) so exported/serialized threads (export.ts) and persisted state align with user-visible messages
index.ts re-exports types, hooks, utils and export functions, making these constructs available to the rest of the frontend; breaking a name here affects external imports
notable_relationships: Frontend reconnect and run-id normalization depend on backend LangGraph/Gateway run id formats and SSE/stream behavior — any change in run id shape or deletion semantics upstream affects reconnect and cleanup logic here
Hidden tool call flags and optimistic message kwargs are part of the contract with Gateway APIs and local sessionStorage; mismatches will produce UI/backend drift

Exported files are user downloads and interact with browser APIs (download, naming) and must be compatible with other tools that might re-import the JSON/Markdown (format expectations should match backend message schemas)
SessionStorage/local disk state maintained by these modules influences UI thread listings globally; other frontend modules that enumerate threads rely on pathOfThread/titleOfThread behavior
recommendations: When editing hooks.ts, preserve reconnect-on-mount logic and run id normalization; maintain compatibility with possible suffixes in persisted run ids and test resume behavior against LangGraph stream endpoints

If changing sendMessage signatures or additionalKwargs handling, update types.ts and re-exported types in index.ts so callers and optimistic UI flows keep binary compatibility; ensure hidden flags (hide_from_ui) remain honored across streaming and serialization paths

Modifications to export formatting or filename sanitization must not change the semantic content shape expected by importers or backend persistence; verify generated Markdown/JSON against representative thread data (including tool call payloads)

Any change to utils.ts helpers that affect path/title/text extraction can impact thread listing, export naming, and persisted keys — run frontend typecheck and UI integration tests that cover thread listing, export, and delete flows

Because index.ts is the public entrypoint for this folder, keep its exported surface stable or coordinate updates to all callers; run the frontend build and type-check (pnpm build / typecheck) after edits and exercise export/download and reconnect scenarios in a browser

frontend/src/core/utils.description: A small collection of frontend utility functions used across the UI: date/time formatting, file metadata and icon helpers, robust JSON parsing, markdown title extraction, and a UUID re-export. These utilities centralize small but widely-consumed behaviors that affect rendering, data parsing, and identity generation in UI components, so changes here have broad consumer impact.
key_behaviors: Locale-aware formatting and display of relative times for UI timestamps.
File metadata handling: derive display name, extension, human-friendly extension names, and render file type icons for file lists/previews.
Best-effort JSON parsing for user or remote content to avoid throwing on malformed input.
Extraction of the first/title-like line from markdown content for previews or headings.
Provision of a stable uuid function (v4) used as client-side identifiers.

insights.title: File icon rendering needs className and image support
insights.problem: getFileIcon previously used a hard-coded className and lacked support for image file thumbnails, preventing callers from controlling icon size or style and omitting realistic previews for images.
insights.root_cause: Tight coupling between the helper and its presentation, with the helper imposing styling and not accounting for binary/image preview variants.
insights.solution: Utility functions that render UI primitives should accept presentation parameters (e.g., className) and explicitly handle image types (returning an or equivalent). When editing these helpers, preserve or clearly document the presentation contract so callers relying on existing class names are considered.

insights.title: Datetime utilities are locale-sensitive and globally impactful
insights.problem: formatTimeAgo uses locale-aware formatting which can produce different outputs depending on the runtime locale and Intl support.
insights.root_cause: Dependence on runtime Intl and application locale configuration.
insights.solution: Treat formatTimeAgo as dependent on the app's locale provider; when changing its behavior, ensure the change aligns with app-wide locale handling and server/client rendering constraints (SSR).

insights.title: JSON parsing must fail gracefully
insights.problem: Client flows may receive partial or non-JSON payloads which should not crash UI components.
insights.root_cause: Consumers sometimes attempt to parse arbitrary text as JSON without guarding against parse errors.
insights.solution: Maintain a best-effort tryParseJSON that never throws and returns a sensible fallback; callers may rely on this behavior, so any change must preserve non-throwing semantics and clearly document returned shapes for invalid input.

insights.title: Markdown title extraction has edge cases
insights.problem: Extracting a title from arbitrary markdown needs to handle headings, frontmatter, empty content, and links without producing misleading results.
insights.root_cause: Markdown is a wide format with many syntaxes and user inputs; naive heuristics can misidentify title content.
insights.solution: Keep extractTitleFromMarkdown conservative and well-documented about what constitutes a title. Consumers rely on its heuristics for previews; any change requires verifying downstream displays and test cases for common edge cases (empty docs, frontmatter, HTML comments).

insights.title: Re-exported utilities affect import surface and bundle
insights.problem: uuid is re-exported as a convenience, which simplifies imports but can affect tree-shaking and bundle behavior depending on how it's imported elsewhere.
insights.root_cause: A wrapper/re-export changes the module graph and may make it harder to trace origin or impact bundling.
insights.solution: When editing or moving the uuid re-export, preserve its stable import path or verify consumer imports; run build and bundle analysis to ensure expected size and tree-shaking remain acceptable.

key_files.why: Primary file-related utilities: filename/extension extraction and the getFileIcon UI helper that directly affects file list and preview components.
Provides locale-aware relative time formatting used by many timestamp displays across the UI.
Centralized robust JSON parsing used to guard against malformed payloads coming from uploads or remote responses.
Extracts preview titles from markdown content; used by document/file previews and lists.
Exports the uuid generator used to create client-side identifiers; affects keys and ephemeral IDs.

construct_relationships: getFileName and getFileExtension are paired helpers used to compute display names and determine which icon getFileIcon should render.
getFileExtensionDisplayName maps extension metadata to human-friendly labels that are shown alongside icons produced by getFileIcon.
getFileIcon must accept presentation parameters (className) so UI components can control sizing/styling; the icon output is consumed directly by list/preview components.

tryParseJSON and extractTitleFromMarkdown operate on textual payloads and are likely composed in file/content handling flows (e.g., uploaded text files or fetched document bodies) to safely derive preview content or metadata.
uuid is used across components to generate stable keys/IDs for items created or processed by the other utilities (file lists, parsed items, etc.).
notable_relationships: These utilities are low-level primitives used broadly across frontend components and pages; changes here can change many consumers' render output and behavior.
Presentation behavior (className forwarding, returned JSX for file icons) interacts with global CSS systems (Tailwind/CSS modules) and Next.js SSR — ensure any DOM output remains SSR-safe.
Locale-dependent datetime formatting ties into the app's global locale/Intl configuration and can produce differing SSR vs client outputs if not harmonized.
The uuid re-export and any changes to export paths may affect importers and the build/bundle pipeline (Next.js tree-shaking and bundling).
recommendations: When editing utilities, preserve public function signatures or update all callers; search the codebase for usages before changing APIs.
Any change to getFileIcon must consider CSS/className propagation and SSR-safe markup (image thumbnails vs icon SVGs) so that file lists and previews keep consistent sizing and layout.
Maintain tryParseJSON's non-throwing contract and document its return behavior for malformed input; add or run unit tests for malformed and edge-case payloads.
Verify formatTimeAgo behavior under different locales and in SSR hydration scenarios; run the frontend build and check for mismatches between server and client render.
After edits, run the frontend lint, typecheck, and build (pnpm lint, pnpm typecheck, pnpm build) and run any UI tests that exercise file lists, uploads, and timestamp displays to catch regressions.

frontend/src/core/agents.description: This folder implements the frontend client-side surface for managing agent entities: typed models, a REST API client that talks to the backend, and React hooks that expose CRUD flows and UI state. It is the primary integration point between UI components/pages and the backend agent management endpoints, so stability of types, error shapes, and exported APIs here is important to avoid cascading UI breakage.
key_behaviors: CRUD interaction with backend agent endpoints (list, get, create, update, delete)
Name-availability validation flow with specialized error classification (AgentNameCheckError)
Adaptation of network responses into React-friendly state via hooks for consumption by pages/components
Centralized type definitions driving request/response shapes shared by API client and hooks
Barrel exports to present a compact import surface for other frontend modules

insights.title: Agent name check error visibility and classification
insights.problem: The previous implementation surfaced a generic error for agent-name checks and did not distinguish network/unreachable backend conditions from validation failures.
