insights.root_cause: API client did not classify error types (validation vs network) or expose enough metadata to the hooks/UI to present distinct feedback.
insights.solution: Ensure the API client returns typed, distinct error shapes (including a dedicated AgentNameCheckError) and preserves underlying network/HTTP error details so hooks and UI layers can render appropriate messages or fallbacks.

insights.title: Tight coupling to backend request/response schema
insights.problem: Types for Agent, CreateAgentRequest, and UpdateAgentRequest are authoritative for the UI but can diverge from the backend contract.
insights.root_cause: Frontend types are defined only in this folder and rely on manual synchronization with backend schemas.
insights.solution: Treat these TypeScript definitions as the canonical local contract: any change to API shapes requires coordinated updates to these types and to every consumer hook/component that depends on them.

insights.title: Hooks depend on API error semantics
insights.problem: React hooks map API responses and errors into UI state; changes to API return values or error classes propagate directly into the hooks and consuming components.
insights.root_cause: Hooks call API functions and translate results without insulating layers that abstract different transport semantics.
insights.solution: When altering API client behavior or error shapes, update hooks to preserve public hook contracts and ensure consumers still receive the expected state shape and error types.

insights.title: Barrel export fragility
insights.problem: index.ts re-exports the folder API, types, and hooks; renaming or removing exported symbols will break imports across the app.
insights.root_cause: Other modules import from the barrel path rather than file-level paths for convenience.
insights.solution: Any change to exported names must be synchronized with all importers or the barrel kept backward-compatible.

key_files.why: Primary REST client for agent management and the origin of specialized error classes (AgentNameCheckError); changes here affect hooks and UI error handling.
Exposes React hooks that wrap the API client into UI state flows; direct consumer surface for components/pages.
Defines the TypeScript shapes for agents and requests used across api.ts and hooks.ts; authoritative for compile-time checks and contracts.
Barrel file that re-exports hooks, api, and types for importers; important for module-level compatibility.

construct_relationships: hooks.ts calls functions exported from api.ts (listAgents, getAgent, createAgent, updateAgent, deleteAgent, checkAgentName) and maps their responses into React state and callbacks.
api.ts depends on types from types.ts for request payloads and response typing; it also defines and throws AgentNameCheckError which hooks.ts consumes to drive name-validation UI behavior.
index.ts re-exports the public symbols from api.ts, hooks.ts, and types.ts so external UI components import the hooks/types via the barrel path.
notable_relationships: Backend REST API: api.ts assumes a stable backend contract (endpoints, status codes, error shapes). Any backend changes to agent endpoints or error formats will require coordinated frontend updates.
UI pages/components across the app import hooks from this folder; edits here can break UI features (agent creation/update pages, agent list views, validation UX).
Global network configuration (base URL, auth headers, interceptors) outside this folder will influence api.ts behavior — changes to global fetch/axios setup can alter error shapes observed here.
Build and typecheck: TypeScript compilation and frontend CI (pnpm typecheck, build, lint) will surface mismatches between exported types and imports across the app.
recommendations: Preserve public function and type names exported from index.ts or update all consumers when renaming/removing exports.

When editing api.ts, maintain or clearly document the error classification semantics (particularly for agent name checks) because hooks and UI rely on them; propagate any error-shape changes to hooks.ts.
If you modify types.ts structures, update api.ts request/response handling and all hook consumers; run TypeScript typecheck and frontend unit tests to catch regressions.
Run the frontend lint/typecheck and build (pnpm typecheck, pnpm build) and any unit/integration tests that exercise agent creation/update flows and offline/network-failure cases after edits.

Be mindful of cross-file imports: hooks.ts imports api functions and error classes; keep signatures stable or update both sides together to avoid runtime issues in components that consume these hooks.

frontend/src/core/api.description: This folder provides the frontend adapter and utilities for invoking the LangGraph API from the Next.js application. It centralizes client creation (including mock vs default client modes), stream option sanitization for run streams, and re-exports the public API surface used across the UI. It is critical because mismatches here affect runtime network calls, streaming behavior, and test/dev mock behavior across the frontend.
key_behaviors: Create and return a LangGraph API client instance, with explicit support for both default (real) and mock client modes.
Provide compatibility helpers to ensure the client instance returned matches the requested mode (preventing cross-mode reuse).
Sanitize and validate run-stream options, warn about unsupported streaming modes, and produce a normalized options object used when initiating streaming runs.
Expose the API utilities via a single re-exporting entrypoint for use across UI components and tests.

insights.title: Client instance reuse can return wrong mode
insights.problem: A single LangGraphClient singleton was reused across mock and default modes, which caused callers requesting a mock client to occasionally receive the default client (and vice versa).
insights.root_cause: Global reuse of a single LangGraphClient instance without mode scoping or recreation logic when switching between mock and default modes.
insights.solution: Maintain separate instances or an explicit factory that returns mode-scoped clients; ensure client creation logic distinguishes mock vs default and does not return the wrong instance when modes change.

insights.title: Unsupported stream modes must be normalized and warned
insights.problem: Clients may pass run stream options that include unsupported modes causing unexpected behavior at runtime.
insights.root_cause: Consumers can provide arbitrary stream-mode values; without normalization, unsupported values propagate to runtime streaming calls.
insights.solution: Sanitize run stream options by whitelisting SUPPORTED_RUN_STREAM_MODES, strip or replace unsupported values, and surface warnings for visibility so calling code can act accordingly.

insights.title: Sanitization behavior is asserted by unit tests
insights.problem: Sanitization logic is relied upon by other parts of the app and must remain stable to avoid regressions.
insights.root_cause: Behavioral expectations (e.g., what is filtered and what triggers warnings) are encoded in tests rather than implicit behavior.
insights.solution: Keep unit tests (stream-mode.test.ts) aligned with sanitizeRunStreamOptions implementation; when modifying sanitization or warning behaviors, update tests and consider their impact on consumers.

key_files.why: Primary factory and adapter for obtaining a LangGraph client (handles mock vs default client modes and stream helpers) and a central place for client compatibility logic.
Encapsulates supported streaming modes, warning logic, and the sanitizeRunStreamOptions utility that normalizes streaming options before starting runs.
Unit tests that lock expected behavior of the sanitization helper; changes to sanitization or warning semantics must keep these tests consistent.
Single re-export entrypoint used by the rest of the frontend; ensures public API surface remains stable for imports.

construct_relationships: getAPIClient / createCompatibleClient (api-client.ts) are the factory entrypoints that produce LangGraphClient instances and must enforce mode semantics (mock vs default).

SanitizeRunStreamOptions (stream-mode.ts) is used before initiating streaming runs via the LangGraph client to ensure only SUPPORTED_RUN_STREAM_MODES are sent and to trigger warnings for unsupported modes.
stream-mode.test.ts asserts the contract of sanitizeRunStreamOptions, so test expectations are directly coupled to stream-mode.ts behavior.
index.ts re-exports the client and stream utilities so consumers import a stable surface; any rename/shape change in api-client.ts or stream-mode.ts must be reflected in index.ts exports.
notable_relationships: Upstream: Backend LangGraph API contract — the client adapter must match server endpoints and streaming semantics; mismatches in supported stream modes or payload shapes can cause runtime failures.
Downstream: UI components, hooks, and tests import from frontend/src/core/api; changes here ripple to many consumer files and to integration tests or stories that rely on mock clients.

Dev/test flows: The mock vs default client behavior affects local development, storybook, and unit/integration tests. Ensuring the correct client is returned for the requested mode is essential for deterministic test outcomes.
Build/exports: index.ts is the public surface for imports; changing exports changes all import sites and may require wide updates across the frontend.
recommendations: When modifying client creation logic, preserve explicit mode scoping (mock vs default) and verify that creating clients in different orders returns the expected instance for each mode.
If changing sanitizeRunStreamOptions or SUPPORTED_RUN_STREAM_MODES, update stream-mode.test.ts to reflect the intended contract and run the frontend unit tests.
Maintain stable exported names in index.ts or update all import sites across the frontend to avoid broken imports; run the TypeScript build/typecheck after edits.
After edits, run frontend unit tests and the full Next.js build (pnpm build) to catch import/export, typing, and runtime streaming regressions.

Be aware of the backend LangGraph API and streaming semantics — any client-side changes affecting request shapes or supported stream modes should be validated against the running backend or its mock implementations to avoid runtime integration errors.

frontend/src/core/artifacts.description: This folder contains frontend utilities for resolving artifact URLs, loading artifact payloads and content, and a React hook to expose artifact content to UI components. It centralizes URL construction and artifact extraction logic so the rest of the UI can present previews and tool-call payloads consistently. These utilities are tightly coupled to the Gateway/LangGraph artifact endpoints and thread data shapes used elsewhere in the app.
key_behaviors: Construct canonical artifact URLs from artifact metadata or thread entries (urlOfArtifact, resolveArtifactURL).
Extract artifact references embedded in thread/run objects (extractArtifactsFromThread) so UI components can list or preview artifacts associated with a run/thread.
Fetch artifact content and tool-call payloads from the backend using loader functions (loadArtifactContent, loadArtifactContentFromToolCall).
Provide artifact content (and associated URL) to components via a hook (useArtifactContent) for previews and downloads.
Re-export loader utilities for simpler imports via index.ts.

insights.title: Artifact loaders must preserve source URL
insights.problem: Consumers needed the original artifact URL (for non-write artifact previews and download links) but the loader/hook only returned text content.
insights.root_cause: Loader and hook returned only the textual response body, discarding the original URL used to fetch the artifact.
insights.solution: Ensure loader functions and hooks return both the fetched content and the original URL so callers can render previews and link back to the source.

insights.title: Centralized URL construction
insights.problem: Multiple callers need to turn artifact metadata or thread references into usable URLs; ad-hoc URL building risks inconsistencies.
insights.root_cause: URL building logic can be duplicated if not centralized, leading to mismatched paths or missing query parameters.
insights.solution: Keep URL construction in utils (urlOfArtifact / resolveArtifactURL) and have loaders & hooks call those helpers to ensure consistent URL formats.

insights.title: Artifact extraction depends on thread schema
insights.problem: extractArtifactsFromThread assumes a particular thread/run structure; changes to thread shapes will break artifact discovery.
insights.root_cause: Tight coupling between artifact extraction logic and the runtime/thread JSON shape returned by backend APIs.
insights.solution: Treat extractArtifactsFromThread as the translation layer: any change to backend thread format requires corresponding updates here and validation in consumers.

insights.title: Re-export surface must remain stable
insights.problem: index.ts re-exports loader utilities; consumers import via the index barrel and rely on stable exported names.
insights.root_cause: Barrel exports create an implicit public API; renames or removals in underlying files can break many imports across the frontend.
insights.solution: Maintain exported symbols and shapes in index.ts or coordinate any public API changes with updates to all consumer imports.

key_files.why: Primary functions that fetch artifact content and tool-call payloads; their return shapes determine what UI consumers receive.
React hook used by components to access artifact content and URL; changes affect all preview/display components.
Centralized helpers for building artifact URLs and extracting artifacts from thread objects; used by loader and hook.
Barrel export for artifact utilities; defines the public import surface for other frontend modules.

construct_relationships: useArtifactContent (hooks.ts) calls loadArtifactContent (loader.ts) to fetch artifact data and relies on loader to provide both content and source URL.
loadArtifactContent and loadArtifactContentFromToolCall (loader.ts) call urlOfArtifact / resolveArtifactURL (utils.ts) to compute request endpoints.
extractArtifactsFromThread (utils.ts) is consumed by higher-level UI code to enumerate artifacts associated with a thread/run; loaders may accept artifact objects produced by this extractor.
index.ts re-exports loader functions so consumers import loader APIs from the barrel rather than individual files.
notable_relationships: Backend Gateway / artifact endpoints: URL construction must match backend routing and any auth/query parameters expected by the API (frontend loaders call these endpoints).

Thread/run representations elsewhere in the frontend: extractArtifactsFromThread must remain consistent with the thread schema produced by the backend and consumed by other UI modules (message lists, run details).
Components that render previews or download links depend on the loader/hook return shape (both content and URL); changing return shapes may require updating multiple UI components.
Any change to URL formats or artifact access (e.g., signed URLs, CORS, or proxying through the gateway) is an upstream concern that will affect loader and utils behavior.
recommendations: Preserve or intentionally update the public return shapes of loader functions and useArtifactContent hook; verify all consumers are updated if shapes change.
When modifying URL construction, validate generated URLs against backend routes and any required query/auth parameters used by the Gateway.
Run frontend typechecks, unit tests for hooks/loaders/utils, and integration checks for artifact preview/download flows when making edits.
Check all imports that rely on the barrel export (index.ts) if you rename or remove exported symbols to avoid breaking imports across the frontend.
If you alter extractArtifactsFromThread, coordinate with backend thread schema changes and update any components that enumerate artifacts from threads.

frontend/src/core/mcp.description: This folder provides the frontend primitives for managing the MCP (MCP server/config) surface: a small typed API client, React hooks that expose fetch/update/enable flows to UI components, and a central place for the MCP-related type definitions and re-exports. It is the single source of truth in the frontend for MCP config shape and the client-side behaviors around reading/updating/enabling MCP servers, so changes here directly affect any UI that shows MCP servers or toggles them on/off.
key_behaviors: Fetch the full MCP configuration from the backend and expose it to React components
Send updates to MCP configuration back to the backend (patch/replace flows)
Expose an enable/disable operation for an MCP server with appropriate async state
Provide typed shapes (MCPServerConfig, MCPConfig) to guarantee compile-time correctness and to model API payloads
Act as the public export surface for MCP functionality used by other frontend modules

insights.title: Frontend types must remain aligned with backend MCP schema
insights.problem: If the MCPConfig/MCPServerConfig types diverge from the backend response shapes, consumers will get runtime errors or subtle UI bugs despite type-checking.
insights.root_cause: Types are defined locally in the frontend and can drift when backend endpoints change.
insights.solution: Treat types.ts as the canonical frontend contract for MCP payloads; when modifying API paths or payload shapes, update types.ts and all callers. Ensure CI/typechecks run after changes.

insights.title: Update flows must keep UI cache consistent
insights.problem: After calling update or enable operations, the UI can display stale MCP state if the hooks do not re-fetch or invalidate cached values.
insights.root_cause: API functions and hooks are separate layers; an update call may not trigger the fetch/invalidation lifecycle used by useMCPConfig consumers.
insights.solution: When editing the API or hooks, preserve or explicitly implement revalidation/invalidation and loading/error state so components relying on the hook reflect the latest server state.

insights.title: Enable/disable operations are side-effectful and can race
insights.problem: Simultaneous enable/disable requests for multiple servers (or repeated toggles) can produce race conditions and inconsistent UI state.
insights.root_cause: Enabling a server is an async operation; without per-server status tracking and careful state updates, responses can overwrite each other.
insights.solution: Ensure hooks provide per-server status (pending/success/error) and that enable/update operations are idempotent or reconciled by subsequent fetches.

insights.title: Index re-exports are relied on by consumers
insights.problem: Removing or renaming exports in index.ts will break imports in many places.
insights.root_cause: index.ts centralizes exports for convenience; consumers import from the folder barrel.
insights.solution: When changing exported symbols, update index.ts and all import sites; keep the barrel stable or coordinate refactors across the codebase.

insights.title: API calls depend on backend endpoints and auth context
insights.problem: Changing endpoint paths, headers, or auth flows will break the MCP API calls used by these files.
insights.root_cause: api.ts wraps network calls whose runtime behavior depends on backend routes and gateway auth/CORS.
insights.solution: Respect existing endpoint URLs and auth conventions; if those change, update api.ts accordingly and verify end-to-end with the backend and any proxy (e.g., nginx).

key_files.why: Implements network calls to load and update MCP configuration; core integration point with backend endpoints.
Provides React hooks (useMCPConfig, useEnableMCPServer) that components import to read and mutate MCP state and to manage async UI state.
Defines MCPServerConfig and MCPConfig types used by api.ts and hooks.ts; changing these affects consumers and compile-time contracts.
Barrel file re-exporting API and types; used by other frontend modules to import MCP primitives from a single path.

construct_relationships: hooks.useMCPConfig calls api.loadMCPConfig to fetch the current MCPConfig and exposes loading/error state to components
hooks.useEnableMCPServer calls api.updateMCPConfig (or a dedicated enable endpoint) to change a server's enabled state and then triggers revalidation of the configuration
types.MCPConfig and types.MCPServerConfig are referenced by both api.ts (for request/response typing) and hooks.ts (for return types and internal state)
index.ts re-exports the functions and types defined in api.ts and types.ts so UI components import from the module root
notable_relationships: Backend MCP endpoints (Gateway/LangGraph) are implicitly depended upon; api.ts must match the backend route paths, request/response schema, and auth expectations.
UI components and pages across the frontend import hooks from this folder; breaking changes here have wide UI impact.
Global networking and auth middleware (e.g., fetch wrappers, axios interceptors, CSRF tokens) outside this folder affect how api.ts should perform requests.
Caching or global state solutions (React Query, SWR, or custom cache) used elsewhere will interact with these hooks—ensure any invalidation/revalidation semantics remain compatible.
recommendations: When editing signatures in api.ts or types.ts, update hooks.ts and index.ts together to keep the public contract stable for consumers.
Maintain and respect per-request loading/error state and ensure hooks trigger revalidation after mutation operations to avoid stale UI.
Validate that types.ts matches backend schema; run TypeScript checks and, if available, API contract tests or integration tests after changes.
If changing endpoint paths or auth headers in api.ts, coordinate with backend/gateway configuration and run an end-to-end frontend build and integration smoke test.
Preserve exported names in index.ts or update all import sites; run the frontend typecheck and build to detect broken imports.
When modifying enable/update flows, consider concurrent interactions: ensure per-server status tracking and that operations are idempotent or reconciled by a fresh fetch.

frontend/src/core/memory.description: This folder provides the frontend memory client surface: typed data models, low-level API wrappers to the Gateway memory endpoints (load/clear/import/export/create/update/delete) and React hooks (react-query) that UI components use to read and mutate memory. It centralizes error normalization and request shapes so the rest of the frontend can treat memory operations consistently and rely on stable query keys and typed payloads.
key_behaviors: Fetch and cache user memory (list of MemoryFact / UserMemory) via react-query hooks.
Mutate memory state: create, update, delete facts and clear all memory using API wrappers with consistent error formatting.
Bulk operations: import and export memory (file upload/download) handled through dedicated API functions and exposed to hooks.
Cache coordination: hooks wrap react-query mutations and queries to ensure UI reflects server state (invalidations/updates).
Type-driven request/response contracts: types.ts defines MemoryFact and related input/patch shapes consumed by api and hooks.

insights.title: Backend error shapes vary; API normalizes errors for UI
insights.problem: Different backend error response shapes were surfacing to UI leading to inconsistent error messages and UI handling.
insights.root_cause: The Gateway/memory endpoints return heterogeneous error payloads (different fields/nesting) and the frontend initially forwarded them raw.
insights.solution: Centralize robust error formatting in api.ts so callers (hooks/components) receive consistent error objects/strings regardless of backend response shape.

insights.title: Mutations must keep react-query cache coherent
insights.problem: Create/update/delete/clear/import/export operations change memory state asynchronously and can leave UI stale if the cache isn't invalidated/updated.
insights.root_cause: Mutations are performed against REST endpoints while UI relies on react-query cached queries keyed by memory; without explicit invalidation or local updates, views can diverge.
insights.solution: Hooks in hooks.ts wrap api mutations and are responsible for reacting to mutation outcomes (invalidating or updating the memory query key) to keep UI in sync.

insights.title: Typed request/response contract between frontend and Gateway
insights.problem: Frontend components depend on MemoryFact and UserMemory shapes; mismatches with backend schema can cause runtime bugs.
insights.root_cause: Evolving server contract or incomplete typing on API responses.
insights.solution: Types in types.ts are the single source of truth for memory types in this subtree; api.ts and hooks.ts rely on them and should be updated whenever the backend contract changes.

insights.title: File transfer semantics and large payload handling
insights.problem: Import/export endpoints require special handling (file downloads/uploads and possibly different response formats) compared to regular JSON CRUD.
insights.root_cause: Export returns downloadable content and import consumes file multipart data, while other endpoints are JSON-based.
insights.solution: api.ts implements dedicated exportMemory/importMemory functions and formats errors accordingly; callers must handle blobs/Content-Disposition and multipart/form-data flows.

key_files.why: Centralized HTTP client functions for all memory endpoints, contains robust error normalization and special-case flows (export/import).
React-query hooks that bridge api.ts with UI components; responsible for mutation behaviors and cache coordination.
Type declarations for MemoryFact, inputs, and UserMemory used throughout api and hooks to enforce client-side contracts.
Public surface re-exporting API and types for consumers across the frontend; changes here affect imports elsewhere.

construct_relationships: hooks.ts imports api.ts functions (loadMemory, createMemoryFact, updateMemoryFact, deleteMemoryFact, importMemory, exportMemory, clearMemory) and exposes react-query hooks that wrap those functions.
api.ts uses the TypeScript types from types.ts for request/response typing and error parsing functions to normalize server responses.
index.ts re-exports api and types so UI modules import memory primitives from a single path.
UI components depend on hooks' query keys and mutation behaviors; hooks are the layer responsible for triggering cache invalidation or local updates after api calls.
notable_relationships: Upstream dependency: Gateway memory REST endpoints and their exact routes/response shapes — the api wrappers assume specific endpoint behavior (including file download/upload semantics).
Cross-cutting dependency: react-query setup (QueryClient provider) at the app root — hooks depend on that provider for caching and mutation side effects.
Authentication/headers: api.ts likely relies on a shared HTTP client or auth token middleware elsewhere in the frontend; changes must preserve header injection behavior.
Downstream consumers: many UI components and pages will import hooks via core/memory/index.ts; changes to signatures, query keys, or types ripples to many places.
recommendations: Keep api.ts error-normalization logic intact: maintain consistent thrown/returned error shapes so hooks and components don't need bespoke parsing.
Preserve react-query query keys and invalidation/update semantics used by hooks.ts; if you change mutation outcomes or names, update invalidation logic and all consumers.
When modifying request or response types in types.ts, ensure they align with Gateway API contract and update api.ts parsing accordingly to avoid runtime mismatches.
Maintain special handling for import/export endpoints (file blobs, multipart/form-data, Content-Disposition) in api.ts and ensure hooks expose appropriate return types/events for consumers.

Any change to index.ts exports affects imports across the frontend — keep export surface stable or update import sites. After edits, run frontend typecheck, lint, and the app build to catch integration issues, and exercise UI flows that perform memory CRUD and import/export.

frontend/src/core/models.description: This folder provides the frontend model layer: a small set of utilities that fetch available model metadata from the backend, expose it to React components via a React Query hook, and declare the UI-facing type shape. It centralizes the API call, caching/consumption contract, and type definitions so UI code can uniformly request and render model lists without duplicating network logic.
key_behaviors: Fetch model list from the backend API endpoint and return parsed model objects
Expose model data to React components via a React Query hook with caching, loading and error states
Provide and export a shared Model type used by UI components and other frontend modules
Act as the single source of truth for the model-fetching query key and network call semantics

insights.title: Model API shape is authoritative for UI types
insights.problem: UI model types and data consumers assume a specific payload shape returned by the backend endpoint.
insights.root_cause: Types and hook implementations are derived from the current backend response schema; changes to the backend payload will break type assumptions or runtime behavior.
insights.solution: When the backend model payload changes, update types.ts and api.ts together; keep api.ts transformations explicit and maintain backward-compatible parsing or graceful fallbacks to avoid runtime errors.

insights.title: React Query cache and query key semantics
insights.problem: Consumers rely on cached model lists and React Query behavior (staleTime, refetch triggers). Uncoordinated changes can invalidate caches or cause unnecessary refetches.
insights.root_cause: The hook centralizes the query but its key and fetch behavior are implicit to consumers; changes to query keys or default options will affect all components using the hook.
insights.solution: Preserve the query key and intended default options or update all consumers simultaneously; document and keep query key constants stable, and if changing refetch policies, ensure components expecting immediate freshness are adjusted.

insights.title: Index re-exports are the public API for models
insights.problem: Other modules import from this folder’s index barrel; changing export names or removing exports will break many imports across the frontend.
insights.root_cause: index.ts re-exports api and types and acts as the canonical module boundary.
insights.solution: Any changes to exported symbols (rename/remove) must be mirrored across consumers or kept as backward-compatible aliases in index.ts to avoid widespread import failures.

insights.title: SSR and environment constraints
insights.problem: Model-fetching code may be used in SSR or client-only contexts; network availability, base URL resolution, and access to browser-only APIs differ across environments.
insights.root_cause: The API call implementation will run in whichever environment the hook or caller is used; assumptions about window/fetch/base path may break in SSR.
insights.solution: Ensure api.ts uses environment-agnostic URL resolution provided by the app (next runtime conventions or configured base path) and that the hook’s use is compatible with server/client rendering semantics used by the app.

key_files.why: Defines the network call and parsing logic that converts backend payloads into the frontend model list; central point for adapting to backend contract changes.
Exposes the React Query hook (useModels) used by UI components—controls cache keys, options, and loading/error semantics across the app.
Declares the Model type used throughout the UI; any change here affects type-checking and component props/logic.
Barrel file that re-exports the API, hook, and types—serves as the public import boundary for other frontend modules.

construct_relationships: useModels (hooks.ts) calls loadModels (api.ts) as the query function to fetch model data
api.ts returns objects shaped to the Model type defined in types.ts (api ↔ types)
index.ts re-exports loadModels, useModels, and Model so consumers import from the folder’s public API rather than individual files
notable_relationships: This folder depends on the backend Gateway model endpoint and the agreed JSON schema for model metadata—backend changes to that endpoint will impact this module.
Consumers across the frontend (pages, components) import useModels and Model from this folder; changes have broad downstream impact.
React Query provider configuration (application root) and global query defaults affect how useModels behaves (cache, retry, refetch), so edits here must consider app-wide React Query settings.
Build/CI checks (typecheck, lint, format) in the frontend pipeline will run on changes in this folder; exported symbol changes will surface as type errors elsewhere.
recommendations: When editing api.ts, preserve or explicitly migrate the payload parsing and update types.ts in lockstep; ensure backward-compatible parsing if the backend can return older/newer shapes.
If adjusting the hook (useModels), keep the query key and default React Query options stable or update all consumers that rely on them; confirm behavior under client and SSR use cases.
Do not change exported symbol names in index.ts without updating all import sites; prefer adding aliases if you must introduce breaking names temporarily.

Run the frontend typecheck, lint, and build (pnpm typecheck, pnpm lint, pnpm build) and run relevant unit/integration tests after changes; verify components that consume useModels still render correctly and that cached behavior matches expectations.
Verify environment-specific behavior: ensure the API base URL resolution and fetch usage work in both client and any server-side rendering contexts used by the app.

frontend/src/core/settings.description: This folder implements client-side settings management for the frontend: default values, persistence to browser storage, thread-scoped model overrides, and React hooks/store wrappers for components to consume and subscribe to settings. It centralizes local-default definitions, storage keys, merging logic, and a small in-browser store so UI code can read/update settings consistently and retain per-thread model choices across reloads.
key_behaviors: Provide default local settings and typed LocalSettings shape to the rest of the app.
Persist and restore user settings from browser localStorage using well-known keys.
Support thread-scoped model selection via per-thread keys and apply overrides when a thread is active.
Expose hooks (useLocalSettings, useThreadSettings) that read from and update the in-browser store and notify subscribers.
Merge runtime updates with defaults and ensure fallback behavior when persisted values are missing or partial.

insights.title: Per-thread model selection persisted in localStorage
insights.problem: Model selection was previously global and not reliably persisted per conversation/thread, causing user-selected models to be lost or applied incorrectly across threads.
insights.root_cause: Lack of thread-scoped storage keys and absent apply/restore logic for thread-level overrides.
insights.solution: Introduce THREAD_MODEL_KEY_PREFIX and functions to save/restore per-thread model names; use those when initializing thread settings so the UI applies persisted per-thread overrides across reloads.

insights.title: Hooks separated from generic state handling
insights.problem: A monolithic useLocalSettings hook was not flexible for thread-scoped needs.
insights.root_cause: Combining storage/merging logic and hook subscription behavior into a single function limited reuse.
insights.solution: Split responsibilities: local.ts defines defaults and storage helpers; store.ts provides subscribe/get/update primitives; hooks.ts composes these to expose React-friendly APIs.

insights.title: Browser-only APIs require runtime guards
insights.problem: Code relies on localStorage and other browser-only behavior that will fail during SSR or in non-browser contexts.
insights.root_cause: Direct access to window/localStorage without guarding.
insights.solution: Use isBrowser checks when accessing localStorage and ensure hooks/store functions are safe to import on the server by short-circuiting browser-only calls.

insights.title: Store exposes subscription but persistence/share semantics must be preserved
insights.problem: Multiple components/tabs may need consistent view of settings; store exposes subscribe/get/update but changes to persistence keys or subscriber protocols can break UI synchronization.
insights.root_cause: Tight coupling between in-memory store snapshots and localStorage keys without explicit cross-tab sync contract.
insights.solution: When modifying update/get behavior, preserve notification and snapshot semantics and maintain localStorage key names so existing subscribers and possible storage-event listeners continue to observe expected updates.

key_files.why: Defines default LocalSettings, persistence keys, and thread-level model helpers — central to how settings are persisted and restored.
Implements the in-browser store, subscription API, and update/get snapshots used by hooks and UI components.
Provides the React-facing hooks (useLocalSettings, useThreadSettings) that compose storage/merge logic and the store for component consumption.
Exports the public types and hooks — changing exports or types here affects all importers across the frontend.

construct_relationships: useLocalSettings (hooks.ts) reads defaults/merge helpers from local.ts, queries the store (store.ts) for snapshots, and calls updateLocalSettings in the store to persist changes.

useThreadSettings (hooks.ts) uses thread model helpers from local.ts (getThreadModelName, applyThreadModelOverride) and the store's thread-specific snapshot/update functions to maintain per-thread overrides.
store.ts relies on the storage key constants from local.ts (LOCAL_SETTINGS_KEY, THREAD_MODEL_KEY_PREFIX) to read/write localStorage and to compute snapshots returned to subscribers.
index.ts re-exports LocalSettings type and hook functions so downstream UI modules import settings functionality from a single entry point.
notable_relationships: These frontend settings persist to browser localStorage and therefore interact with the runtime environment (Next.js SSR). isBrowser guards in local.ts are critical to prevent server-side access errors.

Per-thread model name persistence affects which model is selected for API calls; changes here can change backend model selection behavior indirectly. Any naming changes to storage keys will affect persisted user preferences across upgrades.
UI components across the frontend import the hooks/index exports; changes to the hooks' API, notification timing, or snapshot shape will cascade to many components.
If other frontend modules implement cross-tab sync (storage event listeners) or depend on specific localStorage key formats, changing keys or value shapes will break that integration.
recommendations: Preserve localStorage key names (LOCAL_SETTINGS_KEY, THREAD_MODEL_KEY_PREFIX) or provide migration logic when renaming; assume persisted values may exist in users' browsers.
Keep isBrowser guards around any localStorage or window access so server-side rendering (Next.js) and non-browser environments do not throw errors on import or execution.
Maintain the store's subscribe/get/update semantics and snapshot shapes (base settings vs thread snapshots) so hooks and existing subscribers continue to receive expected updates.

When changing mergeLocalSettings or applyThreadModelOverride behavior, ensure that initialization still applies persisted per-thread overrides and that updates are written back to localStorage atomically.
Run frontend tests and exercise UI flows that rely on settings persistence (thread switching, model selection, browser reload) and verify multi-tab behavior if cross-tab sync is expected.

frontend/src/core/skills.description: This folder provides the frontend client surface for interacting with the Skills subsystem: typed network wrappers for skill-related backend endpoints, React hooks that expose those network calls to UI components, and a small public barrel export. It matters because it is the single place UI code uses to list, enable, and install skills and therefore must remain consistent with backend API contracts, authentication, and the app's state/caching conventions.
key_behaviors: Fetch and return the list of available/installed skills from the backend API
Trigger enable/disable operations for a skill and surface success/error state to callers
Send install requests for skills (payload -> InstallSkillRequest) and parse backend responses (InstallSkillResponse)
Expose hooks that wrap the above API calls for use by UI components, including loading/error state and side effects (cache invalidation, optimistic updates where applicable)
Provide a stable public API via index re-exports so other frontend modules import types, API helpers, and hooks from a single path

insights.title: Network wrappers must match backend request/response shapes
insights.problem: Frontend api.ts constructs requests and parses responses for load/enable/install operations but can diverge from backend shapes (field names, optional properties, error formats).
insights.root_cause: The frontend type definitions and runtime parsing live only in this folder and are decoupled from backend schemas, enabling silent mismatches when backend changes.
insights.solution: Treat the api.ts types and parsing as authoritative for frontend behavior; when editing, validate request and response shapes against the Gateway API (and update tests/mocks). Ensure InstallSkillRequest/InstallSkillResponse reflect backend fields and error formats.

insights.title: Hooks must preserve loading/error semantics and any cache invalidation
insights.problem: useSkills and useEnableSkill encapsulate asynchronous flows and are likely used directly by UI components; changes can break expected loading indicators, error messages, or cache updates.
insights.root_cause: The folder centralizes the hook behavior; callers rely on stable return values and side effects (e.g., refetch or mutate skill lists after enable/install).
insights.solution: When changing hook signatures or behavior, maintain the public shape (returned fields: data, isLoading, error, mutate/refetch) and ensure any necessary cache invalidation or refetching is performed so UIs stay consistent.

insights.title: Skill type must remain compatible across consumers
insights.problem: The Skill type in type.ts is the canonical type used by api.ts and hooks.ts. Divergence (missing fields, changed optionality) will cause type errors or runtime issues in components.
insights.root_cause: A single type definition is referenced throughout the folder and exported via index.ts; changes here ripple outward.
insights.solution: Preserve or carefully evolve the Skill type; update all calling code and tests when changing fields. Ensure index.ts re-exports continue to expose the correct type symbols.

insights.title: Index re-export is the public entrypoint for other frontend modules
insights.problem: Components import API, hooks, and types from this folder's barrel export. Removing or renaming exports will break imports across the app.
insights.root_cause: index.ts consolidates exports; consumers rely on that single path.
insights.solution: Keep index.ts exports stable or add deprecation layers; update all consumer imports and the build if changing exported names or locations.

key_files.why: Contains the network wrappers that call backend skill endpoints (load, enable, install) and defines request/response shapes used by hooks and components.
Implements React hooks (useSkills, useEnableSkill) that encapsulate async logic, loading/error state, and side effects — primary interface for UI components.
Declares the Skill type used across api.ts, hooks.ts, and the rest of the app; changing it affects typing and runtime expectations.
Barrel file that re-exports the folder's API and types; it's the public import path for other frontend modules.

construct_relationships: hooks.ts calls functions exported from api.ts to perform network requests and map results into hook state
api.ts uses InstallSkillRequest and InstallSkillResponse shapes defined in type.ts (or closely associated types) to construct and parse HTTP payloads
index.ts re-exports api.ts, hooks.ts, and type.ts, so UI components import hooks and types via the folder barrel rather than file paths
UI components (outside this folder) depend on the stable return shape from hooks (data, isLoading, error, mutate/refetch) and on api.ts to handle authorization headers and error mapping
notable_relationships: Upstream: Gateway API / backend skill endpoints — network shapes, auth, and error semantics must be honored; any backend changes to /api/skills endpoints require coordinated frontend updates here.

Cross-folder: This folder likely integrates with global networking/auth utilities (e.g., fetch wrappers or axios instances) and app-level state/caching (react-query/mutation cache) — edits must respect those shared utilities and conventions.
Downstream: UI components, pages, and tests consume the hooks and types exported here; changes ripple into many places in the frontend.
Build/CI: TypeScript types and exported symbols affect linting/typecheck and Next.js build; renames or signature changes will cause CI failures unless updated across imports and tests.
recommendations: When editing API wrappers, verify request/response shapes against the backend Gateway API and update InstallSkillRequest/InstallSkillResponse accordingly; run integration tests or API contract mocks.

When editing hooks, preserve the public hook return contract (data, isLoading, error, refetch/mutate) or update all callers; ensure cache invalidation/refetch behavior remains correct so UI reflects enable/install actions.
Keep type.ts changes backward-compatible or update all consuming code; run the TypeScript typecheck and frontend unit tests after any type edits.
Do not remove or rename exports from index.ts without updating all imports; run a repo-wide search for imports and the frontend build (pnpm build) and lints/typechecks as part of validation.
Respect app-wide networking and auth conventions (headers, credentials, error handling) used elsewhere — run relevant frontend tests and CI checks (lint, typecheck, build) after changes.
Add or update unit tests/mocks for api.ts and hooks.ts to cover success, error, and edge cases (network failures, unexpected response shapes) and run the frontend test suite and CI workflows.

frontend/src/server/better-auth.description: This folder contains server-side helper code that wraps and configures the BetterAuth session functionality for the Next.js frontend. It centralizes the session type, a configured auth instance, a client proxy, and a server-side session retrieval utility used by server components and API routes. It matters because these modules define the runtime contract for authentication and session retrieval across the frontend server runtime, and changes here impact SSR, API route auth behavior, and any server-only code that expects session semantics.
key_behaviors: Provide a single configured auth object exported for server-side use (config/index).
Expose a client proxy object to call the BetterAuth session API from server code.
Supply a typed Session shape shared across client and server helpers.
Offer a server-side getSession function that retrieves (and possibly caches) the current user session for server code paths.

insights.title: Shared Session type across modules
insights.problem: Multiple modules declare/use a Session construct; inconsistencies will cause type mismatches across server/client boundaries.
insights.root_cause: Session type is defined/used in both config and client modules and is relied upon by callers expecting a single canonical shape.
insights.solution: Ensure the Session type is exported from a single source and preserved across imports; any modification to fields or optionality must be propagated to all modules and consumers (server components, API routes, and tests).

insights.title: Server-side execution assumptions
insights.problem: Functions assume server runtime APIs (cookies, request context, Node-only APIs) and will fail if used in a browser/edge context.
insights.root_cause: These modules are intended for Next.js server runtime and may use Node or server-only BetterAuth capabilities.
insights.solution: Any edits must maintain server-only usage boundaries; do not introduce browser/edge-only code paths or cause imports that bundle into client code. Validate usage locations and keep server-only guards intact.

insights.title: Session retrieval and caching semantics
insights.problem: getSession may implement caching or rely on a cached session; stale cache could return invalid session data to server renderers.
insights.root_cause: Server-side helper may cache session per-request or process; caches can outlive session changes like logout/refresh tokens.
insights.solution: When changing retrieval logic, respect current cache invalidation boundaries (per-request vs. process) and ensure callers that expect immediate session changes (sign-out, token refresh) get fresh data. Consider request-scoped access patterns.

insights.title: Authentication material handling
insights.problem: Auth configuration and client proxies interact with secrets and cookie headers; accidental exposure or mishandling can leak auth material.
insights.root_cause: Auth modules use environment config and runtime request cookies/headers; improper serialization, logging, or client-side bundling risks exposure.
insights.solution: Keep secret handling server-only, avoid serializing sensitive tokens, and preserve any existing non-logging and secure cookie handling behavior. Do not introduce client-bundled imports of server-only auth internals.

insights.title: Stable public exports expected by consumers
insights.problem: Other files import auth, client, and getSession from these paths; renames or signature changes will break consumers.
insights.root_cause: Index and config modules re-export a configured auth object and types; callers import these symbols directly.
insights.solution: Preserve exported symbol names and their call signatures (including sync/async behavior) or update all callers in tandem. Run frontend type checks and builds to detect breakage.

key_files.why: Defines and exposes the configured auth instance and the canonical Session type used across server helpers.
Implements the client proxy that server code uses to call the BetterAuth session API; changes affect API call shape and error handling.
Provides getSession used by server components and API routes to obtain the current session; central to server-side auth behavior and caching semantics.
Re-exports the configured auth surface for consumers and establishes the module boundary imported elsewhere in the frontend server code.

construct_relationships: config.ts defines and exports auth and Session which are imported by client.ts and server.ts to ensure a single session shape and configured auth instance.
index.ts re-exports the auth object from config.ts, acting as the public entry point for other frontend server modules.
client.ts provides a proxy implementation that delegates to the BetterAuth session API and returns a Session instance typed as the Session from config.ts.
server.ts exposes getSession which uses the configured auth and/or client proxy to read session state in server contexts and may rely on request-scoped data provided by the runtime.
notable_relationships: This folder must integrate with Next.js server runtime boundaries (server components, API routes) — callers from pages/app routes will import getSession to gate rendering or fetch user info.
Auth configuration likely depends on environment variables or top-level frontend configuration; changes here can affect CI/frontend build if types or exports change.
If the backend Gateway or API routes change auth cookies or session shape, this folder's Session type and retrieval logic must remain compatible to avoid runtime auth breaks.
Files in this folder are server-only; incorrect imports into client bundles would create cross-folder leakage and runtime errors.
recommendations: Preserve the exported symbol names and call signatures (auth, Session type shape, getSession) unless all import sites are updated together.
Verify edits only run in server runtime: ensure code paths do not import browser-only modules and do not get included in client bundles.
When changing session retrieval or caching behavior, validate with server-render flows: run frontend build/typecheck and server-side rendering paths that call getSession.
Run TypeScript typecheck, frontend build, and any auth-related unit/integration tests after changes to catch typing and bundling regressions.
Avoid introducing logging or serialization of secrets; ensure cookie/header handling remains server-only and consistent with existing backend cookie/auth expectations.

frontend/src/core/messages.description: Frontend message utilities responsible for normalizing, grouping, and extracting displayable content from a variety of message shapes and for extracting/aggregating token-usage metadata emitted by AI responses. This folder is the translation layer between raw message objects (including streamed fragments, internal tool messages, and assistant subagent output) and UI-facing message groups and usage summaries used by rendering and billing/telemetry components.
key_behaviors: Parse raw message objects into a small set of UI message group types (human, assistant, processing, present-files, clarification, subagent) to drive rendering and interaction.
Hide or surface messages based on internal/internal-tool detection rules so that background/system messages do not appear in user-facing conversations.
Extract text/content from heterogeneous message payloads (streamed fragments, nested tool outputs) for display and for downstream processing (search, summarization).
Collect, normalize and aggregate token usage metadata across streamed or multi-part AI responses to produce per-message usage summaries and formatted counts.

insights.title: Token usage metadata must be normalized and aggregated
insights.problem: Streamed AI responses include usage_metadata fragments that were not typed or aggregated in the frontend, resulting in missing or incorrect usage summaries.
insights.root_cause: Usage metadata arrived as partial/fragmented payloads and there was no typed, centralized utility to extract and accumulate those fragments into a final usage object.
insights.solution: Maintain a typed usage model in the frontend and use the provided extraction and accumulation utilities to merge streamed fragments; ensure formatting utilities produce stable human-readable counts for billing/telemetry displays.

insights.title: Internal/tool messages can leak into the user-facing conversation
insights.problem: Background or internal tool messages (e.g., autosave, telemetry, subagent internals) were presented to users as regular messages.
insights.root_cause: Message parsing/grouping did not consistently recognize or filter hidden/internal tool message shapes across all message sources.
insights.solution: Keep a centralized set of rules in message grouping utilities to detect and hide internal messages; ensure any new message kind is checked against these rules to avoid accidental exposure.

insights.title: Message extraction must handle diverse/ evolving message shapes
insights.problem: Messages can arrive from different producers (main assistant, subagents, tools, streamed partials) with differing payload shapes, leading to fragile extraction logic.
insights.root_cause: A single extraction path assumed a narrow message shape; when tool outputs or subagent payloads differ the extractor either produced empty content or surfaced raw, unformatted payloads.
insights.solution: Ensure extractTextFromMessage / extractContentFromMessage cover the known variants (plain text, structured content, arrays, nested tool outputs) and fail gracefully; keep extraction functions small, well-typed, and central so downstream renderers rely on consistent outputs.

insights.title: Streaming semantics affect both content and usage accounting
insights.problem: Partial/streamed assistant messages need to be merged for display and for token counts; treating fragments independently leads to duplicate or incomplete UI state and incorrect token totals.
insights.root_cause: No unified handling of streamed fragments for both visible text assembly and usage_metadata merging.
insights.solution: Use the accumulation utilities to merge streaming fragments for content and usage; consumers must expect partial updates and rely on idempotent merging logic to assemble final message content and totals.

key_files.why: Core message grouping and content-extraction logic that maps raw messages to UI-facing message group types and implements hiding/filtering rules used by renderers.
Utilities for extracting, typing, aggregating, and formatting token usage metadata from AI responses, critical for billing/telemetry and usage displays.

construct_relationships: groupMessages (utils.ts) categorizes raw message objects into HumanMessageGroup, AssistantMessageGroup, AssistantProcessingGroup, and specialized assistant groups (present-files, clarification, subagent) used by UI renderers.
extractTextFromMessage / extractContentFromMessage (utils.ts) are used by grouping and downstream renderers to produce stable display text from heterogeneous message payloads.

TokenUsage, getUsageMetadata, accumulateUsage and formatTokenCount (usage.ts) form a pipeline: getUsageMetadata pulls usage fragments from incoming message payloads; accumulateUsage merges fragments across streamed messages; formatTokenCount renders totals for the UI.
Visibility/filtering rules in utils.ts interact with extraction logic: messages detected as internal/tool messages are suppressed before they reach display or usage aggregation paths.
notable_relationships: Frontend UI components and message store depend on the grouping types and extraction outputs here; changes will directly affect rendering, message ordering, and interactivity (e.g., file-presenting flows and clarification prompts).

Streaming endpoints or websocket/ SSE handlers that deliver partial assistant messages must produce the usage_metadata fragments and message IDs expected by the usage accumulation utilities; mismatches will cause incorrect token totals.

Backend message shape/schema (Gateway / LangGraph / subagents) is an upstream concern: the utilities assume certain shapes for internal-tool messages, subagent outputs, and usage_metadata—any server-side change to those shapes requires corresponding updates here.
Billing/telemetry and permission-related UI elements read formatted token counts from usage utilities—incorrect aggregation or formatting will impact displayed costs/limits.
recommendations: Preserve existing discriminants and type shapes used to identify message group kinds (human/assistant/processing/present-files/clarification/subagent) to avoid breaking UI components that switch on these types.

When editing extraction or grouping logic, ensure all known message producers (assistant, subagent, tools, streamed fragments) remain recognized; add or update unit tests that cover each producer and streamed partials.

If touching usage aggregation, respect streamed partial merging semantics: accumulateUsage must be idempotent and tolerant of reordered or partial fragments; update any tests that assert final totals.
Do not expose hidden/internal/tool messages to UI renderers; maintain or update the central hide/filter rules in one place so other parts of the app cannot inadvertently surface them.

Run frontend typechecks, lint, and build (pnpm typecheck / pnpm lint / pnpm build) after changes; verify components that consume these utilities (message list, file viewer, billing display) behave correctly in both normal and streaming flows.

frontend/src/core/streamdown.description: This folder provides the streaming rendering presets (plugins) used by the frontend streamdown renderer and re-exports them for consumers. It centralizes formatting, math rendering, human-message handling and optional word-animation plugin sets so UI components can import a standardized plugin surface for rendering streaming AI/chat output.
key_behaviors: Expose a curated set of streamdown plugins and plugin presets as a single import surface for the rest of the frontend.
Provide composable plugin presets: a baseline set, an augmented set with word-by-word animation, and a set specialized for human messages.
Act as the integration point between streamdown core APIs and UI components that render streaming content (math formatting, text formatting, animations).

insights.title: Single re-export entrypoint
insights.problem: Multiple internal plugin definitions need to be imported consistently across the app. Consumers depend on stable named exports.
insights.root_cause: Index file re-exports everything from plugins.ts creating a single public surface; changing export names or removing exports will break imports across the codebase.
insights.solution: When modifying exports, preserve existing named exports or coordinate updates with all consumers; treat index.ts as the stable public API.

insights.title: Plugins may depend on DOM / client-only APIs
insights.problem: Some plugins (e.g., animations or math renderers) are likely to use window/document or third‑party browser-only libraries which will fail during server-side rendering or static builds.
insights.root_cause: Plugins for rendering streams commonly perform DOM mutations or rely on browser globals and heavy client-side libraries.
insights.solution: Any edits must respect client-only execution boundaries: guard side-effectful code, ensure proper lazy-loading or conditional execution so server-side builds and tests do not fail.

insights.title: Heavy third-party libs may be included by presets
insights.problem: Including math renderers or animation code directly in presets can increase bundle size and runtime cost for consumers that don't need them.
insights.root_cause: Presets combine multiple concerns (formatting, math, animations) into single exports that consumers may import wholesale.
insights.solution: Be mindful of import semantics and tree-shaking: maintain modular exports so consumers can import only what they need; verify builds and runtime sizes after changes.

insights.title: Plugin ordering affects output
insights.problem: The order in which plugins are composed can change rendering results (e.g., formatting before animation vs animation before formatting).
insights.root_cause: Streamdown processes plugins in sequence and certain transformations are order-dependent.
insights.solution: When changing plugin lists or ordering, validate key rendering scenarios to ensure transformations still compose correctly for human messages, math blocks, and animated flows.

key_files.why: Public re-export entrypoint used by other frontend modules to import the streamdown plugin presets.
Defines the plugin preset collections (streamdownPlugins, streamdownPluginsWithWordAnimation, humanMessagePlugins) and is the source of rendering behavior.

construct_relationships: index.ts re-exports named plugin presets defined in plugins.ts, making those presets the canonical imports for consumers.
streamdownPlugins and streamdownPluginsWithWordAnimation are variations of the same base plugin set where the latter composes additional animation-related plugins on top of the baseline.
humanMessagePlugins is a specialized preset likely tailored for rendering user/human messages and shares formatting/math plugin constructs with the other presets.
notable_relationships: Consumers in other frontend folders (UI components that render chat/assistant output) import the presets from this folder; any change to export names or plugin behavior will affect rendering across the UI.

Presets implicitly depend on streamdown core runtime and potentially on browser-only libraries (math renderers, animation helpers). These external dependencies and streamdown core APIs must remain compatible.
Next.js SSR/static build pipeline and bundler (pnpm/webpack/next) are upstream concerns: client-only plugin behavior or heavy libs can cause server build failures or inflated client bundles.
recommendations: Preserve existing named exports and their types/signatures or update all consumers in lockstep; treat index.ts as a stable public API surface.
When editing plugin code, check for usage of browser globals (window, document) and ensure code paths are guarded or lazy-loaded to avoid SSR/build-time errors.
Run frontend typecheck, lint, and production build (pnpm typecheck/lint/build) after changes to catch API or bundling regressions; run relevant component/story tests that render streaming content.
Validate rendering outcomes for key scenarios: plain text, formatted text, math blocks, human messages, and the animated word-by-word flow to ensure ordering/transformations remain correct.

Monitor bundle size and tree-shaking behavior after edits (especially if adding or referencing third-party math/animation libs); confirm that consumers that don't import specific presets are not impacted.
Respect accessibility and user preferences when touching animation-related code (e.g., prefers-reduced-motion must be honored by any animation plugins).

frontend/src/content/en.description: This folder contains the English-language documentation content and navigation metadata used by the frontend docs site. It is a static content bundle (MDX pages) plus per-section _meta.ts descriptors that together drive route generation, sidebar/navigation assembly, and locale-scoped site builds for the English locale.
key_behaviors: Compile-time content ingestion: MDX files in this folder are discovered and compiled into static documentation routes during the Next.js/MDX build step.
Navigation assembly: each directory-level _meta.ts exports metadata consumed by the docs/navigation layer to produce sidebars, ordering, titles and visibility.

Locale scoping: files live under the 'en' locale root and are treated as the canonical English content for the corresponding routes; routing and slug generation depend on folder structure and filenames.
Link and cross-page resolution: MDX content and metadata together produce internal links, sidebar entries and anchors that must remain consistent across edits.
Build-time validation: syntax issues in MDX or incorrect exports in _meta.ts will fail static generation and CI docs checks.

insights.title: Section _meta.ts drives routing and sidebar behavior
insights.problem: Changing or removing exported metadata fields in a _meta.ts can cause pages to disappear from navigation or be mis-ordered.
insights.root_cause: The docs/navigation system reads the exported meta record at build time to assemble sidebars and route entries; it does not infer order or titles from MDX content alone.
insights.solution: When modifying filenames, slugs, or display names, ensure corresponding _meta.ts exports remain correct and consistent; treat these files as a contract with the docs router.

insights.title: MDX syntax or export errors break static generation
insights.problem: Invalid MDX or incorrect TS exports will cause the Next.js build (and CI) to fail, preventing deployment of the documentation site.
insights.root_cause: MDX is compiled at build time and TypeScript metadata modules are statically imported; build tooling enforces correctness.
insights.solution: Any edits must preserve valid MDX syntax and correct TypeScript exports; run the frontend build and lint/type checks locally or in CI to validate changes.

insights.title: Locale folder semantics must be preserved
insights.problem: Moving or renaming files in the en locale without updating equivalent localized content or references can break cross-locale links and expectations.
insights.root_cause: The site uses per-locale folder structure to map routes and to support localized variants; other locales may depend on parallel file structure.
insights.solution: Respect the en folder's role as the English canonical content; when changing slugs or paths, verify any localized counterparts and update links that reference locale-specific routes.

insights.title: Internal links and anchors are fragile to filename or heading changes
insights.problem: Renaming MDX files or headings will invalidate internal links in other docs and sidebar references.
insights.root_cause: Links are frequently built from file paths or heading-derived anchors; there is no runtime redirect system inside the static content layer unless explicitly implemented.
insights.solution: When renaming pages or headings, update all cross-references and navigation metadata; run the site to confirm link resolution and search indexing.

insights.title: Doc edits impact CI build and frontend packaging
insights.problem: Changes that introduce type or formatting issues will be caught by frontend CI (formatting, lint, build) and block merges.
insights.root_cause: CI enforces pnpm format/lint/typecheck and Next.js build; documentation files and _meta.ts TypeScript modules are part of that pipeline.
insights.solution: Ensure edits conform to the frontend's linting/typechecking/format rules and verify a successful local pnpm build to avoid CI failures.

key_files.why: Top-level English content metadata that participates in how all English docs sections are exposed to the site navigation and router.
Navigation metadata for the Introduction section; edits affect ordering and titles of the core conceptual docs.
Navigation metadata for the Application section which controls how operational/configuration guides appear in the sidebar and routes.
Representative MDX page with explanatory content that will be compiled into a route; editing it affects visible docs under the Introduction section.
Entry MDX for the Application docs section; renames or path changes will affect the landing route and sidebar grouping for application docs.

construct_relationships: Each directory-level _meta.ts exports a meta object that the docs/navigation system imports at build time to assemble sidebars, order, titles and visibility for the MDX pages in that directory.
MDX files are treated as routeable pages where filename and folder path determine the generated route/slug which is referenced by navigation metadata and internal links.
The 'en' locale folder is part of a larger locale-aware content tree; parallel locale folders (not shown) are expected to mirror structure for translations and route parity.
Navigation components and the static site generator consume both the MDX content and the _meta.ts metadata — changes to either side affect final rendering and route entries.
notable_relationships: Frontend build pipeline (pnpm build / Next.js) — edits in this folder directly affect static generation and must pass the frontend CI job (format, lint, typecheck, build).
Search/indexing and any site-level link-check tooling — path or heading changes here will require re-indexing or may surface broken link reports in CI.
Cross-locale documentation — changes to en may need corresponding updates in other locale folders or translation workflows.
Navigation components in the frontend codebase — these consume the exported meta objects from _meta.ts files and will reflect any metadata changes immediately in the UI.
recommendations: Preserve valid MDX syntax and TypeScript exports in any edited files; run pnpm format, pnpm lint and the Next.js build locally before committing.
When renaming files, headings, or slugs, update the corresponding _meta.ts entries and all internal links/anchors that reference them to avoid broken navigation.
Treat _meta.ts modules as the authoritative contract for sidebar order, titles and visibility; validate that edits keep those contracts intact.
If changing routes or visible titles, check for parallel localized files and update or coordinate with translation workflows to maintain parity.
After changes, verify the docs site locally (static generation) and run CI-equivalent checks (format/lint/typecheck/build) to catch integration issues early.

frontend/src/core/rehype.description: This folder provides a rehype plugin and a convenience hook for splitting CJK (Chinese/Japanese/Korean) text into span elements at render time. It exists to transform HTML/MDX output so CJK characters or words can be wrapped individually (for styling, line breaking, selection behavior, or tooling) and to expose an easy-to-use integration point for the frontend rendering pipeline.
key_behaviors: Transforms rehype AST text nodes to wrap CJK characters/words in span elements.
Exposes a reusable plugin factory/hook for registering the transformation in the unified/rehype pipeline used by the frontend (e.g., MDX/markdown renderers).
Is intended to be safe to include in browser and server rendering flows and to be idempotent when applied once per render.

insights.title: Server-side and client-side output must match to avoid hydration issues
insights.problem: If the plugin produces different markup on server vs client (e.g., due to runtime-only detection or non-deterministic traversal), Next.js hydration warnings or mismatched DOM can occur.
insights.root_cause: Transformations that depend on runtime environment, locale, or non-deterministic iteration over text nodes can yield different outputs between SSR and client hydration.
insights.solution: Ensure the plugin's logic is deterministic and environment-neutral; any branching must be stable across SSR and client. Treat the plugin output as part of the serialized HTML produced by SSR so hydration receives identical structure.

insights.title: Avoid double-wrapping already split text
insights.problem: Applying the plugin multiple times (or in multiple pipeline stages) can wrap spans repeatedly, creating nested spans or corrupt markup.
insights.root_cause: Transforms that act only on text nodes without checking parent/ancestor node types or existing marker classes will reprocess content that was already transformed.
insights.solution: Plugin should detect and skip nodes that are already wrapped (e.g., check for existing span with a class or a marker attribute) and avoid modifying certain elements (code, pre, style, script, a) that should be left intact.

insights.title: Must preserve semantic nodes and attributes
insights.problem: Blindly splitting text nodes can strip attributes, lose semantic tags, or alter content of non-rendered or interactive elements (links, code blocks, images captions).
insights.root_cause: Transforming across the AST without narrowing the set of eligible nodes exposes structural nodes to unintended edits.
insights.solution: Only operate on plain text nodes in permitted parent contexts; explicitly skip code, preformatted blocks, headings if necessary, links, and elements with contentEditable=false. Preserve original text content ordering and any adjacent nodes/whitespace.

insights.title: Processing cost for large documents or many nodes
insights.problem: Walking large ASTs and allocating a span per character/word can be CPU- and memory-intensive, causing slow render or jank.
insights.root_cause: Per-character or per-word DOM node creation scales poorly for long documents or high-frequency usage.
insights.solution: Operations should be efficient and avoid unnecessary allocations. Guard plugin registration so it is not applied redundantly; consider batching or early-exit heuristics and ensure tests/benchmarks cover large-content scenarios.

insights.title: Maintain accessibility and selection semantics
insights.problem: Inserting inline spans can change screen-reader reading order or copy/paste text content if not implemented carefully.
insights.root_cause: Span wrappers may introduce roles, CSS, or attributes that alter accessibility tree or selection behavior.
insights.solution: Keep spans semantic-neutral (no interfering ARIA roles by default), preserve textual content order, and avoid adding show/hide CSS or attributes that remove text from the accessibility tree. Ensure copy/paste produces original text sequence.

key_files.why: Entry point for the folder: exports the rehype plugin (rehypeSplitWordsIntoSpans) and the convenience hook/use factory (useRehypeSplitWordsIntoSpans) used by the frontend rendering pipeline.

construct_relationships: rehypeSplitWordsIntoSpans: a rehype transformer that traverses text nodes in the HTML AST and replaces or wraps CJK character sequences with span elements.

useRehypeSplitWordsIntoSpans: a convenience wrapper/factory that configures or returns the rehypeSplitWordsIntoSpans plugin ready to be registered in the unified/rehype pipeline used by MDX/markdown rendering.
notable_relationships: This plugin is consumed by the frontend markdown/MDX rendering pipeline (e.g., wherever rehype plugins are passed into the renderer). Changes here affect produced HTML used by Next.js SSR and client hydration.
CSS and UI components may rely on specific class names or structure inserted by the plugin; renaming classes or markup shape can have downstream visual and functional effects.
Accessibility, copy/paste, and selection behaviors in other parts of the app (chat messages, docs, editor previews) depend on the exact output structure produced by this plugin.
Tests and snapshot files in the frontend that assert rendered HTML will be directly impacted by changes to plugin output.
recommendations: When editing, preserve the public exports and signatures: maintain a plugin function and a factory/hook with compatible parameters expected by existing callers.
Ensure transformations are deterministic and identical under SSR and client to avoid hydration mismatches; run Next.js dev SSR/hydration flows and build to confirm.
Make the plugin idempotent: detect and skip already-wrapped nodes and explicitly skip sensitive parents (code, pre, style, script, a) to avoid corrupting semantic elements.
Keep span wrappers semantic-neutral (no ARIA roles or behavior-altering attributes) unless callers expect them; verify screen-reader behavior and copy/paste results.
Run frontend build, linting, and relevant UI snapshot/integration tests after changes; check components and CSS that select by classnames or DOM structure that the plugin produces.
Consider performance implications: test with large markdown/HTML samples to ensure traversal and node creation do not regress render time or memory usage.

backend/packages/harness.description: This subtree implements the in-process DeerFlow harness: agent assembly (lead-agent factory, middleware ordering and feature flags), system-prompt composition with a cached enabled-skills section, the memory pipeline (queue + updater + storage helpers), local sandbox path mapping and sandbox-aware tools, and an embedded Python client for running the agent without the server.
