Changes here directly affect runtime behavior, safety boundaries (sandboxing, subagent delegation, loop detection), and in-process integration tests, so edits must preserve non-blocking startup, sandbox invariants, and correct propagation of runtime signals.
key_behaviors: Assemble a runtime lead-agent: resolve model selection, build system prompt (with cached enabled-skills), attach ordered middleware chain and tools, and create per-run Thread/Sandbox state.

Inject and surface uploaded artifacts: UploadsMiddleware prepends structured uploaded-file metadata/outlines into the last human message for agent consumption while preserving runtime kwargs and message IDs.

Debounced asynchronous memory extraction: MemoryMiddleware filters ephemeral/tool chatter, detects corrections/reinforcements, enqueues per-thread contexts to MemoryUpdateQueue, then MemoryUpdater deduplicates/merges and persists facts via storage.
Local sandbox mapping and tool gating: translate virtual/container-style paths to host paths, enforce read-only mounts, mask host paths in outputs, and validate commands/paths before execution.

Subagent task delegation and lifecycle streaming: task_tool spawns background subagents via SubagentExecutor, polls their lifecycle, emits structured stream events, and cooperatively cancels/cleans up on cancellation.

insights.title: Avoid blocking filesystem I/O during imports or request paths
insights.problem: Loading enabled skills or other disk-backed data synchronously at import or during request handling caused slow startups and request latency spikes.
insights.root_cause: Synchronous disk access in prompt construction and import-time cache priming (or doing I/O on the event loop), plus code paths that reloaded skills repeatedly.
insights.solution: Use background refresh workers, non-blocking cache priming, synchronous warm/invalidate helpers for coroutine paths, and move filesystem mkdirs/offloads off the main event loop to avoid blocking.

insights.title: Defensive handling of missing runtime context or fields
insights.problem: Assumptions that runtime.context and thread_id are always present caused TypeError/NPEs and feature failures when LangGraph supplied None or different shapes.
insights.root_cause: Code wrote into or read from runtime.context without null-checks and relied on config.configurable thread_id being present.
insights.solution: Null-check runtime.context and fallback to config/configurable sources where appropriate; guard callers that read/write thread-scoped directories and thread_id.

insights.title: Consistent virtual↔host path translation and read-only enforcement
insights.problem: Path translation could leak host layout, drop trailing separators, mix path separators, and did not enforce multi-mount read-only semantics causing confusing outputs or accidental writes.
insights.root_cause: replace/resolve helpers altered separators/trailing slashes and lacked support for multiple mounts with read-only flags; outputs returned host paths instead of virtual paths.
insights.solution: Preserve virtual path separator style and trailing separators during translation, enforce read-only mount checks prior to write ops and mask host paths in outputs by reverse-resolution before returning results.

insights.title: Preserve and propagate correction/reinforcement signals through queue/updater
insights.problem: Correction and reinforcement flags were lost or overwritten when queue entries were replaced or when memory updater didn’t surface reinforcement hints, reducing signal fidelity for memory extraction.
insights.root_cause: Queue.add replaced pending updates without merging flags; MemoryUpdater and prompts did not include reinforcement/correction guidance or sourceError preservation.
insights.solution: Merge and preserve detection flags (correction_detected, reinforcement_detected) when batching per-thread; ensure MemoryUpdater includes these hints and preserves source metadata when persisting facts.

insights.title: Stable normalization of tool-call arguments and message content types
insights.problem: LoopDetectionMiddleware failed to detect semantically-equivalent repeated calls due to noisy or inconsistently-formatted arguments; it also assumed AIMessage.content was a string leading to type errors with list-based content.
insights.root_cause: Hashing used raw or lightly-stringified arguments and concatenated text assuming str types; providers may yield structured AIMessage.content (lists) and non-dict args.
insights.solution: Normalize and canonicalize tool-call arguments defensively (stable JSON serialization, canonical key ordering), derive order-independent multiset hashes, and handle list-type AIMessage.content when appending warnings/stop-text.

insights.title: Avoid synchronous filesystem operations on the async event loop
insights.problem: Async checkpointer initialization performed blocking mkdirs on the event loop causing potential stalls in async servers.
insights.root_cause: Filesystem setup logic executed directly in async initialization paths rather than using thread offloads or pre-creation.
insights.solution: Ensure filesystem parent-dir creation and other blocking setup runs off the event loop (threadpool or pre-init) and centralize SQLite helper logic to shared runtime/store utilities.

insights.title: Embedded client must mirror server runtime hooks and skill filtering
insights.problem: DeerFlowClient previously did not forward custom stream-mode events, lacked available_skills injection for runtime filtering, and lacked import/export memory helpers expected by programmatic consumers.
insights.root_cause: Stream handling assumed simple value streams, and client construction lacked APIs to pass runtime skill filters or memory import/export utilities.
insights.solution: Expose StreamEvent shapes that include custom events, accept available_skills/runtime overrides when constructing the in-process agent, and provide programmatic memory import/export methods in the client surface.

insights.title: Model resolution, middleware enablement and ordering must be deterministic and configurable
insights.problem: Summarization middleware used unresolved alias strings, TokenUsage middleware needed to be opt-in, and inserting extra middleware risked breaking intended anchor ordering.
insights.root_cause: Middleware factories sometimes received raw config strings instead of resolved model objects; middleware insertion logic lacked strict anchoring semantics unless explicit Next/Prev anchors used.
insights.solution: Resolve model aliases before building middleware, gate telemetry middleware behind config flags, and respect anchoring semantics when inserting extra middleware to preserve execution order.

key_files.why: Primary lead-agent factory: resolves runtime model, assembles middleware chain, and wires model/tools/system prompt — edits here affect the core run lifecycle and middleware ordering.
System prompt builder and enabled-skills cache: controls dynamic prompt injection and background cache behavior that impacts agent guidance and startup latency.
Memory update logic and persistence: centralizes fact parsing, deduplication, correction/reinforcement handling, and storage delegation — changes affect long-term memory correctness.
Local sandbox path mapping and enforcement: translates virtual↔host paths, enforces read-only mounts and sanitizes outputs — critical for sandbox safety and user-visible I/O.
Embedded in-process client: constructs/caches the agent and exposes programmatic APIs (chat/stream/uploads/memory); tests and integrations use this instead of spinning servers.

construct_relationships: make_lead_agent (lead_agent/agent.py) composes the system prompt (prompt.py), resolves a chat model, and attaches an ordered middleware chain produced via create_deerflow_agent (agents/factory.py).
Prompt builder (prompt.py) supplies the enabled-skills prompt section that is injected into the system prompt assembled by make_lead_agent; the cache is primed at import via agents/__init__.py.

UploadsMiddleware (middlewares/uploads_middleware.py) mutates the last HumanMessage before agent execution so downstream middlewares (e.g., MemoryMiddleware) and tools see injected uploaded-file metadata.

MemoryMiddleware (middlewares/memory_middleware.py) filters messages and detects correction/reinforcement, then enqueues ConversationContext objects into MemoryUpdateQueue (memory/queue.py), which delegates processing to MemoryUpdater (memory/updater.py) and ultimately to pluggable MemoryStorage backends.

LocalSandbox (sandbox/local/local_sandbox.py) provides path resolution used by sandbox tooling (sandbox/tools.py); tools call replace_virtual_path/resolve helpers to gate reads/writes and ensure outputs are masked back to virtual paths.

task_tool (tools/builtins/task_tool.py) validates sandbox policy and constructs SubagentExecutor (subagents subsystem) with injected runtime metadata (e.g., available skills), then streams lifecycle events consumed by clients (client.py uses same event shapes).
notable_relationships: This harness is consumed both by the LangGraph server entrypoint and by the embedded DeerFlowClient; changes affect in-process clients, server-side runs, and tests that use the client to exercise agent behavior.

Sandbox helpers and tools interact with broader sandbox providers (local vs aio/dockerized) and with filesystem-backed skill mounts (/mnt/skills); edits here impact both local dev and Dockerized sandbox behavior and tests that exercise file operations.

Memory pipeline relies on storage adapters and checkpointer providers (checkpointer/async_provider/provider modules) and thus has cross-cutting interactions with persistence backends (sqlite/postgres) and event-loop initialization semantics.

Middleware ordering and anchoring affects other subsystems (token usage telemetry, summarization, uploads, clarification): reordering or changing anchoring semantics can alter what data middlewares see and therefore has behavioral impact across runs.
recommendations: Always null-check runtime.context and defensive-read thread_id where code assumes their presence; validate both LangGraph server and in-process client invocation shapes.
Avoid synchronous blocking I/O on imports or the async event loop: use background workers, thread offloads, or pre-init helpers for filesystem ops (skills loading, sqlite parent dir creation).

When modifying path translation or sandbox behavior, preserve virtual-path formatting (trailing separators, separator style), enforce read-only mounts pre-write, and ensure outputs are reverse-resolved to virtual paths to avoid leaking host layout.

Preserve and merge memory-related flags (correction_detected, reinforcement_detected) when batching/updating queue entries; ensure MemoryUpdater and prompts include these signals and preserve source metadata.
Maintain stable normalization for loop detection: canonicalize tool-call arguments, order-independent hashing, and handle structured AIMessage.content (lists) when appending warnings/hard-stop text.

Respect middleware anchoring and feature flags when inserting or removing middleware; resolving model aliases before passing into middleware factories is required for correct model objects (not raw alias strings).

When editing task_tool and background task lifecycles, ensure cooperative cancellation schedules cleanup and that polling/cancellation interacts correctly with SubagentExecutor; account for runtime paths without thread_id.

Run the backend unit test suite, memory and sandbox integration tests, and lint/format checks after edits; verify in-process client scenarios (streaming events, available_skills filtering, memory import/export) and async initialization paths (checkpointer) as part of verification.

frontend/src/content.description: This folder contains the English-language documentation content and per-section navigation metadata for the frontend docs site. It provides the MDX pages that are compiled into static routes and the _meta.ts descriptors that drive sidebar generation, titles, ordering and visibility during the Next.js/MDX build. Changes here directly affect route generation, navigation structure, and the localized documentation bundle consumed by the UI.
key_behaviors: Compile-time content ingestion: MDX files are discovered and compiled into static documentation routes during the Next.js build step.
Navigation assembly: directory-level _meta.ts files export metadata objects that the docs/navigation layer consumes to build sidebars, ordering, and route titles.
Locale scoping and routing: files live under the 'en' locale root and file paths/filenames map to route slugs and localized pages.
Content vs metadata separation: MDX pages hold page content while _meta.ts files provide structured navigation properties (visibility, order, display names).

insights.title: MDX filenames and folder structure drive static route generation
insights.problem: Renaming, moving, or removing MDX files will change generated slugs or break existing routes and links.
insights.root_cause: Next.js / MDX build discovers content by filesystem layout and uses file paths to create routes.
insights.solution: When editing content files, ensure any dependent routes/links and navigation metadata are updated; run a production build to validate generated routes.

insights.title: Section _meta.ts exports are authoritative for sidebar assembly
insights.problem: Incorrect or missing exports or schema shape in _meta.ts files will disrupt sidebar generation or cause runtime errors in the docs navigation layer.
insights.root_cause: _meta.ts files are imported by the docs/navigation logic and expected to match a MetaRecord shape (specific named exports).
insights.solution: Preserve export names/signatures and the expected metadata properties (order, title, visibility). Any change requires co-updating the navigation consumers and validating sidebar output.

insights.title: Locale-rooted content determines language-specific builds
insights.problem: Placing content in the wrong locale directory or duplicating slugs across locales can produce incorrect localized pages or conflict during build.
insights.root_cause: The docs system treats each locale folder as the canonical source for that language and maps files to locale-scoped routes.
insights.solution: Keep English content inside the en/ folder and ensure slugs are unique within that locale; verify i18n routing after changes.

insights.title: Documentation content changes require Next.js/MDX compile and CI checks
insights.problem: Content-only edits can still fail CI if MDX/TS exports or types are invalid, or if the build-time docs pipeline expects a specific structure.
insights.root_cause: MDX is compiled at build time and TypeScript metadata files are type-checked/linted in CI.
insights.solution: After edits, run the local dev server and a production build, and execute the frontend CI checks (pnpm build, lint, typecheck) to catch compilation or metadata issues early.

key_files.why: Top-level English docs metadata that influences global navigation and may be imported to assemble the site sidebar and route ordering.
Canonical conceptual documentation used by the introduction section; changing it alters core user-facing explanations.
Section-level metadata for the Application docs that controls titles, ordering and visibility of pages under /application.
Reference documentation that is likely referenced by operations or deployment guides; edits affect technical guidance pages.
Metadata for the Harness section which the docs navigation consumes to display and order harness-related pages.

construct_relationships: Each _meta.ts exports a MetaRecord object that the docs/navigation layer imports to build the sidebar and determine ordering/visibility of the MDX pages in that directory.
MDX files are compiled into static routes where the filesystem path (under en/) maps to the route slug consumed by Next.js; navigation metadata and link components reference those slugs.
Locale root (en/) groups content for English; route generation and slug resolution read file paths relative to this locale root.
The docs build pipeline imports both MDX content and _meta.ts metadata together to produce the final navigation structure and page rendering.
notable_relationships: Next.js build and MDX compiler: changes in this folder must be validated by the Next.js/MDX build step — the frontend runtime depends on the compiled output.
Docs/navigation consumer code elsewhere in the frontend expects specific export shapes from _meta.ts files; modifying exports impacts other frontend modules.
CI and lint/typecheck workflows: the repository's CI will run frontend build, lints and type checks which can fail on invalid metadata or MDX syntax.
Search indexing, sitemap generation and any static-site tooling downstream may rely on stable slugs and metadata fields (titles, visibility).
recommendations: Preserve export names and the expected MetaRecord/metadata shape in all _meta.ts files; if altering fields, update any consuming navigation code.
Do not change filenames or move pages without verifying and updating all references/links, since paths map directly to route slugs.
After edits, run the frontend dev server and a production build (pnpm build) and CI-local checks (lint/typecheck) to catch compilation or metadata errors.
Validate locale placement: keep English pages under en/ and ensure slug uniqueness within the locale to avoid routing conflicts.
When editing MDX, avoid introducing imports or runtime code that depend on components not bundled into the docs build; verify rendering in the built site.

frontend/src/core.description: The frontend/src/core folder centralizes the frontend's shared runtime primitives: API clients and adapters (LangGraph, agent management), artifact URL/loaders, config/URL resolution (SSR-aware), i18n/localization providers and strings, and lightweight React state layers (subtasks, settings, uploads, threads, models, memory, skills). It is the single shared surface consumed by most UI pages/components and must remain stable because changes propagate widely (network calls, server-side rendering, and cross-component hooks/providers).
key_behaviors: Create and return API clients for LangGraph and backend services (supports mock vs real modes) and sanitize run/stream options before initiating streams.
Expose typed REST API wrappers and react-query hooks for CRUD flows (agents, skills, models, memory, MCP, uploads) used by UI pages.
Construct artifact and endpoint URLs and fetch artifact payloads; surface both content and the canonical URL to consumers.
Provide application-wide context/providers and hooks (i18n, Subtasks, settings) that maintain in-memory state and persist where appropriate (cookies/localStorage).
Ensure SSR-safe behavior when deriving origins/URLs and when using utilities that may run in Node (Next.js server-side rendering).

insights.title: Agent name check error classification
insights.problem: Agent name validation returned a generic error for all failures, masking network vs backend validation vs other failure modes in the UI.
insights.root_cause: The agent name check path did not classify or surface different error shapes (network unreachable, HTTP error vs validation result), so callers could not render specific messages.
insights.solution: When changing agent name validation or API wrappers, preserve or improve error classification so callers can distinguish network/unreachable errors vs server-side validation failures; maintain typed/identifiable error shapes returned by list/check API functions.

insights.title: LangGraph client singleton misuse across mock/default modes
insights.problem: A single LangGraphClient instance was reused across both mock and default modes, which could result in returning the wrong client when switching modes at runtime (or in tests).
insights.root_cause: Client creation used a shared singleton without separating mode-specific instances.
insights.solution: Any edits to api-client must respect mode separation: ensure creation and retrieval APIs return the client instance that corresponds to the requested mode and do not inadvertently reuse a previously created incompatible instance (affects runtime, dev mock flows, and tests).

insights.title: Artifacts loaders must preserve original URL
insights.problem: Artifact loader and hook returned only fetched content (text) and dropped the original artifact URL, which downstream consumers needed for previews and non-write behaviors.
insights.root_cause: Loader API returned a single content value instead of a tuple/object containing both content and the source URL.
insights.solution: When modifying artifact loader or hook signatures, keep or reintroduce an API that returns both content and url (or document breaking change clearly) so callers that need the original artifact URL continue to function.

insights.title: Base origin and URL helpers must be SSR-safe and return absolute URLs
insights.problem: getBaseOrigin and base URL helpers accessed window.location.origin unguarded and returned undefined during SSR; callers assumed string absolute URLs and could fail during server-side rendering.
insights.root_cause: Utilities directly read browser globals without fallback for server-side contexts and sometimes returned raw/env-relative values instead of absolute URLs.
insights.solution: Respect SSR: functions in config must avoid unguarded window access, return a stable string value (use configured fallbacks) and produce absolute URLs expected by consumers; any changes must maintain SSR-safe behavior and the contract of returning absolute strings.

insights.title: New UI flows require updated localization entries
insights.problem: New UI features (network error strings, memory import/export, token usage, command palette/shortcuts, tooltips, and create-skill prompts) lacked i18n entries, causing missing strings or fallback behavior.
insights.root_cause: Locale files were not kept in sync with evolving UI text needs.
insights.solution: When adding UI text or changing flows that surface user-facing strings, ensure locale files (en-US and others) are updated in lockstep so runtime components consuming useI18n do not encounter missing keys. Maintain typed shape of Translations to catch missing entries at compile time.

key_files.why: Central factory/adapter for LangGraph clients (mock vs default). Changes here affect streaming behavior, tests, and every consumer that initiates LangGraph runs.
Provides base-origin and backend/langgraph base URLs used across API clients and artifact URL builders; must remain SSR-safe and return absolute URLs.
Canonical artifact fetching logic; its return shape (content + url) is consumed by hooks and UI previews.
REST client surface for agent CRUD and name checks; error shapes returned here are surfaced to the UI and agent hooks.
Defines SubtasksProvider and update semantics (partial merge) used by multiple UI components to display and modify subagent/subtask state.

construct_relationships: api-client.ts creates LangGraphClient instances that are used by stream-mode.ts for sanitizing run stream options and by many higher-level hooks that start runs.
config/index.ts is read by api-client.ts and artifact utils (urlOfArtifact/resolveArtifactURL) to construct absolute backend and LangGraph endpoints; these values must be SSR-safe.
artifacts/loader.ts provides loadArtifactContent which is consumed by artifacts/hooks.ts; the hook expects both content and url for previews.
agents/api.ts implements low-level HTTP calls used by agents/hooks.ts; hooks adapt those calls into React-friendly states (react-query) and UI flows.
tasks/context.tsx exposes a SubtaskContext used by UI components and possibly by any component that renders subagent progress or receives subagent updates from streams/events.
notable_relationships: Frontend API adapters and artifact URL builders depend on backend Gateway/LangGraph endpoints and therefore on config values that differ between dev, Docker, and production (use container hostnames vs localhost).
SSR/Next.js build-time usage: config and rehype/rehype-plugin must be safe to import and run on server; any change that introduces browser globals will break SSR.
Mock vs real client modes affect tests and local dev flows. api-client singleton issues can cause flakiness in unit/integration tests that switch modes.
i18n changes must be propagated across locale files; missing keys break typed Translations contract used across the app and can surface as runtime UI fallbacks.
Artifact and stream handling interact with thread/streaming primitives (threads folder) — changes to stream options or artifact URL shapes can impact thread reconcilers and UI streaming components.
recommendations: When editing api-client.ts, ensure mode-specific client instances remain isolated (mock vs default) and verify tests that create clients in different modes still pass.

Any changes to config/index.ts must avoid unguarded window access; validate with Next.js SSR build and server-side unit tests to ensure getBaseOrigin and base URL helpers return absolute strings in server contexts.
If modifying artifacts/loader or artifacts/hooks return shapes, update all callers to accept content+url; run UI flows that preview artifacts to confirm behavior.

When changing agents/api error shapes (especially name-checking), update agents/hooks.ts and any UI that inspects errors; run agent-related UI flows and unit tests that depend on specific error classifications.
Respect SubtasksProvider update semantics (partial shallow merges) — consumers rely on that behavior for in-place updates; verify components that read subtasks still render correctly after changes.

Run the impacted test suite and build steps after edits: frontend unit tests (including stream-mode.test), TypeScript compilation, pnpm build (Next.js build) / SSR checks, and lint/format to ensure no regressions across modes.
When adding or changing user-facing strings, update locale translation files (en-US and others) and the typed Translations shape so missing keys are caught at compile time.

backend/tests.description: This folder contains the backend unit and integration-style tests that validate the runtime contracts, security constraints, and wire-protocol shapes of the DeerFlow backend. Tests exercise agent factory/middleware assembly, sandbox path mapping and tools, memory import/update/queue behavior, uploads and skills routers, the in-memory streaming bridge and SSE framing, and the DeerFlow in-process client — all of which encode precise API shapes, error mappings and side-effects that production code must preserve.
key_behaviors: Verification of exact public API shapes and wire formats (HTTP JSON payloads, SSE frames, stream event sequences).
Behavioral and security assertions around sandbox path mapping, read-only enforcement, and host/virtual path masking.
Middleware and lead-agent wiring correctness checks: model resolution, middleware insertion, prompt/skill assembly and cache lifecycle.
Memory system verification across three layers: router (HTTP), updater (merge/dedup/normalization), and asynchronous queue (flag merging and forwarding).
Concurrency and streaming invariants: subagent/task lifecycle events, StreamBridge ordering/END semantics, bounded history replay, and deduplication/order-independence hashing (loop detection).

insights.title: Tests lock to exact SSE/streaming shapes and JSON keys
insights.problem: Many tests assert exact SSE formatting (e.g., 'data: null' for end events), stream event ordering, and presence of specific JSON keys (message, name, id).
insights.root_cause: Clients and other code (frontend/SDK) rely on the precise textual/frame-level SSE format and event payload shapes used by format_sse and stream-producing code.
insights.solution: Any code edits that change SSE framing, JSON payload keys, or event ordering must preserve the exact output shapes or update the test-suite and downstream consumers accordingly.

insights.title: Tests patch module-level imports and helpers
insights.problem: Many tests monkeypatch or patch functions/constructs that were imported at module import time inside the tested modules (e.g., get_app_config, get_memory_storage, SubagentExecutor).
insights.root_cause: Test isolation depends on replacing module-level references rather than deep dependency injection.
insights.solution: When refactoring, preserve the module-level import names and locations (or update tests) so patch/monkeypatch sites remain valid; keep public entrypoints/signatures stable.

insights.title: Path mapping and masking semantics are security-critical
insights.problem: Tests assert preservation of path separators/trailing slashes, correct virtual→host and host→virtual translation, strict path traversal rejection, read-only enforcement (EROFS), and masking of host paths in outputs.
insights.root_cause: Sandbox tools and utilities enforce isolation and prevent host-path leakage; many components and routers assume these translation semantics.
insights.solution: Changes to sandbox path mapping, command path substitution, or masking behavior must maintain trailing-separator semantics, Windows-style handling where tested, read-only enforcement behavior and explicit error types/errno values expected by tests.

insights.title: Memory updater/queue/router preserve flags and source metadata
insights.problem: Tests ensure that correction_detected/reinforcement_detected flags are merged and forwarded, that source/sourceError fields are preserved across import/export, and that LLM-produced shapes are normalized before parsing.
insights.root_cause: Downstream consumers and external exports depend on these metadata fields and normalization for correctness and observability.
insights.solution: Maintain memory API contracts: merging rules, flag preservation, confidence validation and exact error mappings; if storage API changes (e.g., get_memory_storage API), update all tests and callers consistently.

insights.title: Stable hashing/normalization invariants are enforced by tests
insights.problem: LoopDetectionMiddleware tests expect order-independence, canonicalization of args (dict vs JSON string), reversal-insensitivity for ranges, and content-sensitivity where appropriate.
insights.root_cause: Loop detection relies on deterministic hashes to detect repeated behavior across runs.
insights.solution: Any change to hashing, normalization, or salient-field selection must preserve these invariants or tests (and operator expectations) will break.

insights.title: Filesystem tests are sensitive to OS permissions and symlink behavior
insights.problem: Some tests create symlinks and expect behavior that can fail on Windows without elevated permissions or developer mode, causing test flakes or errors.
insights.root_cause: Symlink creation and chmod semantics differ on Windows vs POSIX.
insights.solution: When changing code that tests rely on (uploads manager, symlink handling, permission relaxation), be mindful that CI or local runs on Windows may require special handling or conditional skips.

insights.title: Task tool and stream code depend on coroutine behavior and background cleanup scheduling
insights.problem: Tests were updated when task_tool became async and expect specific cancellation propagation, asyncio.sleep patching, and cleanup scheduling behavior.
insights.root_cause: Task execution flows include cooperative cancellation, polling timeouts and deferred cleanup; tests simulate these with patched async primitives.
insights.solution: Preserve coroutine signatures and cancellation semantics for task-tool-related functions; if changing to sync/async forms, update all tests and patch sites consistently.

key_files.why: Validates DeerFlowClient public surface: construction, agent caching, streaming events and text-extraction heuristics — changes to client APIs or streamed event shapes will break many tests.
Asserts LocalSandbox path mapping semantics, read-only enforcement and command substitution — critical for sandbox security and tools that translate container↔host paths.
Covers memory merging, deduplication, source/sourceError preservation and normalization — edits to memory storage or updater logic must retain these contracts.

Exercises in-memory StreamBridge ordering, bounded history replay, END sentinel semantics, and concurrency assumptions — stream delivery invariants are relied on by streaming clients and SSE endpoints.

Validates make_lead_agent model fallback, thinking capability toggling, and middleware insertion based on resolved model capabilities — changes to model resolution or middleware assembly affect runtime agent behavior.

construct_relationships: Tests patch module-level helpers imported by routers and middleware (e.g., get_app_config, get_memory_config, get_memory_storage) — test isolation depends on those module-level references.
Lead-agent prompt/skills tests validate that make_lead_agent composes the prompt using get_skills_prompt_section and that the enabled-skills cache lifecycle affects prompt generation.
Sandbox tests exercise PathMapping and LocalSandboxProvider translation functions and rely on these for the sandbox-backed built-in tools (glob, grep, read_file/write_file) used by other tests.

Memory tests span three layers: memory middleware filters input and detects signals, MemoryUpdateQueue merges flags and forwards to MemoryUpdater, and memory router exposes import/export HTTP endpoints — tests assert consistency of flags and source metadata across these layers.

Task tool tests interact with SubagentExecutor and stream writers; StreamBridge tests validate event ordering consumed by the streaming endpoints — together these verify end-to-end task/subagent event flows.
notable_relationships: Tests rely on backend/src implementations: agents (make_lead_agent), middlewares, sandbox providers, subagents executor/registry, gateway routers (uploads, memory, skills), and utilities (SSE formatter, upload manager).
Router tests mount FastAPI routers into ephemeral TestClient apps and expect specific error-to-HTTP mappings (e.g., ValueError → 400, missing memory fact → 404) produced by the router code.

Many tests assume deterministic filesystem behavior (temp dirs, symlinks, file permissions) and that LocalSandboxProvider vs AioSandboxProvider differences (local bash gating, mount semantics) are enforced.

Front-end/SDK expectations: SSE and stream event formats asserted here are consumed by frontend and in-process clients; changing these shapes affects cross-service integration (nginx routing and LangGraph streaming consumers).
recommendations: When modifying code in backend/src referenced by tests, preserve public function signatures, module-level import names, and error types/messages that tests assert (including JSON key names and HTTP status mappings).

Keep sandbox path mapping semantics intact: trailing-separator preservation, Windows-style separator handling, virtual↔host translations, read-only enforcement (raise EROFS/OSError as expected), and output masking of host paths.
Do not change SSE/text framing or stream event ordering without updating tests and downstream consumers; validate format_sse output and stream replay semantics after edits.

If altering memory APIs or storage access, ensure merge/dedup rules, correction/reinforcement flag propagation, source/sourceError preservation, and confidence validation remain compatible with tests (or update tests accordingly).
Any refactor that converts sync→async (or vice versa) must be reflected across tests that run coroutines, patch asyncio primitives, or expect cancellation/cleanup scheduling behavior.

Run the backend test suite (make test or cd backend && uv run pytest) after edits; watch for OS-specific failures (symlink/permission tests on Windows) and for tests that rely on module-level monkeypatch/patch sites — update tests only if the public contract truly changes.

backend/docs.description: This folder contains the backend operational and design documentation for DeerFlow: architecture maps, feature writeups (title generation, memory, uploads), configuration guidance, middleware execution flow, RFCs and implementation summaries. These documents describe runtime contracts (API endpoints, virtual filesystem paths, middleware order, config schema and OAuth expectations) that callers, deployers and maintainers rely on when modifying code or deploying services.
key_behaviors: Documenting runtime contracts and integration points between LangGraph (agent runtime), the Gateway API, sandbox providers and the frontend (API paths, virtual mounts, OAuth endpoints).

Describing middleware lifecycle and state propagation (thread/workspace creation, upload injection, sandbox allocation, memory and title injection) so code changes must respect ordering and state names.
Capturing operational configuration requirements and pitfalls (config.yaml keys, config_version, checkpointer, GitHub PAT guidance, MCP OAuth) that affect deployment and third‑party integration.
Specifying user-facing features and their runtime coupling (file upload flow and markitdown rendering, automatic title generation using TitleMiddleware, memory injection behaviors).
Recording design decisions and proposed API/SDK changes via RFCs that influence cross-repo interfaces (agent factory, shared modules, sandbox tools).

insights.title: Third-party API quota and PAT guidance
insights.problem: Users of certain built-in skills (e.g., Deep Research) can hit GitHub rate limits without being instructed to use a GitHub PAT, causing unpredictable failures.
insights.root_cause: Documentation does not consistently call out per-skill external API rate limits or where to set PATs in config.yaml.
insights.solution: When making code or docs changes that touch external API usage or skill configuration, ensure config schema and examples clearly show where to provide tokens and explain consequences of missing tokens; treat PAT guidance as part of the deployment contract.

insights.title: Middleware ordering determines runtime state
insights.problem: Features (title generation, memory injection, uploads, sandbox) assume a strict middleware order; changing order or state keys breaks downstream behavior.
insights.root_cause: Middleware write to and read from shared per-run state (e.g., thread/workspace, state.title, injected memory facts) and depend on specific side effects produced earlier in the chain.
insights.solution: Any edits that alter middleware behavior, ordering, or state names must preserve the documented execution flow and state keys; validate flows that depend on state mutations across middleware stages.

insights.title: Automatic title coupling to TitleMiddleware config
insights.problem: Title generation behavior is tied to TitleConfig and runtime params (state.title) and can be brittle if config loading semantics change.
insights.root_cause: Multiple docs reference both a runtime middleware and separate implementation helpers (get_title_config, load_title_config_from_dict) which must align with the middleware's expectations.
insights.solution: When touching title generation code or its config, ensure documentation and implementation remain consistent about config shape, defaulting behavior, and where resulting title is stored in run state.

insights.title: Virtual filesystem and upload semantics
insights.problem: File upload behavior, API path conventions and virtual mount points (workspace/uploads/outputs) are relied upon by skills and sandbox tools; mismatch causes file-not-found or permission issues.
insights.root_cause: Docs and code share implicit paths and markitdown rendering expectations; sandbox providers (local vs docker) expose different capabilities and mounts.
insights.solution: Treat documented path examples and upload API signatures as part of the public contract; when altering sandbox mounts, upload handling, or markitdown behavior, verify that virtual paths in examples and runtime providers remain consistent.

insights.title: Memory injection and settings schema
insights.problem: Memory system improvements and injection behavior depend on a concrete settings schema and sample payload; discrepancies lead to incorrect fact extraction or prompt injection.
insights.root_cause: Multiple docs (improvements, summary, settings review, sample JSON) describe how memory is configured and injected into prompts, and these must align with memory middleware implementation.
insights.solution: When modifying memory code or settings, ensure the sample JSON, review guide, and summary remain aligned with the implementation and that the middleware order preserves injection timing described in docs.

insights.title: MCP server and OAuth expectations
insights.problem: MCP server setup and OAuth flows are operationally sensitive; incorrect documentation about redirect URIs, scopes, or server config can break authentication.
insights.root_cause: Docs describe OAuth and MCP server configuration as part of deployment; implementation and deployment scripts expect concrete values.
insights.solution: Any changes touching MCP, OAuth configuration keys, or related routes must be cross-checked against documented redirect URIs, example config, and any SDK/CLI RFCs to avoid breaking auth flows.

key_files.why: Maps high-level runtime topology (LangGraph, Gateway, sandbox, API routing) that other docs and deployers assume as the authoritative architecture.
Describes the exact middleware order and state propagation; changes to middleware must preserve the behaviors documented here.
Holds config.yaml schema guidance and operational notes (checkpointer, config_version, PAT usage) that deployments and code rely on.
Specifies file upload API endpoints, payloads and markitdown integration; implementation and client code must match these contracts.
Documents the TitleMiddleware internals and config helpers; authoritative for how title-related runtime state is produced and consumed.

construct_relationships: TitleMiddleware (TitleConfig, get_title_config, load_title_config_from_dict) writes state.title which downstream docs and UI consumers expect to exist.

ThreadData/Thread workspace creation (documented in middleware-execution-flow) provides virtual paths (/mnt/user-data/{workspace,uploads,outputs}) that the SandboxMiddleware must mount and file upload endpoints use.
Memory middleware injects extracted facts into the run/system prompt; memory settings JSON and sample payloads in docs must match the middleware's extraction and injection schema.
FILE_UPLOAD API (POST /api/threads/{thread_id}/uploads) populates uploads that are consumed by sandbox tools (read_file, list_dir) and markitdown rendering described in docs.
MCP server documentation describes OAuth configuration and endpoints that the Gateway and any MCP adapters in the agent tool table need to reference.
notable_relationships: Docs depend on code in backend/src (lead_agent, middlewares, sandbox providers, gateway routers); any code change to middleware, sandbox mounts, API routes or config keys must be reflected in these documents to avoid deploy/runtime mismatches.

Frontend and harness client (outside this folder) rely on API paths and behaviors documented here (uploads, thread APIs, title presence); changes in code must be validated against these documented contracts.

Deployment artifacts (Docker compose, nginx routing) and CI environment variables refer to config keys and runtime expectations described in these docs; misalignment can break local and CI deployments.
RFCs in this folder influence cross-package APIs (SDK factory, shared modules, sandbox tools) that affect other repositories or packages in the monorepo; they represent design-level dependencies.
recommendations: When editing any documentation in this subtree, validate against the matching runtime code: middleware order, state keys (e.g., state.title), API routes, and sandbox mount points must match implementation.
Run backend unit tests and relevant integration tests after changes that touch middleware, sandbox, upload handling, memory or MCP/OAuth (including sandbox-enabled tests and any SSE/subagent flows).
If changing config keys, config_version, or example config.yaml, ensure sample files (memory-settings-sample.json) and any referenced environment variables / CI build envs are updated in lockstep.
Verify file upload behavior end-to-end (POST /api/threads/{thread_id}/uploads → sandbox visibility → markitdown rendering) when modifying upload docs or related code paths.
When altering token/auth related behavior (GitHub PAT guidance, MCP OAuth), confirm redirect URIs, scopes, and token storage/consumption paths match deployment scripts and gateway/mcp code.
Preserve or explicitly update middleware-execution-flow documentation whenever middleware ordering, side effects, or state schemas change; downstream features depend on that ordering.

backend/app.description: This folder contains the Gateway and IM channel integration surface for DeerFlow: FastAPI routers and services that expose Gateway endpoints (runs, uploads, skills, memory) and a pluggable set of instant-messaging channel adapters (Slack, Telegram, Feishu, WeCom, WeCom AES readers, plus lifecycle/manager/store). It matters because it bridges external clients and IM platforms to the LangGraph lead agent, manages thread-scoped files/artifacts and sandboxes, and coordinates run lifecycles and streaming replies so external users see consistent, safe interactions.
key_behaviors: Instantiate and manage lifecycle of IM channels and a ChannelManager that bridges inbound channel messages to agent runs via a MessageBus and Gateway run lifecycle services.

Translate inbound IM events (text, commands, attachments) into canonical InboundMessage objects, ingest attachments into per-thread uploads workspace, and provide uploaded_files blocks into agent run context.
Dispatch outbound streaming and non-streaming replies from the agent back to channels via the MessageBus with per-channel formatting, threading, reactions and file uploads.

Expose Gateway HTTP APIs for runs (SSE), uploads, skill lifecycle, and memory management; normalize incoming run payloads to compatible LangGraph RunnableConfig shapes and handle SSE framing/streaming semantics.
Resolve and validate virtual sandbox paths to host filesystem paths and mirror/adjust permissions for non-local (AIO/docker) sandboxes to ensure sandbox runtimes can access uploaded artifacts.

insights.title: Uploads must be mirrored and permissioned for containerized sandboxes
insights.problem: Files uploaded by the Gateway are stored on the host and then mounted into isolated AIO sandboxes where UID/GID mismatches can prevent the sandbox runtime from modifying or reading files.
insights.root_cause: Host-side writes use host user permissions; containerized sandbox runtimes run as different user IDs leading to permission errors when accessing mounted files.
insights.solution: When mirroring uploads for non-local sandboxes, make uploaded files world-writable/adjust ownership and ensure accompanying companion conversions are produced atomically so sandbox runtimes can access and mutate files.

insights.title: Deterministic error on missing retry configuration
insights.problem: Retry loops in several channel adapters raised last_exc which could be None, producing confusing type errors instead of a clear failure when retry attempts were misconfigured or zero.
insights.root_cause: Code assumed an exception would always be captured in a retry variable; no explicit guard or deterministic error when retry attempts were set to zero or absent.
insights.solution: Channel send/upload loops must raise a clear, deterministic error if no retry attempts are configured or if retry exhaustion occurs, avoiding re-raising None and preserving meaningful stack/context for callers and logs.

insights.title: Avoid incompatible run payloads (context vs configurable) and preserve assistant_id
insights.problem: The Gateway emitted 'configurable' fields even when callers provided a plain 'context', or failed to map caller-supplied assistant_id into configurable.agent_name, causing LangGraph to reject runs (400) or to not load per-agent assets.
insights.root_cause: Routers duplicated run-building logic and did not centralize normalization of 'context' vs 'configurable' semantics, nor consistently map assistant_id into the agent factory configuration.
insights.solution: Normalize run input such that if callers supply 'context' it is not redundantly translated into 'configurable'; forward assistant_id as configurable.agent_name so make_lead_agent receives per-agent routing info.

insights.title: Detect and reject concurrent runs to same thread
insights.problem: Concurrent run attempts targeting the same IM thread could cause race conditions, lost feedback, and confusing failures for users.
insights.root_cause: No unified guard or user-friendly detection existed at the channel/manager layer to prevent multiple in-flight runs from targeting the same thread.
insights.solution: ChannelManager should detect thread-busy conditions and return a clear user-facing busy message; upstream call sites must respect this guard rather than retrying or creating multiple overlapping RunRecords.

insights.title: Unified ingestion and safe filename handling
insights.problem: Multiple upload/ingestion implementations duplicated normalization, traversal checks and conversion handling, creating inconsistency and potential security gaps.
insights.root_cause: Upload logic lived in several places (channels, Gateway, client) without a single canonical ingestion pipeline or shared helper enforcement.
insights.solution: Centralize ingestion semantics: claim safe filenames, normalize paths, reject traversal, and produce uploaded_files metadata consistently so agent contexts and sandbox mounts always observe the same contract.

key_files.why: Application factory and lifespan: initializes LangGraph runtime and ChannelService; entrypoint for startup/shutdown behavior and router wiring.
Bridges inbound IM events to LangGraph runs, handles attachment ingestion, streaming conversion to displayable snapshots, and thread-busy detection.
Core async pub/sub hub and message datatypes that decouple channel adapters from the agent dispatcher and drive outbound delivery paths.
Resolves per-channel config and env precedence, instantiates channel implementations and orchestrates lifecycle (start/stop) of channels and the ChannelManager.
Gateway endpoints for thread-scoped uploads, listing and deletion; responsible for normalization, sandbox mirroring and permission adjustments for AIO sandboxes.

construct_relationships: Channel implementations (SlackChannel, TelegramChannel, FeishuChannel, WeComChannel) publish InboundMessage instances to MessageBus, which queues them for the ChannelManager/dispatcher to pick up.

ChannelManager consumes queued InboundMessage objects, ingests attachments into per-thread uploads directories (using the uploads manager), converts inputs into run payloads and invokes the Gateway run lifecycle service to start agent runs.

Gateway run lifecycle service (gateway.services) constructs RunnableConfig, creates RunRecord via RunManager, and uses the StreamBridge to stream SSE events back to HTTP clients; ChannelManager maps streaming events into channel-specific outbound snapshots and publishes OutboundMessage via MessageBus to channel listeners.

ChannelService is responsible for instantiating ChannelManager and channel adapters using import paths and config/env-resolved Gateway/LangGraph URLs; changes to ChannelService affect all channel lifecycle behavior.

Uploads router and resolve_thread_virtual_path validate and map sandbox-visible virtual paths to host filesystem paths so channels and Gateway endpoints can read/write artifacts; this mapping must remain consistent with sandbox provider semantics used elsewhere in the codebase.
notable_relationships: This folder depends on the sandbox abstraction (not contained here) to mirror uploads and to decide whether to relax permissions for AIO sandboxes — changes here must align with sandbox provider behaviors (Local vs AIO).

Gateway run logic ties into the LangGraph runtime singletons (StreamBridge, RunManager, checkpointer) provided via gateway.deps; router handlers assume these singletons are present and will raise HTTP 503 when missing.

Skill management and memory routers in this folder interact with global caches and stores outside this folder (prompt cache, memory persistence); skill install/edit operations must trigger non-blocking cache refresh semantics respected by the agent factory.
ChannelStore persists chat→thread mappings to disk atomically; external tooling that relies on thread IDs (exports, backups) will depend on the JSON file layout and atomic save semantics.
Env/config precedence for backend service URLs (LangGraph/Gateway) is important for Docker Compose deployments — using localhost vs container DNS names will change runtime connectivity for channels.
recommendations: Preserve thread-safety and atomic file operations: respect existing locks and atomic_save semantics when modifying ChannelStore or uploads to avoid corruption under concurrent access.

When changing channel send/upload code paths keep deterministic error behavior for retry configuration (do not re-raise None). Ensure callers can surface clear exceptions for logging and user-facing messages.

Any edits that alter run payload shapes must maintain normalization rules: avoid emitting both 'context' and 'configurable' together, and ensure assistant_id is forwarded as configurable.agent_name so make_lead_agent receives per-agent routing info.
When touching upload/mirroring behavior ensure you update permission relaxations for non-local sandboxes and keep conversion/companion file generation atomic so sandboxes see consistent artifacts.

Run the Gateway and channel integration tests (and, where relevant, run inside Docker Compose) after edits to exercise AIO sandbox, long-lived channel threads, and SSE streaming behavior; verify health/startup via create_app lifespan and gateway.deps singletons to avoid HTTP 503 regressions.

backend/packages.description: This subtree implements the in-process DeerFlow harness: factories and SDKs that assemble the lead agent, ordered middleware, prompt construction (with a cached enabled-skills section), memory extraction pipeline (queue + updater + storage helpers), local sandbox path mapping and sandbox-aware tools, and an embedded Python client. It is the runtime glue that determines agent behavior, safety boundaries (sandboxing, loop detection, subagent delegation), and non-blocking startup characteristics — changes here directly affect runtime semantics and integration tests.
key_behaviors: Agent assembly: resolve runtime model and build the lead agent with a system prompt (including an enabled-skills section), ordered middleware chain and toolset.
Prompt and skills cache: produce dynamic system prompts while maintaining a background, refreshable cache of enabled skills to avoid blocking imports or request paths.

Memory pipeline: filter run-time conversation, detect corrections/reinforcements, debounce per-thread memory updates into a queue, then apply LLM-driven extraction/deduplication and persist via pluggable storage.
Sandboxing and tools: map virtual/container paths to host paths, enforce read-only mounts, validate and mask paths/outputs, and provide sandbox-aware builtin tools (bash, read/write, grep, glob).
Subagent/task delegation: validate and start background subagents, stream lifecycle events, support cooperative cancellation, and poll background execution while preserving runtime context metadata.

insights.title: Avoid blocking filesystem I/O at import or on the event loop
insights.problem: Synchronous skill loading or filesystem operations during import or on the async event loop cause slow startup or block the loop.
insights.root_cause: Code that loads enabled skills, ensures sqlite directories, or reads config ran synchronously at import-time or directly on the event loop.
insights.solution: Use background workers or non-blocking helpers for cache priming, shift synchronous filesystem mkdirs off the event loop, and provide warm/invalidate primitives to avoid blocking request paths.

insights.title: Runtime context may be None or incomplete
insights.problem: Middleware and sandbox helpers assume runtime.context is present and contains fields like thread_id, leading to TypeErrors or incorrect behavior on some invocation paths.
insights.root_cause: Different LangGraph invocation paths sometimes supply None or provide thread_id via alternate containers (e.g., config.configurable), and code wrote into runtime.context without null-checks or fallbacks.
insights.solution: Null-check runtime.context, provide deterministic fallbacks (e.g., config.configurable), and avoid writing into runtime.context without ensuring it exists.

insights.title: Preserve reinforcement/correction signals and deduplicate consistently
insights.problem: Correction and positive-reinforcement signals were not propagated through the queue/updater, and memory deduplication treated case-variants as distinct.
insights.root_cause: MemoryMiddleware detected corrections but reinforcement detection and signals were not preserved by MemoryUpdateQueue; MemoryUpdater used case-sensitive keys.
insights.solution: Propagate correction/reinforcement flags through queue entries and updater flows; deduplicate facts using casefolded/normalized keys and preserve sourceError/correction hints when persisting.

insights.title: Path mapping must preserve separators and mask host layout
insights.problem: Virtual→host path replacement could drop trailing separators or mix host separators, and outputs exposed host paths, confusing users or leaking host layout.
insights.root_cause: Path replacement logic altered separators/trailing slashes and returned host-native paths in outputs rather than container-style virtual paths.
insights.solution: Preserve virtual path separator style and trailing slashes in replacements, reverse-map host outputs back to virtual paths, and enforce read-only mount semantics with proper errno semantics.

insights.title: Stable normalization and message-type-aware appends for loop-detection
insights.problem: Loop detection could be evaded by noisy argument serialization and crashed when AIMessage.content was a list rather than a string when injecting hard-stop text.
insights.root_cause: Tool-call keys were produced from unstable stringifications and code assumed str-type message content when appending warnings.
insights.solution: Harden argument normalization to produce stable keys across semantically equivalent inputs and handle AIMessage.content being list/structured blocks when appending warnings or hard-stop text.

insights.title: Cooperative cancellation for background subagents
insights.problem: When the task polling coroutine was cancelled, background subagents continued running because threads could not be forcibly terminated.
insights.root_cause: Polling coroutine cancellation did not initiate cooperative cancellation or schedule deferred cleanup for the background subagent executor.
insights.solution: On CancelledError, request cooperative cancellation from the subagent executor and schedule deferred cleanup to avoid orphaned background work.

insights.title: Deterministic config discovery and reload behavior
insights.problem: Config resolution based on cwd and global variables led to nondeterministic behavior when services start from different working directories or concurrent contexts.
insights.root_cause: Config path resolution relied on the process working directory and global singleton caching without robust overrides.
insights.solution: Resolve config path with clear precedence, support deterministic override mechanisms, and reload the cached AppConfig when the file mtime changes.

insights.title: Prefer logging over prints in runtime subsystems
insights.problem: Several modules emitted print() calls for lifecycle and error messages, making structured logs and integration with logging systems difficult.
insights.root_cause: Debugging prints were left in production codepaths (memory queue, middleware) instead of using the logging framework.
insights.solution: Use the project's logging facilities to emit debug/info/error messages so test harnesses and production systems can capture and filter output.

key_files.why: Central factory that resolves runtime model, builds the system prompt and middleware chain — edits change agent creation and runtime model selection.
Constructs dynamic system prompt and maintains background enabled-skills cache; changes affect prompt composition and startup blocking behavior.
Implements LLM-driven memory updates and persistence; edits affect deduplication, correction/reinforcement handling, and storage integration.
Maps container-style paths to host paths, enforces read-only mounts and sanitizes outputs — core for sandbox safety and path masking.
Entry point for subagent delegation and background task lifecycle; impacts cooperative cancellation, task metadata and runtime polling semantics.

construct_relationships: make_lead_agent (lead_agent/agent.py) composes the system prompt (prompt.py), resolved model, tool-table (sandbox/tools.py & builtins), and an ordered middleware chain built via factory.create_deerflow_agent.
Prompt builder (prompt.py) supplies an enabled-skills section that is read by make_lead_agent and can be filtered at runtime by DeerFlowClient.available_skills or task_tool-injected overrides.

MemoryMiddleware (middlewares/memory_middleware.py) filters conversation and detects correction/reinforcement, then enqueues ConversationContext into MemoryUpdateQueue (memory/queue.py) which delegates persistence to MemoryUpdater (memory/updater.py) and storage adapters.

UploadsMiddleware (middlewares/uploads_middleware.py) prepends an <uploaded_files> block to the last HumanMessage; this mutated message flows into the prompt and into memory filtering, so uploads handling affects prompt content and persisted memory.

LocalSandbox (sandbox/local/local_sandbox.py) and sandbox helper tools (sandbox/tools.py) jointly translate paths and mask outputs; task_tool constructs SubagentExecutor that relies on these sandbox semantics to validate subagent types (e.g., disallow host-bash).
notable_relationships: This harness code is consumed by LangGraph/Gateway runtime registration (agents package imported by LangGraph server). Ensure import-time effects (skills cache priming) are non-blocking for server startup.

Memory storage adapters and checkpointer providers integrate with external persistence backends (sqlite/postgres); changes here affect DB initialization and must consider cross-service configuration (config.yaml and environment vars).
Sandbox and builtins interact with front-end supplied uploads and Gateway APIs (file upload endpoints); path mapping and upload metadata assumptions carry across frontend→gateway→harness boundaries.

DeerFlowClient creates an in-process agent that re-uses the same factories and prompts as the server path; edits must maintain parity between embedded client behavior and server-side agent construction.
Checkpointer.async_provider and provider implementations touch filesystem and event loop resources; their behavior influences server lifecycle and CI test setups.
recommendations: Preserve non-blocking startup: avoid adding filesystem I/O at import-time or synchronous blocking on the async event loop; use background refresh workers or async-safe helpers when priming caches.
Null-check runtime context and provide robust fallbacks for missing fields (thread_id, context); ensure middleware and sandbox helpers do not assume non-None runtime.context.
When editing memory flows, ensure correction/reinforcement flags are propagated from MemoryMiddleware → MemoryUpdateQueue → MemoryUpdater and that deduplication uses normalized (casefolded) keys.

Maintain sandbox path invariants: preserve trailing separators and virtual path styles in replacements, reverse-map host paths in outputs, and enforce read-only mounts with appropriate errno behavior.
Make loop-detection and message-injection code resilient to different AIMessage.content types (string or structured lists) and use stable normalization for tool-call arguments.
When modifying task/subagent code, respect cooperative cancellation semantics, schedule cleanup for cancelled polls, and ensure thread_id resolution is tolerant of different runtime sources.

Replace any remaining print() calls with structured logging and run unit/integration tests covering: skills-cache warm/invalidate, memory queue debounce and flag preservation, sandbox path mapping & masking, task cancellation behavior, loop-detection thresholds and message appends.

Run the harness unit test suite and CI lint/type checks after edits; consider integration tests that exercise both the in-process DeerFlowClient and the server agent path (LangGraph) to verify parity.

docs.description: This folder contains developer-facing documentation and short-form records that explain recent code changes and a specific bugfix (skill name conflict resolution). It serves as a lightweight source of truth for why certain code behaviors exist and where to look for the rationale behind changes that affect the skills and registry subsystems.
key_behaviors: Record per-file change summaries and diffs for recent modifications to the codebase
Document a targeted fix for skill name conflicts (algorithms and guards used to generate/validate skill keys)
Act as a placeholder for broader README-level guidance that is currently missing or incomplete

insights.title: Per-file change summaries are maintained as manual diffs and notes
insights.problem: Without an up-to-date, file-level changelog, reviewers and maintainers lack quick traceability from code changes to rationale/intent.
insights.root_cause: Ad-hoc edits to code without automatic or mandated documentation updates leave historical context only in commit history.
insights.solution: Keep CODE_CHANGE_SUMMARY_BY_FILE.md updated with concise diffs and notes when editing files that materially change behavior; treat it as a lightweight, human-readable adjunct to commit history and PR descriptions. Recognize that if this file is stale it can mislead reviewers.

insights.title: Skill name conflict resolution is documented and references code-level constructs
insights.problem: Skill name collisions create ambiguous behavior at runtime (which skill is invoked, how enablement is resolved).
insights.root_cause: Skill identity was previously dependent on non-canonical or non-unique names/paths and a missing or unclear resolution strategy.
insights.solution: SKILL_NAME_CONFLICT_FIX.md documents the intended behavior and the key constructs (get_skill_key, is_skill_enabled) that implement it; any changes to skill key generation or enablement logic in code must be mirrored here so implementers and reviewers can validate intended semantics against implementation and tests.

insights.title: Top-level README content is a placeholder
insights.problem: There is no authoritative high-level guidance in this folder, increasing cognitive load for contributors who expect contextual docs alongside change notes.
insights.root_cause: Documentation work was deferred, leaving a placeholder rather than finished guidance.
insights.solution: Treat README_PLACEHOLDER.md as an explicit signal that higher-level documentation may be missing; when editing features with broader developer impact, check for assumptions that would otherwise be documented here and ensure code changes do not rely on undocumented conventions.

key_files.why: Primary record of per-file diffs and notes — used by reviewers to understand recent code changes and intent.
Documents the fix and rationale for skill name conflict resolution and references specific code constructs (get_skill_key, is_skill_enabled) that implement the behavior.
Signals missing top-level documentation; indicates where higher-level guidance is expected to live.

construct_relationships: SKILL_NAME_CONFLICT_FIX.md documents and references get_skill_key and is_skill_enabled, which are implemented in the runtime/skills registry code (the skills registration/enablement layer).
CODE_CHANGE_SUMMARY_BY_FILE.md maps human-readable diffs and notes to concrete files in the codebase, creating a linking artifact between code edits and developer rationale.

README_PLACEHOLDER.md is the intended landing place for overview documentation that would describe the interactions between skills, registries, and sandboxes; its absence implies that behavioral assumptions are primarily captured elsewhere (e.g., per-file docs and code).
notable_relationships: Documentation here is an input to reviewers and maintainers across the repository; changes to skill naming, registration, or enablement in backend code must be reconciled with SKILL_NAME_CONFLICT_FIX.md to avoid documentation drift.

CODE_CHANGE_SUMMARY_BY_FILE.md functions as an auxiliary artefact to commit history and PR descriptions — CI or release processes may rely on coherent human-readable change summaries for release notes or auditing.
The documented constructs (get_skill_key, is_skill_enabled) are part of the skills/subagent surface area; edits in that subsystem are tightly coupled to the contents of SKILL_NAME_CONFLICT_FIX.md.
recommendations: When editing code that modifies skill identity, registration, or enablement behavior, update docs/SKILL_NAME_CONFLICT_FIX.md to reflect new function signatures, semantics, and examples.
Record concise diffs and rationale in docs/CODE_CHANGE_SUMMARY_BY_FILE.md for any file-level behavior changes so reviewers can quickly see intent without relying solely on commit history.
Before making changes that rely on higher-level conventions, inspect README_PLACEHOLDER.md — treat it as potentially missing context and verify assumptions against code and other docs.
Preserve Markdown formatting and the existing structure of these docs when updating; keep entries discoverable and linked to the code paths they describe.

Run the relevant skill/subagent tests and any integration tests that exercise skill registration/enablement after changing code referenced by SKILL_NAME_CONFLICT_FIX.md, and ensure test outcomes match the documented behavior.

frontend.description: The frontend folder contains the Next.js React application that provides the workspace and chat UI for DeerFlow. It composes client-only and server-capable pages and many small UI primitives (chat pages, message rendering, prompt input, artifacts, suggestions, command palette) that integrate with thread-scoped runtime hooks (useThreadStream/useThreadSettings) and backend services (Gateway, LangGraph, artifact URLs). Proper handling of SSR vs client-only behavior, attachment/streaming lifecycles, and backend routing are critical for correctness and user experience.
key_behaviors: Thread lifecycle and streaming: initialize/resume threads, start/stop streaming, update browser history on thread creation, show desktop notifications on run completion.

Chat UI composition: pages compose header (ThreadTitle/TokenUsageIndicator), MessageList, InputBox/PromptInput, follow-ups and todo lists, and defer browser-only input rendering to avoid hydration mismatches.

Message rendering & grouping: group flat messages into logical blocks (assistant/human/clarification/present-files/subagent), render chain-of-thought steps and tool-call results, and surface artifacts and uploads.
Prompt and attachment handling: collect text and files, support paste/drag-drop/IME/speech, share prompt state via provider when needed, and guard submission during IME composition or streaming.
Subtask/subagent integration: detect task tool calls, render SubtaskCard with live status and results, and integrate with backend subagent/status streams.
Artifact preview and actions: render code/markdown/HTML, open/download/copy artifacts, resolve virtual /mnt paths to backend-provided public URLs, and optionally trigger skill-install flows.
Global UX features: command palette and keyboard shortcuts registration (platform aware), suggestion chips with staggered animations, and localized documentation via Nextra.

insights.title: Client-only rendering must be gated to avoid hydration mismatch
insights.problem: Several dynamic components (InputBox/PromptInput and code that inspects navigator/userAgent) caused server/client markup/behavior mismatches and hydration errors.
insights.root_cause: Browser APIs and runtime-dependent UI were rendered on the server or inspected during SSR without guards.
insights.solution: Mark components as client-only when they depend on DOM/browser APIs and defer rendering of interactive input until mounted; guard navigator/userAgent access behind client mount checks.

insights.title: Thread-scoped settings must override global defaults
insights.problem: Pages sometimes used global local settings hooks, ignoring per-thread overrides (model selection and other per-thread preferences).
insights.root_cause: Components were wired to a global settings hook rather than the thread-scoped settings API.
insights.solution: Use the thread-scoped settings hooks (useThreadSettings) consistently in thread-specific pages and components so per-thread model/mode overrides apply.

insights.title: Respect IME composition and manage SpeechRecognition lifecycle
insights.problem: Enter submissions fired during IME composition and SpeechRecognition instances leaked or used stale closures.
insights.root_cause: Input submit handlers did not check IME composition state; speech recognition effects captured changing props/refs without stable dependencies or cleanup.
insights.solution: Prevent submit during composition and ensure SpeechRecognition instances are created/cleaned up deterministically with stable refs to callbacks.

insights.title: Artifact preview and copy/download must use correct source and citation rules
insights.problem: Non-write artifacts needed URL-based previews (iframe) but were only available as raw text; copying content sometimes included citation blocks that produced messy clipboard text.
insights.root_cause: Artifact loaders returned raw text for some artifacts while preview UX expects a URL; copy logic didn't consistently strip or normalize citations.
insights.solution: When previewing, prefer backend-provided artifact URLs for remote resources; copy/download handlers should use normalized content without unwanted citation blocks unless explicitly intended.

insights.title: Agent creation must persist and be read-back with eventual consistency handling
insights.problem: Creating an agent could expose internal/partial save artifacts or fail read-after-write leading to confusing UI state.
insights.root_cause: Backend writes are eventually consistent; UIs relied on immediate availability without retries and lacked an explicit Save action.
insights.solution: Expose explicit Save action and implement read-after-write retry/backoff when fetching the newly created agent to tolerate eventual consistency. Surface network-specific errors distinctly.

insights.title: Only render Subtask UI for task-type tool calls
insights.problem: Attempting to render SubtaskCard/UI for non-task tool call IDs caused runtime TypeErrors when task.status was accessed.
insights.root_cause: Message grouping attempted to render subtask UI for every tool_call id without filtering for 'task' tool calls.
insights.solution: Detect and render SubtaskCard only for recognized task tool calls; safeguard accesses to task status fields for non-task calls.

insights.title: Demo/static builds must disable server-backed actions like skill install
insights.problem: Skill-install affordances in static/demo builds could fail silently or confuse users when backend support is not available.
insights.root_cause: UI did not respect environment gating for server-backed actions.
insights.solution: Gate skill-install and other server-bound actions behind environment flags and show explanatory tooltips when disabled.

insights.title: Local dev rewrites are required for proper gateway/langgraph routing
insights.problem: Frontend API calls to LangGraph/Gateway needed environment-specific rewrites for local development to reach containerized services.
insights.root_cause: Next.js default routing would not map to internal docker service names; tests/local dev expect overrides.
insights.solution: Maintain next.config rewrites that map internal API prefixes to configured env URLs and allow public env overrides.

key_files.why: Primary generic chat page that composes Thread header, MessageList and defers InputBox rendering to avoid SSR hydration issues; central to thread stream lifecycle.
Agent-scoped chat page that initializes/resumes agent-specific threads, sends agent-context messages, and must respect per-thread settings and token usage UI.
Core PromptInput implementation handling attachments, IME composition, drag-and-drop, and optional speech — changes here affect message submission and attachment lifecycle.
Groups and renders conversation content into specialized UIs (MessageListItem, MessageGroup, SubtaskCard) and orchestrates presentation of streaming/loading states and follow-ups.
Handles artifact preview/download/copy and skill-install triggers; interacts with backend artifact URLs and must respect demo/env gating.

construct_relationships: Chat pages (generic and agent-specific) call useThreadStream and useThreadSettings to manage thread lifecycle and mount MessageList + InputBox; they update browser history on new thread creation via history.replaceState without router remount.
InputBox composes PromptInput; PromptInputProvider and PromptInputController can share prompt/attachment state across InputBox consumers and are used to programmatically set textarea input.

MessageList transforms flat messages with groupMessages and delegates rendering to MessageListItem, MessageGroup, ArtifactFileList and SubtaskCard; MessageGroup converts messages to chain-of-thought steps and recognizes tool_call types.
SubtaskCard renders content for 'task' tool calls and displays subagent status/results produced by the backend subagents/executor; MessageList only instantiates SubtaskCard for recognized task calls.

ArtifactFileDetail is used by message rendering components to preview files: CodeEditor for code, Streamdown for markdown, iframe for URL-served resources, and it triggers backend skill-install endpoints when enabled.
CommandPalette registers global keyboard shortcuts via a useGlobalShortcuts hook and triggers workspace navigation/actions (new chat, open settings) that alter app routing/state.
notable_relationships: Next.js rewrites (next.config.js) must map /api and /api/langgraph to Gateway and LangGraph service URLs; these rewrites are environment-dependent and used in dev vs production.
Artifact preview, file downloads, and skill-install flows depend on backend endpoints that serve artifact URLs and skill APIs; static/demo builds disable some of these operations.

The demo script (scripts/save-demo.js) fetches thread history and copies outputs/uploads from backend storage into public/demo for static demos, so modifications to artifact URL formats or backend storage paths affect demo capture.
ESLint/Prettier configurations and TypeScript build are enforced by CI; changes that affect formatting or rule conformance will need to pass frontend CI (pnpm build/format/lint/typecheck).
