recommendations: Preserve clear SSR/client boundaries: use 'use client' where DOM APIs or navigator are accessed and defer rendering of InputBox/PromptInput until mounted to avoid hydration mismatches.

Respect and use thread-scoped hooks (useThreadStream, useThreadSettings) for per-thread model/mode overrides and history.replaceState navigation behavior; avoid replacing with global settings hooks without ensuring equivalence.

When modifying prompt submission or key handling, ensure IME composition prevention is preserved and SpeechRecognition instances are created/cleaned deterministically to avoid leaks and stale closures.

Maintain artifact handling semantics: preview remote resources via backend-provided URLs, keep write-file virtual artifact mapping behavior, and ensure copy/download handlers normalize or strip citations as the UX expects.
Only render SubtaskCard for validated 'task' tool calls and guard accesses to task status fields to prevent runtime TypeErrors for other tool_call types.

Account for environment gating: skill-install and other server-backed UI actions must respect demo/static build flags; changes that affect these flows should consider both live and static/demo builds.

Run the full frontend CI/checks after edits: pnpm format, pnpm lint, pnpm typecheck and pnpm build (Next.js) to surface SSR/hydration, type, and formatting regressions; exercise chat flows that involve stream lifecycle, attachments, and subtask rendering against a running backend when possible.

docker.description: This folder contains Docker and runtime orchestration artifacts for DeerFlow: Docker Compose manifests for dev and prod stacks, Nginx reverse-proxy configs, and a standalone Sandbox Provisioner service (FastAPI) with its Dockerfile and documentation. It matters because these artifacts control service wiring, container runtime behavior, host<->container mounts, DNS/reload behavior in Docker, and the lifecycle of isolated Kubernetes-backed sandboxes used by the backend.
key_behaviors: Compose-driven multi-container orchestration for both dev and production: wiring internal service base URLs, environment propagation, and host bind mounts.
Reverse-proxy routing: Nginx config determines upstreams for gateway, langgraph, and frontend and must remain responsive to container IP changes.
Sandbox provisioning lifecycle: HTTP API that creates/destroys a per-sandbox Kubernetes Pod and a NodePort Service, initializing K8s client either from a mounted kubeconfig or in-cluster config.
Idempotent sandbox lookup: provisioner detects existing Pod/Service for a sandbox_id and returns an already-assigned nodePort/host rather than creating duplicates.
Runtime path and mount semantics: compose files and provisioner must agree on container mount paths (e.g., config.yaml locations) and host path handling must be robust across OSes.

insights.title: Host path handling and thread_id safety
insights.problem: Provisioner built hostPath volumes via simple string concatenation and lacked robust thread_id validation, which can break on Windows-style paths and enable unsafe hostPath names.
insights.root_cause: Assumptions about POSIX paths and unsanitized identifiers when composing hostPath volume strings.
insights.solution: Any edits must preserve functions that normalize/join host paths in a platform-aware way and keep or improve thread_id validation to prevent malformed paths or injection into hostPath constructs.

insights.title: Provisioner must initialize K8s client robustly and return accessible NodePort
insights.problem: Provisioner must support both mounted kubeconfig and in-cluster config and reliably wait for kubeconfig availability to create Pod and NodePort service; failures may cause unreachable sandboxes or partial cleanup.
insights.root_cause: Runtime variance between development (host-mounted kubeconfig) and cluster deployments; lifecycle timing issues during resource creation.
insights.solution: Maintain logic that chooses kubeconfig vs in-cluster, waits for kubeconfig availability where applicable, creates namespace if missing, and handles partial cleanup/errors while reporting them to callers.

insights.title: Idempotent create/destroy and partial-cleanup reporting
insights.problem: Sandbox creation must be idempotent and sandbox destruction can fail partially; callers depend on returned nodePort/URL and accurate error reporting.
insights.root_cause: Concurrent or repeated create/destroy requests and transient K8s errors.
insights.solution: Preserve idempotent detection of existing Pod/Service, maintain atomic-ish semantics for create/destroy where possible, and ensure cleanup errors are surfaced without leaving inconsistent state.

insights.title: Compose mounts, env propagation, and shell variable escaping
insights.problem: Dev compose needed explicit DEER_FLOW_HOME and internal base URLs and additional host bind mounts for CLI auth; production compose previously attempted shell substitution at parse time and mis-mounted config paths.
insights.root_cause: Incorrect or missing environment propagation, missing host mount entries in dev compose, and unescaped shell variables or wrong container paths in production compose.
insights.solution: Respect current compose semantics: ensure DEER_FLOW_HOME/internal URLs are provided to containers in dev, preserve CLI auth bind mounts, keep shell variables escaped where required, and preserve correct container mount paths for config files used by LangGraph/Gateway.

insights.title: Nginx must handle Docker DNS changes
insights.problem: Nginx cached container IPs and proxied to dead addresses after container restarts, resulting in persistent 502s until nginx was reloaded or re-resolved.
insights.root_cause: Default nginx upstream behavior does not re-resolve Docker service DNS; long-lived nginx process needed explicit resolver/reload configuration.
insights.solution: Keep the runtime DNS re-resolution configuration and any resolver directives intact in nginx configs so upstreams remain accurate after container restarts.

key_files.why: Implements the Sandbox Provisioner API and the core Kubernetes Pod/NodePort creation, idempotency checks, kubeconfig/in-cluster client initialization, and path/thread ID handling — backend depends on its responses for sandbox endpoints.
Builds the runtime image for the provisioner service (Python 3.12 base and EXPOSE 8002) — changes affect how the provisioner is deployed within Docker environments.

Development compose manifest that controls environment propagation (DEER_FLOW_HOME, internal base URLs) and host bind mounts necessary for CLI auth; edits affect local dev reproductions and credential availability.

Production compose manifest responsible for correct config mounts and escaped shell variable handling for containers like LangGraph; mistakes cause services to fail to locate config files or misparse env flags.
Central Nginx configuration controlling upstream resolution and proxy behavior; must preserve DNS re-resolution settings to avoid proxying to stale container IPs.

construct_relationships: docker-compose-dev.yaml injects DEER_FLOW_HOME and internal base URLs into container environments; services inside containers use these to locate user data and other internal endpoints.
Nginx configs (nginx.conf, nginx.local.conf) define upstreams (gateway, langgraph, frontend) that must match ports/hostnames exposed by the compose manifests and running containers.

provisioner/app.py depends on kubeconfig path (KUBECONFIG_PATH) or in-cluster config and exposes an HTTP API (FastAPI) that the backend can call to request a sandbox URL; its Dockerfile packages that service for containerized deployment.
docker-compose.yaml must mount application config files at the exact container paths expected by services (e.g., LangGraph/Gateway) so those services can locate config.yaml at runtime.
Dev compose bind-mounts for CLI auth directories (e.g., ~/.codex, ~/.codex) feed credentials into model CLIs used by services; absence of those mounts causes credential-not-found failures.
notable_relationships: The backend relies on the provisioner service to produce reachable sandbox endpoints; any change to provisioner behavior, API, or image port affects sandbox creation and downstream sandbox-based tools.
Nginx routing is upstream of frontend, LangGraph, and Gateway; its resolver behavior impacts availability of those services when containers restart under Docker.

Compose mount paths and environment propagation link container runtime expectations to repo config locations (config.yaml, .deer-flow). Misaligned mounts break service startup across LangGraph/Gateway.
Dev host bind mounts for CLI auth link developer host state into containerized model tooling; these are not required in production but are necessary for local dev reproduction of credential flows.
recommendations: When modifying provisioner/app.py preserve or validate the kubeconfig/in-cluster initialization flow, idempotent create/destroy semantics, and thread_id/path-joining behavior; verify behavior on both Linux and Windows host paths if hostPath logic is altered.

Any change that affects the provisioner API, returned URL format, or exposed port must be coordinated with the backend callers and the provisioner Dockerfile/compose entries; run sandbox create/destroy integration tests and verify NodePort reachability.

When editing compose files, maintain DEER_FLOW_HOME and internal base URL propagation in dev compose, keep auth directory bind mounts required for dev CLIs, ensure config file mounts target the exact container paths services expect, and escape shell variables in production compose to avoid parse-time substitution.
When changing nginx configs keep resolver/reload semantics that allow runtime DNS re-resolution for Docker upstreams; test container restart scenarios to ensure no persistent 502 errors.

After edits, run end-to-end smoke tests: docker-compose up (dev/prod as appropriate), provisioner sandbox lifecycle (create, lookup, destroy), and verify frontend/Gateway/LangGraph routing through nginx, and model CLI credential availability in dev mode.

..description: Repository root coordinating DeerFlow's backend agent runtime, IM channel integrations, gateway HTTP surface, sandbox abstractions, and an embedded harness client. This subtree contains the lead agent factory, middleware & prompt construction, sandbox path mapping and tools, channel lifecycle and ingestion logic, gateway services (SSE/run lifecycle/uploads), and tests that validate channel behavior — all of which together govern how external inputs are normalized, executed in sandboxes/subagents, and surfaced back to callers.
key_behaviors: Inbound IM events (channels) → ChannelManager ingest (attachments normalized into per-thread uploads) → MessageBus publishes to lead agent run.
Gateway endpoints normalize run input (context/configurable/assistant_id), build RunnableConfig, create RunRecord and stream SSE events to clients using StreamBridge/SSE formatting.

Lead agent factory assembles runtime model, tools, and ordered middleware (uploads, sandbox, summarization, memory, loop-detection, todo-list, etc.) and injects dynamic prompt sections (enabled skills, subagent rules).

Sandbox layer maps virtual container paths to host paths (or container mounts), enforces read-only mounts and permission semantics, and provides tools (bash, read/write, glob, grep) that sanitize commands/paths and mask host paths in outputs.

Subagent/task tooling starts background executors, polls lifecycle events, and supports cooperative cancellation; memory middleware queues filtered conversation for asynchronous memory updater which deduplicates and persists facts.

insights.title: Backend service URL resolution must be explicit and container-aware
insights.problem: Channels and services sometimes used 'localhost' or assumed environment defaults that fail in containerized deployments.
insights.root_cause: Resolution precedence and defaults relied on working-directory or host-local semantics instead of honoring config/env overrides and container DNS.
insights.solution: Resolve LangGraph/Gateway URLs from explicit config first, then environment variables, then safe defaults; treat 'localhost' as unsafe in compose contexts and prefer container service names where appropriate.

insights.title: Host-written uploads need permission and path normalization for non-local sandboxes
insights.problem: Files written to host uploads dir could be unreadable/unwritable inside isolated AIO sandboxes due to UID/GID mismatches and unsafe filenames.
insights.root_cause: Gateway wrote files with host perms and didn't relax permissions or consistently normalize/validate filenames before mirroring into sandboxes.
insights.solution: Ensure filename normalization and traversal guards are centralized; when mirroring into non-local sandboxes make files world-writable (or sandbox-accessible) and preserve companion metadata conversion consistently.

insights.title: Runtime context and thread_id are not always present
insights.problem: Code paths assumed runtime.context and runtime.context.thread_id always exist; operations (uploads resolution, task_tool thread_id, sandbox init) crashed when None.
insights.root_cause: Different invocation surfaces (LangGraph server vs in-process client) provide context/config in different places (context vs configurable), and some callers omit context entirely.
insights.solution: Guard against runtime.context being None, accept thread_id from either runtime.context or config.configurable, and null-check before mutating runtime context to avoid NPEs.

insights.title: Slow or blocking skill loading can impact prompt construction
insights.problem: Synchronous disk I/O when loading enabled skills slowed startup and could block request paths.
insights.root_cause: Prompt construction reloaded skill lists from disk on-demand and lacked a non-blocking refresh/cache strategy.
insights.solution: Maintain a background refresh worker and provide synchronous-safe cache access/invalidation primitives so prompt sections can be read without blocking critical request flows.

insights.title: Assistant ID and user-provided context must map into agent factory inputs
insights.problem: Custom assistant_id passed to runs was not injected into configurable.agent_name so per-agent artifacts (SOUL files, skills) were not loaded.
insights.root_cause: Gateway normalization constructed 'configurable' unconditionally and did not map assistant_id into configurable.agent_name.
insights.solution: When normalizing run inputs forward assistant_id into configurable.agent_name and avoid creating conflicting 'context' and 'configurable' entries that LangGraph rejects.

insights.title: Loop detection needs stable, defensive normalization
insights.problem: Middleware missed semantically identical repeated tool calls due to noisy argument representation and crashed when appending messages to non-string AIMessage.content.
insights.root_cause: Tool-call hashing used raw or inconsistent stringified args and assumed last message content was a string.
insights.solution: Normalize arguments deterministically (stable hashing, JSON canonicalization), defensively handle non-string AIMessage.content shapes, and maintain per-thread sliding windows with LRU eviction.

key_files.why: Central factory that builds the lead agent, resolves runtime model, inserts middleware chain and is the effective entrypoint for agent runs.
Constructs the dynamic system prompt including enabled skills cache and background refresh logic that influences agent behavior and which skills are visible at runtime.
Maps virtual to host paths, enforces read-only mounts, and masks host paths — changes here affect all sandbox file and command operations.
Translates inbound IM events into agent runs, ingests attachments into per-thread uploads, and formats streaming updates — core of IM-to-agent integration.
Encapsulates run lifecycle, SSE formatting, and input normalization (context/configurable/assistant_id) used by gateway routers to start/stream runs.

construct_relationships: ChannelManager ingests inbound attachments → writes into per-thread uploads dir → uploads router/sandbox tools expose these files to sandboxes and the UploadsMiddleware injects metadata into the last HumanMessage.

Gateway services normalize POST /runs input → build RunnableConfig (including configurable.agent_name) → creates RunRecord → invokes make_lead_agent-built agent (lead_agent.factory) to execute runs and streams events via StreamBridge/SSE.

lead_agent.make_lead_agent assembles middleware chain (UploadsMiddleware → SandboxMiddleware → SummarizationMiddleware → MemoryMiddleware → LoopDetectionMiddleware → ...) → middleware mutate runtime/context and ensure sandbox is initialized before tools execute.

Prompt cache (prompt.py) is read by lead_agent to render system prompt → enabled-skills refresh worker updates cache asynchronously; changes to skill files or skill management endpoints (skills router) must be visible to prompt cache invalidation flow.
Sandbox tools (sandbox/tools.py) and LocalSandbox path mappings are relied on by builtin tools (read_file/write_file/grep/glob) and by task_tool/subagent execution to validate and mask paths.
notable_relationships: Docker and compose wiring (docker/) influence ChannelService URL resolution and sandbox provider selection: edits to service URL resolution must respect composing rules and container DNS (use container names rather than localhost).
Uploads REST endpoints (gateway/routers/uploads.py) must remain consistent with ChannelManager ingestion and harness uploads manager to avoid duplicate normalization logic and security gaps.

Frontend expects specific SSE framing and stream event shapes from gateway/services.sse_consumer; changes to SSE formatting or event structure will impact UI streaming, desktop notifications, and client reconnection logic.

Tests under backend/tests validate channel message ordering, ChannelStore persistence semantics and _extract_response_text behavior — modifications to ChannelManager or MessageBus should preserve those behaviors.
Sandbox provider choices (Local vs AIO) change file permission semantics and required mirroring logic; Dockerized AIO sandboxes require mirrored uploads to be world-writable or appropriately chowned.
recommendations: Always respect runtime.context may be None: guard accesses and accept thread_id from either runtime.context or config.configurable when present.
When touching uploads, sandbox, or channel ingestion code ensure filename normalization/traversal checks are centralized and preserved, and preserve companion metadata (converted markdown outlines).
When changing sandbox path mapping or masking, keep reverse-resolution behavior intact (container paths in outputs) and enforce read-only mount semantics (raise EROFS where appropriate).

Any change to run input normalization must preserve mapping of assistant_id → configurable.agent_name and avoid generating both 'context' and 'configurable' in ways rejected by LangGraph; validate with integration tests against LangGraph >=0.6.0 semantics.
Respect background workers and caches: prompt enabled-skills cache must remain non-blocking with explicit invalidate/prime helpers; avoid synchronous disk I/O on hot paths.
Preserve SSE frame shape and StreamEvent variants expected by frontend/clients; run gateway and frontend dev servers to validate streaming behavior after edits.
When modifying task/subagent tooling, maintain cooperative cancellation behavior and polling semantics; verify subagent lifecycle events continue to be emitted and stored.

Run unit tests and linters: at minimum run backend tests (pytest via make test), linting/formatting (make lint/format), and exercise Docker dev compose for sandbox/permission-related changes to catch host vs container filesystem issues.
